title: /handoff skill in mu — wire-format artifact exporter
description:
Small skill in mu that produces a HANDOFF artifact (mu-nt7j wire format) from the current session state. User-facing entry point for the niche cases identified in the design discussion.

## Scope

Just the entry point. The mechanics live in the wire-format crate (mu-nt7j) and the skill-system runtime (mu-1rmo).

## What this skill does

1. Capture current mu session state: repo state, open beads, recent tool calls, focused files, mode settings, memory anchors
2. Render to HANDOFF_<datetime>.json (canonical) and HANDOFF_<datetime>.md (prose, derived from JSON)
3. Optionally print the prose to terminal (for copy-paste workflows — the historical /handoff use case)

## What this skill does NOT do

- Drive any compaction (mu native uses internal compaction; wrapper uses /clear-and-paste)
- Auto-load on next session (mu uses event-log resume)
- Manage cross-mu-agent handoff (delegate handles that)

These all fall under the wider exo-interop layer, not this skill.

## When to use

- User wants to hand off to a non-mu runtime (claude-code, mobile claude.ai, etc.)
- User wants a durable record of session state for archive / postmortem
- User wants a copy-pasteable block for a different account or environment

For everything else mu has native primitives that don't need an artifact.

## Dependencies

- mu-1rmo (skill system): blocks — needs the skill discovery + dispatch mechanism
- mu-nt7j (wire format): blocks — defines what gets written

title: t4c catalog: add br (beads issue tracker) as a curated capability — bash.br, so 't4c find "track an issue / file a task / what work is ready"' surfaces it. Pair with agent.memory (already curated). Include --help-ai if br speaks it, else br --help.
mu-phl v0 Phase D — wire RecallProviders into daemon; add cwd to CreateSessionRequest / DelegateSessionRequest
description:
Step 5 of mu-phl v0. Wires the recall providers into the daemon's session-creation path and adds optional `cwd` to the session-creation protocol messages.

## What

- `crates/mu-coding/src/serve/handlers/session.rs`: in `build_and_register_session`, instantiate both `SubprocessRecallProvider` and `ProjectFileRecallProvider`, call `.recall(cwd, capability)` on each, build a `ProjectContext`, attach to `AgentConfig::project_context`.
- `crates/mu-core/src/protocol/session.rs`: add `pub cwd: Option<PathBuf>` (with `#[serde(default)]`) to `CreateSessionRequest` and `DelegateSessionRequest`. Daemon falls back to `std::env::current_dir()` when absent — back-compat preserved.
- `crates/mu-coding/src/serve/daemon_info.rs`: hold `Arc<Vec<Arc<dyn RecallProvider>>>` so tests can inject stub providers.

## Why

Pre-built `ProjectContext` at session-creation time keeps the agent loop hot-path free of subprocess spawning. Optional `cwd` field preserves back-compat for clients that don't yet send it.

## Acceptance

- `cargo test -p mu-coding session::` covers the daemon-side wiring (with a stub `RecallProvider`).
- Existing daemon tests pass — back-compat preserved.
- Spec amendment per mu rule #9: this is a wire-protocol change (new field on `CreateSessionRequest`). Spec update in `specs/mu-XXX.md` or inline doc-comment for the protocol type.
- One commit with `## Files` block and `(closes <this-bead>)`.

## Blocked by

- mu-phl v0 Phase C.1 (step 3, subprocess provider)
- mu-phl v0 Phase C.2 (step 4, project-files provider)

## Stop-criterion #9 alert

This step amends the wire-protocol surface. Either include the spec update in the same commit, or halt and document the proposed change for human review.
close_reason: Landed (Phase D) on feat/mu-phl-v0. CreateSessionRequest + DelegateSessionRequest gain optional cwd: PathBuf via #[serde(default)]. DaemonInfo holds Vec<Arc<dyn RecallProvider>>; production wires up both v0 providers. build_project_context helper iterates the providers against the effective cwd + session capability, returns Option<ProjectContext>. Spec amendment landed as specs/mu-045-session-cwd.md (per repo-rule #9). 769 tests pass; clippy -D warnings clean. Out-of-band: ask.rs constructs CreateSessionRequest with cwd:None (Phase E switches to current_dir); protocol.rs test fixtures updated.

title: mu-tui-f3-pi-style: pi-inspired section-marker style for F3 transcript (lighter boxes, stronger section headers)
description:
Adopt pi_agent_rust's section-marker style for the F3 transcript pane. Replace mu's full bordered boxes (`┌─ you ` … `└─`) with lighter pi-style inline prefixes (`**You:**`, `**Assistant:**`, `[Running tool...]` … `[tool done]`) + rule lines between major sections + panels-with-titled-borders only where appropriate.

## Operator preference (2026-05-14)

> "for the F3 tab to look like the pi_ts interface. More specifically, mostly the layout; I prefer how you highlight the sections that come back."

The full bordered-box style mu uses today is visually heavier than pi's section-header approach. pi delineates sections with bold inline prefixes and accent colors but doesn't enclose entire content blocks in box-drawing characters.

## Pi style reference

From `~/src/flywheel/pi_agent_rust/src/tui.rs`:

- **User echo**: `[bold]You:[/] <message>` followed by blank line (lines 301-307)
- **Assistant**: `[bold]Assistant:[/] ` then markdown-streamed text (lines 310-317)
- **Tool start**: `[bold yellow][[Running <name>...]][/]` (lines 148-152)
- **Tool end (ok)**: `[bold green][[<name> done]][/]` (lines 155-161)
- **Tool end (err)**: `[bold red][[<name> failed]][/]`
- **Errors**: `[bold red]Error:[/] <text>` (lines 166-172)
- **Warnings**: `[bold yellow]Warning:[/] <text>`
- **Success**: `[bold green]<text>[/]`
- **Info**: `[bold blue]<text>[/]`
- **Panels** (when used): cyan border + title (lines 206-217)
- **Rule lines**: `─── <title> ───` (lines 240-249)

## Scope

### In

- Replace `push_block(label, color, body, wrap_width)` in `crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs` with a `push_section(label, color, body, wrap_width)` that emits:
  - First line: `<color-bold>label:</color-bold>` (no `┌─ ` decoration)
  - Body lines: indented (`  `), color-tinted, with wrap respecting the inner width
  - Final spacer line
  - Optional: a thin rule line `─` at end of section (matches pi's between-section visual)
- Tool sections use status-colored brackets: `[Running <name>...]` yellow, `[<name> done]` green, `[<name> failed]` red.
- User: bold cyan "You:" prefix, content indented 2 spaces.
- Assistant: bold white "Assistant:" prefix, content indented 2 spaces. Markdown rendering composes (mu-mvk).
- Streaming: bold yellow "Assistant (streaming…):" prefix while live; transitions to "Assistant:" on session.done.
- Error: bold red "Error:" prefix.
- "Done" line: keep the existing `─── done · stop_reason · elapsed ───` rule.

### Out

- Markdown rendering (already mu-mvk).
- Syntax highlighting in code blocks (could be a follow-on bead — pi uses `rich_rust`'s syntect integration).
- Replacing the F3 outer Block::ALL border (the pane itself stays bordered; only inner-block boxes go).

## Composition

- **mu-mvk** (markdown rendering): when this lands, pi-style + markdown together get close to pi's full visual.
- **mu-2zs** (wrap-survival, already closed): pi's inline-prefix style sidesteps wrap-border-loss entirely — no `│` per-line means no wrap break. This bead simplifies that concern away.
- **mu-u8a** (color polish): pi's palette (cyan/yellow/green/red/white) is closer to muted-but-distinct than mu's current saturated set. Probably aligns naturally.

## Why P3

Visual polish, not functional. Daily-driver feel matters but doesn't unblock anything. Composable with mu-mvk for full pi-fidelity output.

## Acceptance

- F3 transcript renders without bordered boxes; uses pi-style bold-prefix-then-indent sections.
- Tool sections show bracketed status markers in pi's color scheme.
- Streaming-to-finalized transition is smooth (color shift from yellow to white on session.done).
- Long content wraps cleanly without losing prefix association (the heavy bordered-box concerns of mu-2zs become moot).
- cargo test --workspace green; clippy -D warnings; fmt --check.
- Visual test: real session in F3 looks recognizably "pi-shaped."

## References

- pi reference: ~/src/flywheel/pi_agent_rust/src/tui.rs (rich_rust-backed; reads as the design spec)
- Current mu impl: crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs `push_block` + `render_transcript_lines`
- Composes with: mu-mvk (markdown), mu-u8a (color), mu-cha (F2 session tree)

title: mu-nk3 (re-attempt): split crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs (2038 LOC) into focused modules
description:
Re-attempt of mu-nk3 after the 2026-05-16 overnight worker produced a commit (PR #52) whose description was fictional — claimed to create 6 module files in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/` but actually created zero new files. PR #52 closed; this bead supersedes mu-nk3.

## Target shape (unchanged from original mu-nk3)

Split `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs` (currently ~2050 LOC after mu-wk2 lands) into:

- `agent/loop_/mod.rs` — public orchestrator: `AgentLoop` struct, public event/input/config types, `run()` entrypoint, planner helpers
- `agent/loop_/invoke.rs` — provider invocation: stream handling, status emission, buffered-input bridge
- `agent/loop_/execute_tools.rs` — tool-call dispatch: capability/retry gates, ToolHistory window, result collection
- `agent/loop_/autonomy.rs` — `RunMode` enum + autonomy-iteration accessors (iteration logic stays in mod.rs::run())
- `agent/loop_/compaction_integration.rs` — `CompactionBaseline` snapshot + post-compact rope extension

`agent/loop_tests.rs` (also large) stays where it is for this PR; splitting tests alongside is a follow-up if needed.

## Discipline

- Pure refactor. **No behavior changes.** Public surface byte-for-byte identical.
- `cargo test --workspace` green at every commit
- Run `./scripts/pre-pr-check.sh` before opening PR (fmt + clippy -D warnings + test)
- Verification gate for the human/agent doing this: **paste `git diff-tree --no-commit-id --name-status -r HEAD`** in the PR body to demonstrate the new files actually exist in the commit, not just in the message

## Stop-criterion #2 watch

If the split requires touching any public trait OR a public surface in another crate, halt and decide architecturally. The intent is a private intra-module split.

## Provenance

- Original mu-nk3 bead body had the same intent — see `br show mu-nk3` if still around
- Postmortem 2026-05-16 to be written (this bead will be referenced from it)

title: mu-agent-loop: inject 'no tools available' clause when tool_specs is empty (prevents hallucinated tool_use loops)
description:
## Problem

When a `mu ask` (or any session) runs without registered tools (`tool_count == 0` in `context_assembly`), some providers/models will still emit `stop_reason: "tool_use"` and call hallucinated tools. The agent loop's tool dispatcher returns "tool not found" errors that the model interprets as "try a different tool name", and the loop spins for many turns burning prompt tokens for no productive work.

Empirical evidence: 2026-05-22 validation run for mu-q3v6 against `google/gemini-3.5-flash` via openrouter. Daemon `f7b1715a207c7d97`, session-1. Single user prompt `"hello?"` triggered **7 model calls** (6 turns of hallucinated tool_use, 1 final end_turn) because Gemini brute-forced tool names:

```
Turn 1: tool_use → "thebri_project_mcp_and_custom_jobs:system_info"
Turn 2: tool_use → "thebri_project_mcp_and_custom_jobs:execute_bash"
Turn 3: tool_use → "thebri_project_mcp_and_custom_jobs:execute_command"
Turn 4: tool_use → "bash"
Turn 5: tool_use → "execute_command"
Turn 6: tool_use → "execute_bash"
Turn 7: end_turn (gave up)
```

All six tool_call events resolved to `"tool not found"`. The conversation accumulated 200,890 prompt tokens / $0.20 / 38.3s for what should have been a single-turn "hi" exchange.

## Hypothesis

When `tool_count == 0`, some providers don't suppress tool_use from the model side (or the underlying model ignores the empty tools array and falls back to its training prior of "if asked something, try tool X"). The fix that costs nothing and works across all providers: inject an explicit clause into the wire-payload system block telling the model that no tools are available in this session, so it stops trying.

## Proposal

In `crates/mu-core/src/context/assembly.rs` (or wherever the System span is composed for the rope), when the agent loop's `tool_specs.is_empty()`, append the following sentence to the system prompt span:

```
You have no tools available in this session. Answer the user's question directly using only the context provided above. Do not emit tool calls.
```

Some variants worth considering during impl:
- Length: keep tight (~1-2 sentences) to minimize the cacheable-prefix bloat.
- Phrasing: borrow Anthropic's strict "you do not have access to tools" phrasing, which is the most-trained-against pattern.
- Placement: end of the System span, not the start — so model attention lands on it after the recall context.

Alternative or complementary: ensure each provider's `build_request_body` correctly emits an empty `tools: []` field where the API supports it. Anthropic does this (the tools array is omitted entirely when empty). OpenAI's Responses API tools field can be elided. OpenRouter passes through to the underlying model. Verify all three providers actually send "no tools" semantics — if any send `tools: ["..."]` accidentally, that's a wire-projection bug to fix as part of this.

## Acceptance

- New behavior: when `tool_specs` is empty, the assembled rope's System span ends with the "no tools available" clause.
- Reproducer test: a `mu ask` against gemini-3.5-flash with no tools doesn't loop (≤ 2 model calls total: one to receive the answer, possibly one more if the model fact-checks itself).
- Token cost for the gemini-3.5-flash "hello?" reproducer drops below 50k prompt tokens total.
- The wire-payload for each provider's build_request_body either omits `tools` entirely or sends `tools: []`. Verify with mocked HTTP capture in the existing provider test suites.

## Out of scope

- Defensive cap on the loop itself (separate bead, sibling-bead see below).
- Adding tools to `mu ask` by default (orthogonal product decision).

## Provenance

Filed 2026-05-22 by tcovert. Discovered during mu-q3v6's post-merge validation of the wire-projection stack. The token-volume drop from PR #115/#116 was confirmed (1.74M → 200k = -88%), but the 7-call loop for "hello?" surfaced this orthogonal cost-leak.

Cross-refs:
- bead mu-q3v6 (validation measurement)
- daemon f7b1715a207c7d97 session-1.jsonl (the 7-call reproducer JSONL)
- sibling bead [filed next]: agent-loop tool-not-found termination guard
close_reason: done

title: spec-format: migrate inline rust blocks in specs/ to AsciiDoc with tagged-region includes
description:
25 spec files under `specs/` currently embed rust source as fenced ` ```rust ` blocks (e.g. `specs/mu-001-protocol-types.md` line 76-295 is ~220 lines of code from `crates/mu-core/src/protocol.rs`). This is a drift trap: every protocol/trait change requires manual spec re-sync, which workers don't reliably do (and which stop-criterion #9 in `specs/process/goal-protocol.md` then fires on or fails to fire on).

## Chosen approach

**AsciiDoc + `include::file.rs[tag=foo]` directives**, with `// tag::foo[]` ... `// end::foo[]` markers on the rust side. Mature, native, refactor-safe (tagged regions survive line moves).

Alternatives surveyed and rejected:
- mdbook `{{#include file.rs:120:180}}` — fragile (line ranges break on refactor)
- `#[doc = include_str!(...)]` doctests — flips direction; forces spec to be valid rust which constrains prose
- CI-side regex regression — belt-and-suspenders, useful later but not the primary mechanism

## Incremental rollout (operator preference 2026-05-16)

1. **Pilot on one block** — pick a single inline rust block in one spec, convert to AsciiDoc + tagged include. Wire up the minimal build pipeline (or `make docs` script) that resolves the include.
2. **Surface issues** — toolchain pain points, formatting friction, what to do about rust-doc comments in tagged regions, etc.
3. **Correct + adjust** the pattern based on the pilot.
4. **Expand cautiously** — convert ~3 more blocks, verify the pattern holds.
5. **Bulk migration** — only when the pattern is confident, convert remaining ~21 blocks in one PR.

Do NOT attempt a 25-spec migration up front.

## Affected files (current state)

25 spec files have ` ```rust ` blocks:
mu-001, mu-002 (both delegation + stdio-transport variants), mu-003, mu-004, mu-005, mu-006, mu-007, mu-008, mu-009, mu-011, mu-013, mu-015, mu-016, mu-017, mu-018, mu-019, mu-022, mu-026, mu-029, mu-031, mu-035, mu-036, mu-037, mu-038.

## Why this matters

Stop-criterion #9 in mu's goal-protocol ("provider/wire-protocol change without spec amend") currently fires on manual omission. With tagged-includes, the spec text mechanically reflects the source — drift becomes impossible at the displayed-snippet level (prose around the snippet can still drift, but that's a smaller surface).

## Blocked by

None. But sequencing preference: complete the 2026-05-16 overnight-rework cycle (PRs #51/#52/#53) before opening this — single-file change, no scope conflict with anyone.

## Acceptance

- Pilot PR converts ONE block, lands cleanly, build pipeline resolves the include at doc-render time
- Pattern documented in `specs/README.md` or similar
- Stop-criterion #9 in `specs/process/goal-protocol.md` updated to reference the new mechanism
- Bulk migration PR later

title: mu-clip-coding-tests-deadcode: 2 dead_code in mu-coding test files (run_mu_ask, read_n_lines)
description:
Two dead_code warnings in mu-coding test files. Likely abandoned test helpers; probably delete (test fixtures that aren't called are pure noise).

## Items

1. `run_mu_ask` (`crates/mu-coding/tests/.../mod.rs:29`) — function defined but never called from any test.
2. `read_n_lines` (`crates/mu-coding/tests/serve_smoke.rs:62`) — async helper defined but never called.

## Decision

For each: either (a) delete if truly unused, OR (b) `#[allow(dead_code)]` if there's a recent reason to keep it (e.g. just removed the last caller and a future test will need it back).

Default: delete. Test code shouldn't accumulate dead helpers — VCS history holds the prior version if needed.

## Acceptance

- Both dead_code items addressed (deleted or allow-with-comment)
- cargo clippy passes (for these specific lints)
- cargo test --workspace green (especially: confirm no test depends transitively on either helper)
- One commit closes this bead
close_reason: Landed in jj revision vqnttvrx. read_n_lines deleted (no callers); run_mu_ask kept with #[allow(dead_code)] matching the existing convention for shared test helpers used by some-but-not-all binaries. cargo test --workspace 361 green; both dead_code lints gone.

title: mu-tui F3 multi-line input overwrites the status bar above it instead of pushing layout up
description:
## Symptom

In F3 (session view), when the prompt input grows past a single line — e.g., typing a long message during an active conversation — the second/third line of the input is rendered ON TOP of the divider/status bar above the input area. Characters from the input collide with the divider's `─` characters and the `F3` label at the start of the status line, producing scrambled output that's hard to read while typing.

Observed 2026-05-20 by operator while chatting in session-2 (daemon ebccc9). User typed:

> "One nice thing is that there have been a few occasions where we've gone 'why did that happen?' or 'what just happened?', and by reviewing the event logs we were able to tell immediately. Or, we found that there was a key event that was missing. And we were able to add it in quickly and then use it on subsequent runs to analyze the system."

Mid-paragraph, the second visible line shows the input characters interleaved with the divider/status-bar characters from the row above:

```
─── done · end_turn · 27129ms ───
(task_telemetry)
─────────────────────────────────  (divider)
─────────────────────────────────  (another divider — partially overwritten)
─>─One─nice─thing─is─that─there─have─been─a─few─occassions─where─we've─gone,─"why─did─that─happen?",─or─"what─just─happened?",─and─by─reviewing─the─event─logs─we─were─able─to─tell─immediately.─Or,─we
 F3 foundsthatstherenwas a keyaeventethatgwas5missing.eAnd0we0were able to add it in quickly and then use it on subsequent runs to analyze the system.
```

Note "F3 foundsthatstherenwas" — that's the `F3` label baked into the status bar with `found that there was` typed over it; same for the `─` em-dash sequences embedded in the input text on line 1.

## Suspected cause

The input render path doesn't account for input growing beyond a single line. The layout reserves N lines for input area (probably 1) and N for status bar / divider; when the input fills N+1 lines, the overflow paints into the status bar row without first clearing or pushing the surrounding layout up.

Likely files (per recent F3 work in mu-tui):
- `crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs` — the F3 render path, specifically the input area + status bar layout
- Look at how `render_session_detail` (or whichever render fn handles F3) computes the input rect vs status-bar rect
- mu-o1y7 (the F3 inline-render work from this week) touched this area; the input-multi-line case may not have been covered

Cross-ref the F3 inline-render gap bead mu-304g — it's about streaming render but in the same render path, possibly same Inline-mode layout calculations.

## Acceptance

- Typing a multi-line message in F3 grows the input area DOWN (or pushes the status bar UP) such that input text never overwrites the divider, status bar text, or any prior content.
- Typing a message that wraps to 3+ lines stays legible — each line of input occupies a fresh row with no character collision.
- The `F3 · session ... · done $X.XX` status line remains intact while typing.
- Cursor position remains accurate after wrap.

## Repro

In a session with mu-tui daily-driver, F3 into any session, press `i` to enter input mode, type a paragraph long enough to wrap to 2-3 lines. Observe the second line overlaps the divider/status bar above.

title: mu-tui session-rehydration phase C: writable rehydration (mu-u1ld follow-up)
description:
## Background

mu-u1ld phase A + B (PR #90 / #91) shipped **read-only** session rehydration at daemon startup: scan `~/.local/share/mu/events/<daemon_id>/` for JSONL files, project events into the registry, surface in `session.list` / `session.events` / `session.stats`. Sessions are visible and queryable but inert — you can't `session.ask` against them; they're ghosts.

## Goal

Make rehydrated sessions **writable**: accept new prompts and continue execution from where the on-disk event log left off.

## What that requires

- Reconstruct the runtime side state that phase A skipped: provider client, output queue, agent loop task. Today these only exist for sessions created in-process via `session.create`; rehydrated sessions only have the projection.
- Decide rehydration policy for sessions that were mid-stream when the prior daemon died (incomplete Anthropic SSE, partial tool call, no DoneEvent). Options: refuse to rewrite, surface as 'recoverable' to the operator, auto-continue from last known good event boundary.
- Provider-token + auth wiring: the new daemon process needs to acquire credentials before resuming. Today `mu serve` reads creds at startup; rehydration would need to bind them per-rehydrated-session.
- Capability state: rehydrated sessions need their original Capability set. Today phase A doesn't reconstruct Capability, so even if we wired up execution, the new agent loop would have no authority.
- Tests: round-trip `create → ask → kill daemon → restart → ask again → DoneEvent extends prior event log`.

## Acceptance

- `session.ask` on a rehydrated session works the same as on an in-process one
- Append to the same JSONL (no new event log per restart for the same session_id)
- Mid-stream recovery: documented behavior + tests for at least the simple case (clean DoneEvent on disk)
- Capability + provider creds reconstructed at rehydration time
- No new unsafe / panic paths

## Scope notes

This is the architecturally largest of the three mu-u1ld follow-ups. Worth its own phased PR series rather than one big-bang change. Phase A + B together gave us the projection foundation; phase C is making rehydration symmetric with creation.

## Provenance

Filed 2026-05-18 from mu-u1ld close note (deferred follow-up; handoff document and operator request).

title: mu-tui F5 'sess' column header ambiguous — reads as session-ID, actually session-count
description:
## Symptom

F5 (Usage / Cache view) shows a column labeled `sess` with integer values like `2`, `4`, `3` per provider+model row. Operator misread `claude-opus-4-7  ...  sess=2` as "the opus session is session-2" when in fact it's "there are 2 opus sessions in scope."

Cross-checked against F1's live-sessions panel (snapshot 2026-05-20):
- claude-opus-4-7: session-3 (done $27.05) + session-9 (done $5.04) → 2 sessions ✓
- codex-opus-4-7: session-2/6/7/8 → 4 sessions ✓
- gpt-5.5: session-1/4/5 → 3 sessions ✓

So the counts are correct. Only the label is misleading.

## Suggested fix

Rename the F5 column header from `sess` to one of: `n`, `#sess`, `nsess`, `count`, `sess#` — whichever fits the spacing constraint and reads unambiguously as a count rather than a session identifier.

Look at the F5 render path in `crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs` (table column header generation for the Usage view) and update the label string.

## Acceptance

- Column header in F5 unambiguously reads as a count.
- A new operator viewing F5 cannot misinterpret the column as referring to a single session by ID.

## Discovered

2026-05-20 by tcovert while wrapping up a session; not blocking but mildly annoying.

title: mu-tui: set terminal title to μ - <username> on TUI startup; reset on exit
description:
Set the terminal/pane title to `μ - <username>` when mu's TUI enters alternate screen, mirroring pi_ts's `π - <username>` convention. Reset to empty on exit (alternate-screen leave, panic handler, normal shutdown).

## Scope

IN:
- One call in the TUI startup path (probably in `crates/mu-tui/` wherever the alternate-screen entry happens) using crossterm's `SetTitle` command. `SetTitle` wraps OSC 0 (`\x1b]0;TITLE\x07`), which zellij, kitty, tmux, foot, alacritty all honor.
- Symmetric reset on shutdown: `SetTitle("")` in the same place TUI tears down (and in the panic handler so an abnormal exit doesn't leave the title hanging).
- Username via the `whoami` crate (probably already in the dep tree) or `std::env::var("USER")`.
- A snapshot or byte-level test asserting the emitted escape-sequence shape if practical; otherwise rely on visual verification.

OUT:
- Dynamic title updates (e.g., showing current session id or model). v2 if useful.
- Title-setting from inside individual TUI panels (this is one global startup-time set).

## Acceptance

- Terminal title shows `μ - <username>` when running mu's TUI inside zellij/kitty
- Title resets cleanly on normal exit AND panic
- cargo test --workspace green
- One commit closes this bead

## References

- pi_ts/pi_agent_rust pattern: cached JS module at `~/.pi/agent/cache/modules/...` calls `ctx.ui.setTitle(\`π - ${name}\`)` 150ms after some event. The Rust-side `apply_extension_title_effect` (`pi_agent_rust/src/interactive/agent.rs:1089`) only sets a status message — actual OSC emission likely happens in a JS layer or shell hook there. For mu, doing it directly in Rust via crossterm is cleaner.
- crossterm: `crossterm::terminal::SetTitle` is the API.
- Greek letter choice: μ (U+03BC, lowercase mu) parallels π exactly.

## Notes

Small in-context job (~10-15 lines including panic-handler reset). Not /goal-worthy unless folded into a larger TUI batch. Queued post-mu-cap-aws (experiment 6 in flight as of filing).
close_reason: Landed in jj revision nnnxloko. SetTitle on startup + reset on shutdown + re-set on $EDITOR resume. mu_terminal_title() helper uses USER with fallback. cargo fmt + clippy -D warnings + 361 tests green. Panic-handler cleanup deferred to a separate bead.

title: ai-review gate broken: mu ask --bare truncates reviewer output to 1 word → all PRs ESCALATE (exit 3)
description:
Discovered 2026-06-06 overnight (worker cc-personal-3366ff95 on mu-sqn). scripts/ai-review.sh PANEL gate returns exit 3 (SPLIT→ESCALATE) for a TRIVIAL 14-line Cargo.toml diff because BOTH reviewers emit no parseable VERDICT line.

Reproduced twice (warm, ~16s each — far too fast for a real 30B/20B review of 200KB context):
  run 1: qwen3-coder:30b → "Based" then stop (UNCLEAR); gpt-oss:20b → empty (UNCLEAR)
  run 2: qwen3-coder:30b → "Looking" then stop (UNCLEAR); gpt-oss:20b → empty (UNCLEAR)

Each reviewer captures only the FIRST WORD of the model's stream, then the mu serve session is torn down. stderr (/tmp/ai-review-stderr.72167) shows:
  rmcp::service: task cancelled
  ERROR rmcp::service: fail to close sink e=Tokio join error: task 37 was cancelled
  rmcp::service: serve finished quit_reason=Cancelled

ollama at 10.1.1.143 IS reachable. timeout in ai-review.sh is 300s (line 169/171) — NOT the cause (sessions die in seconds). ai-review.sh:20 already flags 'mu ask historically exits non-zero on a shutdown wart' — this looks like that wart: `mu ask --bare` shuts the session down before draining the model's streamed response, capturing only the first token(s).

IMPACT: gate-wide. If reproducible across diffs (high confidence — both models, both runs, trivial diff), NO overnight worker PR can pass `just ci-aipr` tonight; every PR escalates. Likely affects all `mu ask --bare` streaming consumers, not just ai-review.

NEXT: repro `mu ask --bare --provider ollama --model qwen3-coder:30b --thinking low "say VERDICT: APPROVE"` directly and confirm output truncation; inspect mu ask --bare session-shutdown path for premature cancel of the response stream. Filed under goal 8qyy as a blocker so it doesn't disappear; director to triage.
close_reason: done

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
CORRECTION to root cause (worker cc-personal-3366ff95, 2026-06-06) — my initial title/diagnosis blaming `mu ask --bare` streaming is DISPROVEN. Two isolation tests:

1. Direct `mu ask --bare --provider ollama --model qwen3-coder:30b --thinking low "<short prompt asking for VERDICT: APPROVE>"` → returns FULL output including the VERDICT line, exit 0. So `--bare` streaming/shutdown is NOT broken for normal prompts.

2. `MU_REVIEW_FULL_FILES=0 scripts/ai-review.sh` (same diff, full-file context DISABLED) → exit 0, PANEL PASS, BOTH reviewers (qwen3-coder:30b + gpt-oss:20b) produce full substantive reviews ending in VERDICT: APPROVE.

⇒ ACTUAL root cause: the default MU_REVIEW_FULL_FILES=1 path appends up to MU_REVIEW_CONTEXT_MAX_BYTES (default 200000) of full changed-file content to the prompt (ai-review.sh:123-137). With that large context, the reviewer mu-serve session is torn down after emitting only the first streamed word ("Based"/"Looking") — stderr: rmcp::service task cancelled / "fail to close sink: Tokio join error: task was cancelled" / serve finished quit_reason=Cancelled. NOT the 300s timeout (sessions die in seconds-to-~90s, well under 300s). The large prompt seems to trigger premature session cancellation in the mu ask session path.

REPRO: run `just ci-aipr` on ANY diff that yields a sizeable full-file context; compare to `MU_REVIEW_FULL_FILES=0 just ci-aipr`. Suspect the mu ask session-shutdown/cancel path interacting with large input (context-window pressure on the model, or a stream/cancel race when the first response chunk is large/slow).

WORKAROUND (interim): set MU_REVIEW_FULL_FILES=0 for self-contained diffs, or lower MU_REVIEW_CONTEXT_MAX_BYTES. REAL FIX: ensure the reviewer session drains the full model response before shutdown regardless of input size. Title is stale ("mu ask --bare truncates") — real scope is "ai-review full-file context (FULL_FILES=1) → premature session cancel → false ESCALATE." Still P1/gate-wide.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
ROOT CAUSE FOUND (claude-personal director session 2026-06-06 evening) — both prior diagnoses superseded. It is OLLAMA CONTEXT-WINDOW TRUNCATION, not a mu cancel/stream bug.

TERRAIN PROOF (direct curl to http://10.1.1.143:11434/v1/chat/completions, bypassing mu entirely, 180KB prompt asking for 'VERDICT: APPROVE'):
  finish_reason: length / content: 'I' / usage: {prompt_tokens: 32767, completion_tokens: 1, total_tokens: 32768}

MECHANISM: qwen3-coder:30b runs with num_ctx=32768 on the ollama box. FULL_FILES=1 review prompts (~200KB ≈ 50k tok) exceed it; ollama SILENTLY truncates the prompt to 32767 tokens — filling the window and leaving exactly 1 token of generation budget. Model emits one word ('Based'/'Looking'/'I'), stream ends CLEANLY with finish_reason=length. No error on any layer: mu maps length→StopReason::MaxTokens (openrouter.rs map_finish_reason) but mu ask --bare exits 0 and prints the lone token; gate sees no VERDICT line → UNCLEAR → both reviewers UNCLEAR → SPLIT → exit 3.

Bisect: 108KB prompt (~27k tok) → full 'VERDICT: APPROVE'. 180KB (~45k tok) → 'I'. The rmcp 'task cancelled' stderr lines appear in PASSING runs too — normal shutdown noise, red herring. The 150000 compaction-threshold log is session-start policy registration, also red herring.

FIX SPACE: (a) mu: surface StopReason::MaxTokens in ask --bare — stderr warning + nonzero exit so truncation is never silent; protects every --bare consumer. (b) ai-review.sh: size MU_REVIEW_CONTEXT_MAX_BYTES to the reviewer window (~32k ctx ⇒ ~100KB prompt budget); the 200000 default cannot work against a 32k-ctx reviewer. (c) ollama box: raise num_ctx for reviewer models (Modelfile / OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH) — VRAM cost, operator call. (d) mu deeper: provider-aware prompt budget via /api/show context length; pre-validate and warn/refuse instead of silent backend truncation. Recommended: (a)+(b) now; (c) operator decision; (d) follow-up bead.

title: mu-ovl-A: Enrich SpanKind to projection-relevant variants
description:
Sub-bead of mu-ovl (experiment 4 / 2026-05-13d). Wire-surface phase per mu repo convention.

## Scope

IN: extend `SpanKind` in `crates/mu-core/src/context/rope.rs:71-81` with the variants needed for projection differentiation:
- `ToolCall` — outgoing tool invocation from the assistant
- `ToolSchema` — registered tool definition consumed at session start
- `SkillActivation` — span emitted when a skill becomes active
- `MemoryInjection` — content pulled in via memory recall
- `Compaction` — synthetic span replacing N evicted spans
- `FileLoad` — content loaded from a file (e.g., spec/skill/source file)

Keep the four existing variants (`System`, `User`, `Assistant`, `ToolResult`).

Update `From<SpanKind> for ProviderRole` in `renderer.rs:74` to map the new kinds — most map to `System` (schema, skill activation, memory injection, compaction, file load) or `ToolResult` (tool call result, actual ToolResult). Document the mapping rationale inline.

OUT: rendering rule changes (that's mu-ovl-B). The renderer still produces the existing shallow OperatorView differentiation; this phase only adds the discriminator.

## Acceptance

- New `SpanKind` variants land with serde rename_all snake_case
- `From<SpanKind> for ProviderRole` exhaustive (compiler-enforced)
- Existing 4 retention-class + rope tests still green
- 1-2 new tests: serde round-trip for new variants; ProviderRole mapping coverage
- `cargo test --workspace` green at commit

## References

- Existing SpanKind: `crates/mu-core/src/context/rope.rs:70-81`
- Existing role mapping: `crates/mu-core/src/context/renderer.rs:74-82`
- Spec: `specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md` (section 'Skills, tools, and the active context as a retained pointer set', lines 538+)
- Experiment doc: `~/.claude-personal/experiments/goal-2026-05-13d-mu-ovl.md`
close_reason: Landed in mvnqvrrw ba00ef18 — 6 new SpanKind variants + exhaustive From<SpanKind> for ProviderRole + serde and role-mapping tests. cargo test --workspace green; mu-core 134 → 136. (--force because pre-flight set sub-bead-blocked-by-parent edge backwards; harmless since parent mu-ovl closes after both sub-beads.)

title: mu-7rk-d: Transport-level connection close on Denied auth
description:
**mu-7rk decomposition phase D** — Transport-level connection close on Denied auth.

## Why

mu-7rk-c makes Denied a terminal state at the dispatch layer (rejects all subsequent RPCs). This bead actually CLOSES the connection. Per codex on the original mu-7rk:

> "The transport cannot be closed by handlers, but auth denial semantics require server-controlled termination or a terminal denied state. Leaving close to the caller is not acceptable for adversarial clients."

The dispatch-layer terminal state from mu-7rk-c is sufficient for security correctness (a denied client can't call anything). Closing the connection is the operationally-correct cleanup: prevents resource exhaustion via held-open dead connections, and matches the spec's "connection closed with explicit error code" wording.

## Scope

### In

- `mu_core::transport`: add a `RequestShutdown` signal that handlers can return alongside their response. Mechanism options:
  - Option A: `HandlerResponse { body: Value, then_close: bool }`
  - Option B: a `ShutdownTx` injected into handler context; handler calls `.signal()` and the serve loop closes after sending the response
  - Implementer chooses; document the choice in design_notes
- `crates/mu-coding/src/serve/dispatch.rs`:
  - `peer.auth_initiate` handler returning `Denied{code}` ALSO signals connection close after sending the response
  - Connection close happens AFTER the Denied JSON-RPC response is flushed to the client (so the client sees the structured denial code, not just an EOF)
- Tests in `crates/mu-coding/tests/auth_smoke.rs`:
  - `bad_token_closes_connection_within_2s` — send bad token, assert Denied response received, assert subsequent send fails with broken-pipe within 2s
  - `unsupported_mechanism_closes_connection` — same shape, different mechanism
  - `valid_auth_does_not_close` — regression check that successful auth keeps the connection open

### Out

- Closing on non-auth errors (e.g., session.* method failures) — different concern, different bead
- Half-close semantics — full close is fine
- Reconnect tooling — operationally separate

## Acceptance

- All three test cases above pass
- `cargo test --workspace` green; existing peer.hello + mailbox + auth tests from mu-7rk-b/c still pass
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean
- `cargo fmt --check` clean
- No new workspace deps
- Operator can stress-test: 1000 bad-token attempts in a loop should not accumulate dead connections in the server

## References

- Codex review: `~/.claude-personal/experiments/codex-review-mu-7rk-output.txt` — design_concern #2 (transport close)
- Implementer's note in v0: "transport layer (mu_core::transport::serve) does not currently expose 'close this connection' from a handler" — this bead adds that mechanism
- Original bead spec wording: "rejects → connection closed with an explicit error code"
- Depends on mu-7rk-c for the Denied terminal state

title: mu skill system — slash-command dispatch + SKILL.md loader + body injection
description:
Generalize mu's slash-command surface so that skills (declarative SKILL.md files with frontmatter + body) become first-class commands. Replaces hardcoded match arms in `crates/mu-solo/src/app.rs` (lines 459-493) with a registry-driven dispatch.

## Motivation

tcovert has working skills in `~/.claude-personal/skills/` (jj-runbook, goal-protocol, postmortem, model-routing) plus a planned `/handoff`. These currently only work under claude-code. Bringing them into mu makes mu-solo a peer runtime for the same workflow primitives.

## Phases

### Phase A — discovery + loader
- Define lookup order: `<repo>/.mu/skills/` (project) → `~/.config/mu/skills/` (user) → optional `~/.claude-personal/skills/` compat-read (read-only mirror)
- Parse YAML frontmatter: `name`, `description`, `disable-model-invocation` (default false), `paths` (path-scoped trigger globs, future), `runtime` (optional declared compat: `[mu, claude-code]`)
- Body is markdown — the prompt template the skill injects

### Phase B — slash dispatch wiring
- Replace the hardcoded match arms with a SlashCommandRegistry that includes:
  - Built-in commands (/effort, /focus, /btw, /provider, /model, /clear, /cancel, /q, /quit, /exit, /status, /help) — wire them through the registry so the dispatch path is unified
  - Discovered skills as additional entries
- Unknown command falls through to the existing 'Unknown command' hint (mu-solo/src/app.rs:1080)

### Phase C — model-visible skill index
- Inject one-line skill descriptions into the system prompt at session start (Claude Code's pattern; ~450 tokens for 5-10 skills)
- Skills with `disable-model-invocation: true` are NOT in the index — they only fire via explicit `/<name>` user invocation. Zero context cost until invoked.

### Phase D — skill body injection on invocation
- When `/<skill-name>` invoked: load SKILL.md body, inject as a user message (or system message; pick based on protocol). Body becomes the standing instruction for the next turn.
- Skills can reference other files (e.g., `references/\*.md` siblings) — same lazy-load policy as Claude Code

## Skill compat policy

Three classes:
1. **Runtime-agnostic skills** (jj-runbook, postmortem): pure prompt templates with no runtime calls. Read same SKILL.md from either runtime.
2. **Runtime-specific skills** (goal-protocol): use commands or environment variables that differ between runtimes. Two options: (a) skill detects runtime and dispatches accordingly, or (b) frontmatter `runtime:` field gates loading.
3. **Headless-mode-dependent skills** (goal-protocol's `/goal` invocation): require mu's headless autonomous mode (see bead-pending: `mu solo --goal"<cond>"`). Skill itself can still load + run pre-flight; the launch step waits on the runtime.

## Out of scope (separate beads)

- `mu solo --goal` / autonomous mode — separate from the skill mechanism, needed by goal-protocol
- Path-scoped skill activation (Claude Code's `.claude/rules/` with `paths:` frontmatter) — item #2 from feature-mapping.md, same general mechanism but different surface
- Skill marketplace / distribution — explicitly skipped per feature-mapping Tier 4

## References

- claude-code-feature-mapping.md item #9 (slash-command IS skill system)
- claude-code-feature-mapping.md item #10 (disable-model-invocation flag)
- Current mu-solo dispatch: crates/mu-solo/src/app.rs:459-493
- Compat-read source: ~/.claude-personal/skills/

title: mu-bash-prompt-fires-for-non-allowlisted: triage doc-vs-behavior mismatch in BashMode::Strict prompt path
description:
## 3. (investigation) `--bash-prompt` fires for non-allowlisted commands

The `BashMode::Strict { prompt: bool }` doc says prompts fire on "every (allowlist-passing) invocation" — but in 2026-05-15 visual testing, the modal fired for `curl -I example.com` which is NOT allowlisted. Either:
(a) The doc comment is stale; prompts fire on EVERY invocation including non-allowlisted, then user approval is moot because allowlist still rejects.
(b) The doc is right and prompts shouldn't fire for non-allowlisted, but they do — a bug.
(c) Some third path I'm missing.

Triage: read bash.rs's full prompt-firing site, confirm behavior, fix doc or code.

Effort: investigation first (~30 min), then small code or doc fix.
close_reason: Triaged 2026-05-18: option (a) confirmed — prompts fire for every invocation regardless of allowlist (policy is tool-level, not argument-level; PermissionLevel::Ask is set on the BashTool spec unconditionally when prompt=true). Allowlist + metachar checks happen inside execute() after approval round-trip. Closed via PR 'fix(bash): doc the prompt-fires-for-non-allowlisted behavior' — doc comment now accurately describes behavior. Argument-aware policy that would short-circuit non-allowlisted commands before prompting is a separate, larger refactor (requires Tool trait extension) — file a new bead if/when that's prioritized.

title: t4c config: environment profiles (host / pot / spline-jail) + pot tool-install
description:
tcovert 2026-05-29: t4c registry/catalog resolution should be PROFILE-aware. Different environments expose different toolsets: host has code-index/agent/jj/rg/fd/etc.; a fresh pot is bare until tools are installed; the spline jail has its own set. Today `discover` does catalog ∩ installed-env under one implicit profile -- which is exactly why prior pot dogfoods were catalog-bound/sparse (the named ceiling).

Add named profiles that select a catalog subset + per-profile invoke/safe-defaults (e.g. NFS `--one-file-system` baked into traversal-tool invokes on host; different paths in a jail). `discover --profile <name>` resolves against that profile.

Operational note for pot-based t4c testing (tcovert): we'll need to INSTALL more tools into the pot (fine) so its profile is discriminating, not just mount t4c. Relates: mu-kex4.6 (host vs pot venues), mu-dwzq (usage signal), and the discriminating-dogfood venue decision (host chosen for the gate run because pots are catalog-bound).

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Reframe (operator idea 2026-05-29): the PERMISSION half of per-environment profiles is likely subsumed by making biscuit-auth orthogonal to the catalog — one universal catalog, attenuate a biscuit per context (pot/jail) instead of hand-curating profiles. See new bead on biscuit-orthogonal capability filtering. Profiles may then only need to carry AVAILABILITY/safe-default-flag concerns (env probe + invoke defaults), not permission sets. Visible set = available ∩ permitted.

title: goal-pr-flow: agent /goal sessions work in jj workspaces + submit PRs instead of pushing direct-to-main
description:
Standing convention: every autonomous /goal session works in its own jj workspace (or git worktree), commits on a session-named branch, and ends by opening a PR against `main` — NOT by pushing direct-to-main. The operator merges (or rejects) the PR after CI validates.

## Why

The capability-broker pattern (memory `03b44633`) names *two-person control* as the structural property: single-actor compromise cannot grow scope. PR + merge maps that pattern onto the workflow layer:

| PR-flow | Capability-broker |
|---|---|
| Agent in its own workspace | Worker session attenuated at spawn |
| Branch + commit + push | Agent produces signed work order |
| PR + diff review | Judge inspects the proposal |
| CI runs against the PR | Orchestrator's policy verifies content |
| Operator merge | Orchestrator dispatches |
| Direct-to-main = unconstrained operator-only exception | Unconstrained operator-only path |

The CI we landed via `mu-jza` (workflow triggers on both `pull_request` AND `push: main`) sets the stage perfectly. PR-shaped work gets validated at the gate; the operator's merge button is the two-person-control point.

## Practical benefits

- **Working-copy isolation**: each session has its own `@`. Eliminates cross-revision pollution we hit yesterday (mu-ovl-A worker accumulating into experiment-3's briefing commit; multiple jj-mixing surprises).
- **Reviewable atomic units**: the diff IS the artifact. Operator sees the change before it lands.
- **Same workflow as human collaborators**: no special agent flow, no special agent commands. PR is PR.
- **Reversibility**: PRs close cleanly without merging; direct-to-main commits can only be reverted (visible in history).
- **CI surfaces failures BEFORE merge**: today's direct-to-main + CI-runs-after-push pattern means failed CI on main → ugly recovery. PR-flow + CI-before-merge means failed CI on PR → just close it.

## Concrete pieces required

1. **Per-session workspace setup helper** — wrapper around `jj workspace add` that creates a fresh checkout at `/tmp/<repo>-session-<id>/` or similar. Goal-protocol skill ensures the autonomous /goal session lands in this isolated workspace before any edits.

2. **Branch naming convention** — `agent/<session-id>-<short-slug>` or `agent/<date>-<bead-id>`. Convention should make `agent/*` branches greppable in `git branch -a` so it's obvious which are session-shaped vs human-shaped.

3. **PR-opening helper** — at /goal termination protocol step, the worker runs `gh pr create` with a structured body: bead IDs closed, commits list, test status, briefing-doc reference, experiment-doc reference. (Probably a small shell helper in `~/.claude-personal/skills/goal-protocol/bin/goal-pr-create.sh`.)

4. **Goal-protocol skill update** — SKILL.md's "Termination" section currently says "close the bead." Update to: "commit + push branch + open PR + (don't merge)." The bead doesn't close until the PR merges.

5. **Bead lifecycle update** — beads transition through: open → in_progress (worker spawned) → ready-for-review (PR opened) → closed (PR merged). Need to decide if br supports this directly or if we use status=open + a label like `pr:<num>`.

6. **Per-repo override hooks** — some repos may not want this (e.g., this one, where the operator is doing direct main pushes during active development). The per-repo goal-protocol.md can override.

## What stays the same

- Bead-per-commit discipline (per per-repo)
- Test-green at every commit
- Spec amendment in same commit (mu's spec-amend pattern)
- Briefing format (MORNING_<date>.md)

## Out of scope

- **Auto-merge on green CI.** Operator decides each merge. The two-person-control property requires this.
- **Multi-reviewer setup.** Single operator/reviewer for now.
- **Workspace cleanup on PR merge.** Worth doing but later. Manual `jj workspace forget` is acceptable for v1.
- **Migration of past direct-to-main commits.** History is history.

## Trigger to actually pick up

This is the convention shape. Implementing it means:
- Writing the helper scripts
- Updating SKILL.md
- Testing on one /goal session
- Documenting in tool-conventions.md

Should land BEFORE the next autonomous /goal session that isn't operator-watched (the experiments 1-6 yesterday were all operator-watched — the discipline was Thaddeus reviewing each commit in real-time). For background /goal runs (overnight, while operator is away), this is the safety primitive that makes them safe to leave running.

Specifically: if mu-2w7 (TUI design exploration researchers) gets fired and the operator isn't watching, the researchers should work in workspaces + open PRs. NOT push direct-to-main.

## References

- Capability-broker pattern: memory `03b44633` (capability_broker_pattern_thaddeus_systems_2026_05_13)
- CI workflow: `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (mu-jza, landed)
- mu's per-repo goal-protocol overrides: `specs/process/goal-protocol.md`
- Goal-protocol skill: `~/.claude-personal/skills/goal-protocol/SKILL.md`

## Notes

Filed 2026-05-14 morning. Conversation context: yesterday's 64-commits-ahead-of-main situation showed how direct-to-main with extended operator-presence works but the alternative (PR flow) is the right standing default. Thaddeus's exact framing: 'Normally I'd go to PR first and then merge, but it's only us right now.' This bead inverts that — PR-first is the default; direct-to-main is the exception, justified per case (e.g. during active live-pair sessions where operator IS the reviewer in real-time).

title: Implement mu-036: session.start_autonomous + schedule_wakeup
description:
Spec at specs/mu-036-session-autonomous-loop.md (jj spouxlsp d69719c3).

Capability-gated bounded self-driving sessions. server-enforced bounds (max_iterations / max_wall_clock_ms / max_total_tool_calls_in_autonomy), not goodwill-enforced. goal_check_method supports SelfReport (callout), DelegateGrader (another session as oracle — the structural-grader pattern from spec-summary experiment), or ExternalSignal.

schedule_wakeup parks in RunMode::Sleeping consuming no model budget while idle.

Composes with mu-029, mu-031, mu-033, and post-mu-035 status visibility.

Estimated 2 weeks of focused Rust work. Should land after mu-035 since autonomous sessions benefit hugely from provider_status during long unattended runs.

title: AwsCapability Hash by name only, Eq compares both fields — invariant only enforced via deserializer
description:
## Problem

In crates/mu-core/src/capability.rs, AwsCapability has Hash that only considers 'name' but PartialEq compares both 'name' and 'session_policy'. This is documented as 'go through try_from_iter to enforce one-per-name', but the hash/eq mismatch is a real footgun: HashSet<AwsCapability> can legitimately end up with two equal-by-Hash / unequal-by-Eq entries that collide.

## Suggested fix (per opus review)

Make AwsCapability a thin wrapper around a private (String, Option<Value>) with custom From impls, so the hash/eq mismatch isn't reachable by mistake. OR split into AwsCapabilityName (the hash/eq identity) + AwsCapabilitySet (a wrapper that owns the name→policy map). Either is preferable to a struct where a == b doesn't imply hash(a) == hash(b).

## Source

opus-4-7 review event 194, 'What needs work' item 5.

title: Anthropic cache: post-TTL-expiry call writes only the tools breakpoint — full prefix paid as fresh input, then re-paid as cache_write next call
description: From the mu-814o reanalysis (2026-06-04, session c76f6949 per-call table): after a >5min idle gap (TTL expiry), the first call shows cache_read=0, cache_creation=2,210 (tools block only), fresh_input=full prefix (e.g. 136,486) — the big prefix is NOT written to cache on that call. The NEXT call then pays cache_creation for the whole prefix (136,457). Pattern repeats at every expiry (calls 17/18, 41/42, 62/63, 64/65). Expected: the post-expiry call should write the full prefix once (cache_read=0, cache_creation=~full), halving the double-pay. Likely seam: AnthropicCacheStrategy marker placement / how cache_control interacts with the system+tools blocks vs the message-prefix breakpoint — terrain-check the actual request shape on a post-gap call. Companion knob: expose Anthropic extended-TTL (1h) as a config option for sessions with thinking gaps. Baseline economics: 4 expiries cost ~2x prefix-write each; halving saves ~25% of that session's cache-write spend.

title: Tool-result ingestion hygiene: post-tool-call filter hook + optional haiku sidecar
description:
Reduce tool-result tokens BEFORE they enter the provider context (operator design, 2026-06-04). Two tiers:

TIER 1 — deterministic post-tool-call hook (no model, no risk): strip ANSI, collapse repeated lines, cap pathological line lengths, truncate raw file dumps past a limit with '[truncated, N more lines]'. Pure cruft removal; verbatim content where it matters.

TIER 2 — sidecar delegation for oversized results: spin off a cheap model (haiku 4.5 / ollama:gpt-oss:20b) to process a big tool output and return only the distilled answer to the main session. Keeps the expensive-model context lean; the cheap model eats the bulk tokens.

ECONOMICS NOTE (the operator's cache-miss realization, resolved): the sidecar IS a cache miss every call — but the raw result entering the main context would be never-before-cached tokens anyway. The real win is RENT, not the first read: a 50K tool result in an opus context costs 1.25x write once + 0.1x read on EVERY subsequent call + occupies attention; distilled to 2K the recurring rent drops 25x. Sidecar pays haiku rates once instead.

GUARDRAIL: tier-2 summaries must never paraphrase code the main model will edit — route code-bearing results to tier 1 truncation or elision (mu-or85) instead. Distillation is for logs, test output, search sweeps.

RELATED: mu-or85 (elision = lossless alternative), mu-8bkf (judge compaction = same pattern at threshold time). mu already has spawn_worker on the tool surface — tier 2 may be mostly wiring.
close_reason: done

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: TIER-2 UNBLOCKED by or85 frozen design (2026-06-05): routing split decided — sidecar distillation (tier 2) takes logs/test-output/search sweeps; ELISION (or85) takes code-bearing results, satisfying the never-paraphrase-code guardrail losslessly. Tier-2 implementation can proceed once or85.1/.2 land.

title: mu-tui: F8 events truncation + compact cost/token display on F1 and F3
description:
## Problem

Two information-density issues:

1. F8 events firehose displays full event payloads. Long entries crowd out
   surrounding context in both the F3 sub-window (when F8 is shown as a
   pane) and the full F8 view. Full payload should be reachable but not
   the default display.

2. Cost/token-count summary exists on F5 (usage panel) but isn't visible
   on F1 (command center) or F3 (session detail / session list). When
   working in F3, the user has to switch panels to see what the active
   session is consuming.

## Proposal

### F8 truncation

- Default display: single-line summary per event (event kind, source,
  short payload preview, timestamp)
- Expand selected event with arrow keys or enter to see full payload
- Applies consistently to F8-as-pane (in F3) and F8-fullscreen

### Compact cost/token display on F1 and F3

Add canonical compact form, smaller font, in status area:

  `^n  Vm  $X.XX`  (input up-arrow, output down-arrow, cost)

Colored to match F5 usage thresholds (green/yellow/red).

- On F1: aggregate across all active sessions
- On F3: scoped to currently selected session
- F5 remains the detailed view; F1/F3 get at-a-glance summary

## Acceptance

- F8 default shows truncated single-line entries; full payload on demand
- F1 and F3 show compact cost/token summary inline with existing status
- Format consistent with F5's data so values cross-check trivially

## Cross-refs

- mu-pex (metrics framework — per-call time decomposition + quality
  proxies; this is the user-visible side of similar data)
- Provenance: TUI feedback (mu daemon f3845422 session-2, 2026-05-15)

title: t4c source: MCP-live capabilities — distinguish 'code-index installed' from 'code-index LOADED as MCP this session'. A live mcp.* RegistrySource over mu's loaded-MCP-server state; its provenance (Tree.source_of, mu-kex4.6.8) then outranks curated/memory via add_source order. Split from mu-kex4.6.8.
mu-coding: split serve/dispatch.rs into router + per-domain handler modules
description:
## Problem

`crates/mu-coding/src/serve/dispatch.rs` started as the single entry
point for JSON-RPC request routing + per-method handlers. It's now
holding every method handler for the entire protocol surface (session,
mailbox, daemon, auth, etc.) in one growing file.

Every bead that adds an RPC handler adds another function (or set of
functions) here. Recent: mu-yox added the auth handlers, mu-m84
modified the auth outcome handler, mu-fnn (in flight) is about to
add the auth-required check at the top of every protected handler.
Future: mu-lho extensions, mu-71q MCP adapter, etc.

## Proposal

Refactor into a router file + per-domain handler modules:

```text
crates/mu-coding/src/serve/
  dispatch.rs               // router only: parse Request, route to handler
  handlers/
    mod.rs                  // shared helpers: error builders, auth-required check
    session.rs              // create_session, session.list, session.stats
    mailbox.rs              // mailbox.post, mailbox.list, mailbox.consume
    daemon.rs               // daemon.stats, daemon.outstanding_calls
    auth.rs                 // peer.auth_offer, peer.auth_initiate, peer.auth_response
    peer.rs                 // peer.hello and other peer.* not in auth
    autonomy.rs             // session.start_autonomous, schedule_wakeup
```

Router dispatches based on method name to the appropriate handler
module. Shared helpers (error response builders, auth-required check
landing per mu-fnn) live in `handlers/mod.rs`.

Consistent with the protocol.rs split (companion bead) — by-domain
modularization across both the wire types and the dispatch.

## Acceptance

- dispatch.rs is the router only; handlers live in domain modules
- All existing RPC handling still works (same wire behavior)
- All tests still pass
- `cargo clippy -D warnings` clean
- Future RPC additions target the appropriate handler module

## Cross-refs

- Closely paired with protocol.rs split bead (filed same session)
- AGENTS.md "no 27k-line files" rule
- gpt-5.5 code review of mu, daemon f3845422 session-4 (2026-05-15)
- Touched by recent beads: mu-yox, mu-m84, mu-fnn (in flight)
close_reason: Merged in PR #53 (a32632d) — split serve/dispatch.rs into router + per-domain handler modules

title: End-to-end composed-invariant tests across seams (delegation + capability + rope + persistence + TUI)
description:
Add a test suite that verifies invariants that hold across multiple seams simultaneously — the cases that pass individual unit tests but can drift at the composition layer. High-velocity development (May 9-20: ~250 commits across specs/features/refactors) creates exactly the conditions where unit-pass + composition-drift hides.

Surfaced by gpt-5.5 during daemon ebccc9 session-2 (2026-05-20).

## Test scenarios

1. **Delegation + capability narrowing**: parent delegates to child with narrowed `allowed_tools`. Child cannot widen. Tool results return to child correctly. Child accesses parent mailbox (read-only); parent cannot access child mailbox. Capability expires on time bound.

2. **Rope + provider request (the mu-yqeq closure check)**: full session flow — user message → rope assembly → `ContextAssembly` event → `ProviderRenderer` → provider wire bytes. Assert: rope was actually consulted (not bypassed); `ContextAssembly` event records the spans included; wire bytes match a recorded fixture.

3. **Memory + rope + provider**: memory recall produces `SpanKind::MemoryInjection` spans; spans are included in rope; rope is rendered to provider; `ContextAssembly` event records `memory_id` provenance; the model's eventual reply references the injected content (smoke test on response text, since model behavior isn't deterministic).

4. **Rehydration + archive/delete**: archive session → restart daemon → rehydrate. Assert: archived session not in default list; events still queryable by ID; deleted session purged from index; raw event-log recovery from disk possible.

5. **TUI + event projection**: append events → TUI refresh reflects them; session switches don't corrupt the transcript; scroll position survives event append; F3 inline streaming text reaches the visible buffer (cross-ref mu-304g).

6. **Approval + autonomy + tool denial**: autonomous loop encounters `RetryPolicy::Never` tool, gets denial, records denial event, does NOT retry. Approval-waiting blocks with clear status. Operator approval recorded as separate event from tool result.

## Acceptance

- New test crate or module: `tests/integration/composed_invariants/` (or one file per scenario).
- All 6 scenarios pass against the current `main` (or are explicitly marked `#[ignore]` with a referenced bead for the blocking work — e.g., mu-yqeq.D for scenario #2's rope-actually-flows assertion).
- `cargo test --workspace --test composed_invariants` runs in CI.
- Each scenario's failure mode is clear: assertion message names the seam that was violated.

## References

- Blocks: requires seams.md (scenarios reference seam names from there)
- Related: mu-yqeq.D (scenario #2 fully passes only after that bead closes)
- Related: mu-304g (scenario #5 partially blocked by F3 streaming gap)
- Memory: `iter_7_to_10_cycle_retrospective_2026_05_19` (the cycle that produced ~10 days of feature work without composed-invariant regression — luck or coverage gaps?)

title: mu-recall-wire-projection: System-role spans beyond system-prompt get dropped by codex translator (recall not reaching wire)
description:
mu-phl v0 smoke test (canary verification 2026-05-21) found that session-start recall lands in the rope correctly but does NOT reach the model via codex's wire path.

## Diagnosis

In crates/mu-ai/src/providers/openai_codex.rs:344-356, translate_provider_messages_codex only hoists the ProviderMessage whose first source_span_id equals literally "system-prompt" into the OpenAI Responses API's instructions field. ALL other System-role projected messages — including the memory-recall:* (SubprocessRecallProvider) and project-file:* (ProjectFileRecallProvider) spans introduced by mu-phl v0 — get silently dropped:

- Not put into instructions
- Not put into input (conversation)
- Go nowhere

The Responses API has only ONE instructions slot, so multiple System-role spans must concat into one string.

## Evidence

Canary memory d39d153b (value w11wKFoinTnTRMS) was seeded in agent.sqlite. agent memory context --cwd ~/src/public_github/mu includes the canary. Event log (9a25dd626dd4e42c/session-1.jsonl) confirms the rope received memory-recall:memory-9f01297919bb + 4x project-file:* spans alongside the user message. But mu ask --provider openai-codex --model gpt-5.5 with a canary question returned "no canary found." The recall content is in the rope but never makes it to the API request.

## Why missed earlier

The yqeq5_parity_* tests (mu-yqeq.5 acceptance) assert byte-equivalence between Legacy and Projected paths. Those tests use ropes built WITHOUT project_context — so the only System-role span is system-prompt. With no other System-role spans, the codex translator's narrow check works equivalently to Legacy. mu-phl v0 introduced the conditions that expose the bug; the parity tests were not extended to cover the new case. That gap is the discipline issue.

## Fix (this bead)

1. Codex translator: hoist ALL System-role spans EXCEPT tool-schema:* (tools go separately via body.tools). Concatenate multiple System spans with newlines into instructions.
2. Extend yqeq5_parity_* tests to cover a rope that contains MemoryInjection and FileLoad spans — pre-registering the regression test alongside the fix so the discipline lapse doesn't recur.
3. Re-run canary smoke against gpt — mu ask should surface canary w11wKFoinTnTRMS.

## Out of scope for this bead (follow-up beads)

- OpenRouter Projected arm (mu-yqeq.6) likely has the same shape — same trait-default renderer (Faux), same hoist-only-system-prompt pattern. Verify + file follow-up.
- Anthropic has its own renderer() and cache_strategy() overrides (AnthropicProviderRenderer, AnthropicCacheStrategy). Different projection shape; needs separate verification that MemoryInjection / FileLoad spans survive the projection to the API's system field. Probably file follow-up.

## Composes with

- mu-phl v0 (rope-side recall, complete). This bead is the wire-projection counterpart — recall injection works; wire surfacing was the gap.
- mu-yqeq.5 / .6 / .8 (Projected-arm provider work). Adjacent to that scope.

## Verification

Acceptance:
- cargo test --workspace green including new MemoryInjection/FileLoad parity test
- cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings clean
- mu ask --provider openai-codex --model gpt-5.5 with the canary question returns the canary nonce
close_reason: Fixed. translate_provider_messages_codex now hoists all System-role spans except tool-schema:* into body.instructions (was: only system-prompt). Recall content (MemoryInjection + FileLoad) now reaches gpt-5.5 via the codex wire. 2 regression tests cover the case (mu_2puu_projected_hoists... + mu_2puu_projected_excludes_tool_schema...). cargo test --workspace: 771 pass; clippy clean. Canary smoke (canary w11wKFoinTnTRMS seeded via memory d39d153b) returns the canary exactly when gpt is asked to surface it. End-to-end recall verified against codex. Follow-up scope (OpenRouter Projected arm + Anthropic projection): not in this bead.

title: event source identifier: location + service-id + monotonic timestamps on every event
description:
Extend the event envelope with three correlation/forensics fields drawn from the low-latency-systems pattern operator described 2026-05-16. Each is cheap to add now and expensive to retrofit later.

## Three additions

### 1. Service identifier in task_id

Today: `task_id = format!("task-{unix-nanos}")` — process-global. Breaks for multi-daemon mu (mailbox/mu-lho is already heading there).

Proposal: `task_id = format!("{daemon_id}-{date}-{nanos}")` matching operator's 'unique identifier for the service for the day' pattern. `DaemonInfo::daemon_id()` already exists.

### 2. Location identifier on every event

Today: no field carries 'where in code did this emit fire'. When an event misbehaves, you have to grep the event kind back to its emit site.

Proposal: add `source_location: String` to SessionEvent (or a sidecar EventMeta), populated via a macro that captures `file!()` + `line!()` at the emit site. Zero runtime cost beyond the bytes.

### 3. Monotonic timestamp alongside wall-clock

Today: `timestamp_unix_ms` is wall-clock (`SystemTime::now()`). NTP correction can move it backwards. Sub-millisecond intra-process latency measurement is impossible.

Proposal: add a paired `monotonic_ns: u64` (from `std::time::Instant` converted to ns since a process-local epoch). Use monotonic for 'how long did X take' queries; keep wall-clock for cross-machine correlation.

## Why

Operator pattern 2026-05-16 (production low-latency trading systems): every event has UUID with service-id + nanos, location identifier for the emit site, paired send/receive timestamps so latency only 'hides' in buffers and network — which are themselves measurable from endpoint timestamps. mu's event_log is half this pattern; these three additions close the gap without restructuring.

## Acceptance

- task_id format includes daemon_id
- SessionEvent gains location (string or struct) populated via macro at emit
- SessionEvent gains monotonic_ns paired with timestamp_unix_ms
- Migration path for existing log entries: serde defaults so old JSONL deserializes (location empty, monotonic_ns 0)
- Docs updated explaining when to query each timestamp

## Cross-references

- mu-jlll (per-operation task_id correlation; same conceptual axis)
- mu-5g7i, mu-8alb (the telemetry/forensics work that benefits)
- mu-lho (multi-daemon mailbox — first concrete consumer of the service-id need)

title: pot snapshot naming: 'working-YYYY-MM-DD' pinning beats numeric tags lexically
description:
Discovered 2026-05-16 during mu deployment to agent-runtime pot.

## What

pot's clone subcommand uses `_zfs_last_snap` (common.sh:462) which is literally:

    zfs list -d 1 -H -t snapshot $dset | sort -r | cut -d'@' -f2 | cut -f1 | head -n1

`sort -r` is reverse-LEXICAL, not reverse-chronological. So any snapshot whose tag is alphabetically higher than the numeric epoch (e.g. `working-2026-05-13`) PINS the clone source to that snapshot. New snapshots taken via `pot snapshot -p <pot>` (which assigns a numeric epoch tag like `@1778965913`) sort BELOW `working-*` and are silently ignored by clones.

## Symptom

Template modifications (e.g. `mkdir /opt/pot/jails/agent-runtime/m/usr/local/pot-bin/`) followed by `pot snapshot` appear to succeed but new pot clones do not see the change. Then any `pot mount-in` that targets the new path fails with `mount_nullfs: ...: No such file or directory`.

## Resolution

When updating the agent-runtime template, take a new snapshot whose name lexically beats the existing pinned tag, e.g.:

    sudo zfs snapshot zroot/pot/jails/agent-runtime@working-2026-05-DD-<reason>
    sudo zfs snapshot zroot/pot/jails/agent-runtime/m@working-2026-05-DD-<reason>

(Both the parent and `/m` child datasets need the snapshot.)

Alternative: pass `-s <snap>` to `pot clone` explicitly, but that would require changing agent-spawn, which is more surface area.

## How to apply

- For agent-runtime template upgrades: ALWAYS use a `working-<date>-<reason>` snapshot name, never let `pot snapshot` assign a numeric tag if a working-* snapshot exists.
- Consider periodic `zfs destroy` of obsolete working-* snapshots once a newer one is verified working, to keep the snapshot list small.

## Cross-reference

Should be added to the freebsd-jails skill at `~/.pi/agent/skills/freebsd-jails/SKILL.md` under 'Quirks worth knowing'.

title: mu-tui-design-exploration: spawn researchers to mock up TUI visual language directions
description:
Filed 2026-05-13 after surveying Charmbracelet's aesthetic (Crush, Glamour, Lip Gloss) + ratatui third-party widgets (throbber, scrollview, tree, term) + bubbletea-rs + oatmeal. Operator note: 'they don't have to work, but it might be interesting to spin off a few different researchers/mockups to get an idea of how we might use some of the visual language in AI terminals with our sensibilities.'

## Premise

The reference shelf (mu-zh1) now has substantial visual-language exemplars. Before committing to specific TUI surface changes (mu-pex / mu-jb3 / mu-ib5 / mu-cha / mu-u8a / mu-di4 / mu-mvk), spin up 2-4 small researcher agents — each takes a *different* design angle — and produce text-mockups + rationale. Pick a direction afterward from concrete artifacts rather than abstract preference.

## Suggested research angles (pick 2-4 to spawn)

1. **Minimal monospaced (helix / atuin aesthetic)** — strict typography, minimal color, no chat-bubble decorations. Information density over delight. Mockup: full mu session display with assistant streaming, tool calls, callouts.

2. **Rich Glamour-styled (Charmbracelet aesthetic)** — termimad-rendered Markdown for assistant responses, lipgloss-equivalent styled borders/panels, throbbers, decorative spacing. Maximal visual affordance. Mockup: same scene as #1 but with full styling.

3. **Operator-dashboard shaped (oatmeal-ish + ratatui multi-pane)** — multi-pane layout: conversation main pane, session-tree side pane (tui-tree-widget), mailbox/callouts pane, status bar with throbbers. For operators managing multiple parallel sessions.

4. **Agent-view-first (an agent's perspective)** — the rope renderer's AgentView form (mu-ovl OperatorView's sibling) made visible. Shows what the model 'sees' alongside what the operator sees. Specifically useful for debugging context-assembly.

Each researcher should produce:
- ASCII mockup of a representative scene (~30-60 lines)
- 5-10 line rationale: what this aesthetic prioritizes, what it sacrifices
- Concrete crate dependencies that would be needed
- Which open beads this aesthetic would naturally complete

## Cost discipline

Each researcher: -3 expected (small scope, mockup + rationale). 2-4 spawns = -12 total. Well under any single /goal experiment.

Use Agent tool with subagent_type=general-purpose or claude. Brief them on the reference shelf (mu-zh1) + relevant open TUI beads. Run them in parallel (single message, multiple Agent tool calls) so the round-trip is one wall-clock.

## Out of scope

Implementation. Mockups only. Pick a direction from the artifacts; THEN file concrete implementation beads against the chosen aesthetic.

## When to fire

- Anytime after 2026-05-13 when operator is fresh and engaged
- Best if mu-pex / mu-jb3 are still open (the aesthetic chosen here would inform their implementation)
- Worse if already locked-in on an aesthetic — at that point we're prototyping, not scoping

## References

Pattern shelf: mu-zh1
Concrete adopt-the-crate beads: mu-di4 (throbber), mu-mvk (termimad markdown)
Strategy beads: mu-yc6 (MCP), mu-f6z (semantic code tool)
Open TUI work: mu-pex (metrics), mu-jb3, mu-ib5 (mailbox), mu-cha (session tree), mu-u8a (color)

## Notes

Filed 2026-05-13 late evening. Operator went to bed. Bead is the durable artifact; the in-conversation context evaporates but this carries the proposal shape forward. When operator returns, deciding 'fire it now' is one explicit message — no re-derivation needed.

title: mu-tui: replace untyped JSON wire-decoding with mu-core::protocol typed structs
description:
Today mu-tui hand-decodes notifications and method responses by walking `serde_json::Value`, e.g. `session_row_from_info_value`, `ProviderStatusKind` string-matching in two locations, `cumulative_usage` shape decoding inline. The crate already depends on `mu-core`; it's paying the dep cost without using the typed protocol surface.

## Why

A May-14 Opus 4.7 review of mu-tui pointed at this as the most useful "is the abstraction right" cleanup:

> The TUI as written treats the daemon like a foreign service; it can treat it like a typed library. ... Have `mu-tui::wire` deserialize SessionInfo, ProviderStatusEvent, DoneEvent, CalloutEvent into the typed structs from mu-core::protocol. You'll find the second-consumer compile errors that tell you where the protocol is still loose.

This is the property the architecture *promised*: the TUI as second consumer of the daemon proves the protocol's contract. Today it doesn't — the TUI accepts any JSON shape, so loose protocol surfaces are silently absorbed.

## Scope

### In

- New module `crates/mu-tui/src/wire.rs` re-exporting (or wrapping where ergonomic) the typed protocol structs from `mu-core::protocol`: `SessionInfo`, `ProviderStatusEvent`, `DoneEvent`, `CalloutEvent`, `SessionEventsResponse`, etc.
- Replace all `serde_json::Value`-walking decoders in main.rs with `serde_json::from_value::<TypedShape>(payload)` calls.
- `ProviderStatusKind` decoded once at the TUI boundary; the two current string-match sites become enum-match.
- Audit: track every compile error the migration produces — each one is a loose protocol shape that should be tightened in mu-core::protocol.

### Out

- New protocol surface additions (those land in mu-core::protocol; this bead consumes existing types).
- The cumulative_usage shape audit is separate from the larger ProviderMessages question (mu-fb0 follow-on).

## Why this is P2 not P1

Today's TUI works correctly. This is a structural cleanup that pays off the SECOND time we add a consumer (web dashboard, programmatic test harness, etc.) — or the first time we change a protocol shape and want compile errors rather than runtime parse fails. High-value but not urgent.

## Acceptance

- mu-tui no longer calls `.as_str().unwrap_or(...)` chains on session-level fields.
- ProviderStatusKind decoded into typed enum once.
- New `mu-tui::wire` module owns the boundary.
- Existing tests pass; new tests for typed-decode round-trip on captured fixtures.
- cargo test --workspace green; clippy -D warnings; fmt --check.

## References

- Provenance: Opus 4.7 review in mu daemon `fd76f5`, session-1, 2026-05-14.
- Typed protocol: `crates/mu-core/src/protocol.rs`.
- Untyped decode sites: search `main.rs` for `.as_str()`, `.as_object()`, `.as_array()` chains in `tick`, `refresh_session_list`, `refresh_transcript_for_selection`.

title: mu-tui: assistant-block border breaks on wrapped continuation lines
description:
The bordered \"assistant (streaming…)\" block renders its left `│` only on the first line of content. When the text wraps, continuation lines lose the inner-box left border — the wrapped text starts flush against the outer pane's `│`, visually escaping the bounded section.

## Reproduction (2026-05-14)

Opus 4.7 session in mu-tui, right-pane transcript view. Assistant text was long enough to wrap. Observed:

```text
│┌─ assistant (streaming…)                                                          │
││ I'll review the mu workspace systematically. ... examine the agent loop and one  │
│system. Excellent. Let me examine the agent loop and one of the architectural      │
│└─                                                                                 │
```
