- Tests verify role mapping and OperatorView rendering for each SpanKind
- `cargo test --workspace` green
- Bead-close commit cites file:line for the impl and tests

title: WORKER: implement multi-line prompt growing upward in F3 inline mode (mu-o1y7 phase 3g)
description:
## Worker task

Implement multi-line prompt that grows upward in F3 inline mode of mu-tui. Currently `render_inline_session_detail` renders a single-line prompt with horizontal scroll for content longer than the viewport width. With multi-line content (via Alt-Enter), newlines are rendered as the ↵ glyph in the single-line view.

## Spec

In `crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs` `render_inline_session_detail` and `App.send_prompt` paths:

- When in `InputMode::SendPrompt` AND `prompt_buffer` contains newlines, render the prompt as multiple visual rows
- Bottom row: continues to host the cursor + most recent line
- Rows above it: previous logical lines (split by `\n`)
- Cap input region height: viewport_height - 2 (one row for separator, one for footer)
- If content exceeds visible rows, vertically scroll so the cursor row is always visible
- Each row's content can wrap horizontally if longer than viewport width (or just clip — operator preference, document the choice)

## Cursor positioning

Tricky bit. Today's logic computes `cursor_pos = Position { x: ..., y: input_y_offset }`. For multi-line:
- Compute which visual row the cursor is in (split prompt_buffer by `\n`, count lines before cursor's byte position)
- Compute column within that row
- Compute final y by mapping logical-row to viewport-row (with vertical scroll offset)

## Tests required

- Single-line prompt: behaves exactly as today (regression test)
- Two-line prompt: both lines visible, cursor on bottom
- Prompt with N > viewport-rows-available: vertical scroll engages, cursor row visible
- Cursor at end of buffer: positioned at the last char + 1
- Cursor at start of buffer: positioned at the first prefix
- Empty line in the middle of buffer: renders as an empty row, doesn't break layout

## Acceptance

- New code in `crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs` only (scope tripwire: no other files)
- All existing 31 tests + new tests pass
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` clean
- `cargo fmt --all -- --check` clean
- Commit message has proper `## Files` block
- Bead closed in commit message

## Dispatch

Worker invocation (orchestrator runs):

```
agent-router --auth claude-oauth --task-file /tmp/mu-o1y7-phase3g-worker.md --cwd /home/tcovert/src/public_github/mu --output summary
```

Where the task file contains: the bead description, the goal-protocol-skill standing rules, mu's per-repo overrides, and explicit stop criteria.

## Budget

$15 cap (conservative; ~150 LOC est at 10:1 ratio)

## Stop criteria

Default seven + (specific to this task):
- Worker tries to refactor existing single-line rendering instead of additively extending — STOP, the goal is multi-line CAPABILITY not a rewrite
- Worker touches `emit_transcript_delta_inline` — STOP, that's a different concern
- Cursor positioning becomes byzantine (>50 lines of cursor math) — STOP, simplify or use a sub-helper

title: auth token store: non-atomic save (truncate-in-place, no lock) can tear ~/.config/mu/auth/*.json under concurrent refresh
description: FileSystemTokenStore::save (crates/mu-ai/src/auth/mod.rs ~:94) writes via OpenOptions write+create+TRUNCATE+write_all — truncate-in-place, no tmp+rename, no advisory lock; load() is plain fs::read. Under concurrent token refresh from multiple mu instances (common — many mu ask processes), two saves can interleave/tear the JSON, or a reader catches a half-written file -> corrupt token store. None observed yet; real latent race. FIX: write a temp file in the same dir, then atomic rename(2) over the target preserving mode 0600. This is the ACTUAL concurrency hazard in the store — distinct from and opposite to the mistaken file-lock-contention hypothesis in mu-xac (there is NO locking). Surfaced 2026-06-01 diagnosing the codex refresh failure.

title: mu-mcp-adapter: MCP server wrapper exposing 5-6 LLM-friendly tools over mu's RPC surface
description: MCP server wrapper (mu mcp subcommand) speaking MCP over stdio to Claude Code (or any MCP client) and JSON-RPC over unix socket to a mu daemon (via mu-detached-mode; lazy-spawns if absent). The pattern-B' entry point: Claude Code is the orchestrator brain, mu is the parallel-execution coprocessor. Exposes 6 tools (design judgment, not a count cap): mu.spawn_worker, mu.assign_task, mu.collect_results, mu.status, mu.kill_worker, mu.verify_alive. Subscribes to session.mailbox_message notifications, pushes to LLM client as MCP resource-changed hints (no polling). Per-tool permission gating (auto-approve read-only, prompt for mutating). Idempotency keys deduplicate within a session. The 6-tool surface is the cognitive-ergonomics design — small enough for LLM clarity, big enough for real orchestration. Specialized clients (mu-tui, test harnesses, scripts) talk JSON-RPC directly without this adapter. Substrates mu-detached-mode, mu-auth-handshake, mu-hh1. Composes with mu-qqb (correlation_id first-class in assign_task/collect_results) and mu-z5v (spawn_worker → delegate with pre-issued back-channel).

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: 2026-05-27: This bead is downstream of mu-yc6. Once the daemon has MCP client connections (mu-yc6), this adapter exposes them as tools in the session. Per Opus mu-solo session: the gap is verified — zero MCP tools in mu-solo sessions today. Architecture: MCP tools register in the daemon's tool registry alongside built-ins, flow through ask_session like any other tool. mu-solo doesn't know the difference.

title: openrouter wire-projection: System-role spans beyond system-prompt get dropped (recall not reaching wire)
description:
Same pattern as mu-2puu, sibling provider. translate_provider_messages_openrouter in crates/mu-ai/src/providers/openrouter.rs:265-309 only emits the leading role=system message when the source_span_id equals literally "system-prompt", silently dropping every other System-role span (memory-recall:*, project-file:*, plus any future SkillActivation / Compaction kinds).

## Fix

Mirror mu-2puu's fix shape, adapted for the Chat Completions API:
- Hoist ALL System-role spans EXCEPT tool-schema:*
- Concatenate with newlines into ONE leading role=system message
  (OpenAI Chat Completions canonically has one system slot at the
  start of the messages array; multiple are accepted but inconsistent
  in handling across providers).

## Regression tests

Mirror the mu_2puu_projected_* tests on the OpenRouter side:
1. Recall-injected rope produces a leading system message containing
   memory + project-file content alongside system_prompt
2. Tool-schema spans don't leak into the system message

## Verification

- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy clean
- Manual smoke: optional (OpenRouter is paid-per-token; the unit test
  asserting wire shape is the primary verification). If a live test
  is wanted, mu ask --provider openrouter --model <some-cheap-or>
  with the canary question should surface w11wKFoinTnTRMS.

## Composes with / depends on

- mu-2puu (codex sibling, landed in PR #115)
- mu-yqeq.6 (OpenRouter Projected arm — original landing of the
  buggy translate function)
close_reason: Fixed in PR #116. translate_provider_messages_openrouter now hoists all non-tool-schema System-role spans (concatenated with \n\n) into a single leading role=system message in the Chat Completions messages array. 2 regression tests cover the case (mu_745h_projected_concatenates... + mu_745h_projected_excludes_tool_schema...). 773 tests pass, clippy clean. Live smoke against OpenRouter not run in this PR (paid-per-token; unit-test contract sufficient).

title: mu-session-lifecycle: add session.remove, archive, restore, list_archived RPCs + TUI affordances
description:
Add session removal + archive operations to mu's session lifecycle. Today mu can `create_session` but has no way to remove or archive — sessions accumulate forever in the daemon and TUI.

## Why

Triggered by reviewing Anthropic's `claude agents` view (2.1.142 changelog, 2026-05-15): they ship a "running/blocked/done" session list with implicit lifecycle (sessions end). Mu's `session.list` shows everything ever created with no way to retire entries. As mu sees daily-driver use, this becomes operational debt — the F3 picker gets cluttered with stale sessions, RAM holds dead session state, and there's no "view this old session's transcript" path that doesn't require keeping it active.

## Scope (likely decomposes into 2 sub-beads — file this as parent epic)

### A. Server-side RPCs

- `session.remove { session_id, reason? }` — hard-delete from registry + event log
- `session.archive { session_id }` — mark archived; remove from default `session.list` results; keep event log + state retrievable
- `session.restore { session_id }` — un-archive
- `session.list_archived` — paginated listing of archived sessions
- `session.get_archived_transcript { session_id }` — read-only access to an archived session's rope/event log (no resumption)

Question: are archived sessions read-only forever, or restorable to active? Likely restorable, but the boundary needs naming (e.g., archived → resumed → active, or archived stays archived and you spawn a new session forked from its state).

### B. TUI affordances

- F3 picker: `d` (or similar) to archive selected session; confirmation dialog
- F3 picker filter: toggle between "active sessions" and "include archived"
- Per-archived-session: shortcut to view transcript read-only (could compose with mu-mh4 read-side persistence)
- Audit firehose entry on archive/restore/remove

## Composes with

- **mu-mh4** (session persistence read-side) — archive ≈ "persist but hide"; reading an archived transcript IS the mu-mh4 use case
- **mu-f5k** (proxy-bridge Phase 1) — proxy event log captures the auth-side wire; archived session = stop emitting, keep the log
- **mu-kgu compaction** — archiving may want to apply compaction policy on the way out, to shrink storage

## Acceptance (rough — refine in sub-beads)

- `session.remove`, `session.archive`, `session.restore`, `session.list_archived` RPCs exist with typed protocol shapes
- Default `session.list` excludes archived; archived count surfaced separately
- F3 picker can archive a session and toggle visibility of archived
- All four RPCs gated by appropriate Capability (likely a new `SessionLifecycle` axis, or extend existing `SessionAdmin`)
- Tests: archive→list excludes→restore→list includes; remove→list excludes permanently
- Existing peer.hello / session.list tests still green

## Out of scope (defer to follow-ups)

- Automated archival policy (e.g., "archive idle >7 days") — manual operator action only in v1
- Cross-daemon archive sync (cluster-level lifecycle)
- Garbage-collection of removed-session storage (immediate hard-delete is sufficient for v1)

## Provenance

Operator observation 2026-05-15 while reviewing `claude agents` page in Anthropic's 2.1.142 changelog. Quote: "we have the ability to add sessions to mu, but not to remove them. We may also want an archive so we can choose to restore/view an old session."

title: GPT session ends on tool_use; daemon may close stream before final assistant turn
description:
## Symptom

Two sessions on daemon 8c7823, same prompt:
- session-1 (GPT, model TBD) ended on a tool read (no final text response)
- session-2 (Opus) responded with text normally

The GPT session terminated after the model issued a tool call and the tool result was streamed back. Expected: the model produces a text turn synthesizing the tool result; observed: the loop ends.

## Operator hypothesis

> "wondering if we're too fast"

Suggests mu's agent loop or stream completion may close the assistant turn before GPT's provider-side bookkeeping signals it's done. Could be:
- mu's stream-end detection (Anthropic-shaped) misreads OpenAI's `stop_reason: "tool_calls"` as terminal when there's a follow-up assistant message coming
- mu's tool-result injection races the provider's pending text frames
- session lifecycle (mu's loop body) returns early after tool result is delivered rather than re-entering the model with the tool result attached

## Repro pointer

Daemon ID `8c7823...` — events should be in `~/.local/share/mu/events/8c7823.../`. Compare session-1 (GPT, hung-on-tool) vs session-2 (Opus, completed). The transition from `tool_result` → next `model_call` is the likely culprit.

## First places to look

- `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/` — the agent loop modules split via mu-nk3.
- `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/openai_codex/` — OpenAI stop-reason handling.
- `crates/mu-core/src/serve/dispatch/` (post-mu-2jg split) — the handler that sequences tool_result back to the model.

## Why P1 (not P0)

Doesn't crash, doesn't lose work — just stalls the conversation. Workaround: tell GPT to "continue" or restart the session.
close_reason: Fixed via PR #81 (merged 2026-05-18) — StopReason::IterationCap variant added + emitted at the cap-exit; per-provider max_turns defaults; TUI surfaces iteration_cap distinctly. Close-on-merge automation didn't fire; closing manually.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
## Diagnosis (2026-05-18)

Investigation comparing the two sessions in daemon `8c78230c467e1de7`:

**session-1 (GPT)**: 20 tool calls. Last 4 events: `tool_call → tool_result → done(end_turn) → task_telemetry`. No `context_assembly` or `assistant_message_event` after the final `tool_result` — mu did not re-enter the model.

**session-2 (Opus)**: 15 tool calls. Last events: `tool_result → context_assembly → provider_status_update → assistant_message_event(text) → done(end_turn)`. Normal final synthesis turn.

**Root cause**: `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/mod.rs:689-704`. When `turn_count >= config.max_turns` (default 20), the loop emits `AgentEvent::Done { stop_reason: StopReason::EndTurn }` without invoking the LLM. `StopReason` has no `IterationCap` variant; the code hardcodes `EndTurn`, which lies to every downstream consumer.

The "we're too fast" operator hypothesis was directionally right: mu *did* terminate before getting a final assistant turn. The mechanism is the turn budget, not stream timing.

## Reframe — three independent fixes

1. **Add `StopReason::IterationCap` variant** in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/types.rs:103-119`, emit it from the cap-exit at `mod.rs:693`. The `AgentConfig` doc comment at `mod.rs:318` already promises `Outcome::IterationCap` — the *event* protocol just never got the matching variant. Smallest, sharpest fix.

2. **Inject a synthesis turn** before terminating: a final invocation with a system note like "you've hit the turn budget; summarize and stop." Without this, the user is left staring at a stale tool result with no closing message.

3. **Per-model defaults**: 20 is fine for Anthropic models, low for OpenAI. Either raise the default or have per-provider-kind defaults.

## Surfacing

TUI / transcript should render `IterationCap` distinctly: "Hit turn limit (N). Use `--max-iterations M` to raise, or ask a follow-up to continue." Currently the user just sees the conversation stop.

## Code locations

- `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/mod.rs:344` — default `max_turns: 20`
- `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/mod.rs:689-704` — the broken cap-exit (emits `EndTurn`)
- `crates/mu-core/src/agent/types.rs:103-119` — `StopReason` enum needs a new variant
- `crates/mu-coding/src/serve/` — handlers that map `StopReason` to the wire protocol (need pass-through for new variant)
- TUI: search for `StopReason::EndTurn` in `crates/mu-tui/`

title: Session reanimate/reconstitute picker (six-action; ALIVE/MUMMIFIED/FOSSILIZED/REANIMATED)
description:
Implement the six-action rewind/reanimate picker that lets the operator restore a session to a previous prompt-checkpoint, with the agent vocabulary tcovert prefers: ALIVE / MUMMIFIED / FOSSILIZED / REANIMATED.

## Source

`specs/architecture/claude-code-feature-mapping.md` §3 (Tier 1, "Session management — resume / branch / rewind"):

> Per-prompt picker with six actions:
> 1. Restore code AND conversation
> 2. Restore conversation only (keep code)
> 3. Restore code only (keep conversation)
> 4. Summarize FROM here forward (compress recent context)
> 5. Summarize UP TO here (compress early setup)
> 6. Never mind

The dual rewind/summarize axis is the gem: **Restore is destructive** (undo state). **Summarize is non-destructive** (compress context). Both target the same prompt-checkpoint.

## Vocabulary (operator preference, 2026-05-22)

Per [[humor_cultural_callbacks_in_code_naming]] memory `42b06542`, the operator welcomes cultural callbacks in code identifiers when they crystallize intent at the type level. The Re-Animator film reference here:

- **ALIVE**: currently live, attached to a session/agent (= `LiveProjection: true` in mu-c7zh terms)
- **MUMMIFIED**: archived but preserved; expects to be dusted off and continued
- **FOSSILIZED**: deeply archived, requires the reanimate process to bring back
- **REANIMATED**: a previously dormant session restored to ALIVE
- **DUST OFF**: operation moving MUMMIFIED → ALIVE without compaction (cheap revival)

mu-c7zh (session-lifecycle spec) should adopt this vocabulary in its state model; this bead implements the user-facing surface.

## Dependencies

Blocks on: **mu-c7zh** (session-lifecycle spec must define ALIVE→MUMMIFIED→FOSSILIZED→REANIMATED state model first).

## Scope

In:
- Picker UI surface (key chord registered in mu-tui, likely Esc-Esc on empty input per CC convention, or a /reanimate command per the cross-provider command abstraction in mu-pgpb)
- Six actions presented; first three call into reconstitute APIs from mu-c7zh
- Pre-edit file snapshots for "restore code" semantics — checkpoint storage keyed by `(session_id, prompt_id, file_path)`. Bash-modified files NOT tracked (match CC behavior; document the gap)
- Summarize actions invoke compaction at the chosen prompt boundary (FROM-here vs UP-TO-here)
- Vocabulary applied to API/type names (e.g. `Session::reanimate(from: FossilizedSessionId)`, `Session::dust_off(from: MummifiedSessionId)`)

Out:
- External-edit detection — defer
- Cross-worktree session reanimation — defer to a follow-on bead once worktree adapter (specs/architecture/claude-code-feature-mapping.md §B) lands

## References

- specs/architecture/claude-code-feature-mapping.md §3 (rewind/branch/resume)
- mu-c7zh (lifecycle spec)
- mu-d033 (/handoff event)
- mu-lcf7 (paused-vs-closed state)
- Memory 42b06542 (humor + cultural callbacks naming convention)
- CC source: code.claude.com/docs/en/sessions, /en/checkpointing

## Acceptance

- Picker keychord or command registered in mu-tui
- Six actions present
- "Restore code" actually restores from snapshot
- Summarize actions reach the compaction pipeline
- ALIVE/MUMMIFIED/FOSSILIZED/REANIMATED vocabulary used in code, surfaced in mu-c7zh

title: goal session 2026-05-13c: mu-ktq foundational traits (experiment 3, first API-key /goal, architecture-shaped work)
description:
Tracking bead for experiment 3 (2026-05-13c).

## Primary: mu-ktq (foundational traits, scoped tighter than the original bead)

Implement the FOUNDATION half of mu-ktq:
- ProviderRenderer + CacheStrategy trait definitions
- Supporting types: ProjectionTarget, CacheBoundary, ProviderMessages (minimal)

- Tests: rope → traits → expected output

Provider-specific impls (AnthropicCacheStrategy, AnthropicProviderRenderer) are DEFERRED to a follow-on bead — they need more design thought given existing mu-i6j cache_control work and the current Anthropic adapter shape.

## Auth experiment: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (not OAuth)

Max5 OAuth is at 97% with 19h to reset. Run this experiment on the API key path. Records data on whether /goal + --max-budget-usd works on anthropic-api the same way it does on OAuth.

## Research value

This is the first ARCHITECTURE-SHAPED /goal experiment. Prior experiments (1 + 2) tested spec-execution-shaped work. mu-ktq has real design ambiguity (trait method signatures, type names, module organization, what RetainedRope's stub shape should be). If /goal handles this well, the methodology generalizes beyond rote spec implementation.

## Budget

$20 (higher than experiment 2's $15 because high-variance work; could come in cheaper).

## Sub-beads

- mu-ktq (required; scope-narrowed per the description above)

## Acceptance

This bead closes when: mu-ktq scoped-down version closed; cargo test --workspace green; MORNING_2026-05-13c.md briefing written; experiment doc post-run filled; this tracking bead closed.

## Pre-flight

- API-key smoke test required (verify --print + /goal + --max-budget-usd works on anthropic-api auth path; <$1 budget)
- Smoke can re-use the same goal text shape as experiment 1's verification

## References

- Predecessor experiments: mu-o6v, mu-bqj
- mu-ktq bead (the foundational draft)
- Draft text for mu-ktq: ~/.claude-personal/drafts/mu-bead-2-provider-cache-strategies.md

- Skill: ~/.claude-personal/skills/goal-protocol/SKILL.md (now includes tool-conventions reference)

No stop criteria fired. Stretch A and Stretch C completed; Stretch B skipped because trait names match spec verbatim. First architecture-shaped /goal session — methodology validated for design work when spec proposal is sufficiently developed.

title: Auditor role: Capability::auditor() constructor + AuditorView projection over the event log
description:
## Goal

mu-capability-substrate.md calls out 'first-class Mu session role, not only an AWS role.' Right now the event log makes the information available, but there's no role/capability that says 'this session has broad read, narrow/no write' baked in. Land it.

## Sketch (per opus review event 194)

1. Capability::auditor() — constructor producing a Capability with read-tools-only allowed_tools, autonomy disallowed, no write tools, no AWS write grants.
2. AuditorView — a projection over the event log that exposes the read-only audit surface (session events, capability lineage, tool-call provenance) as typed queries.
3. AuditorView is what 'session.audit' (a new RPC, possibly) returns — or what the orchestrator's audit-pane shows.

## Why

This is the architectural promise of the capability substrate cashing out as a concrete role. Sits next to mu-yz48 / observability work as part of the 'forensics + audit' positioning per the strategic-direction review.

## Composes with

- mu-hh1 (SupervisorCapability axis) — both add typed roles to Capability.
- Forensics analytics (mu-8alb, mu-mk9l) — AuditorView is the canonical reader for that data.

## Source

opus-4-7 review event 194, 'Future code direction' item 5.

title: Verify tool-use rendering in transcript
description:
Ask the model to do something that triggers a tool call (e.g. "read this file" or "glob for *.md"). Verify the magenta `tool: NAME` block renders with:
- args: { ... } line (truncated at 200 chars)
- → ok or !! error result line (truncated at 400 chars)
- result is paired with its call by call_id

If the args/result rendering needs tuning, file follow-ups.

Context:
- render_transcript_lines in crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs pairs ToolCall/ToolResult by call_id
- ToolResult-only rows are skipped (rendered inline under their ToolCall)

title: mu-auth-handshake: SASL-shaped pluggable connect-time auth (BEARER default, GSSAPI/OAuth/TLS-cert feature-gated)
description: See mu-hh1 comment and pattern-B' architectural discussion (2026-05-15 session) for full body. Pluggable SASL-shaped connect-time handshake. Default-compiled mechanisms: BEARER (file-perm-protected token) + EXTERNAL (unix socket peer-uid). Feature-gated mechanisms: auth-tls-client-cert (uses already-in-workspace rustls), auth-gssapi (libgssapi system dep), auth-oauth (JWT validation). Verify-once-cache-then enforcement: handshake produces a Capability cached against the connection; per-RPC checks are O(struct field read). Threat model = ssh-agent / Docker socket posture for local-only, deferred-to-mechanism for distributed. Co-designs with mu-hh1 (capability is the auth payload; mechanism populates, daemon enforces). Operator constraint: desktop users should not have to pull libgssapi etc. — feature flags are load-bearing.
close_reason: Superseded by mu-7rk-{a..g} decomposition (mu-vha, mu-yox, mu-fnn, mu-1p6, mu-5pn, mu-4fd, mu-oeo). v0 diff retained as reference at jj rev twztzykv 78978fdb.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Closing as v0 attempt; superseded by decomposition into mu-7rk-{a..g} after codex antagonistic review found 4 blockers + 9 important issues. Decomposition: mu-vha (-a, protocol types) → mu-yox (-b, BEARER handler) → mu-fnn (-c, P1, enforcement gate, the security property) → mu-1p6 (-d, transport close), with mu-5pn (-e, client side, parallel from b) and mu-4fd (-f, hardening) / mu-oeo (-g, multi-step, deferred). Reference v0 diff at jj rev twztzykv 78978fdb (workspace .delegations/mu-7rk-2026-05-15) — kept around for new workers to reference good parts.

title: justfile: discovery + composition for common mu workflows
description:
Add a top-level `justfile` with thin recipes that mirror today's most-frequent flows. Goal: reduce cognitive load (one `just --list` to see what the project supports) and shorten the most common one-liners (`./scripts/pre-pr-check.sh` → `just check`).

## Why now

Operator (2026-05-16): 'reduce my cognitive load and keep my context smaller. Hopefully it does the same for agents.' Both human and agent sessions currently re-derive the same incantations (the pre-PR check path, the dev-mode mu ask, the jj bookmark push dance for PRs). A justfile is the project's front door for these.

## Scope

Add recipes for: `check`, `check-quick`, `fmt`, `clippy`, `test`, `smoke`, `ask`, `serve`, `versions`, `pr <bookmark>`. Recipes call the existing scripts/cargo invocations — no duplication.

## Non-goals

- Replacing scripts/ or gh-wrapper. Recipes call scripts; gh-wrapper still enforces pre-pr-check at PR promotion.
- Adding new enforcement. The justfile is for discovery + composition, not for new gates.
- Bundling all rules into one giant recipe. Each recipe does one thing.

## Acceptance

- `justfile` at repo root
- `just --list` produces a readable menu
- `just check` runs pre-pr-check.sh
- `just pr <name>` pushes the bookmark and opens gh pr create
- README mentions just briefly under 'Quick start' or 'Development'
- pre-pr-check passes
close_reason: Merged in PR #58 (c45267c) — top-level justfile

title: mu-slat: reap must tear down the pot jail + idempotent mounts; etcd-lease per spawn for collision-free names + liveness-reap
description:
Surfaced by the 2026-05-31 mu-slat dogfood (mu-k4i3 Part 2). Three coupled distributed problems:

1. POT-JAIL LEAK: host-side reap kills the worker CLAUDE process (reaped=true) but does NOT destroy the pot jail. Every spawn leaks a running jail. Saw sessions 2-5 leaked in one session; 54 stacked fdescfs mounts on reused pots.
2. NON-IDEMPOTENT MOUNTS: mu-spawn re-mounts fdescfs/linprocfs/linsysfs/tmpfs-shm non-idempotently across reused pot names, so they STACK; pot destroy leaves them orphaned on /opt/pot/jails/<pot>/m/...
3. POT-NAME COLLISION: every daemon numbers sessions from session-1, so the worker is ALWAYS mu-worker-session-2 (faux caller=session-1) -> guaranteed name reuse across daemons -> reuse poisons the next worker with stale pot state (observed: a worker ran a stale 'mu summary' task instead of its uname prompt).

FIXES (operator suggested etcd):
- reap/destroy path: umount the 4 linux-fs (deepest-first) BEFORE pot destroy.
- mu-spawn: make each mount idempotent (check-before-mount).
- etcd-LEASE per spawn for a unique pot slot name (like agent-spawn-v2's agent-01..10 slots) -> collision-free names + lease-expiry-driven liveness reap (kills orphans/mu-xac automatically).

Drain recipe that works today (band-aid): loop umount mu-worker mountpoints deepest-first until 'mount|grep mu-worker' empty, then pot destroy. Memory 521c83c2 (follow-up #3). Validated dogfood: ~/tmp/mu_slat_e2e.py + mu_slat_e2e_llm.py.

title: mu-036 Phase C: schedule_wakeup + RunMode::Sleeping
description: Add session.schedule_wakeup RPC handling. RunMode::Sleeping holds a tokio sleep future; on wake, return to RunMode::Autonomous with the wake reason injected as the next iteration's motivation. Bounds: while sleeping, only wall_clock counts against autonomy budget (INV-5). Tests: schedule_wakeup(100ms) parks the session, no provider calls during sleep, resumes at iteration N+1.
close_reason: Implemented by PR #209 (mu-28u Phase C) — schedule_wakeup parks the loop per this bead's spec: sleep future, no provider calls during sleep, resume at N+1 with wake reason as motivation.

title: Mid-session full prefix-cache invalidations (~580K wasted cache-writes, ~$2.20 of an $8.62 session) — early span content mutating under a stable span id; add prefix/span content hashes to ContextAssembly for cache forensics
description: Session c76f6949f56efbe6 (sonnet, 2026-06-03): cache performance was otherwise outstanding (median per-call hit rate 97.2%%, 65%% cost saved vs uncached — $8.62 vs $24.58). But four NON-compaction events (calls 18, 42, 63, 65) rewrote the entire prefix seconds after successful calls (gaps 3-45s — NOT TTL), with cache_read collapsing to exactly 2,210 tokens each time. ContextAssembly diffs across those boundaries (per-#161 instrumentation) show ONLY normal append growth: first_span_ids identical, cache_boundary_count 1→1, tool_count 10→10. Conclusion: an early rope span MUTATED CONTENT while keeping its id (candidates: FileLoad rehydration re-reading a changed file — note PRs were merging into this very repo during the session; memory-recall span regeneration; renderer projection of an old span changing). Evidence insufficient to convict because ContextAssembly captures ids, not content identity. FIX (cheap): add a prefix_hash (hash of rendered content up to the cache boundary) and/or per-span content hashes (first N) to ContextAssembly — then any invalidation is a one-diff diagnosis: the call where prefix_hash changed names the byte range, and span hashes name the span. ~25%% of this session's cache-write spend was this bug; mu-tlri's compaction eviction was another chunk. Cross-ref: mu-tlri, PR #161, session INDEX in the bead trail.
close_reason: Merged PR #167 — prefix_hash + prefix_span_hashes on ContextAssembly; invalidations now one-diff diagnosable

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: REANALYSIS 2026-06-04 (full per-call table from durable log): the four 'phantom invalidations' (calls 17/41/62/64) all follow PREDECESSOR gaps of 340-1941s — >5min cache TTL expiry from idle, not a span mutating under a stable id. The bead's 'gaps 3-45s, NOT TTL' measured the SUCCESSOR gap. All 13 minute-crossings without a long gap kept high cache_read (also disproves the system-prompt time-line suspect). Residual real issue: post-expiry first call writes ONLY the 2,210-tok tools breakpoint and pays the full prefix as fresh input WITHOUT caching it; next call then pays full cache_write again (call 17: cw=2,210/fresh=136,486; call 18: cw=136,457). Mitigations worth a bead: (a) marker placement so the first post-expiry call writes the full prefix once; (b) optional Anthropic extended-TTL (1h) knob. The #167 hashes remain right for catching true mutations; none occurred in this session.

title: mu: add ollama provider (OpenAI-compat, baked-in 10.1.1.143) + dynamic route_catalog discovery
description:
Add a first-class `ollama` provider to mu, mirroring OpenRouterProvider (OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions, reuse SSE parser + tool translation). Baked-in default base http://10.1.1.143:11434, overridable via OLLAMA_API_BASE/PATH; OLLAMA_API_KEY optional (ollama needs none).

Scope (provider only; NO spawn_worker changes — that's the later consolidation):
- crates/mu-ai/src/providers/ollama.rs (mirror openrouter.rs)
- Ollama { model } variant in ProviderSelector (mu-core/protocol/session.rs)
- 'ollama' arms in selector_from_cli + build_provider_from_selector (mu-coding/serve/factory.rs)
- module exports (mu-ai/providers/mod.rs)
- Dynamic route_catalog: query ollama /api/tags at catalog build, merge entries, degrade gracefully when ollama down (short-timeout blocking probe; from_env stays sync).

Payoff: 'mu ask --provider ollama --model qwen3-coder:30b' works in-loop, AND agent-spawn-v2 spawns pot-isolated ollama workers with ZERO script change (AGENT_SPAWN_RUNTIME=mu AGENT_SPAWN_PROVIDER=ollama AGENT_SPAWN_MODEL=...). Verify a pot can reach 10.1.1.143 over vnet. Gate: just ci.
close_reason: done

title: TUI: $EDITOR integration for prompt editing (Ctrl-X Ctrl-E)
description:
## Why

For prompts longer than a few lines, in-place editing in the TUI is limiting even after mu-wd2 (basic readline). zsh's edit-command-line binding (`Ctrl-X Ctrl-E`) is the canonical pattern for 'this is too long to type comfortably here; let me bounce into a real editor'.

Discussed 2026-05-11. Helix-core embedding was considered and rejected — $EDITOR delivers the same value (you get any editor, including Helix when $EDITOR=hx) at a fraction of the implementation cost.

## How the pattern works

The TUI's state lives in-memory (App struct in Rust). The terminal display is the *output* of that state, not the storage. So the editor handoff is just 'who currently owns the terminal':

1. Persist current prompt buffer to a tempfile (with a .md suffix so editors syntax-highlight markdown by default).
2. Tear down our terminal grip: `disable_raw_mode()`, `execute!(stdout(), LeaveAlternateScreen)`. The shell's prior scrollback comes back.
3. Spawn $EDITOR (default 'vi' if unset) on the tempfile. It inherits stdin/stdout/stderr from us; it owns the terminal.
4. Block on the editor's status (synchronous; UX-wise, the TUI 'pauses' while the editor is open).
5. On editor exit: `execute!(stdout(), EnterAlternateScreen)`, `enable_raw_mode()`. We're back in a fresh alt-screen canvas; next render tick repaints the App.
6. Read the tempfile back, populate the prompt buffer.
7. Clean up the tempfile.

Total: ~30 LOC plus a key-binding entry.

## Keybinding

Ctrl-X Ctrl-E (matching zsh / bash readline). Or, if Ctrl-X is reserved for something else in the TUI, F12, or :edit palette command.

## Edge cases

- Editor exits with non-zero status (user did :cq in vi to abort): treat as cancel — don't update the prompt buffer.
- Tempfile cleanup on panic: use a RAII guard (`tempfile::NamedTempFile` does this).
- Slow editors / large files: not a real concern for prompts; mu's prompts are typically <10KB. If this ever matters, async/spawn-thread variants exist.

## Acceptance

- Pressing Ctrl-X Ctrl-E from input mode opens $EDITOR with current prompt content.
- Saving + exiting populates the prompt buffer with the edited content.
- Cancelling (non-zero exit) leaves the prompt buffer unchanged.
- Works with vi, nvim, hx, nano, ed in CI tests (or at minimum, a faux-editor that just echoes 'X' into the file).

## Cross-reference

- mu-wd2 (arrow keys in-place) — complement, not replacement. Most short prompts will still be edited in-place; $EDITOR is for the long-prompt case.
close_reason: done

title: claude-code-in-pot: spawn workers that use Max5 subscription
description:
Spawn claude-code in agent-runtime pots as a third runtime option (AGENT_SPAWN_RUNTIME=claude alongside mu and pi). Goal: pot workers can leverage the existing Max5 subscription instead of paying per-token via openrouter.

## Policy clearance (already obtained)

Memory 612ce8ab (2026-05-03 explicit authorization): 'When spawning Claude sub-agents (e.g. via SDK, claude-code spawn, future --mode implement against Claude), use Thaddeus's OAuth and subscription. This is explicitly authorized.' Distinct from the Meridian zone (memory 282cb61e) — that was about third-party SDK proxying; this is the real claude-code CLI.

## Technical inventory (verified 2026-05-16)

- claude-code is Linux x86-64 ELF (`for GNU/Linux 3.2.0`, interpreter `/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2`), 233MB. Runs on host via FreeBSD's Linux ABI emulation.
- Host has linux.ko/linux64.ko loaded + /compat/linux chroot present.
- Pot template currently has `pot.attr.linprocfs=NO` — Linux compat is DISABLED in clones today.
- OAuth credentials at ~/.claude/.credentials.json and ~/.claude-personal/.credentials.json (JSON with accessToken + refreshToken).

## Deploy pieces required

1. **Enable Linux ABI in agent-runtime pot.** Flip `pot.attr.linprocfs=YES` (and any sibling 'allow linux64' flag pot exposes). Verify a known-good Linux binary (`/compat/linux/bin/uname -a` or similar) runs in a fresh clone.
2. **Mount /compat/linux into pot.** Read-only nullfs from host. Required because claude-code's ELF interpreter resolves through it.
3. **Mount claude-code binary + version-dir into pot.** Curated approach (matching mu): create ~/pot-bin/claude/ with the relevant tree, add fscomp.conf entry.
4. **Plumb OAuth credentials into pot.** Open design question — see below.

## Open design question: refresh-token sharing

Read-only nullfs mount of ~/.claude*/.credentials.json into the pot has a subtle hazard: claude-code rotates refresh tokens periodically and writes them back. If the credentials file is read-only inside the pot, the rotation will fail silently. If it's read-write, every concurrent claude-code-in-pot might race on rotation, possibly corrupting tokens.

Three sketch resolutions to evaluate:
- (a) per-spawn copy of credentials into pot tmpfs, accept that the refreshed token is lost on teardown (token still works for the next spawn if the original hasn't expired)
- (b) read-only mount + a host-side daemon that handles refresh and writes back on a synchronization-aware schedule
- (c) per-runtime credentials file (`~/.claude-pot-worker/.credentials.json` separate from interactive sessions), refreshed independently — probably needs an Anthropic API change to support, may not be feasible

(a) is the obvious starting point. Cost: every spawn uses the same long-lived token until host-side rotation. Acceptable for sub-hour-old tokens; problematic for long-deferred refresh cycles.

## Open architectural question: pot-isolation vs host-direct

`~/src/claude-personal/scripts/agent-router --auth claude-oauth` already invokes claude-code on the host with the subscription — no pot. The marginal value of pot-isolating claude-code is *isolation* (a runaway worker can't touch host fs/state). That's only valuable for delegations whose prompt source isn't fully trusted. For delegations the operator authors, host-direct may suffice.

Decision criteria: does the operator want to delegate work to claude-code workers from prompts that originate outside their own session (memory store, automated triggers, etc.)? If yes, pot isolation matters. If no, host-direct is enough.

## Scope decision

Deferred to Sprint 2 — after the telemetry chain lands (mu-6dyg → mu-5g7i → mu-8alb → mu-8ypx → mu-mk9l). Reasons:
1. Telemetry data tells us *which* outcome classes claude-code would improve on, so we can scope the pot integration deliberately.
2. The credential-sharing design question deserves prototyping before committing to a deploy pattern.
3. agent-router already provides host-direct claude-code spawn for any urgent claude-OAuth need.

## Acceptance (when work begins)

- agent-runtime pot can run a Linux binary (smoke test).
- claude-code runs in a pot clone via `jexec`, completes a faux/cheap prompt against Max5.
- agent-spawn gains AGENT_SPAWN_RUNTIME=claude path.
- OAuth credential mount strategy chosen and documented.
- Tier 2 + Tier 3 validation against claude-code-in-pot returns clean output.

## Cross-references

- Memory 612ce8ab — explicit authorization
- Memory 282cb61e — operating arrangement (distinguishes from Meridian)
- mu-2s84 — pot snapshot naming gotcha (relevant during template updates)
- ~/src/claude-personal/scripts/agent-router — existing host-direct claude-oauth dispatcher

title: mu-fvy0-classify: post-completion outcome classifier (clean / dirty / hollow / lying / narrative)
description:
After a worker emits TaskTelemetry (mu-fvy0-telemetry), post-process to classify outcome. The classifier compares the worker's claimed work product against the actual repository state and outputs a discrete outcome class for analytics.

## Outcome classes

- **clean_success** — exit Done + claim matches diff + CI green
- **cosmetic_failure** — exit Done + CI fmt-only-fail (cheap rework, no semantic bug)
- **bug_in_output** — exit Done + CI test/clippy fail (real bug, but commit content was honest)
- **hollow_commit** — exit Done + commit message claims files NOT in `git diff-tree --name-status` (the mu-nk3 failure mode from 2026-05-16)
- **lying_state** — exit Done + state.json `result` claims PR # that doesn't exist on origin
- **narrative_no_action** — exit Done + zero file modifications + narrative completion message (the `mu ask` + codex failure mode from 2026-05-16)
- **budget_halted**, **error_exit**, **timeout**, **operator_intervention** — non-success terminal states with reason captured

## Classifier inputs

- TaskTelemetry envelope (from mu-fvy0-telemetry)
- Worker's commit (if any) — extract claimed file list from message via regex/heuristic
- Actual `git diff-tree --no-commit-id --name-status -r <commit>` for that commit
- Optional: PR number from state.json; verify on origin
- Optional: CI status if PR exists and CI has run

## Discipline

- Classification is **best-effort** — false positives are expected. Surface confidence (Definite / Probable / Inferred) and let the analytics layer aggregate appropriately.
- The hollow_commit detector specifically needs the "claim parser" to identify file paths from prose. Start narrow: regex for `crates/.../X.rs` and similar; expand based on real false-positive cases.

## Acceptance

- `forensics::classify_task(envelope, commit_info) -> Outcome` function
- `Outcome` enum with all classes above + confidence level
- Unit tests for each class using fixtures from 2026-05-16 incident data (hollow: mu-nk3 first attempt; lying: mu-2jg first attempt; narrative: codex mu-el3c; clean: mu-r94 after rework)
- Classification result attached to or emitted alongside TaskTelemetry

## Blocks

Blocked by: mu-fvy0-telemetry (envelope must exist first)
close_reason: Merged in PR #60 (bc8b748) — post-completion outcome classifier

title: Live compaction is a no-op by default: resolves to NoCompactionPolicy unless default_policy=heuristic; hash-and-summary not wired to serve path
description:
## Finding (2026-05-30 critical review)

Live-session compaction is effectively disabled by default, and the showcase policy is not reachable on the live path at all. Traced while investigating why a real session ran to 164k/200k (config trigger_threshold_tokens=150000) with no compaction.

### Mechanism

1. The trigger IS wired into the live loop (crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/mod.rs:971): when pre_compaction_tokens > threshold, it picks config.compaction_policy_override OR provider.compaction_policy().
2. provider.compaction_policy() defaults to NoCompactionPolicy (crates/mu-core/src/agent/provider.rs:162) and NO concrete provider (Anthropic/OpenRouter/Codex) overrides it.
3. The serve path (crates/mu-coding/src/serve/handlers/session.rs:285) sets compaction_policy_override only for default_policy == "heuristic"; anything else (incl. unset) => None.

Net: with a threshold set but no default_policy, the trigger fires every turn past threshold and runs NoCompactionPolicy (a no-op). Compaction "runs" and keeps everything.

### Two gaps to fix

(a) DEFAULT IS A SILENT NO-OP. Threshold defaults ON (150k) but policy defaults to no-op, so the default experience is "threshold configured, nothing happens." Either default default_policy to a real policy, or warn when a threshold is set with policy=none. (Also: CompactionAssembly events are non-durable per mu-za92, so even a no-op run leaves no trace in the event log to notice this by.)

(b) HASH-AND-SUMMARY NOT WIRED TO THE SERVE PATH. Only "heuristic" (SpanFamilyDropPolicy) is selectable in session.rs's match. The HashAndSummaryPolicy (the model-judge policy; mu-kgu.3/.11) is implemented and tested but cannot be selected on a live session. So the README's marquee "live-Haiku judge" compaction result is bench-only today.

### Immediate operator workaround
Set [compaction] default_policy = "heuristic" in ~/.config/mu/config.toml and verify SpanFamilyDropPolicy actually drops spans on a long session.

Cross-ref: mu-za92 (durability — why it is invisible in the log), specs/architecture/agent-experience-frictions.md (Sequencing review, "Build now").
close_reason: done
