title: mu orchestrate: minimal plan.toml driver (scout / planner / coder / auditor demo)
description:
## Goal

Make 'mu orchestrate plan.toml' real with a deliberately-minimal v1 that demos the architecture end-to-end. The infrastructure is already there:

- session.delegate with capability narrowing
- peer.hello + mailbox for inter-session coordination
- Discovery backends (file, local registry; etcd on roadmap)
- Autonomous mode with bounded escalation
- (Soon) biscuit-signed capability proofs over the wire (mu-hoe0)

What's missing: one example that ties it together.

## Proposed v1 plan.toml shape

[orchestrator]
goal = "survey the AWS sandbox, plan a small fix, implement, audit"

[[session]]
id = "scout"
capability = "aws.scout.readonly,read"
prompt = "Find any S3 buckets without versioning in the sandbox account. Output to mailbox 'scout-findings'."

[[session]]
id = "planner"
capability = "read,grep"
depends_on = ["scout-findings"]
prompt = "Read scout-findings; draft a one-fix plan; output to mailbox 'plan'."

[[session]]
id = "coder"
capability = "read,write,edit,bash:terraform apply"
depends_on = ["plan"]
prompt = "Implement the plan; commit; output to mailbox 'change'."

[[session]]
id = "auditor"
capability = "auditor"  # broad read, no write
depends_on = ["change"]
prompt = "Review the change; produce audit summary."

mu orchestrate is then a thin driver over: session.delegate per [[session]], mailbox.subscribe for depends_on, session.start_autonomous to fire the loop. Plus an --audit-log flag that emits the cross-session lineage as a single artifact.

## Why this matters strategically

Per opus review event 194, this is THE wedge demo: 'scout reads, planner plans, coder edits, auditor reviews — each with attenuated capabilities, none can widen, all logged.' The blog-post version writes itself once the demo runs.

## Composes with

- mu-hoe0 (biscuit cross-daemon) — needed for multi-daemon deployment, optional for v1 single-daemon.
- mu-7lwn (auditor role) — the auditor session uses Capability::auditor().
- mu-d033 (/handoff) — orchestrator could emit handoff events at session boundaries for resume.

## Source

opus-4-7 review event 194, 'Future code direction' item 7 + 'Strategic direction' section B.

title: mu-tui: F3 pi-style multi-screen layout + smaller-font status bar
description:
## Problem

F3 tries to put a lot of information on one screen. With all the F-screens
now available (F1-F9), the cramming is no longer necessary — each view can
be more focused. User comparison: pi's layout, which separates concerns
cleanly across panels and uses a quieter visual design.

## Proposal

F3 layout refresh, pi-inspired:

- Larger transcript area (primary content)
- Bottom status bar in smaller font (tokens/cost/budget/session-name
  compactly visible without dominating)
- Use F-screen tab system for ancillary information (tools, usage,
  events) — F3 itself focuses on the session transcript

Broader than mu-0hd (which is pi-inspired section-marker style for the
transcript itself); this bead covers overall page layout.

## Acceptance

- F3 layout visually quieter, content-focused
- Status bar smaller-font but legible
- Per-session ancillary info reachable via other F-keys or popups, not
  crammed into F3

## Cross-refs

- mu-0hd (pi-inspired section-marker style — this is broader layout)
- mu-2w7 (design exploration meta-bead)
- mu-u8a (TUI color polish)
- Provenance: TUI feedback (mu daemon f3845422 session-2, 2026-05-15)

title: Session templates: startup-time spawn presets for common (provider, model) combos
description:
## Why

Filed 2026-05-12 from the design discussion after mu-ium landed.

User workflow surfacing: typical daily work involves a small recurring set of (provider, model) sessions — e.g. opus-4-7 for hard reasoning, haiku-4-5 for cheap subtasks, gpt-5.5 for codex routing. Today, opening these requires 3+ rounds of `:provider` / `:model` / `n` per session. A 'session template' shortcut would let the user pick a named preset that spawns all the sessions in one keystroke (or auto-spawns on launch when a default is configured).

This is the multi-session generalization of mu-ium (which made one-at-a-time provider selection runtime-controllable).

## What

Two reasonable mechanisms, complementary not exclusive:

### A. Interactive startup picker

When mu-tui launches with no auto-template configured, show a small picker overlay:

```
  Choose a session template:
  ▶ daily       (3 sessions: opus, haiku, codex)
    review      (2 sessions: 2× sonnet)
    cheap       (1 session: haiku)
    [skip]      Start with no sessions
```

Selection spawns all the sessions in the template; user lands in F1 with them already created.

### B. Config-driven autostart

Templates live in `~/.config/mu/templates.toml` (or similar):

```toml
[templates.daily]
sessions = [
    { provider = "anthropic_api", model = "claude-opus-4-7",     name = "opus" },
    { provider = "anthropic_api", model = "claude-haiku-4-5...", name = "haiku" },
    { provider = "openai_codex",  model = "gpt-5.5",             name = "codex" },
]

[startup]
default_template = "daily"   # auto-spawn on launch
# default_template = ""        # show picker instead
```

A CLI flag overrides: `mu-tui --template review` (and a corresponding `--no-auto` or `--template -` to skip).

### Optional session naming

Templates can name their sessions (the `name` field above). When set, the session list (F1 / F3 picker) shows the name alongside the session_id — much friendlier than 'session-1, session-2, session-3' when you're juggling 3+.

## Cross-references

- mu-ium (closed): runtime :provider / :model commands. This bead extends to multi-session, presets.
- mu-cha (open, P2): F2 session tree. The picker variant could be folded into F2's session-management surface.
- mu-h04 / mu-wd2 (closed): TUI input ergonomics. Same author-of-frustration ('I do this every day, why can't it remember').

## Acceptance

- mu-tui launches with `--template daily` and creates the sessions listed in that template.
- If no template flag and config has `default_template`, that template auto-spawns.
- If neither, an interactive picker appears (or, if config disables it, the existing 'empty F1' state).
- Optional names for sessions are honored when set.
- Bad template (missing API key for a referenced provider) gracefully skips that session with a firehose warning, doesn't abort the whole template.

title: Compaction benchmark: plumb live ProviderJudge so HashAndSummary uses real Haiku, not a mock
description:
## Goal

Per MORNING_2026-05-14g and opus review event 194: the compaction benchmark currently runs HashAndSummaryPolicy against a mock Provider. Plumb a live ProviderJudge so we can produce a real head-to-head:

- HashAndSummaryPolicy (live Haiku judge)
- SpanFamilyDropPolicy (heuristic, no LLM call)
- NoCompactionPolicy (identity baseline)
- Anthropic's auto-compaction (the 5-minute Opus pass)

That's the headline measurement the README can stand on: 'mu's heuristic beats X by N×, mu's live-Haiku judge beats Anthropic auto-compaction by M× with comparable summary quality.'

## Acceptance

- Benchmark CLI accepts --provider haiku (or similar) and uses it for HashAndSummary's summary calls
- Output table includes real numbers for all four policies
- Calibration writeup lands in specs/measurements/ (mu-rcvk)

## Source

opus-4-7 review event 194, 'Future code direction' item 4.

title: mu-web-fetch: web/HTTP tool surface (consolidate curl + yt-dlp + pdf converters into MCP)
description:
Today, agents fetch web content via shelling out to `curl` / `yt-dlp` / `pdftotext` from the Bash tool. This works but: (1) every model invocation rebuilds the right invocation, (2) Bash's broad surface means web access has the same blast radius as arbitrary code execution, (3) no structured output (URL, content-type, status, body — all as text from stdout).

Operator preference (2026-05-14): consolidate into an MCP tool server, not built-ins. Control gain: the MCP gets its own attenuated capability (web.fetch.readonly, web.download.bucket, etc.), distinct from bash-everywhere.

## Scope

IN:
- Stdio MCP server exposing structured tools: `web_fetch(url, headers?)`, `youtube_transcript(url)`, `pdf_extract(path_or_url)`, possibly `html_to_md(url)`. Underlying impls wrap curl / yt-dlp / pdftotext / pandoc as today, but the AGENT sees typed tool calls.
- Capability namespace: `web.fetch.readonly` (any URL), `web.download.<scoped>` (e.g. specific domain prefix) — modeled on the AWS capability namespace pattern (mu-f5o struct-of-axes).
- Output: structured (status, content-type, body or extracted text).
- Implemented as MCP so it can be reused by other agents/harnesses, not just mu.

OUT:
- Built-in Rust HTTP client. The MCP server can be in any language; the tool registration shape is the wire surface.
- Full browser automation (Selenium/Playwright). v1 is read-only fetch + extract.
- Streaming responses (server-sent events from upstream). v1 collects then returns.

## Acceptance

- MCP server binary or script that mu can register via the MCP support landed in mu-yc6 (blocked-by)
- At least three tool methods working: fetch, youtube transcript, pdf extract
- Each tool's capability surface is documented in the server's tool schema
- Integration test: a mu session can read a URL via the MCP, content arrives as structured output

## Dependencies

- **Blocked by mu-yc6** (MCP plugin/tool-server support) — until mu has an MCP client, this server has nowhere to register.
- Capability namespace design (struct-of-axes from mu-f5o gives the shape; need to add a `web` axis or fold into a more general `network` axis).

## References

- mu-yc6: MCP support in mu-coding
- mu-f5o: capability struct-of-axes pattern; this web axis mirrors the AWS axis shape
- Operator note 2026-05-14: 'Right now webfetch is curl, yt-something to process youtube, and pdfto* programs. Certainly can consolidate into an MCP to help with control rather than having to use bash.'

title: triggered recall on action verbs: memory injection at point of action
description:
Novel hook concept from Opus mu-solo session (2026-05-27). Before the agent executes a 'danger' verb (rm, git push --force, jj abandon, sed -i, cargo publish, anything against production), the harness intercepts and queries the memory layer: 'is there a rule about this?'

If yes, surface it at the point of action — not at session start where it gets lost in the wall of context.

This is the 'attack the weapon' pattern applied to memory: don't load-bear on the agent remembering rules at action time, make action time the recall trigger.

Two variants:
1. PreToolUse blocking — hard prohibitions (sed -i, host Python). The harness refuses and shows the fix.
2. PreToolUse advisory — soft rules (VCS discipline, deploy windows). The harness surfaces context, agent decides.

The key insight: documentation doesn't survive 40 turns of focused work. Intervention at the decision point does.

Implementation: hook detects action verb pattern in bash/tool args → queries memory/decision store → injects relevant rules as system note → agent proceeds with context it would otherwise have forgotten.

Related: 6-layer model bead (Layer 3), topical recall on prompt submit (hook #2 in priority list).
close_reason: Merged PR #173 — v1: bash-scoped advisory, refuse-once-then-allow, fail-open, is_error:false (RetryPolicy::Never interplay). Generalized PreToolUse hook = follow-up with this as evidence.

title: mu-fvy0-analytics: telemetry sink + 'mu analytics' subcommand
description:
Project TaskTelemetry events into a queryable sink + expose pattern-detection queries via a new `mu analytics` subcommand. This is where the "blotter" becomes useful — actionable analytics over per-task envelopes for detecting provider drift, complexity ceilings, time-of-day patterns, etc.

## Sink

`~/.local/share/mu/telemetry.sqlite` (or extend existing mu data dir). Schema denormalized for query convenience:

```sql
CREATE TABLE tasks (
  task_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  session_id TEXT,
  parent_task_id TEXT,
  provider TEXT,
  model TEXT,
  started_at INTEGER,  -- unix ms
  ended_at INTEGER,
  wall_clock_ms INTEGER,
  prompt_tokens INTEGER,
  completion_tokens INTEGER,
  exit_reason TEXT,
  actual_spend_usd REAL,
  outcome_class TEXT,
  outcome_confidence TEXT,
  files_added INTEGER,
  files_modified INTEGER,
  lines_added INTEGER,
  lines_deleted INTEGER,
  claim_actual_delta INTEGER,
  local_hour INTEGER,
  day_of_week INTEGER,
  -- ...etc
);
CREATE INDEX idx_tasks_provider_model ON tasks(provider, model);
CREATE INDEX idx_tasks_started_at ON tasks(started_at);
```

Projection from event log → sqlite happens at task termination (or via a periodic compactor for batch backfill).

## CLI: `mu analytics`

Subcommands for the patterns the operator wants to spot:

- `mu analytics summary [--since 7d]` — totals by outcome class, by provider, by model
- `mu analytics rate --metric hallucination --by provider,week` — rate of {hollow_commit | lying_state | narrative_no_action} per (provider, model, week)
- `mu analytics correlation --x prompt_tokens --y outcome_class` — does larger prompt → worse outcome?
- `mu analytics drift --window 7d --baseline 30d` — flag any (provider,model) whose hallucination rate or mean wall_clock_ms shifted >2σ from baseline
- `mu analytics export --format csv` — raw dump for external analysis

Defaults are READ-ONLY. No mutation commands here.

## Discipline

- Queries are PRESET, not freeform SQL. Each query represents a specific question worth being able to ask.
- Don't over-engineer the query layer until real data exists — start with summary + rate; add drift/correlation when patterns are visible.

## Acceptance

- Sink populates from new TaskTelemetry events at task end
- `mu analytics summary` works and is sensible on at least 1 week of real data
- `mu analytics rate --metric hallucination` works
- Doc'd in `specs/mu-NNN-analytics.md`

## Blocks

Blocked by: mu-fvy0-telemetry, mu-fvy0-classify
close_reason: Merged in PR #61 (9cf7019) — telemetry sink + 'mu analytics' subcommand

title: mu-304g.x: F3 streaming preview doesn't close on assistant_text_finalized in agent loops
description:
Follow-up to mu-304g. The phase-reorder fix in nwmyvmnu (af0a75d5) makes F3 inline streaming render correctly for single-turn sessions (e.g. "hello?"). For multi-invocation agent loops, it leaves a cosmetic artifact.

## Repro

`just provider=anthropic model=claude-haiku-4-5 tui` → `n` → F3 → `i` → "review the mu project" → Enter.

Expected: each model invocation in the agent loop produces one cleanly-closed `┌─ assistant ... └─` block.

Observed: the FIRST invocation produces an empty `┌─ assistant` header (no body, no closer) at the top of the first assistant turn. The committed AssistantMessage then renders below it correctly via phase B. Subsequent invocations render correctly. Tool calls render correctly.

## Diagnosis

In single-turn sessions, `session.done` fires after `assistant_text_finalized` and clears `streaming_text`. Phase A's `(None, true)` arm sees `streaming_text == None && header_emitted == true`, emits the closer, increments pending, phase B filters the AssistantMessage. Clean.

In agent loops, `session.done` doesn't fire until the END of the entire session — between invocations, only `assistant_text_finalized` fires. My handler updates `streaming_text` to the canonical text but doesn't trigger close. Phase A stays in `(Some(text), true)` waiting for more streaming. The committed AssistantMessage commits to `transcript_events_by_sid`. Phase B renders it (pending = 0 because no close ever incremented).

Net visible artifact: empty header for the first invocation (which had no `\n` in its body, so phase A's body-emit-on-newline never fired), then committed block from phase B. For invocations 2+, header_emitted is still true (from invocation 1), so phase A's `(Some, false)` arm doesn't re-fire — no second empty header.

## Fix sketch

`assistant_text_finalized` should be treated as a "close this invocation's preview block" signal:

1. New App field `f3_streaming_force_close: HashSet<String>` (sids whose preview block should be closed on next emit).
2. `assistant_text_finalized` handler: snapshot current `streaming_text` into `f3_streaming_final_text` AND set `f3_streaming_force_close[sid]`. Also clear `streaming_text[sid]` so the next invocation's `text_delta` starts fresh.
3. Phase A: BEFORE the state-machine match, check `f3_streaming_force_close[sid]`. If set AND `header_emitted`: flush trailing buffer + emit closer + remove `header_emitted` + remove `chars_emitted` + increment `f3_preview_complete_pending` + clear `force_close`. Then continue to the state-machine match (which will now see `(None, false)` since `streaming_text` and `header_emitted` are both cleared).
4. Test: streaming_text re-grows on the next invocation's `text_delta` correctly (not appended to previous invocation).

Risk: the daemon's `assistant_text_finalized` semantics — does it fire per-invocation or only at session end? If only at session end, this fix is a no-op for agent loops and the artifact persists. Verification needed before implementing.

## Pivot context

Operator decided 2026-05-22 to build a new standalone TUI (mu-solo) rather than continue iterating on mu-tui's complex state machine. This bead is filed for record-keeping but may be deprioritized — the new TUI starts from a clean slate without the Inline/Fullscreen mode tangle.

## Cross-refs

- mu-304g (parent bead; primary fix landed in nwmyvmnu/af0a75d5)
- mu-lwiu (mu-tui main.rs split — would make this fix easier when it happens)
- mu-solo (new standalone TUI, in-progress)

## Acceptance

- "review the mu project" against haiku-4-5 produces clean assistant blocks for every invocation (no orphan headers)
- AssistantMessage commits filtered correctly in phase B
- `streaming_text` resets between invocations (no cross-invocation accumulation)
- `cargo test --workspace` green
close_reason: Closed as SUPERSEDED (not fixed-in-place), operator decision 2026-05-31. These are mu-tui F3-inline-render bugs; mu-solo is a SEPARATE clean-slate TUI that does not exhibit them (render.rs:109-119: re-render-from-model each frame, no incremental-commit drift). mu-tui's F3 path is not fixed and is slated for a rewrite. Closing to clear the active queue; bead retained in the DB for reference (br search finds closed beads) when mu-tui is rewritten. NOT a claim that a fix landed in mu-tui.

title: Implement mu-038 FileBackend — cross-daemon same-machine discovery
description:
Spec at specs/mu-038-projection-queries-and-discovery.md (jj qrpzxpuq 1af4f26a).

FileBackend writes TOML files under ~/.local/share/mu/daemons/<daemon_id>/sessions/<session_id>.toml on announce, deletes on withdraw. session.list with include_remote=true enumerates files across all daemon directories.

Liveness via daemon.heartbeat file mtime — daemons touch it every N seconds. Readers treat sessions from a daemon with stale heartbeat (>30s) as 'may be dead'.

Cross-daemon trust is OUT of scope here — same-user-on-same-machine assumed. Auth comes later (likely biscuit-auth).
close_reason: done

title: Phase A: events JSONL reader + token attribution for mu-u6hc context-window prototype
description:
Phase A of mu-u6hc (context-window observability prototype). Library-only — no UI.

## Scope

Parse mu's events JSONL (`<session>/events.jsonl`) and produce a typed data structure that attributes tokens to context regions.

## Inputs

- `context_assembly` events: layer-by-layer breakdown of assembled context
- usage events: input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens
- `tool_call` events: per-call token cost attribution

## Output type (sketch)

```rust
pub struct ContextAttribution {
    pub window_max: u32,
    pub total_used: u32,
    pub regions: Vec<Region>,           // top-level: system, CLAUDE.md, MCP, conversation, file-reads, etc.
    pub turn_usage: Vec<TurnUsage>,     // per-turn: input, output, cache_read, cache_creation
    pub top_consumers: Vec<Consumer>,   // largest single-source contributors (file:path -> tokens)
}
```

## Acceptance

- Pure function: `parse_events(jsonl: impl BufRead) -> Result<ContextAttribution>`
- Unit tests against fixture JSONL files (use existing mu-solo session captures as fixtures)
- `cargo test` green
- No new dependencies unless justified

## Out of scope

- Renderer (Phase B)
- CLI surface (Phase C)
- TUI panel (Phase D)

title: Token estimation: ProviderTokenizer trait for compaction / budget / F5 to agree with the model
description:
## Goal

mu-kgu.10 already swapped chars/4 to tiktoken cl100k_base — but that's only for compaction. The compaction estimator is a function, not a trait. As we add providers, each model has its own tokenizer (Anthropic uses a BPE that may diverge from cl100k_base by 5-15%; Codex uses OpenAI's tokenizer; OpenRouter passes through to whatever).

Per opus review event 194: a ProviderTokenizer trait would let compaction thresholds, budget ledgers, and the F5 dashboard report numbers the model itself agrees with — per-provider.

## Sketch

trait ProviderTokenizer { fn count(&self, text: &str) -> u32; fn name(&self) -> &str; }

impl on Tiktoken (cl100k_base) — current default for compaction
impl on AnthropicTokenizer (when their tokenizer is public; or call /tokenize endpoint)
impl on OpenAITokenizer (per model)

Provider::tokenizer() -> &dyn ProviderTokenizer

Compaction / budget / F5 use provider.tokenizer().count(...) rather than the global estimate_tokens.

## Why P3

mu-kgu.10 covers the compaction case 'good enough.' This is the next-level accuracy upgrade once we have multi-provider real numbers and care about <5% drift.

## Source

opus-4-7 review event 194, 'Future code direction' item 8.

title: mu-yox follow-up: deferred codex review minors and nits (M1 M3 N1 N2)
description:
## Context

mu-yox (#46) shipped with codex verdict `ship_with_minor_changes`:
0 blockers, 0 importants, 3 minors, 2 nits. The most important minor
(M2 — AuthState mutex poisoning produces lying Accepted) was filed
separately as **mu-m84 (P1)** and shipped alongside mu-yox. The
remaining deferred items are captured here.

## Items

### M1 — Debug-format test coverage for `{:#?}` variant

`auth_config_debug_redacts_tokens` test covers `{:?}` but not `{:#?}`.
The manual `fmt::Debug` implementation doesn't switch on the alternate
formatting flag (no `f.alternate()` check), so behavior is identical —
but the test should assert this rather than rely on code review.

**Fix:** add a second assert in the existing test using `{:#?}`,
verifying the same `[REDACTED; N entries]` output.

### M3 — `AuthStepOutcome::Challenge` variant exposed but unrouted

The `Challenge` variant of `AuthStepOutcome` is part of the public-ish
type surface, but the dispatcher doesn't route `peer.auth_response`
(multi-step state registry is mu-oeo / mu-7rk-g). Comments already
call out the ordering footgun. BEARER never emits `Challenge`.

**Decision required:** either (a) hide the Challenge variant until
mu-oeo lands (cleaner type surface, requires re-exposing later), or
(b) keep it exposed with a top-level note in `auth/mod.rs` plus a
runtime assertion in the dispatcher that any handler returning
Challenge currently produces an error (defensive — catches accidental
future regressions). Pick one before mu-oeo lands.

### N1 — Spec citation: RFC 7628 vs RFC 6750

Comment cites RFC 7628 (SASL OAUTHBEARER) for BEARER mechanism. If
the intended semantic is closer to HTTP bearer tokens (RFC 6750),
the citation should be updated. Pick one to be authoritative and use
it consistently in comments + future docs.

### N2 — Constant-time smoke test is timing-flaky by design

The `constant_time` smoke test in `auth_smoke.rs` is inherently flaky
(timing in tests is hardware-dependent). The real guarantee is
`subtle::ConstantTimeEq::ct_eq` over fixed-size digests (audited in
code review). The test serves as a regression tripwire.

**No action required** unless flake rate causes CI noise; if it does,
gate behind `#[ignore]` or move to a separate `cargo test --features
timing` opt-in target.

## Acceptance

- M1 fix landed (test covers `{:#?}`)
- M3 decision made + implemented per chosen option
- N1 citation aligned (7628 or 6750 chosen, used consistently)
- N2 left as-is OR moved to opt-in feature flag if flake rate causes
  CI noise

## Cross-refs

- mu-yox (this is follow-up to that bead; closed via PR #46)
- mu-m84 (codex M2, filed and shipped separately)
- mu-oeo / mu-7rk-g (Challenge multi-step state registry — referenced by M3)
- PR #46 body (full codex verdict context, including the verbatim 5 v0
  corrections audited)
- Provenance: codex-review-mu-yox-output.txt

title: mu-capsicum: pluggable Capsicum/Casper policy library for OS-enforced subprocess sandboxing
description:
Future-direction bead from operator 2026-05-21 (during mu-phl v0 goal session). Full design in agent memory `capsicum_casper_pluggable_sandbox_layer_2026_05_21`.

## What

A `mu-capsicum` crate (FreeBSD-only via cfg) implementing small policy primitives (ReadOnlyFs, NoNetwork, WhitelistedPaths, NoExec, NoNewFds, ...) that can be composed and entered before fork/exec of subprocess agents. Plus a Casper-client helper for mediated operations.

## Why

Honor-system permission modes inside subprocesses (claude-code, etc.) are bypass-able by prompt injection, bugs, or compromised behavior. Capsicum is OS-enforced — once cap_enter()'d, the kernel rejects disallowed syscalls regardless of process behavior. Stacked with mu-ywr's in-language capabilities, this is defense-in-depth.

The killer move: cap_enter() **before** fork/exec → child inherits the envelope. mu can sandbox the claude-code subprocess (mu-slat) at the OS level, not just the harness level.

## Scope

- Policy primitives as a library (ReadOnlyFs, NoNetwork, ...)
- Casper client for operator-mediated escape hatches
- Composition with mu-ywr's Capability model
- TOML config in `~/.config/mu/config.toml` for per-subprocess policy
- Eventual Linux parity (`mu-landlock`, `mu-seccomp`) under the same trait surface

## Composes with

- mu-slat (CC subprocess hosting) — natural first consumer
- mu-ywr (discovery/use capability model) — sibling axis
- mu-c7zh (session-lifecycle spec) — extends INV-LIV-1 to syscall level

## NOT in scope

- This bead is the architectural placeholder. Implementation depends on mu-slat shipping first (so we have a real subprocess hosting consumer to sandbox).

## Status

Filed 2026-05-21 as future direction. Implementation TBD; depends on mu-slat.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
## Related technique (operator 2026-05-21): libmap.conf + LD_LIBRARY_LOAD shim → jail_attach

A different layer of the same problem. Capsicum (this bead) does syscall-level capability mediation inside the process. The libmap-shim technique uses an LD_LIBRARY_LOAD library to overload vfork/fork/posix_spawn so that on the child-return-pre-exec branch, the shim calls `jail_attach(childpid)`. The exec'd binary runs inside a FreeBSD jail; the parent is unaffected and oblivious.

The two techniques compose — jail for filesystem/network containment, Capsicum for syscall control inside the jail.

Full design in agent memory `libmap_vfork_jail_attach_subprocess_isolation_2026_05_21`. Probably folds into this bead's implementation as a configurable option ("subprocess isolation mode: capsicum / jail-attach / both / none"), or splits into its own bead `mu-shim-jail-attach` if implementation scope grows.

Open questions captured in memory: IPC across jail boundary (unix socket mount-in), pot shared-vs-vnet networking, posix_spawn / clone3 shimming beyond vfork, pre-created jail vs ephemeral jail-per-spawn.

Operator's verbatim framing also in memory.

title: pre-pr-check: capture test output and name the real failing tests on failure
description:
When the cargo-test step fails, run_step's live (uncaptured) output scrolls away and the last thing visible is cargo's 'to rerun pass -p <crate>' footer — which routinely misattributes the failure (operator has been grepping logs by hand for this; on 2026-06-04 it blamed mu-core twice for a mu-coding flake, costing 3 gate runs during PR #176).

FIX SKETCH (scripts/pre-pr-check.sh): tee the cargo-test step to a temp log; on failure, grep -E 'FAILED|panicked at' the log and print the matching lines (the actual test names + panic messages) after the FAIL banner, plus the log path for full archaeology. Keep live output (tee, not redirect) so a watching human still sees progress.

Evidence/origin: mu-d3v6 session 2026-06-04; the ETXTBSY flake it would have named instantly is fixed separately in PR #176.

title: beads concurrency: merge-driver already handles it — verify jj merge-tool + consider periodic jsonl auto-commit (no separate repo, no daemon)
description:
Decision (operator, 2026-05-31): do NOT make a separate beads repo. Keep .beads/ in the code repo.

Terrain-check dissolved most of the presumed problem:
- br v1 has NO working daemon (`br --no-daemon` is "no-op in v1"; daemon.lock/bd.sock in .gitignore are aspirational). Do not pursue a daemon.
- Concurrency is ALREADY handled: .gitattributes has `.beads/issues.jsonl merge=beads-jsonl` and git config `merge.beads-jsonl.driver = br merge-driver %O %A %B`. Concurrent sessions committing bead changes auto-merge semantically (br's bead-aware 3-way merge), so any session can just commit .beads/issues.jsonl. The "who owns the commit / will I clobber others" caution was overblown.

Residual, narrow issues + proposed actions:
1. jj-workspace stale db: `jj workspace add` copies the tracked issues.jsonl but NOT the gitignored beads.db, so `br` in a new workspace rebuilds a divergent/stale db (memory 79686657). Mitigations: target `--db <canonical>` for writes, OR run `br sync --import-only` on workspace entry to rebuild the workspace db from the current jsonl. ACTION: pick one and document it (workspace-setup hook?).
2. jj merge-tool: the GIT merge driver is registered, but jj needs its own merge-tool entry (~/.config/jj/config.toml per docs/beads-merge-driver-setup.md) for the semantic merge to fire on jj merges. ACTION: verify/configure it.
3. Reaching origin: br sync never auto-commits. Bead closes live in the db immediately but only reach origin when someone commits the jsonl. ACTION (optional): a periodic/automated `chore(beads)` commit (cron or quiet-moment routine) so no agent has to own it — rather than per-session commits.

Net: lightweight. No new repo, no daemon. Mostly "confirm the merge-driver path works under jj + decide on auto-commit cadence + standardize workspace db hygiene."

title: agent-runtime pot template: include tq binary so workers can parse TOML too
description:
## Why

Companion to mu-yzvi (replace awk with tq in agent-spawn). Once host-side scripts standardize on tq for TOML parsing, the same dependency needs to be available inside spawned worker pots so worker scripts / pi extensions / future tooling can use the same toolchain instead of falling back to awk.

Operator note 2026-05-21: "we should make sure that tq is in the jails/pots as well."

## Approach

Established pattern from memory `agent_spawn_v1_overnight_2026_04_30` (mu-runtime template update for `pi`):

```sh
# Copy the binary into the template's filesystem
sudo cp ~/.cargo/bin/tq /opt/pot/jails/agent-runtime/m/usr/local/bin/tq
sudo chmod +x /opt/pot/jails/agent-runtime/m/usr/local/bin/tq

# Snapshot the template
sudo pot snapshot -p agent-runtime
```

(Exact pot commands per the existing template-update procedure — see the agent-runtime template's documented build/refresh steps.)

## Pre-flight checks

1. **Binary compatibility.** `~/.cargo/bin/tq` on the threadripper host is a FreeBSD-native ELF (compiled via `cargo install` on FreeBSD). The agent-runtime pot is FreeBSD with the same architecture (amd64). Verify with `file ~/.cargo/bin/tq` and `file /opt/pot/jails/agent-runtime/m/usr/local/bin/sh` (or similar pot binary) — both should report `ELF 64-bit LSB executable, x86-64, ... FreeBSD`.

2. **Static vs dynamic linkage.** Rust binaries are typically dynamically linked against `libc` and may need the same FreeBSD version inside the pot. Run `ldd ~/.cargo/bin/tq` to confirm; if it depends on libraries not present in the pot's slim filesystem, either:
   - Statically link tq for this purpose (`cargo install tq --features static` if supported), OR
   - Copy the dynamic libs alongside, OR
   - Install tq inside the pot via cargo (heavier).

3. **Version pinning.** Whatever tq syntax/flags mu-yzvi standardizes on should match the version installed in the pot. Document the version in the agent-runtime template's manifest.

## Acceptance

- `tq --help` runs successfully from inside a freshly-spawned worker pot.
- The TOML-extraction pattern from mu-yzvi works identically inside a pot worker.
- Documentation of the pot template's tooling manifest mentions tq with version.
- A new pot template snapshot baseline (replacing/superseding the current `working-2026-05-18-verified-clean` per memory `pot_orchestration_validated_2026_05_18`) reflects the addition.

## Coordination with mu-yzvi

mu-yzvi (host-side awk → tq) and this bead (pot template) are independent: mu-yzvi can land first (host has tq already); this bead can land any time before any pot-internal tooling depends on tq. No hard sequencing required, but ideally both land within the same template-refresh cycle so the toolchain is uniform across host + workers.

## References

- Sibling bead: mu-yzvi (awk → tq in agent-spawn)
- Template-update pattern: memory `agent_spawn_v1_overnight_2026_04_30` (id `fab12f5a`)
- Template snapshot baseline: memory `pot_orchestration_validated_2026_05_18`

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Update 2026-05-21 (operator follow-up): "though I don't know if [workers] actually need to run the agent program."

Reframe: this bead may be premature. What we know from memory `agent_spawn_v1_overnight_2026_04_30`:

- agent-spawn extracts API keys on the HOST via awk-on-toml (the mu-yzvi target).
- Keys are passed to the worker via `jexec env OPENROUTER_API_KEY=... ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=... pi --provider X --model Y -p PROMPT`.
- The worker's `pi` process gets keys via env. It does NOT need to read host TOML config.
- `agent` (the memory CLI) is similarly host-side; workers don't invoke it.

So the case for tq-in-pot rests on speculative future use: a worker-internal pi extension that wants TOML config, a future worker-side memory operation, etc. **Today, no concrete worker-side TOML parsing happens.**

## Revised scope

Replace the unconditional "install tq" plan with an audit:

1. **Audit** what worker pots actually read from disk at runtime. Targets:
   - `pi` binary's filesystem accesses (strace/ktrace a sample worker invocation)
   - Pi extensions present in the agent-runtime template (`/usr/local/etc/pi/extensions/` or wherever)
   - Any startup scripts in the pot's `/etc/rc.d` that touch TOML files
2. **Decide**: if the audit finds zero worker-side TOML parsing, close this bead as "not needed; revisit when a worker-side use case appears." If it finds any, then install tq as the original description outlines.

## Recommendation

Keep open as P3 audit task. The audit itself is small (~1 hour of ktrace + grep on the template filesystem). The "install tq" decision is contingent on the audit's findings.

Don't block mu-yzvi (host-side awk → tq) on this — that work stands alone regardless of the worker side.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Correction to the prior comment's audit item 1: `strace` is a Linux tool and doesn't exist on FreeBSD. The right tools for tracing pi's filesystem accesses inside a FreeBSD pot are `ktrace` (kernel-level trace; `kdump` to read the output) and `truss` (lightweight syscall tracer, similar in spirit to strace). `truss -f -e -o trace.out pi -p "hello"` from inside the worker pot would be the typical incantation. Removing the strace mention to avoid copy-paste confusion.

title: P1: doc status convention (ready/proposed/vision/archived) in AGENTS.md + headers
description: Define 4-value status convention in AGENTS.md; add a status header to each spec. Honest labels: ~26 specs marked PLANNED/PROPOSED should not read as shipped architecture.

title: mu-solo scrollback rendered a duplicated section mid-answer (repeated 'Where it's novel in combination...' block)
description: Operator capture, session c76f6949f56efbe6 (2026-06-03, the 'novel approach' answer): the scrollback shows a chunk of the answer twice — '...feels novel in the' cuts mid-sentence, then re-renders from 'a distinct engineering surface...' through the same '### Where it's novel in combination' section before continuing. Likely a streaming re-render/pager artifact in mu-solo (block pager landed in PR #160 same day). Repro material: the durable transcript in the event log will show whether the assistant text itself contains the duplication (provider-side) or it is render-only — check assistant_message_event content for the duplicated paragraph; if absent there, it is a viewport bug.
close_reason: Merged PR #170 — route_message at the reader thread: notifications async-only, responses sync-only, Eof/ReaderError fan out; exactly-once by construction; drain/buffer paths deleted

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Terrain-checked: the durable transcript contains the section ONCE (one assistant text, 5,613 chars, 1 occurrence of the duplicated heading). Confirmed render-only — mu-solo viewport/pager bug, not provider output.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Second occurrence, same session (the compaction-introspection answer, ~21:40): scrollback repeats a multi-paragraph region — cuts mid-sentence at '...kept or dropped as a unit' area, re-renders from 'turns was kept. The memory injection...' onward. Terrain-checked again: transcript has the text ONCE (4,095 chars, 1 occurrence of the repeated heading). Pattern across both occurrences: long (4-6K char) assistant texts, duplication starts mid-answer and replays a block from earlier in the same message — consistent with a streaming re-render/viewport-overlap bug in the block pager (PR #160), not provider output. Reliably reproducible with long answers, so a fix should be testable against this session's transcripts.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: ROOT CAUSE NARROWED + SEVERITY UP: not just a render artifact — mu-solo is processing some notifications TWICE. Smoking gun from the same session's /status vs durable log: log says 983,224 in / 29,560 out / 15 done events (inline stat line agrees exactly); /status said 1000k / 30k / 16 asks. The deltas match ask 15 (in=17,350, out=623) re-counted EXACTLY: 983,224+17,350=1,000,574→'1000k', 29,560+623=30,183→'30k', 15+1=16. So the client's session.done handler ran twice for the final ask — same class as the two double-vision renders (mid-message block replays). Look for: notification reader re-delivering buffered lines, reconnect/retry path re-emitting, or the app.rs handle_notification being invoked twice for one frame. Cost display is money-visible (operator reconciles against Anthropic billing), so this is P2 not P3.

title: GOAL 2026-05-31: close the discovery loop (mu-onq8 native FindTool + mu-k011 bootstrap)
description:
GOAL session 2026-05-31 (second of the night; first was mu-g71u baseline + justfile CI gate, COMPLETE via PR #140). Close the DISCOVERY LOOP so the in-loop mu agent can find capabilities on demand instead of relying on front-loaded recall — the fix for Friction B ("folkloric capabilities"), the single biggest named friction across recent sessions.

Worklist (sequential):
1. mu-onq8 — the MEANS leg. Native capability-discovery agent tool (find/recall by intent) in ToolRegistry, exposed to the IN-LOOP agent. Today capabilities/discover (mu-onq8 .6.4) is RPC/CLI-only, so the agent's only path to discovery is a bash shell-out that the strict allowlist blocks. Add a first-class in-loop tool. Verifiable: cargo test --workspace + a faux-provider smoke showing the agent invokes the native discover tool (not a blocked bash call).
2. mu-k011 — the INSTRUCTION leg. A default ~10-line discovery-bootstrap system-prompt fragment telling the in-loop agent to discover on demand: `agent memory search/recent --type user|feedback` for identity/decisions, and the native discover tool (mu-onq8) / `t4c find` for tools. Companion to MU_NO_RECALL (mu-54xj). blockedBy mu-onq8 (the bootstrap points at the tool onq8 adds). Design Q to resolve in-session: always-on vs only-when-recall-off, and how it composes with the existing system prompt. Refs: ~/tmp/mu-discovery-bootstrap.md, claude-basic CLAUDE.md, memory e575d64e (mu-discover-on-demand-teachable-2026-05-30).

Goal-complete condition: both sub-beads closed, cargo test --workspace + just ci green, one PR per bead (operator merges). SAFE scope only: pure edit + cargo/just, verifiable, NO worker/pot spawning (avoids the flaky mu-7yy7 / mu-xac orchestration path). Stop criteria: default seven (references/stop-criteria.md) + mu per-repo (specs/process/goal-protocol.md) — note mu-onq8 touches the agent loop + ToolRegistry + system prompt; if a change would alter the Provider trait / a wire/EventPayload variant / a capability invariant, STOP per criteria 8-9. On stop: MORNING_2026-05-31-discovery.md briefing (suffixed to avoid clobbering the first goal's MORNING_2026-05-31.md).
close_reason: Discovery loop closed: PR #141 (mu-onq8 acceptance smoke) and PR #142 (mu-k011 bootstrap) both merged to main 2026-05-31. Sub-beads mu-onq8/mu-k011 closed (mu-557i deduped). All CI green (strict, re-run after rebasing onto main #139).

title: Future: readonly / inspection mode for mutating tools
description:
Add a readonly/inspection run mode. Agents may perform read-only work autonomously, but write/edit/bash/network/delegate/mutating actions pause at the exact proposed tool call for human inspection.

Approval UI should show session/task ownership, proposed args, paths/commands, diff/impact preview where available, and choices: allow once, allow scope/pattern for this session, deny call, cancel tool, cancel session/delegate, or quiesce all.

Composes with session.input_required, tool policy, and emergency cancel-only mode.

Related memory: 73505005.

title: agentic-bench: re-run with mu ask --bare — does removing the kernel change the numbers?
description: All agentic-bench numbers to date (qwen3.6 q8_0/27b 0.933, qwen3-coder 0.833, gpt-oss:20b 0.667) were measured WITH the ~28KB memory kernel injected into every session. PR #187's --bare makes the kernel-free measurement a one-flag change: add --bare to run_case() in ~/src/public_github/agentic-bench/run_benchmark.py (its own repo, not mu) and re-run all 4 models x 10 cases x 3 reps. Hypotheses to test: gpt-oss:20b's lost-question greetings ('Hey C-137') and empty-output rate should drop (persona was the prime suspect); everyone's prefill gets ~7k tokens cheaper; scores may shift enough to reorder the bottom of the table. Compare against results-2026-06-05-040000-combined.jsonl; if deltas are material, the dashboard benchmarks page should show both conditions. DEFERRED past work hours 2026-06-05 — the box is serving live review/agentic traffic during the day (which is the point).

title: ai-review.sh: E2BIG on large diffs — prompt passed as argv to mu ask
description: scripts/ai-review.sh line ~95 passes the review PROMPT (which embeds the full diff) as a single command-line argument: timeout 300 $MU ask ... "$PROMPT". On a ~1000-line stack diff this exceeds the kernel argv limit: '/bin/timeout: Argument list too long' (observed 2026-06-05, mu-8bkf 5-commit stack; stderr /tmp/ai-review-stderr.76080). mu ask exec fails -> no VERDICT line -> gate UNCLEAR/BLOCKED with a misleading 'reviewer may have erred' hint. Fix: deliver the prompt via stdin or a temp file (mu ask --prompt-file if it exists, else stdin). Also consider: the earlier same-day UNCLEAR where the reviewer emitted its idle greeting instead of a review may be the truncation/empty-prompt cousin of this — worth checking how mu ask handles empty/oversized argv. Retry-on-greeting is a cheap additional guard.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Third gate-reliability incident same day (2026-06-05 ~16:06Z, mu-solo-scrollback-dup-recommit-8hva workspace, files_changed=13): reviewer session started normally (MCP imports logged), ran ~45s, then 'task cancelled' in stderr — no VERDICT line, gate UNCLEAR/BLOCKED. Distinct from E2BIG (prompt argv) and from the idle-greeting failure. Three flavors in one day argues for hardening ai-review.sh beyond the argv fix: (a) auto-retry once on UNCLEAR before blocking (UNCLEAR is empirically infra-noise more often than reviewer confusion), (b) classify the failure flavor in the log (argv/cancel/no-verdict — stderr capture exists), (c) prompt via stdin/file per the original bead. Cost today: every UNCLEAR costs an orchestrator adjudication round.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: CORRECTION to the 'third incident' comment: the 2026-06-05 ~16:06Z cancellations were NOT gate infra. Root cause: PR #183 merged + jj git fetch moved main; the 8hva workspace stack was based on pre-#183 main, so ai-review's base=main diff included the whole console as DELETIONS (~1MB with beads-jsonl churn) around a 20KB fix — reviewer choked on noise. Second 'failure' compounded by orchestrator cwd error (review launched from backing repo). LESSONS for the script all the same: (a) the script could detect base-behind-workspace (merge-base != main) and diff against the actual fork point instead — that's the structural fix; (b) a diff-size sanity print (bytes, files) before invoking the reviewer would have made this a 10-second diagnosis. The argv-E2BIG and idle-greeting incidents stand as real infra issues.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Spline-side differential benchmark (operator, 2026-06-05, preserved in their review-events.jsonl): gpt-oss:20b measured FP rate on real PRs = 2 of 3 diffs falsely blocked, ALL false blocks in the multi-file/build-system category (contradictory Cargo claims, XML attr hallucination); recall stays 1.0. qwen3.6:27b adjudicated in one shot and is a credible skeptic (its APPROVE carried 3 real findings). STANDING PATTERN on spline now: warm-20b primary -> its REJECTs escalate to warm-27b -> unanimous rejects block. mu's ai-review.sh ALREADY implements this as opt-in (MU_REVIEW_ESCALATE_PROVIDER/_MODEL, differential-diagnosis path); both models confirmed warm-coexistent on the box. RECOMMENDATION for this bead: make the ladder the default in just ci-aipr (export the two vars in the recipe) — today's mu-side data agrees with the spline measurement (3 REJECT adjudications, all 3 vacated as 20b FPs, every one a multi-file diff; each cost an orchestrator adjudication round the 27b rung would have absorbed). Also note (operator operational finding): the gate's behavior is versioned with the tree you stand on — jj new <old-commit> runs the OLD script.

title: ai-review.sh: full-file context overflows local reviewer windows — panel degenerates to double-UNCLEAR
description:
Observed 2026-06-06 ~09:15-09:30Z, three independent runs: (a) mu-28u 9-file diff — both reviewers UNCLEAR twice (panel ESCALATE, events at 09:16:56Z/09:22:04Z); (b) mu-sqn manifest diff including Cargo.lock (114KB) — both UNCLEAR, qwen3-coder's ENTIRE output was the single token 'Based' (context truncation garbage); (c) same sqn diff with MU_REVIEW_FULL_FILES=0 — real verdicts immediately.

ROOT CAUSE: PR #204's full-file review context appends every changed file up to MU_REVIEW_CONTEXT_MAX_BYTES (200KB ≈ 50k tokens) but ollama's default num_ctx is far smaller — the prompt (instructions at the TOP) gets truncated away and the models never see the VERDICT contract.

FIX CANDIDATES (any/all): exclude lockfiles + generated files from context (Cargo.lock, *.lock, *.svg, fixtures); per-file byte budget instead of one global cap; set explicit num_ctx via mu ask / ollama options and size the cap to it; detect degenerate reviewer output (<N chars) and retry diff-only automatically (self-healing beats UNCLEAR).

WORKAROUND: MU_REVIEW_FULL_FILES=0 (used for PR #208 adjudication and mu-28u salvage).

Values are operator-controlled env today, so no live risk — but a model name containing a quote would corrupt review-events.jsonl. Harden with a tiny json-escape helper or jq -n. Non-regression follow-up; calibration tooling reads this log (mu-mucm dashboards).

title: ai-review: replace 28KB memory-kernel system prompt with a minimal reviewer prompt
description: Wire capture 2026-06-04 showed mu ask sessions carry the ~28KB daemon-default system prompt (operator identity kernel + memory context) — including ai-review.sh gate calls, where it is a pure distractor: ~7k tokens of persona prepended to 'review this diff'. Suspected contributor to persona-bleed failures (model greeting 'Hey C-137' instead of reviewing) and attention dilution on multi-file diffs (measured 20b FP fingerprint). Server-side truncation was ruled out empirically (sentinel probes: both reviewers evaluate >=23k tokens intact). Fix: ai-review.sh passes --append-system-prompt (mu-x83o; overrides daemon default) pointing at a minimal reviewer system prompt file; MU_REVIEW_SYSTEM_PROMPT env override. Saves ~12s prefill per review and removes the distractor.

title: ai-review.sh: two-reviewer panel gate (qwen3-coder:30b + gpt-oss:20b)
description:
Operator-ratified panel design (memory d88e133e, 2026-06-06): keep BOTH local reviewers on the ci-aipr gate — complementary weaknesses ('like a team'); never frame as picking a champion. code-review-bench + agentic-bench show inverted strengths (gpt-oss:20b first in single-shot review / last agentically; qwen3-coder:30b the reverse).

SEMANTICS: run both reviewers on the same diff. both-APPROVE = pass (exit 0). both-REJECT = block (exit nonzero). split = ESCALATE to operator (exit nonzero, verdict line clearly distinguishable from block; do NOT self-override). MU_REVIEW_OVERRIDE=1 keeps working as the operator's override on block/escalate.

BUILD ON EXISTING TERRAIN: scripts/ai-review.sh already reads the verdict from reviewer STDOUT (not exit code — mu-qc08 shutdown wart), has MU_REVIEW_ESCALATE_PROVIDER/_MODEL re-review machinery, and logs to MU_REVIEW_LOG (~/.local/share/mu/review-events.jsonl). Panel = generalize to two first-class reviewers; log BOTH verdicts per run (one event per reviewer or a panel event with both). 24h ollama keep-alive (memory 3d973420) makes the second invocation cheap when warm; both models fit 48GB.

MOTIVATING INCIDENT: 2026-06-06 — gpt-oss:20b APPROVEd correct PR #200 and REJECTed correct PR #201 on a false premise (hypothetical non-Copy compile error on the line that fixes it), contradicted by green just-ci in the same recipe run; escalated to operator, merged with bot approval. Panel absorbs single-reviewer blind spots.

ACCEPTANCE: just ci-aipr runs both reviewers and applies panel semantics; split produces an unambiguous ESCALATE message naming each reviewer's verdict; review-events.jsonl carries both verdicts; script header docs updated (env vars, panel semantics); shellcheck-clean if repo convention applies.
close_reason: PR #204 merged 2026-06-06: two-reviewer panel gate (PASS/BLOCK/ESCALATE exit 0/1/3, full-file review context, both-verdict logging). Dogfooded: panel PASSed its own diff. Director-reviewed, bot-approved.

title: anomaly-ranked marking worklist: IsolationForest over per-session telemetry to aim scarce operator marks at statistically weird sessions
description:
Operator-approved 2026-06-06 (step 1 of the ML-over-telemetry discussion). Build scripts/anomaly_worklist.py in ~/src/claude-personal/scripts: (1) DuckDB per-session feature table from the existing mu_stats views (ask_telemetry/harness_costs/session_marks): per-session aggregates — asks, tokens in/out, cache read/write + tiers, wall p50/p95, gap counts, tool calls, hour-of-day/day-of-week of start, model/provider one-hot. BOTH fleets (harness_costs covers mu + cc). (2) IsolationForest (sklearn; uv add scikit-learn to the scripts env is allowed per repo convention — terrain-check the import first; xgboost NOT needed for this). (3) Output: markdown worklist ranked by anomaly score, marked-status column, fleet-prefixed session_refs, top-N weirdest unmarked sessions with their most-deviant features named (per-feature z-scores for inspectability) — operator marks from the top. Write ~/mu-stats/anomaly-worklist.md; print top 10 to stdout.

PURPOSE: labeling accelerator — attack the 2/32 marked bottleneck; active-learning loop toward step-3 supervised viability (~30-50 labels).

IN-CODE CAUTIONS: exploratory not inferential — anomaly = statistically unusual, NOT bad; confound note (cost~length~difficulty~time-of-day); findings graduate to pre-registered tests or stay curiosities. Deterministic (fixed random_state).
close_reason: Commit 067e1691: IsolationForest worklist over both fleets, deterministic, z-score explanations, three-tier marks lookup, fleet-prefixed refs, exploratory cautions in code+artifact. Validated: known $48 oddball surfaces #3; 3dd40c3c ranks #2 on genuine merits (cw-1h z=+17.2). Interpreter terrain (pkg not uv on FreeBSD) banked as memory. Director re-ran: deterministic, artifact regenerates.

title: dual-mode queue substrate: defensive mail + load-aware orchestration routing
description:
Design convergence from Opus mu-solo session (2026-05-27). Mail queue and orchestration queue share one in-process bounded-queue-with-stats data structure, differing only in who reads the stats.

Mail case (push notifications to agent):
- Defensive: queue defends recipient from being overwhelmed
- Stats exposed to agent reading (depth, priority distribution)
- Subject+importance only in queue (body in mailbox, fetched on demand)
- Approved-sender gating (push is privileged; mailbox is unprivileged)
- Eviction: duplicates → oldest-low → oldest. Never silently drop above threshold.

Orchestration case (task dispatch to workers):
- Routing: queue gives honest signal so sender chooses well
- Stats exposed to senders (depth, p95 latency, drain ETA, failure rate, capabilities)
- Power-of-two-choices: sample 2 valid backends, pick shorter queue. Almost as good as globally optimal, much simpler.
- Stats returned as side-channel on accept() — senders learn passively, no polling.

Shared design:
- In-process, bounded depth
- Each entry: (sender_id, topic, priority, timestamp, ref_to_full_message)
- Backpressure signal on accept (queue depth bucket)
- Per-(sender, priority-band) quota
- Coalescing window for high-frequency sources
- Audit log from day one

Bell Labs / Mitzenmacher insight: gap between no-information and any-information routing is enormous. agent-router's static policy (memory 2da785e5) becomes the default; load-awareness overrides when stats available.

Related: mu-037 (leases), 189d8fc1 (orchestration supervisor), mu-ppyi (schemaless metrics).

title: t4c bugs from cold dogfood: run suggested-call hardcodes subcommand; agent.memory node too coarse; folkloric get
description: Surfaced by the 2026-05-29 discriminating cold-dogfood (see memory t4c-discriminating-dogfood-phase3-justified). (1) t4c run suggested-call for bash.agent.memory hardcodes 'search' -> 'agent memory search get X' invalid; agent.memory node too coarse (split into .recall/.search/... or invoke 'agent memory' + subcommand-as-arg). (2) folkloric 'agent memory get' reproduced (CLAUDE.md advertises get; CLI has none) -> fix doc + t4c help should list real subcommands. (3) agent-memory FTS5 hyphen bug: search 'codex-oauth' -> 'no such column: oauth'.

title: Future: emergency stop / cancel-only control plane
description:
Design and implement explicit operator controls for runaway/unsafe agent work.

Modes to support eventually:
- normal
- readonly / inspection
- cancel-only / quiesce: no new model calls, tool calls, or delegates; only reduce exposure / summarize / stop
- abort / evict all: cancel active tool calls, stop delegates, terminate subprocess groups/jails
- hard stop / recovery: immediate kill with explicit reconciliation on restart

Dangerous broad actions should use guarded-trigger UX: show affected sessions/tools/delegates, require typed nonce confirmation, optionally reason/operator, and emit durable audit events. Inspired by industrial emergency-stop controls and matching-engine cancel-only / order-eviction controls.

Related memories: 1440515e, 516fedc7, 0d326bed, c8473c42.

title: worker verification gate: git diff-tree --name-status must match claimed file list before declaring 'done'
description:
## Problem

PR #52 (mu-nk3) closure (2026-05-16) surfaced a distinct failure pattern: **hallucinated completion at the commit-message level**. A worker (claude haiku 4.5 co-author) wrote a multi-page commit description claiming to split crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs into 6 module files (mod.rs, invoke.rs, execute_tools.rs, autonomy.rs, compaction_integration.rs, tests.rs) with specific LOC counts. Actual diff: zero new files in crates/mu-core/src/agent/, only bleed-through from sibling workers' MORNING_*.md briefings + 8 random lines in mu-tui/main.rs.

This is a different failure than 'hallucinated state.json' (claimed completion in a sidecar) — here, the commit message ITSELF is fictional. Pre-merge CI caught it (fmt/clippy/test all FAILED), but only because the broken bleed-through tripped them. A more careful hallucination would have produced a syntactically-valid empty-or-trivial change with a fictional description that passes CI.

## Proposed gate

Add a verification step in the worker harness (or pre-PR hook) that:

1. Parses the worker's commit message for any claimed file list (e.g., 'creates X.rs', 'modifies Y.rs', LOC counts).
2. Runs git diff-tree --name-status -r <commit> to get the actual file list.
3. If claimed files are not present in actual, fail with diagnostic. If claimed LOC counts differ by >20% from actual, warn.
4. Optional: structured commit-message section ('## Files', auto-parseable) so the gate isn't NLP-heuristic.

## Provenance

- Failure: jj kolvssqm 1718272a / PR #52
- Operator closure comment: https://github.com/sahuagin/mu/pull/52#issuecomment-4467658904
- Reviewer prescribed gate verbatim: 'needs a new verification gate that checks git diff-tree --name-status matches the worker's claimed file list before declaring done'

## Acceptance

- Gate script lives in scripts/ or pre-pr-hook
- Runs as part of worker exit, AND as part of pre-PR promotion via gh wrapper (mu-7s3x family)
- Test case: synthetic commit with description claiming file X added, actually adding file Y — gate fails with clear diagnostic
- Documented in the worker / goal-protocol skill

## Cross-references

- Related to mu-nk3 (still open — actual refactor needs to be done by a worker the gate trusts)
- Related to the worker-verification axis broadly (state.json hallucination, etc.)
close_reason: Merged in PR #64 — verify-claims gate live, dogfooded on its own commit, 8 synthetic tests passing, goal-protocol skill rule 11 documents the format

title: agent runtime: background goal runner with completion notification
description:
Build a first-class way for mu/agents to spin off a background goal, let it iterate until success/blocked/budget, and notify the parent/operator on completion. Motivation: overnight iteration without tying up the foreground session; equivalent to a /goal protocol with durable state.

Protocol sketch:
- goal.create {repo/bead/cwd, prompt, success_criteria, budget, notify_to/session_ref}
- goal.status/list/show/cancel
- runner loop: claim bead if supplied, create isolated workspace, execute steps, run tests/ci-aipr as requested, push PR or leave handoff, emit Done/Blocked/NeedsHuman
- durable event log per goal under ~/.local/share/mu/goals or mu events
- notification path: parent session mailbox if alive, terminal/desktop notification, task_log row, optional agent-mail/MCP message

Acceptance:
- foreground session can submit a goal and continue; completed background goal posts a concise result with links/log paths/PR/bead status.
- goal survives parent terminal disconnect; status is queryable.
- safety: destructive operations still require explicit human confirmation or stop as NeedsHuman.
- implementation can initially wrap existing sprint-start/sprint-end + agent-router/claude-code worker rather than inventing a full agent VM.

title: watch tool: tier-1 output filtering for wakeup summaries (mu-2e0h)
description: mu-watch-tool-wakeup-o03p (PR #211) ships the watch tool with a simple byte-tail (OUTPUT_TAIL_BYTES=4000, last-bytes on char boundary) for the exit-status+output summary injected on wakeup. The bead's original design called for tier-1 filtering per mu-2e0h (drop noise, keep signal). Wire the watch summary through the tier-1 filter so chatty commands (e.g. verbose CI logs) inject signal, not 4KB of raw scrollback. Related: mu-watch-tool-wakeup-o03p, mu-2e0h.

title: Tool trait: add validate(args) pre-hook called before approval gate
description:
## Problem

Tool policy is **tool-scoped, not argument-scoped**. `PermissionLevel::Ask` on a tool's `ToolSpec` causes the agent loop to dispatch a `session.input_required` approval prompt before *every* invocation, regardless of arguments. Tools that reject specific arguments (bash's allowlist, aws_recon's unsupported_capability) only learn about the rejection inside `execute()`, which runs *after* the approval round-trip.

User-visible result (mu-20l): on `--bash-prompt`, mu asks the user to approve `curl -I example.com`, the user approves, mu then rejects it on the allowlist anyway. Wasted approval click on a doomed call.

Original mu-030 commit message claimed "allowlist still gates first; on allowlist match, the agent loop emits session.input_required" — that was the author's intent. The mu-029 architecture (policy check before dispatch) silently undermined it; mu-030 inherited the broken order without noticing.

## Proposal

Extend the `Tool` trait with an argument-aware pre-flight check:

```rust
pub trait Tool: Send + Sync {
    fn spec(&self) -> ToolSpec;

    /// Argument-aware pre-flight check. Tools that reject specific
    /// arguments implement this. Dispatchers SHOULD call it before
    /// any approval round-trip; rejecting here avoids asking the
    /// user to approve a call that will fail.
    /// Default: Ok (no pre-checks).
    fn validate(&self, _arguments: &Value) -> Result<(), String> { Ok(()) }

    async fn execute(&self, arguments: Value, cancel_rx: oneshot::Receiver<()>) -> ToolResult;
}
```

Dispatcher (`execute_tools.rs`) calls `tool.validate(&args)` *before* the `PermissionLevel::Ask` gate. On `Err`, short-circuit with a `ToolResult { content: reason, is_error: true }` — no approval round-trip dispatched.

## Implementation

1. **`crates/mu-core/src/agent/tool.rs`**: add `validate` to the trait with default `Ok(())`.
2. **`crates/mu-coding/src/tools/bash.rs`**: implement `BashTool::validate` lifting the metachar + tokenize + allowlist checks from inside `execute()`. `execute()` keeps a defense-in-depth `validate(&args)?` call at the top so direct-call unit tests still work.
3. **`crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/execute_tools.rs`**: add the pre-flight call before the `PermissionLevel::Ask` branch (~line 171). Short-circuit with an `is_error` ToolResult on `Err`.
4. **Tests**:
   - `BashTool::validate` unit tests (metachar reject, non-allowlisted reject, allowed accept).
   - Dispatcher test: validate-failed call doesn't fire `InputRequired`.
   - Existing bash tests pass unchanged.
5. **Doc**: rewrite the `BashMode::Strict` prompt comment to describe the now-honored intent. Drop the design-boundary note from the abandoned doc-only fix (PR #82).

## Backward compatibility

- Trait gains a method with default impl → existing `Tool` impls (read, write, edit, grep, glob, ls, aws_recon) compile unchanged. They get the no-op default.
- aws_recon could later implement `validate` for its `unsupported_capability` rejection — out of scope for this bead but a natural follow-up.
- Wire protocol unchanged. No `EventPayload` changes.

## Acceptance

- `cargo nextest run --workspace` green
- `--bash-prompt` + non-allowlisted command (e.g. `curl`): user sees the rejection immediately, no approval prompt dispatched
- `--bash-prompt` + allowlisted command: approval prompt dispatched as today
- Source intent honored: mu-030 commit message's "allowlist still gates first" is now true by construction

## Supersedes / closes

- **mu-20l** — the symptom. Triage decision was option (a) doc fix; this bead implements option (b) architectural fix instead. Will close mu-20l along with this bead in the same PR.

## Source

Architectural triage during mu-20l work, 2026-05-18. User feedback after I proposed doc-only fix (PR #82, since closed): prefer to honor original intent.
close_reason: Implemented via this branch. Tool::validate(args) added with default Ok; BashTool implements it lifting metachar+allowlist checks; execute_tools.rs calls validate before the PermissionLevel::Ask gate; 5 unit tests + 1 dispatcher integration test added; full workspace gate green (687 tests). Honors the original mu-030 intent.

title: AnthropicProviderRenderer + AnthropicCacheStrategy (mu-ktq follow-on)
description:
Follow-on to mu-ktq (foundational traits, closed). Provide the Anthropic-specific implementations of the new traits introduced in crates/mu-core/src/context/.

## Scope

In:
- `AnthropicProviderRenderer` impl of `ProviderRenderer` — render a RetainedRope into Anthropic-shaped Messages API request payload (system prompt + messages array, content blocks). Honor `ProjectionTarget::AgentView` (full fidelity for the model); operator-view rendering can be added later.
- `AnthropicCacheStrategy` impl of `CacheStrategy` — place a single cache boundary at the last stable+cacheable span position (per spec lines 567-578); annotate that message with `CacheMarker::Ephemeral`; the Anthropic adapter translates that into `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" }` at the boundary message.
- Tests: at least one rope shape with a clear stable/volatile split; verify boundary placement; verify annotation lands on the right message.

Out:
- Adoption in the live agent loop (separate bead — see "live-loop adoption").
- OpenAI-specific rendering (separate bead).
- Full RetainedRope (= mu-nat).

## Design questions to resolve before implementing

1. Coexistence with existing mu-i6j cache_control work — where does the current `cache_control` annotation get applied today? Does the new strategy replace that code path, or does it sit alongside until the adoption bead lands?
2. The current Anthropic adapter takes `&[AgentMessage]` (mu-core/src/agent/provider.rs:62). Does the new renderer return ProviderMessages and a separate adapter step converts to the Anthropic wire JSON, or should AnthropicProviderRenderer go all the way to the wire shape?
3. System-prompt placement: Anthropic has a top-level `system` field (mu-n48), distinct from messages. Where does the system-role span fall in the rendered output?

## Acceptance

Trait impls compile and link against existing mu-ai Anthropic code paths; tests verify boundary placement; cargo test --workspace green; bead-close commit with file:line citations.

## References

- mu-ktq (closed; foundational traits)
- specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md:564-612
- mu-i6j (existing cache_control work)
- mu-n48 (system prompt support)
close_reason: Landed in PR #4, commit 98e174f (squash-merged from agent/bn4-anthropic-2026-05-14). AnthropicProviderRenderer + AnthropicCacheStrategy in crates/mu-ai/src/context/anthropic.rs. Design questions resolved in commit body + module docs: sit alongside mu-i6j; ProviderMessages return type; System-kind → System role (future adapter hoists to top-level system field). 13 new tests + 156+ workspace green. Unblocks mu-fb0 (live-loop adoption).
