title: F3 transcript view: cannot scroll back through long responses
description:
## Symptom

When F3 (session detail / transcript view) shows a long AI response (e.g. >1 viewport), there's no way to scroll back to see the start. PageUp/PgDn don't appear bound; j/k may move selection but not scroll the transcript pane.

Discovered 2026-05-19 during iter-10 UX verification of PR #108. Operator ran F3 → i "Can you read the mu readme to me please" and got a multi-screen response with no way to scroll up.

## Out of scope / not the cause

This is pre-existing, NOT introduced by iter-9. iter-9's changes were strictly inside `render_events_explorer` (F8) and the firehose-drain call site. F3 lives in different render functions.

## Possible fixes (refine before starting)

- F3 likely doesn't bind PageUp/PgDn for transcript scrolling at all; check the keymap in App's event handler for `(KeyCode::PageUp, _) if matches!(self.mode, ViewMode::SessionDetail)` and similar
- j/k in F3 may have a "scroll by 2 lines" behavior similar to pre-iter-7 F8; could be lifted/extended
- F3 may need its own viewport-following cursor analogous to F8's iter-9 work, though the use case differs (F3 is a transcript, not a list)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] PageUp/PgDn scroll the F3 transcript pane up/down by ~viewport-height
- [ ] Home jumps to start of transcript; End jumps to end
- [ ] Existing F3 keybindings (n, i, /, etc.) still work
- [ ] Status-line key-hint reflects the new bindings
close_reason: not-a-bug: F3 scrolls via mux scrollback by design (tmux Ctrl-b [ / zellij Mod-s); confirmed working. See bead comments for full retrospective.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Closed 2026-05-19 as not-a-bug after testing revealed mu-i3ew was filed against a false premise.

## Original symptom

During iter-10 manual UX verification of PR #108, operator reported 'cannot scroll in F3' after a long AI response. I filed mu-i3ew assuming F3 lacked scroll capability.

## Actual design

F3 (transcript / session-detail view) runs in Inline mode, which renders the transcript inline (one-line-per-push) into the terminal's stdout. The terminal multiplexer captures these in its scrollback buffer. Scrolling F3 is done via the **mux's scrollback** — `tmux Ctrl-b [` or `zellij Mod-s` — NOT via keys inside mu's TUI.

This is explicitly documented in HELP_LINES (`?` overlay in mu-tui):

```
" (In Inline F3, transcript scrolls into mux scrollback —"
"  zellij mod-s, tmux Ctrl-b [ to navigate; older history below.)"
```

Operator verification post-close: zellij scrollback (Mod-s) works correctly for F3. The 'cannot scroll' symptom was 'didn't try the right keys,' not a missing capability.

## What the iter-i3ew worker did (discarded)

The worker hit the $2 budget cap before commit, but DID produce:
- Switched F3 from Inline to Fullscreen mode (= internal scroll instead of mux scrollback)
- Added `f3_scroll_y(total_lines, inner_height, offset) -> (u16, u16)` pure helper
- Added 4 unit tests for `f3_scroll_y`
- Updated HELP_LINES to advertise PageUp/PgDn/Home/End (REMOVING the mux-scrollback hint)

All discarded — the worker fixed a non-existent bug. The HELP_LINES change in particular would have made the actual working scroll method (mux scrollback) less discoverable.

## What to actually do (if anything)

mu-i3ew was a false alarm. No code change needed. If discoverability of mux scrollback comes up again, file a NEW bead about improving the status-line hint or HELP_LINES emphasis. Don't reopen this one — it has a wrong-premise diagnosis history that would confuse future searches.

## Cost ledger

- Worker dispatch: $2.00 (hit budget cap)
- Orchestrator review + cargo-config-conflict diagnosis: time-only, no dispatch
- Net: $2.00 spent confirming non-bug. Cheaper than letting a bad fix ship — the worker's switch to Fullscreen would have removed the working mux-scrollback design.

## Cross-refs

- agent memory `iter_7_to_10_cycle_retrospective_2026_05_19` (28288ee1) — the prior cycle that surfaced this false-alarm bead
- PR #108 (merged): iter-7→10 cycle. Manual UX verification surfaced 'cannot scroll' which led to mu-i3ew being filed.

## Retrospective: why the false-alarm bead happened

When the operator reported "cannot scroll in F3," I (orchestrator) accepted the symptom at face value and filed mu-i3ew with possible-fix language like "F3 may not bind PageUp/PgDn." I should have asked first: "did you try mux scrollback (your zellij/tmux's own scroll keys)?" — that one question would have closed the bead immediately as not-a-bug. Lesson: when a bug report sounds like "feature X doesn't work in mu," ask whether the workflow assumes a layered tool (mux, shell, etc.) before assuming mu is responsible.

title: Wire cache_control markers on system prompt + tool definitions in mu-ai anthropic provider
description:
## Why

mu-pex F5 dashboard hand-test (2026-05-11) confirmed cache% = 0% on a real anthropic-api+haiku run:

- 51,388 input tokens accumulated across 14 tool calls + 1 final response
- 0 cache_read_input_tokens
- 0 cache_creation_input_tokens

The Usage struct (mu-core/src/agent/types.rs) and the dashboard already *report* cache metrics — but mu isn't *marking* prompt content for caching, so the API never caches.

At haiku pricing ($0.80/M input, $0.08/M cached-read), that ask cost ~$0.04 instead of the ~$0.005 it would have cost if the system prompt + tool defs were cached. ~85% savings on a representative mu workload, plus a 30-50% latency reduction on the prompt-processing portion of TTFT.

## What

Add Anthropic-style `cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"}` markers on the long-lived parts of the request body:

1. System prompt (one marker at the end of the system text block)
2. Tool definitions (one marker on the last tool spec, which Anthropic interprets as 'cache everything up to and including this')

These don't need per-call configurability — they're the parts of the request that are stable across asks within a session. The variable parts (per-turn user messages, tool_results) stay uncached.

Anthropic supports up to 4 cache_control breakpoints per request. We use 2; that leaves headroom for future cacheable segments (e.g., a chunked conversation prefix once context windows grow).

## Where

`crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs` (and possibly `anthropic_tests.rs`).

## How to A/B test

This bead is uniquely testable against the work we just landed: run F5 dashboard against this branch and against main, compare the `cache%` column after a session with multiple asks against the same prompt. Expected: 0% on main, 30-90% on this branch depending on how much of the request is the stable prefix vs. per-turn content.

## Acceptance

- Workspace tests still pass.
- Anthropic provider unit tests assert cache_control markers are present on system prompt + tool defs in the request body.
- F5 dashboard cache% column shows nonzero on a multi-ask session against anthropic_api.

## References

- mu-pex F5 dashboard: commit yqoktozo 0d3a3f23
- Anthropic prompt caching docs (external): https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-caching
- Discovered during 2026-05-11 hand-test (51k input tokens, 0% cache hit)
close_reason: done

title: mu-clip-coding-mechanical: 6 mechanical clippy lints in mu-coding (sort_by_key x2, clamp_like_pattern, wildcard_in_or_patterns x2, obfuscated_if_else)
description:
Six clippy lints in mu-coding, all with clear mechanical fixes that clippy itself suggests (`try this:` blocks). None involve design decisions; this is straight refactoring to the suggested forms.

## Items

1. `sort_by_key` (×2 locations) — replace `sort_by(|a, b| a.field.cmp(&b.field))` with `sort_by_key(|x| x.field)`.
2. `clamp_like_pattern` (×1) — replace `x.max(lo).min(hi)` with `x.clamp(lo, hi)`.
3. `wildcard_in_or_patterns` (×2) — replace `Foo | _` with just `_` (wildcard already covers the named variant).
4. `obfuscated_if_else` (×1) — `crates/mu-coding/src/tools/grep.rs:293` — replace `x.then_some("a").unwrap_or("b")` chain with `if x { "a" } else { "b" }`.

Exact locations are emitted by `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features`; suggestion blocks in each error message are drop-in.

## Acceptance

- All 6 mechanical lints resolved per clippy's suggested forms
- cargo clippy passes (for these specific lints) — overall clippy still fails on the other cleanup beads' items
- cargo test --workspace green
- One commit closes this bead
close_reason: Landed in jj revision qppzqwyp. Six mechanical lints applied via clippy's suggested forms (sort_by_key x2, clamp, wildcard x2, if-else). 361 tests green; remaining warnings 20 → 10 (mu-lm8 + mu-j97).

title: mu admin import-claude-history — convert claude-code JSONL transcripts into mu event log + provider-agnostic event schema extension
description:
## Two-part scope

This is one bead because the parts are tightly coupled. You can't faithfully import claude-code's transcripts without somewhere to put the richer event data; the schema extension IS the prerequisite for honest import.

### Part 1: importer

`mu admin import-claude-history <path>` subcommand (or standalone script if a subcommand surface change is too invasive for v1). For each claude-code JSONL transcript file under the input path, emit a mu-format JSONL at `~/.local/share/mu/events/<imported_daemon_id>/<session_id>.jsonl`.

Mapping (verified by reading actual claude-code JSONL on 2026-05-19):

| claude-code JSONL | mu EventPayload | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `type=user` | `user_message` | direct 1:1 |
| `type=assistant` text/thinking content | `assistant_message_event` + `assistant_thinking` (new — see Part 2) | split content array |
| Assistant `content[N].type=tool_use` | `tool_call` | extracted out of the assistant message; assistant remains for the text portions |
| `type=tool_result` | `tool_result` | direct 1:1 |
| `stop_reason` on terminal assistant | `done` (synthetic) | computed at turn boundary |
| `usage` on assistant | partial `task_telemetry` | token + cache counts map directly |
| `permission-mode`, `bridge-session`, `system`, `task_reminder`, `hook_*`, etc. | drop or emit as `audit_event` variant (Part 2) | UI chrome; not session-data |
| `edited_text_file` | enrich `tool_result` for Edit calls, OR emit as `file_edit_record` (Part 2) | claude-code records per-file edit metadata; mu currently doesn't |

Session-id mapping: claude-code UUID → mu display name `claude-<uuid-prefix-8>` so they're distinguishable from native mu sessions in F2/F3 listings.

Model attribution: claude-code records the model name (e.g., `claude-sonnet-4-6`); imported sessions get `provider=anthropic_api, model=<recorded>`.

Timestamp: ISO → `timestamp_unix_ms u64` conversion.

### Part 2: event-schema extension (provider-agnostic)

The import surfaces what mu's EventPayload variants are missing today. Adding these variants makes mu's event log a SUPERSET that can faithfully represent any provider's session — including future codex/gpt JSONL imports.

New variants (proposed):

- **`assistant_thinking`** — extended-thinking blocks (claude-code's `content[i].type=thinking`). Includes the thinking text + signature. Mu's loop today drops thinking before emit; this variant preserves the data when it's there.
- **`audit_event`** — generic for chrome events (permission mode changes, bridge-session linkage, queue operations, etc.). Tagged with `subtype` for filtering. Lets imports preserve everything without inventing one variant per claude-code event type.
- **`file_edit_record`** — per-file edit metadata (path, before/after hash, line count delta). claude-code already emits this; mu could too on its Edit tool.
- **`parent_event_id`** field on existing variants (optional) — preserve claude-code's parentUuid DAG structure for forensic walk-back. mu's events are linear today; this is metadata only, doesn't change replay logic.

Once these exist, the import becomes lossless: every claude-code event has a target.

## Why now-ish (P3, not P2)

- Operator value is large: every past claude-code session becomes a queryable historical mu session. Existing operator has ~6+ months of conversations.
- Importing claude-code first informs the codex / gpt importer second — same shape, different source JSONL.
- The schema extension makes future providers (Gemini, etc.) plug in more naturally — mu becomes the convergence-format for agentic session data.

## Dependencies

- **mu-u1ld phase A** (shipped — PR #90): the read-only rehydration pickup that makes imported sessions appear in `session.list` automatically. Imports place files in `~/.local/share/mu/events/<daemon_id>/` and next `mu serve` start picks them up.
- **mu-1flg** (open — phase C writable rehydration): the natural upgrade path so imported sessions can be RESUMED, not just viewed.
- **mu-w73n** (open — `session.delete_persistent`): paired affordance so imported sessions can be retired when they age out of relevance.
- **mu-lcf7** (open — paused-vs-closed distinction via timestamp): timestamps on imported events let the UI surface 'last active 3 weeks ago' instead of just 'done' for stale imports.

## Out of scope

- Bidirectional sync (writing changes back to claude-code's storage). One-way import only.
- Live-tailing claude-code's JSONL as it writes. Initial v1 is batch-only.
- Image content / file attachments in messages. Drop or stash separately.
- Tool name normalization across mu/claude-code/codex (claude-code's `Task`, `Read`, `Edit`, etc. → mu's tool ids). Pass through as-is initially; standardize later.

## Acceptance

- `mu admin import-claude-history <path>` (or equivalent script) reads claude-code JSONL files and emits valid mu event JSONL
- All four (or however many we land) new `EventPayload` variants exist with serde-defaulted fields so older code doesn't break
- `Outcome::Unclassified` doesn't fire for any imported session whose claude-code session was `stop_reason=end_turn` (forensics regression test)
- TUI F3 renders an imported session's transcript using the existing per-turn loops (you/assistant/tool: ...) — operator can browse historical conversations
- F8 events explorer can filter imported events by their new variants

## Provenance

Operator 2026-05-19 ~03:15 EDT: 'Do those entries provide enough information that we could convert them into our eventlog?' + 'extra information like the thinking sections will also be useful to make our events more robust across different providers.' The latter half is the key insight — import isn't just a viewer for claude-code history; it's a forcing function that makes mu's event schema honest about the full agentic-session domain.

Initial investigation done in conversation: claude-code JSONL has 19 distinct `type` values; structural mapping covers all of them; richer-than-mu fields (thinking, edited_text_file) become new mu variants. The shape works.

title: Authority-coloring as refinement on top of state-coloring (mu-pex)
description:
Refines mu-pex's per-session state-coloring model with an authority/role layer. Memory `fb5cd5be` (claude-code 2.1.139 → mu mapping) and memory `5ff7511e` (color palette stays coarse — ~3 colors, never more than 5).

## Motivation

mu-pex's state-coloring distinguishes session states (idle / streaming / tool-running / done). claude-code 2.1.139's role/tier concept layers on top: which AUTHORITY does a session carry (root capability, attenuated user capability, delegate capability)? These two axes are independent — a streaming root-authority session looks different from a streaming attenuated-delegate session.

## Scope

In:
- Authority tier enum (Root / User / Delegate / Restricted). 3–4 tiers max — coarse per `5ff7511e`.
- Tier rendered as a coloring overlay (border color, label) ON TOP of the state coloring. Two visual axes: state (fill/animation) + authority (border/label).
- Wiring: SessionStatusSummary gains `authority_tier` derived from `Capability`. TUI list view picks up the tier.

Out:
- Authority tier transitions (delegation already handles this).
- Beyond-coarse palettes (memory `5ff7511e` caps at ~5 colors).

## Acceptance

* `Capability` → `AuthorityTier` projection function with unit tests.
* TUI session list shows authority tier visually distinct from state.
* No more than 4 authority tiers; rationale documented.

## References

- Refines: mu-pex
- Memory: `fb5cd5be` (claude-code 2.1.139 mapping), `5ff7511e` (coarse palette)

title: agent/loop_/mod.rs autonomous-mode arm: clean up dead let bindings or comment intent
TUI: expose provider/model selection in 'n' new-session flow
description:
## Why

Sessions in mu's architecture are independent — each carries its own ProviderSelector at create_session time. So having multiple concurrent sessions in one daemon, each with a different provider+model, is already supported wire-side. But the TUI's 'n' (new session) keybind always uses self.default_provider, which is a single tuple set at startup.

Net effect: a feature mu already supports is locked behind a CLI flag (--provider / --model on mu-tui launch) instead of being an in-TUI affordance.

## What

Options, in increasing scope:

A. Palette commands `:provider <kind>` and `:model <id>` that update default_provider for the next 'n' press. Smallest change; trades discoverability for terseness. ~30 LOC.

B. Two-step 'n' flow: 'n' opens a small picker (provider on top, model below, both with keyboard navigation), then create_session fires on Enter. ~80-150 LOC depending on picker polish.

C. Both: palette for keyboard-fast use; picker for discoverability.

Recommend (A) first — it gets the feature usable in ~30 LOC; (B) can layer on later when the rest of the TUI gets palette-/picker-friendlier.

## Acceptance

- Running mu-tui without --provider/--model, then issuing :provider openai_codex / :model gpt-5.5 in the palette, then pressing 'n', creates a session against openai_codex/gpt-5.5.
- Existing default-provider behavior preserved when no palette command is issued.
- Session list (F2 when it lands, or the session row in F1) shows which provider+model each session ran against.

## References

- Default flow today: crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs::App::create_session uses self.default_provider.
- ProviderSelector wire type: crates/mu-core/src/protocol.rs::ProviderSelector.
close_reason: done

title: mu-tui F3: Esc cancels in-flight RPC; Esc-Esc ends session; Ctrl-C unchanged
description:
## Symptom

F3 (SessionDetail) has no cancel affordance. In `on_key_normal`
(crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs:1423), Esc falls through to the no-op
catch-all and there is no chord-state machine for Esc-Esc. The only
in-F3 stop gesture today is Ctrl-C, which quits the whole TUI — a
sledgehammer when all you wanted was to interrupt the model.

The underlying RPCs already exist:

- `session.cancel_outstanding` (mu-035) — kills the in-flight RPC for
  the focused session without ending the session itself.
- `cancel_session` — ends the focused session entirely.

This bead wires those into F3 key handling with an Esc / Esc-Esc
chord, leaving Ctrl-C as the global "quit mu" gesture.

## Fix

In `crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs`:

1. Add `last_esc_at: Option<Instant>` to `App` for double-tap detection.
2. Add a pure `classify_esc(last_esc_at, now, window) -> EscChord`
   helper (unit-tested independently of the RPC layer).
3. In `on_key_normal`, add an Esc arm guarded by
   `matches!(self.mode, ViewMode::SessionDetail)`:
   - `EscChord::Single` → call `session.cancel_outstanding` on the
     focused session and reset `last_esc_at = Some(now)`.
   - `EscChord::Double` → call `cancel_session` on the focused session
     and clear `last_esc_at`.
4. New helpers: `cancel_outstanding_for_focused_session`,
   `cancel_focused_session`. Both surface success/failure to the
   firehose (and pending_inline_markers when in Inline mode) so the
   operator gets visible feedback.
5. Ctrl-C unchanged.

Double-tap window: 500 ms (typical chord-key feel; matches editor
conventions).

## Acceptance

- Single Esc in F3 calls `session.cancel_outstanding` and emits a
  firehose/inline marker reflecting success or "nothing in flight".
- A second Esc within 500 ms calls `cancel_session` and emits a marker.
- Esc outside F3 still no-ops (no regression).
- Ctrl-C still quits the TUI in F3.
- `classify_esc` unit tests cover: no prior Esc, prior Esc within
  window, prior Esc past window, exactly-at-window boundary.
close_reason: fixed in b9fc9cf4 on fix/mu-iuou-f3-esc-cancel; PR #119; graduated Esc chord (cancel_outstanding then cancel_session) + 4 unit tests + help-text update

title: Implement mu-035: session.provider_status + cancel_outstanding
description:
Spec at specs/mu-035-session-provider-status.md (jj spouxlsp d69719c3).

Three deliverables:
1. session.provider_status periodic notification with kind (awaiting_first_token | streaming | thinking | tool_executing | awaiting_tool_result | idle), started_at_unix_ms, elapsed_ms, optional bytes_received / tool_call_id.
2. session.cancel_outstanding narrow cancel (kills provider call, session stays alive).
3. daemon.outstanding_calls fleet view.

INV-4 is load-bearing: the emit-tick runs on a tokio timer independent of the provider future, so a stalled provider still produces visible status.

Once landed:
- TUI's phase line gets authoritative ground truth (no more session.list-derived heuristics)
- mu_client.py defensive layer becomes belt-and-suspenders
- Refinement experiment's silent stalls become instantly diagnosable

Estimated 1 week of focused Rust work.

title: AskSessionRequest: per-ask provider/model override
description:
## Why

Today, a session's (provider, model) is fixed at create_session time. To use a different model for one ask — e.g., "use haiku for this cheap subtask in the middle of a sonnet session" — there's no path short of creating a new session (losing context).

Surfaced 2026-05-11 during a multi-iteration discussion about model routing. The agent-router script (~/src/claude-personal/scripts/agent-router) already does this externally; doing it natively in mu means a session can mix model choices without going through a wrapper.

## What

Add an optional `provider_override` field to AskSessionRequest. When present, the agent loop builds the provider for THIS ask using the override instead of the session's stored provider, but doesn't persist it — the session keeps its original (provider, model). Subsequent asks use the session default again unless overridden.

The 'persistent swap' variant is its own bead (see mu-??? session.set_provider) — this one is per-call only.

## Wire shape (proposal)

```json
{
  "session_id": "session-1",
  "user_message": "...",
  "provider_override": { "kind": "anthropic_api", "model": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001" }
}
```

## Considerations

- Cost: opens the door to 'route each ask to the cheapest viable model' patterns. This is what the agent-router script does today externally.
- Capability surface: must validate that the override provider supports the tools the session has bound; otherwise error early.
- Cache impact: overriding the model means the previously-cached prefix (system prompt + tools) becomes a cache miss on the override target. The dashboard's cache% column will reflect this naturally.

## Acceptance

- Unit test: AskSessionRequest with provider_override hits the override provider, not the session's stored one.
- The override doesn't mutate session state — subsequent asks without override use the session's original (provider, model).
- F5 dashboard shows the override as its own (provider, model) row (since the daemon.usage_history aggregator groups by provider_kind + model on each call, this should fall out naturally).

title: mu-core: emit approval-lifecycle events (input_required_emitted, input_required_responded) for audit-trail completeness
description:
## Problem

mu's bash tool with `--bash-prompt` mode triggers an approval modal via
`session.input_required` notifications. The user clicks approve/deny in
the TUI. The notification → approve → tool-run → tool_result chain is
real, but the only persistent trace in the per-session JSONL is:

- The `tool_call` event (request made by agent)
- The `tool_result` event (final outcome)

The intermediate `input_required` notification, the wait for user
response, and the user's decision (approve / deny / reason) are NOT
recorded as discrete events. For denials, the tool_result content
includes the text `tool denied by user via session.respond_to_input_required`
— that's the only fingerprint. For approvals, there's no fingerprint
at all; the approval is invisible in the persistent log.

This was discovered 2026-05-16 when debugging daemon f3845422 session-9:
answering "did popups fire?" required reconstructing from timing gaps
(800ms–47000ms between tool_call and tool_result on bash invocations)
and careful inspection of error text, because the events themselves
don't record the approval lifecycle.

## Proposal

Add two new event kinds to mu's persistent event log:

1. **`input_required_emitted`** — fires when daemon sends a
   `session.input_required` notification. Records:
   - request_id (for correlation with the response)
   - target tool name + arguments (what's being approved)
   - rationale (why approval is required: `--bash-prompt` mode,
     capability check, etc.)
   - timestamp

2. **`input_required_responded`** — fires when daemon receives the
   client's `session.respond_to_input_required` RPC. Records:
   - request_id (correlates with the emitted event)
   - decision: `Approve` | `Deny`
   - decided_by: `user` | `timeout` | `auto-policy` (if mu ever adds
     auto-approval)
   - reason (optional, for denials with explanation)
   - timestamp

Both events appear in the per-session JSONL the same way other events
do, with the standard `id` / `session_id` / `timestamp_unix_ms` /
`actor` / `payload` envelope.

## Acceptance

- Approval modal firings are visible in the persistent event log
- Approve and deny decisions are both visible (not just denials via
  tool_result text)
- A query like "how many bash invocations were approved vs denied vs
  auto-rejected for this session?" is answerable from the event log
  alone
- Existing tool_call/tool_result events still work as before
- Timing analysis is no longer the only way to detect approval-modal
  firings

## Why this matters

mu's design philosophy is "observability as product feature" (per
gpt's code review). Silent approval flows contradict that.

With the events recorded, automated post-hoc analysis (e.g., "this
model hits 5x more approval prompts than that model in the same
daemon") becomes a one-query observation rather than the timing-
archaeology exercise it currently is.

## Cross-refs

- Source incident: session-9 in daemon f3845422 (2026-05-16), where
  Claude's bash-heavy strategy triggered numerous popups but the
  approval lifecycle was invisible in the JSONL
- mu-gih (modal approval dialog implementation — this bead adds the
  audit side of what mu-gih landed the UX side of)
- mu-6hl (bash policy legibility — companion observability work)
- mu-pex (metrics framework — approval rates feed into quality proxies)
- Provenance: live debugging of TUI session asymmetry, 2026-05-16

title: mu-clip-coding-too-many-args: 3 functions in mu-coding with 9-10 args (clippy::too_many_arguments)
description:
Three functions in mu-coding trigger clippy::too_many_arguments (signatures with 9-10 args; clippy's default threshold is 7). These are signature-design decisions, not mechanical fixes.

## Items (exact locations from clippy)

- 1× function with 10/7 args
- 2× functions with 9/7 args

(Specific locations + names in clippy output; not pre-listed here because the decision per-function may differ — bundle into a config struct, builder pattern, or just `#[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]` with rationale.)

## Per-function decision space

For each: choose one of —

A. **Bundle into a config struct.** Most common for ergonomic call sites. Adds one struct + Default. Best when args naturally group (e.g. "render options" struct, "server config" struct).
B. **Builder pattern.** When some args are optional and call sites vary. More boilerplate; usually overkill for 3-arg overflow.
C. **`#[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]` with rationale.** When refactoring would harm clarity at call sites (e.g. internal dispatch fns where every arg is mandatory + unrelated). The allow must include a one-line comment naming why.

## Acceptance

- Each of the 3 functions has either: (a) refactored to ≤7 args via struct/builder, OR (b) explicit `#[allow]` with rationale comment
- Decision rationale documented in commit body per-function
- cargo clippy passes (for these specific lints)
- cargo test --workspace green
- One commit closes this bead

## Why this bead is higher-judgment than the mechanical one

Signature changes ripple to every call site. If we go option A or B, we must update all callers. If option C, we admit the function has irreducible complexity. The right answer per function depends on call-site usage patterns — worth a quick survey of each function's callers before deciding.
close_reason: Landed in jj revision omulvzmu. Three config structs (AskOptions, NewSession, BuildSessionRequest<'_>) replace 9-10 arg signatures. 7 call sites updated. Workspace clippy -D warnings + fmt --check + 361 tests all green. CI gate should now pass on the next push.

title: F5 dashboard: add tool_total_ms p95 column
description:
## Why

The mu-pex Phase 1 RPC (`daemon.usage_history`) returns `tool_total_ms` (per-(ToolCall, ToolResult) pair durations, aggregated as median + p95) in every row, but the F5 dashboard's Phase A renderer (mu-xln, commit yqoktozo 0d3a3f23) omits this column. Discovered 2026-05-11 when a 29s/14-tool ask showed 17s of streaming + 116 output tokens — most of the wall was tool execution, and the dashboard couldn't show it.

"Where is the wall time going?" is one of the canonical metrics-dashboard questions. tool_total_ms is the answer for tool-heavy workloads and TTFT / streaming together are the answer for prose-heavy ones. Without the tool column, the dashboard only answers half the question.

## What

In crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs render_usage:

1. Add a 'tool p95' column header between 'wall p95' and 'in tok'.
2. Pull row["tool_total_ms"]["p95"] and format via the existing fmt_ms_p95 helper.
3. Update the widths array to include the new column (8 char width fits 'XXXXms' + spacing).
4. Verify the table still fits in a standard 120-col terminal (current rendered width is ~106 chars + 2 borders; adding 8+1 = ~115, fits).

## Acceptance

- F5 pane shows a 'tool p95' column.
- Empty rows (no tool calls) render '—' or '0ms' via fmt_ms_p95 (it returns '—' on None).
- No layout regression on narrow terminals (test at 80, 100, 120 cols).

## Note on column ordering

Current order favors timing-decomposition reading left to right: ttft p95 → stream p95 → wall p95. Inserting tool p95 between stream and wall preserves that 'what happened during the wall' progression: AwaitingFirstToken time → streaming time → tool time → total wall.
close_reason: done

description: Phase 1 of beads close-on-merge automation (plan: wobbly-juggling-simon). New .github/workflows/beads-orphans.yml triggered on pull_request: install br via cargo install --git (beads_rust is public, no release binary), rebuild db from tracked issues.jsonl (br sync --import-only), run br orphans --json --details, filter to orphans whose latest_commit is in BASE..HEAD (PR-scoped), post a NON-BLOCKING job summary + optional sticky PR comment nudging close-in-PR. Always exit 0. Reuse ci.yml verify-claims commit-iteration pattern.

title: mu-tui: view for associated claude sessions (via operator/mailbox/etcd)
description:
## Problem

mu can observe its own sessions but doesn't surface associated claude-code
sessions (the supervisor/operator/orchestrator pattern from the 3-layer
hierarchy, memory 64b4a3a6). When mu is the substrate for claude
orchestration, the user should see what associated claude sessions are
doing without leaving the mu TUI.

## Proposal

Add a view (F-key panel or extension of F2/F3) showing associated claude
sessions via one or more channels:

- Direct relationship via mu-as-supervisor-of-claude (operator pattern)
- Via mailbox messages (mu-lho phase 1 + planned mu-71q MCP adapter)
- Via etcd-tracked tasks (if orchestrator-mcp + claude-proxy wired)

Display: session ID, current status, last activity, brief task description.

## Acceptance

- Panel/view lists known associated claude sessions
- Data refreshed via whichever channel(s) are wired
- Selecting one shows enough detail to know what it's doing
- No need to leave the TUI

## Cross-refs

- mu-ib5 (F9 mailbox view — mailbox channel discovery overlaps)
- mu-cha (F2 session tree — could be extended to show external sessions)
- mu-71q (MCP adapter)
- memory 64b4a3a6 (3-layer orchestration pattern this surfaces)
- Provenance: TUI feedback (mu daemon f3845422 session-2, 2026-05-15)

title: task_id correlation field on intermediate event payloads
description:
Stamp `task_id` (the same UUID-like identifier carried in `EventPayload::TaskTelemetry`) on every intermediate event emitted during a task: `ToolCall`, `ToolResult`, `ContextAssembly`, `AssistantMessageEvent`, `ProviderStatusUpdate`, `Callout`.

## Why

Operator observation 2026-05-16 after mu-5g7i landed: 'if a session was task-oriented and working on multiple simultaneous tasks, associating a task identifier with each operation would allow for other accounting.'

Today's events carry `session_id` + monotonic `event_id` only. Per-task attribution requires walking forward from a `UserMessage` to the next `Done` and gathering everything in between. Two consequences:

1. **Classifier work (mu-8alb)** has to do that reconstruction walk for every classification. Cheap in isolation, but every analytics query that wants per-task aggregation pays the same cost.
2. **Future intra-session concurrency** (parallel tool calls, mailbox-driven side-tasks, autonomous-loop concurrent iterations) breaks the walk-based reconstruction entirely — interleaved events become indistinguishable from one task with weird timing.

## Today's concurrency model (for context)

mu sessions are sequential today: `ask_session` blocks until Done, mailbox messages process in normal turn cycle, autonomous loops iterate sequentially. So the reconstruction walk works correctly NOW. The argument for adding task_id is forward-looking + ergonomic, not bug-fixing.

## Design sketch

- Forwarder tracks 'current task_id' between `UserMessage` and the matching `Done` / `Error`. The same id ultimately appears in `EventPayload::TaskTelemetry.task_id` at termination.
- Each `to_log_event` projection injects the current task_id into the payload.
- Backwards-compat: serde with `#[serde(default)]` so older log entries deserialize as `task_id: None` (or a sentinel). Classifier code can treat None as 'pre-correlation-id era' and fall back to walk-based reconstruction.

## Scope of changes

- `EventPayload` variants gain a new `task_id: Option<String>` field (or wrap in a common envelope; latter is cleaner but more invasive). Touching every variant is small mechanical work.
- Forwarder gains task-id tracking state (a single `Option<String>` field per session).
- AgentLoop doesn't change (forwarder injects).
- mu-8alb (classifier) can be simplified once this lands — direct query instead of reconstruction.

## Acceptance

- New field defined and serde-compat (with default for old logs).
- Forwarder populates it for every event emitted between UserMessage and the matching Done/Error.
- Tests verify task_id consistency across a multi-event task.
- Classifier (mu-8alb) opportunistically reads it when present; falls back to walk otherwise.

## Cross-references

- mu-5g7i (TaskTelemetry envelope, where task_id is defined at task termination)
- mu-8alb (classifier — currently does reconstruction walk; would benefit from this)
- mu-8ypx (analytics sink — same)
- mu-mk9l (backfill — would set task_id on synthetic entries)

title: typed obstacle/gap signal for workers (obstacle.observed event)
description: A low-friction typed event a worker can emit instead of either improvising a worse result or writing a prose apology. Framing is load-bearing: NOT 'the exit when you can't solve it' (drives under- or over-use) but 'the signal when the SOLVE is becoming COMPENSATION' — i.e. when an improvisation is masking a missing capability rather than being generative (improvisation is often generative and should not be suppressed). Pair with a session-level statement that naming the gap is a COMPLETE output shape, not a partial one. Three stacked pieces, weakest to strongest: legitimacy-of-shape framing < low-friction form < structured typed channel. Note: a channel existing != it being used; the default that shapes usage is a separate concern. Source: internal session d7cb0b99 synthesis.

title: mu-d04a Phase 3: nested collapsible child events (Btw/tool-call as inline child of the outer turn)
description:
Decomposed from mu-d04a (mu-mlcg, 2026-05-31). Today a /btw renders as a SEPARATE top-level magenta block (app.rs:1088-1093); tool calls render inline but flat.

WORK: add a nested child-event item so a /btw aside — and, uniformly, a tool call — renders as a timestamp-anchored collapsible CHILD inside the outer assistant block. Add TurnItem::Btw(Turn) (or a generic Child(Turn)) to the enum (render.rs:124-138) + render_turn_item support (render.rs:181); collapsed = one-liner (+you/+assistant), full expand deferred to Phase 5.

ACCEPTANCE: render_turn renders a nested child block (render.rs unit test, sibling to the existing 9); a /btw nests under its triggering turn; cargo test --workspace + just ci green. blockedBy Phase 2 (per-session model).

title: EventLogView query object — range/kind/span-touching queries over the event log (foundation for context observability + consolidator)
description:
## Summary

Promote `SessionEventLog`'s ad-hoc query surface into an `EventLogView` query object that supports range queries, kind filters, span-touching queries, and provenance walks. Foundation under all downstream context-observability and consolidator work.

## Why this is foundational

Today `SessionEventLog::snapshot() -> Vec<SessionEvent>` is the only generic read path. It returns everything. Every downstream caller (the operator-facing `mu context` CLI in mu-u6hc, the TUI panel in mu-sa6q, the proposed agent-facing `context.list()` tool, the proposed concurrent consolidator, mu-phl's relocation-on-cull) needs structured slices of the log — by sequence range, by event kind, by span lineage. Each one inventing its own iteration over `snapshot()` is wasteful and brittle; the substrate should provide the query.

## Scope

### In

- New `EventLogView` query object in `crates/mu-core/src/event_log.rs` (or its own submodule if it grows).
- Query primitives:
  - `events_between(seq_a, seq_b) -> impl Iterator<Item = &SessionEvent>`
  - `events_of_kind(kind: EventKindFilter) -> impl Iterator<...>` — kind filter is a discriminator over the existing `EventPayload` variants.
  - `events_touching_span(span_id: &str) -> impl Iterator<...>` — for spans that appear in `ContextAssembly::first_span_ids`, or future per-event `affected_spans` fields.
  - `events_after_seq(seq) -> impl Iterator<...>` — tail watcher seam for the consolidator.
- Provenance accessor: `provenance_chain(event_id) -> Vec<&SessionEvent>` walks `parent_event_ids` to the root, mirroring what `RetainedRope::provenance` does for spans.
- Borrowed views (no allocation in the iteration path) — the substrate is append-only so borrowing is safe across reads as long as no writer is active.
- Documentation in `specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md` (amend the "Event store" / "Projections" sections) noting that `EventLogView` is the canonical read surface.

### Out

- Mutating operations — `EventLogView` is read-only. Mutations stay on `SessionEventLog::append` and its variants.
- Cross-session queries — single-session for v1. The `daemon_id:session_id:event_seq` addressing scheme (see specs/architecture/agent-experience-frictions.md "Lowest-level memory addressing") is the seam for v2 cross-session walks; not built now.
- Persistent indexes — v1 scans the in-memory snapshot. If profiling shows the linear scans dominate, a B-tree or similar over seq is a follow-up.

## Acceptance

- `EventLogView` exposed from `mu_core::event_log`.
- All five query primitives implemented with tests over a fixture event log.
- At least one existing caller of `SessionEventLog::snapshot()` (e.g., the usage rollups in `event_log.rs`) refactored to use `EventLogView` — proves the abstraction earns its keep.
- `event-sourced-context.md` amended to document `EventLogView` as the canonical read surface.

## Dependencies

- **Blocked by:** mu-za92 (CompactionAssembly + RopeEvent durability bug). Until those events are actually in the durable log, querying for them returns nothing useful. The query API can be built before mu-za92 lands, but its downstream consumers (consolidator, observability) depend on mu-za92.

## Related

- specs/architecture/agent-experience-frictions.md (Friction F: no self-introspection of context contents)
- mu-u6hc — first operator-facing consumer
- mu-sa6q — first TUI consumer
- mu-phl — needs the query surface for relocation-on-cull and recall

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Reframed under mu-ki1f (event log as a first-class system): this is its query/replay read API. Real near-term consumers are rehydration (mu-u1ld) and mu-solo on-demand buffers (/dump, scrollback-from-log), not the consolidator. Scope the first cut to range/kind/replay-from-seq; defer provenance-walk and span-touching until the agentic stack lands. See the Sequencing review in specs/architecture/agent-experience-frictions.md.

title: JudgeRankingEntry.auth is stored and logged but never honored in provider construction
description: Found by the gpt-5.5 judge during mu-8bkf review (2026-06-05): [compaction.judge].ranking[].auth ('api_key' | 'oauth') is part of the config schema and is logged when a judge provider is selected, but ranking_entry_to_selector / build_provider_from_selector never interpret it — ProviderSelector carries provider/model only, so auth resolution falls to the provider factory's own ranking (providers.anthropic_auth_ranking etc.). Either (a) thread the auth preference into provider construction so a ranking entry can pin its auth source, or (b) drop the field from the schema and docs to stop implying a contract that isn't honored. Decide (a) vs (b) before implementing; (b) is a breaking config change (deny_unknown_fields).

title: mu-ci: add GitHub Actions CI workflow (fmt + clippy -D warnings + test) and clean pre-existing warnings
description:
Add v1 CI workflow at .github/workflows/ci.yml gating PRs and pushes-to-main on three jobs:
- cargo fmt --all -- --check
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings
- cargo test --workspace --all-features --no-fail-fast

Atomic with cleanup of pre-existing warnings surfaced today (event_log.rs:545 unused ToolCall import; mu-tui dead-code warnings in main.rs/mu_client.rs/mod.rs/serve_smoke.rs) so the first CI run is green.

## Scope

IN:
- .github/workflows/ci.yml (new file)
- Fixes to pre-existing warnings until cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings is clean
- Cargo fmt --check pass (fix any pre-existing fmt drift)

OUT:
- macOS / FreeBSD matrix (Linux only for v1; FreeBSD verified locally)
- cargo-audit / cargo-deny (separate beads if/when wanted)
- Release artifacts / cross-compilation
- Per-PR templates / contributor docs

## Acceptance

- .github/workflows/ci.yml present, syntactically valid YAML, three jobs
- cargo fmt --all -- --check exits 0
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings exits 0
- cargo test --workspace --all-features green
- One commit closes this bead

title: mu discovery-bootstrap: default system-prompt fragment that tells the in-loop agent to discover on demand — companion to MU_NO_RECALL (mu-54xj)
close_reason: Discovery-bootstrap default (recall-off-only, conservative composition) added in PR #142. INSTRUCTION leg complete.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
RATIONALE (opus discovery experiment, 2026-05-30, memory mu-discover-on-demand-teachable-2026-05-30 e575d64e):

The instruction leg of discover-on-demand. MU_NO_RECALL (mu-54xj) turns OFF front-loaded recall, but an uninstructed Opus then just declines: recall-off + NO bootstrap => 'I don't have this — tell me / point me to a file' (0 searches). recall-off + a ~10-line bootstrap (via --append-system-prompt) => Opus searched (7 turns) and produced the BEST, leanest answer of the whole experiment (116-token startup). So discover-on-demand is teachable, not automatic — mu needs a default bootstrap, especially when recall is disabled.

Content (cf. ~/tmp/mu-discovery-bootstrap.md and claude-basic's CLAUDE.md): point the agent at 'agent memory search/recent --type user|feedback' for identity/decisions, and the native discover tool (mu-onq8) / t4c find for tools. Design Qs: always-on vs only-when-recall-off; how it composes with the existing system prompt.

Pairs with mu-onq8 (native FindTool — the *means* leg; without it the shell-out to agent/t4c is blocked by the strict bash allowlist).

title: Audit goal-protocol skill vs current mu-slat orchestration, then dogfood orchestration on first scaffolding-epic item
description:
Two-part follow-up, sequenced AFTER mu-d04a (streaming rework) and after the #3 scaffolding epic is planned. Raised by operator 2026-05-28.

## Part 1 — bring goal-protocol skill into compliance with current orchestration
The mu-slat orchestration evolved substantially (esp. 2026-05-28): host-side reap on result-post, Rust-owned pty + vt100 cadence spawn (replacing script(1)), mu-spawn linux-fs mounts (linprocfs/linsysfs/tmpfs-shm/fdescfs-linrdlnk for the CPU fix), and the reply_to→calling-session fix (mu-d04a's sibling, commit 03015a51). The goal-protocol skill (~/.claude-personal/skills/goal-protocol) predates most of this. Audit it against the current spawn_worker / pot lifecycle / mailbox-result / reap reality and reconcile any drift.

## Part 2 — dogfood
Once the #3 scaffolding epic exists, dispatch its FIRST item through the orchestration (spawn_worker / a goal session) rather than doing it by hand — a real-world test of the loop on non-toy work. Watch: does the worker complete + post a usable result, does reap fire, does the result wake the operator session (the mu-d04a behavior).

## Provenance
Operator aside during the mu-d04a planning session, 2026-05-28.

title: mid-session provider/model switching: set_route RPC + two-step picker
description:
Enable switching provider and model mid-session without restarting.

## Daemon side
- New RPC: session.set_route { provider, model } — atomic, validated
- Between turns: tear down current provider, rebuild with new one
- Log a model-change event to the event log
- Decompose create_session into new_session + set_route (create_session becomes sugar)
- Rehydration analogy: mid-session switch = rehydrate against different backend

## Daemon discovery
- daemon.list_providers RPC: returns available providers with configured status + model lists
- Hide unconfigured/credentialless providers from the picker

## mu-solo UX
- Two-step picker: select provider first, then model filtered to that provider
- One atomic set_route sent only after both selected (no transient invalid state)
- Favorites/short-list from solo.toml config, full list behind 'more' separator
- /provider and /model commands become live (currently display-only stubs)

## Cache behavior
- Event log and rope unchanged on switch
- Re-project through new provider's tokenizer on next ask
- Cache invalidation is the provider's problem (different model = different prefix hash = cache miss, correct behavior)

title: mu-agent-loop: cap consecutive tool-not-found responses (force end_turn after N=3 default)
description:
## Problem

The agent loop in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/mod.rs` has no termination guard for repeated `"tool not found"` responses. When a model emits `stop_reason: "tool_use"` for a tool that mu hasn't registered, the dispatcher returns a tool_result with error message `"tool not found: <name>"`, and the loop continues. Some models will then try a different (also-nonexistent) tool name, and the loop spins until the model gives up on its own.

This is the same loop observed in mu-q3v6's validation reproducer: 6 consecutive tool_use turns chasing hallucinated tools before Gemini-3.5-flash finally emitted end_turn. Each round burned ~30k prompt tokens.

## Why a defensive guard even after the sibling bead lands

Sibling bead [the no-tools-clause one] addresses the root cause for the `tool_count == 0` case. But:

1. Some providers/models may emit tool_use even when explicitly told not to (prompt non-adherence is not zero).
2. The same pattern can arise with `tool_count > 0` if the model hallucinates a tool name that isn't in the registered set. Today: a session with one registered tool (e.g. `bash`) and a model that emits `execute_bash` will get "tool not found" → loop continues.
3. Even with a clean system prompt, providers under load may produce degraded outputs that retry-loop.

A defensive cap is a belt-and-suspenders safety net. It says: "regardless of what the system prompt said, if the model has emitted N consecutive tool_calls that don't resolve, force end_turn."

## Proposal

In the agent loop, track consecutive `"tool not found"` responses per session. After **3 consecutive** unresolved tool_call attempts:

1. Synthesize a final tool_result that says: "You have attempted to call 3 tools that do not exist in this session: {list of attempted names}. Respond to the user without tool calls."
2. Force the next provider call to use a synthesized `stop_reason: "end_turn"` constraint (if the provider supports it) OR detect end_turn in the model's next response and break the loop.
3. Emit an `AgentEvent::ToolNotFoundLoopAborted` event so observability captures this happened.
4. Telemetry (`task_telemetry`) gains a `tool_not_found_loop_aborted: bool` field so post-hoc analysis can count occurrences.

## Acceptance

- Reproducer: with the no-tools-clause from sibling bead REVERTED (so the model still tries to call tools), a `mu ask` against a known-hallucinator model breaks out of the loop after 3 attempts instead of going to 6+.
- `AgentEvent::ToolNotFoundLoopAborted` is emitted at the abort point with the list of attempted tool names.
- `task_telemetry.tool_not_found_loop_aborted` is `true` for the aborted session.
- Sessions with legitimate tool use (registered tools, valid calls) continue to work normally.
- Configurable threshold (`MU_TOOL_NOT_FOUND_LIMIT` env var or daemon config; default 3) so operators can tune.

## Out of scope

- Heuristics that try to map hallucinated tool names to registered ones (e.g. fuzzy-match `execute_bash` → `bash`). Tempting but introduces a different class of bug; leave for a future bead if the data justifies.

## Provenance

Filed 2026-05-22 by tcovert. Sibling to bead [the no-tools-clause one]. Both surfaced from mu-q3v6's post-merge validation reproducer (daemon f7b1715a207c7d97, session-1).

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
## Cold-bare-Claude dogfood — phase-3 gate evidence (half 1 of 2)

Date: 2026-05-29. Fresh session (post-build), picked a low-priority bead to test
whether `t4c find` in the bootstrap reduces flailing. Bead chosen: **mu-d33g**
(verify-claims.sh git-trailer bug). t4c 0.0.1, br 0.2.5, both live.

### What `t4c find` returned (genuine first-move queries)

1. `t4c find "extract git trailers from a commit message"`
   → top hit **bash.git.status** (then jj.status, agent.memory, fd, grep, jq, rg, t4c).
   WRONG. The correct tool the spec itself names — `git interpret-trailers
   --only-trailers` — is not in the catalog. Only the `git.status` subcommand node
   is registered, so the ranker matched on the bare token "git" and surfaced status.

2. `t4c find "parse text blocks line by line in a shell script"`
   → top hit **bash.grep**. WRONG. The work is editing an `awk` block; `awk` is not
   in the catalog at all. Ranker fell back to grep on "line"/"search" token overlap.

### Verdict: t4c did NOT change my next move on this bead

The suggestions were plausible-but-wrong, so I proceeded on my own knowledge. The
actual win on this bead came from the **map-vs-terrain discipline** (reading the
current files), NOT from t4c: the bead was already resolved by commit `150c6d7`
(fix at verify-claims.sh:88 + regression test case 9). Re-ran the suite: 9/9 green.
Closed mu-d33g as stale-open.

### Why it missed (the actionable part for phase 3)

Discovery quality is bottlenecked by two things, neither of which is the plumbing
(plumbing works: find/help/run/JSON all functional):

- **Catalog shallowness.** Subcommand granularity is too coarse — git is
  represented by a single `git.status` node, not its ~150 subcommands. The tools the
  bead actually needed (`git interpret-trailers`, `awk`) are absent. A curated
  hand-list cannot keep up with real intents.
- **Token-overlap ranker.** It rewards surface-string match ("git", "line") over
  intent, so a coarse catalog yields confident-but-wrong top hits — the
  folkloric-capabilities failure mode reproduced inside t4c's own output.

### Implication for the gate

This ARGUES FOR phase 3 (mu-native RegistrySource over mu's `ToolRegistry` +
`skill::loader::discover_skills`), because the lever is **catalog breadth/depth**,
which the mu-native sources supply automatically (enumerable registries vs. a hand
catalog). But it also means phase 3 must pair the registry plumbing with a real
ranker (semantic, e.g. via RecallProvider) — registry coverage without ranking
quality just produces more confident-wrong hits over a bigger surface.

Standalone t4c (curated bash catalog) is a useful invoke/learn surface
(`help --schema`, `run`) but is NOT yet a flailing-reducer for `find` on real
intents at v0.0.1.

(Still owed for the full gate: the gpt-in-mu second-opinion — half 2 of 2.)

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
PHASE-3 GATE EVIDENCE — discriminating cold-dogfood (2026-05-29, ranking build, HOST venue). Full writeup: memory t4c-discriminating-dogfood-phase3-justified.

Cold unguided bash-only agent, sole orientation = t4c pointer, task = find this env's model-routing policy (answer lives in a SKILL + memory, not in any local file).
- ORGANIC ADOPTION: yes (reached for `t4c find` first, unprompted; find -> help -> run).
- SEMANTIC RANKER: works cold (t4c find correctly surfaced bash.agent.memory for the routing intent — the ranking-layer win holds for a non-builder consumer).
- DECISIVE GAP: t4c is BLIND TO SKILLS. The canonical answer was the model-routing SKILL; the agent found it via the harness skill-list, not t4c. t4c routed to the secondary source (memory), structurally blind to the primary (skill).

VERDICT: argues FOR phase-3. The lever shifted from ranker-quality (done, merged) to COVERAGE — the skill source here (sub-bead b: discover_skills -> skill.<name>) would have surfaced the authoritative artifact. Bugs found: bead mu-avsa.

comments.author: claude
comments.text:
ESCALATED for operator decision (goal mu-fjo5, 2026-05-31; pre-registered DIFFICULTY stop (c) "load-bearing embedding-model/infra decision" + default-criterion #2 cross-crate / affects-another-open-bead). Terrain-check before implementing:

- The bead names "Ranker over mu-core RecallProvider", but RecallProvider is `recall(cwd,cap)->Vec<RecalledItem>` (context/recall/mod.rs:110) — it has NO embedding capability, and mu-core has no embedder. The actual semantic path lives in t4c: trait Ranker (rank.rs:27), SemanticRanker<E: Embedder> (semantic.rs:61), Embedder (embedder.rs:20), and a real ConfigEmbedder (embedder.rs:88-155). t4c_source.rs already exposes discover_semantic<E: Embedder>. So ".6.3" really means: wire t4c's SemanticRanker into the in-process discover path, replacing the LexicalRanker floor.
- ConfigEmbedder is a BLOCKING, NETWORK, PAID embedder: reqwest::blocking POST to OpenRouter embeddings (default qwen/qwen3-embedding-8b), reads ~/.config/agent/config.toml for the key. Wiring it into the in-loop `discover` tool (mu-onq8) means live paid embedding calls + per-call VectorCache rebuild (embed all caps + intent every discover) + blocking-in-async (needs spawn_blocking) on a hot path.
- The bead also says "Pairs with the merged ranking layer (embeddings/SemanticRanker)" + "Optional usage-prior nudge = mu-dwzq" — implying a broader ranking design I can't fully see.

DECISION NEEDED (the cost/caching/default-posture call is the operator's):
(A) Ship now, conservative: wire SemanticRanker behind a config flag DEFAULT-OFF (LexicalRanker stays the default), ConfigEmbedder::from_config()->FakeEmbedder fallback, spawn_blocking, unit-tested with FakeEmbedder. Zero default behavior change, zero forced cost; operator opts in later. Mirrors mu-re0s/mu-k011 posture.
(B) Defer until the merged-ranking-layer design + mu-dwzq are settled (avoid pre-empting the architecture).
(C) Other (e.g. cache embeddings to disk keyed by manifest hash; or a non-network local embedder).

Did NOT implement to avoid committing a design direction against the pre-registered stop. mu-mlcg (the other goal item) is DONE.

comments.author: claude
comments.text: ESCALATION RESOLVED (operator, 2026-05-31): (1) live network/paid embeddings are routine + accepted (agent memory recall already uses them) — not a blocker; (2) the discover tool is run INFREQUENTLY (install / new env / new tools), NOT every turn — so per-call re-embedding is not a hot-path cost; caching deferred as unnecessary. Desired shape: embed LOCAL (Ollama) first, fall back to LIVE (OpenRouter) when Ollama is down/model-not-loaded. Proceeding: wire t4c SemanticRanker into the in-process discover path with a graceful LexicalRanker floor when no embedder resolves; embedding via a new t4c ChainEmbedder (Ollama-first -> live-fallback); spawn_blocking for the blocking embed. One PR.

title: phase3d: daemon JSON-RPC capabilities/discover (find/walk/help) + mu CLI subcommand
description: Expose t4c find/walk/help as an in-process daemon JSON-RPC capabilities/discover method (same struct as tool-result data) + a mu CLI subcommand. The Layer-1 manifest surface inside mu.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
DONE (green, full-workspace test pass, dogfooded). Two surfaces per the design decisions:

1. Daemon RPC capabilities/discover (session-scoped, attenuated):
- protocol/discovery.rs: CapabilitiesDiscoverRequest{session_id,intent,limit}/Response{results:Vec<CapabilityView>}; open-string dispatch (pure addition, no enum surgery).
- handlers/capabilities.rs: gets the session's attenuation Capability (Sessions::capability), projects via build_manifest_for_tools, discover_view.
- SKILLS WIRED INTO DAEMON (the gap): serve_with_io_with_config now discover_skills at startup (default_search_dirs(cwd) + $MU_SKILLS_DIR), threads Arc<Vec<LoadedSkill>> through the dispatch closure -> dispatch() -> handler. Closes the 'daemon knew only tools' gap (.6.2 lesson).
- Added build_manifest_for_tools (attenuate a tool SLICE by cap.check_allow, no ToolRegistry/RetainedRope detour since the daemon holds Vec not Registry).
- CapabilityView gained Deserialize (clients parse the response) + serde defaults on optionals.

2. CLI in-process standalone: 'mu capabilities discover <intent> [--limit --tools]' builds the manifest in-process (build_tools + discover_skills + build_manifest), discover_view, prints JSON. No daemon/session. Un-attenuated.

Live dogfood: 'mu capabilities discover "search file contents" --limit 3' => tool.grep first, WITH effects{filesystem:read} (.6.6 ToolPolicy projection), allowed_by_session:true (.6.6), source:mu-tools (.6.8 provenance) — all four phase-3 beads visible in one output.

Tests: protocol roundtrip + method const, CapabilityView wire roundtrip; full 'cargo test --workspace' green; fmt+clippy clean.

Deferred: walk/help RPC variants (discover is the core; bead said find/walk/help — walk/help can follow); daemon-RPC integration test (pieces unit-tested + CLI dogfooded). semantic ranking in CLI (uses lexical floor; embedder is opt-in).

title: t4c: capability effect/safety annotations + installed-vs-allowed (session-constraint filter)
description: Cold-agent session c9ecd980 (its OWN words, unprompted) named this 'the biggest practical gap': the tool surface should encode effects so the model can ask read-only? writes-repo? writes-outside? network? spends API $? spawns daemons? mutates VCS? Add an effects/policy block to t4c Capability (e.g. {filesystem:read|write, vcs, network, spend, cpu}) + a session-constraint filter so list/find can mark available:true but allowed_by_session:false with a reason (e.g. user said no builds/tests). mu already has ToolPolicy on ToolSpec -> project it. Separates 'installed' from 'appropriate'.
close_reason: done

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Implemented (mu-kex4.6.6), green. Two halves landed:

PART 1 — effects on the t4c leaf (crates/t4c/src/capability.rs):
- New `Effects` { filesystem: FsEffect(None|Read|Write), vcs, network, spend, process } + `SessionConstraints` { no_writes/no_vcs/no_network/no_spend/no_process }.
- `Capability.effects: Option<Effects>` — None = UNANNOTATED (deliberately distinct from a known-benign Effects::default(); avoids the mu-d33g confident-wrong trap). All 11 t4c construction sites updated; effects roundtrips through TomlCap so the CLI's durable config persists annotations.
- `Effects::disallowed_by(&SessionConstraints) -> Option<String>` returns a human reason when an effect violates a constraint.

PART 2 — mu bridge (crates/mu-core/src/t4c_source.rs):
- `effects_from_policy(&ToolPolicy) -> Effects`: projects mu's single SideEffects axis (lossy, documented) — ReadOnly->fs:Read, Mutating/Destructive->fs:Write, External->network; required_aws_capability=>network+spend. Tools get Some(effects); skills get None (no policy = unannotated, not falsely-benign).
- `CapabilityView` gains effects + allowed_by_session + disallowed_reason (skip_serializing_if for clean wire shape).
- `discover_view_constrained(tree, intent, limit, &SessionConstraints)`: a disallowed cap is still RETURNED (it ranked — model should see it) but marked allowed_by_session:false + reason. Separates 'installed' from 'appropriate' per cold-agent c9ecd980 feedback. discover_view = the unconstrained delegate.

Tests: t4c 44 (+6), mu-core 407 (+2: policy_projects_side_effects, read_only_session_marks_a_writer_disallowed). fmt + clippy clean.

NOT done (deliberately deferred): annotating the curated CLI catalog (catalog.rs) with real effects — mechanical follow-up; left effects:None there. cpu/compute axis folded into 'process' (spawn-daemon) as the concrete actionable signal.

title: t4c catalog: mandatory/safe-default invoke flags per capability (--color=never, fd --one-file-system)
description: tcovert + cold-agent session c9ecd980 both flagged: capabilities should bake mandatory/safe-default flags into invoke so agents don't get broken output. eza/exa/ls with --color=always emit ANSI that corrupts agent parsing (orchestrator hit this live: an ls color alias mangled a path capture); fd/find want --one-file-system on the 12TB-NFS host (t4c-notes data point 2). Per-capability invoke prefix of mandatory flags; advisory display-flags separate. Relates mu-269g (profiles: safe-defaults are profile-scoped).
close_reason: done

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Implemented (mu-kex4.6.7), green + dogfooded live.

Design: mandatory safe-default flags are PER-IMPL on a new chain::Impl{cmd, mandatory_flags}, baked into the resolved Capability.invoke so 't4c run <slot>' always applies them. Kept on the chain/Impl layer (not a new Capability field) to avoid re-churning the 11 Capability construction sites; help/install-probe still use the bare cmd.

Backward-compat: Impl deserializes via untagged ImplRepr from EITHER a bare string ('rg') or a table ({cmd='fd', flags=['--one-file-system']}); flagless impls serialize back to bare strings. Existing impls=['rg','grep'] configs parse unchanged.

Catalog flags applied (per-impl, terrain-correct):
- bash.find-files: fd --one-file-system ; BSD find -x  (the 12TB-NFS confinement, t4c-notes pt2)
- bash.ls: eza/exa --color=never ; BSD ls NONE (ls errors on --color; defaults no color) <- the per-impl divergence the field exists for
- bash.search (rg/grep), bash.compress: no flags (rg/grep auto-disable color off-tty)

Live dogfood: 't4c run bash.find-files -- --help' => 'running fd --one-file-system --help'. Confirmed baked.

Tests: t4c 47 (+3: mixed bare/table parse, flags-baked-into-invoke, toml compact roundtrip). fmt+clippy clean. mu-core unaffected.

NOT done (deferred): advisory/display-flags-separate field (bead mentioned it; mandatory was the incident-backed core) -> note for follow-up. Curated flat-cap effect annotations remain .6.6-deferred.

title: t4c sources expansion: MCP-loaded state + project-local (justfile/Cargo/AGENTS) + per-capability provenance
description: Cold-agent session c9ecd980 wants the manifest to merge MORE sources + tag provenance. (1) MCP: distinguish 'code-index installed' from 'code-index LOADED as MCP this session' (live mcp.* source). (2) project-local: read justfile -> project.just.*, plus Cargo/.beads/AGENTS/CLAUDE as capability/context sources. (3) per-capability 'source:' field (harness schema|PATH|curated catalog|justfile|AGENTS|memory|MCP-live) so 'live MCP says loaded' outranks 'memory says installed last week'. Extends the RegistrySource trait model (already multi-source).

comments.text:
Provenance slice DONE (green). Implemented per-capability provenance as a TREE-LEVEL fact, not a Capability field (avoids re-churning 11 constructors a 3rd time, and provenance is genuinely 'where the cap entered the tree'):
- registry.rs: build() records path->source.name() in a parallel map; Tree::source_of(&CapPath)->Option<&str>. Because later sources win collisions, recorded provenance is always the authoritative one.
- The bead's 'live MCP outranks memory' RANKING is already solved by add_source order (later=higher authority wins the path); provenance just makes the WINNER visible.
- mu bridge: CapabilityView.source surfaces it on the wire (skip_serializing_if).
- t4c 48(+1 provenance_records_the_winning_source), mu-core 407 (discover_view test asserts source). fmt+clippy clean.

The other two parts of this bead are split to their own beads (new RegistrySources, each tagged by the provenance plumbing just landed):
- MCP-live source -> (new bead above)
- project-local (justfile/Cargo/AGENTS/CLAUDE) -> (new bead above)
Deferred here: CLI find/list provenance display (the mu-bridge view has it; CLI is a separate rendering surface).
