For agent-managed paths, a small daemon-side observer subscribes to `config_proposed` events and emits `config_approved` (or `config_rejected`). v1 can hardcode trivial policies (e.g., "always approve"); future iterations plug in real safety + capability checks. Same pattern as mu-kgu.3's compaction-judge — narrow trait surface, one job.

## Composition (revised)

mu-w4o (biscuit-auth): cross-daemon config sync = sign config-event-stream + transfer + verify + replay.
mu-fb0 (live agent loop, closed): rope substrate the config events flow through. Config projection is one more projection alongside transcript + capability views.
mu-mh4 (session persistence read-side): the same event-log persistence that survives daemon restart for sessions ALSO survives for config events. "Resume from where I left off" includes config state.
mu-pex (metrics framework, P2 ready): config_set events become natural metric source ("how often does the operator change X? how does mu adapt the judge ranking over time?").

## Provenance

Three architectural unifications added 2026-05-14 across one conversation:

1. **Properties → capability semantics** (immutable / operator-runtime / agent-managed / per-session-override = read/write/delegate scopes).
2. **Config = capability axis** (intersect() bounds config; biscuit-token serialization gives cross-daemon).
3. **Config changes = events** (same event log; projection-based current-state; audit/replay/observer all free).

Result: mu's config system is not a separate subsystem. It's another expression of the rope + capability + event substrate already in mu-core. The README story tightens: "one substrate, many projections."
close_reason: v1 foundation landed: Config struct + layered TOML resolution + three demonstrator consumers ([compaction.judge] data-only, [session].state_dir resolves events_dir, [ui.tui].default_provider/model in mu-tui). Capability-axis and event-sourcing extensions deferred to follow-up beads.

title: Bash tool: Box::leak in fmt_allowlist deduplication leaks String per rejected command
description:
## Bug

In crates/mu-coding/src/tools/bash.rs, the allowlist-formatting helper uses:

  if seen.insert(Box::leak(joined.clone().into_boxed_str())) { ... }

Intentionally leaks one String per de-duplication call. The set is rebuilt on every refused-command error message. For a long-lived mu serve with many rejected commands, this is a slow memory leak.

## Fix

Use HashSet<String> and seen.contains(&joined) / seen.insert(joined) instead of seen.insert(Box::leak(...)). Readability win too.

## Source

opus-4-7 review session 3262c036eaca7daa event 194, 'What needs work' item 4.

title: mu-core/protocol: distinguish 'paused between turns' from 'session truly closed' — terminal-state ambiguity
description:
## Problem

`SessionStatus::Done` (and the daemon's session phase `done`) is currently overloaded with two distinct meanings:

1. **The current ask completed** — `end_turn` fired, no streaming in flight, session is waiting for the next user_message. Sending another `ask_session` is the normal way to continue the conversation.
2. **The session is finished forever** — the operator explicitly closed it (e.g. via `close_session`), the provider hit a permanent error, the daemon evicted it, etc. Sending another `ask_session` either fails or silently no-ops.

These are the same wire-level state but have completely different semantics. From the TUI's perspective, a session you sent one prompt to 30 seconds ago looks identical to a session you sent one prompt to a month ago and never came back to.

## Provenance

Surfaced 2026-05-19 during mu-o1y7 phase 3a smoke test. Operator tried `i Hello?` Enter on a faux session that was in `done` state, got no response. Phase 3a added a 'send blocked' marker that fired on any Done session — which the operator correctly identified as wrong, because it would block legitimate session-resume.

Operator analogy (Monty Python S3E37 'Gestures to Indicate Pauses in a Televised Talk'): Mr. Orbiter explains he needs distinct gestures to indicate pause-while-still-speaking vs. fully-finished. mu has only one gesture for both.

## Proposal

### Wire-level

Add a separate terminal state to the session phase enum (or a flag on the row). Candidates:
- `closed` — explicitly retired; `ask_session` returns an error
- `abandoned` — daemon evicted (probably automatic after long idle)
- `crashed` / `errored` — terminal error from the provider; partial transcript preserved

Or one bit: `is_terminal: bool` alongside the existing phase. Simpler, less expressive.

### RPC

`session.close { session_id, reason? }` — moves a session into the terminal state. Composes with mu-770 (session lifecycle epic).

### TUI

Display the terminal-state sessions distinctly (e.g. grayed out in F3 picker; phase glyph changes). Re-add a 'send blocked' marker for those specific cases.

## Composes with

- **mu-770** — session.archive / session.restore / session.list_archived; archive ≈ 'persist but hide.' The terminal-state distinction is an underlying primitive for that.
- **mu-w73n** — session.delete_persistent (mu-u1ld follow-up); only makes sense if you can identify which sessions are terminal.

## Out of scope

- Auto-detect that a session has been idle 'too long' and mark it terminal. Initial pass should be operator-driven only.
- UI for the close action — defer to mu-770.

## Acceptance (rough — refine in implementation)

- Protocol type carries a distinct terminal indicator
- `session.close` RPC exists
- TUI renders terminal-state sessions visually distinct
- `ask_session` against a terminal session returns a typed error
- mu-o1y7's TUI can re-add a 'send blocked' marker, scoped to actual terminal sessions
notes:
## 2026-05-19 design refinement (operator)

The 'distinct terminal state' framing in the original description may be over-engineered. Simpler primitive: **the Done event already has a timestamp; the elapsed time between Done and the next ask IS the signal**.

- Done emitted at t0
- Next \`ask_session\` arrives at t1
- t1 - t0 = the operator's natural classifier:
  - Seconds → continuing a turn (the assistant just finished talking)
  - Minutes → continuing the conversation
  - Hours / next day → coming back after a break
  - Weeks / months → reactivating an old session

No new terminal-state flag needed at the protocol level. The data is already there in the event log. What's missing is **surfacing it in the UI**:

### TUI affordances

- In F2 / F3 session listings, replace 'done' with 'last active 3 days ago' / 'last active 2m ago' — a human-readable elapsed time. Glyph stays the same; the secondary info changes.
- On F3 entry to a Done session where t_since_done > some threshold (1 hour? 1 day?), surface a one-line marker in scrollback: \`─── resuming session — last active 3 days ago ───\` so the operator has context for the continuity gap.
- F3 picker tooltip / sub-line: show last-active so the operator can pick by recency.

### Doesn't replace mu-770 (session lifecycle)

This time-based approach handles 'paused vs. resumed' continuum cleanly. But there are still cases where a session is genuinely terminal:
- Operator explicitly chose to retire it (\`session.close\`)
- Daemon hit a permanent provider error
- Compaction policy decided the session is over

Those want mu-770's \`archive\` / \`close\` RPCs + a real terminal indicator. So this bead now covers: **surface elapsed-time-since-Done as a first-class affordance**. The terminal-state distinction is mu-770's job.

### Acceptance (refined)

- F2/F3 listings show 'last active <relative time>' instead of just the 'done' glyph (for done sessions)
- F3 entry to long-stale Done session emits a context marker in scrollback
- Picker sub-line surfaces last-active for ranking by recency
- Timestamp is already in the event log — just plumb it through

## Provenance

2026-05-19 conversation: 'if the Done has a timestamp at time of creation, just reading the next message will give an indication if it was just part of a conversation, picked back up after a weekend, or a year.' Cleaner than the original 'add a separate terminal state' framing — uses existing data instead of inventing new state.

title: TUI: consume OperatorView rendering in mu-pex / mu-jb3
description:
Follow-on to mu-ovl (closed by xszxyzyy 8920297c). The `FauxProviderRenderer` now produces distinct OperatorView output per SpanKind (`crates/mu-core/src/context/renderer.rs:221+`). The TUI surfaces in `mu-pex` (system_prompt indicator) and `mu-jb3` (transcript pane) currently render the agent-side message stream directly. They should source OperatorView output from a `ProviderRenderer::render(rope, OperatorView)` call instead.

## Scope

IN:
- Identify the rendering site(s) in `mu-pex` / `mu-jb3` (and any siblings) that currently show transcript content.
- Route them through `FauxProviderRenderer` (or whichever production renderer is in scope by then — mu-bn4 / mu-3aa land later) with `ProjectionTarget::OperatorView`.
- For tool-call / tool-result / skill-activation / memory-injection / compaction / file-load spans, surface the OperatorView badge / summary / collapsible inline.
- Drill-down: clicking / focusing a collapsed projection should reveal the underlying span content (AgentView) in a side pane or modal — this is the 'operator can see what the agent actually saw' affordance from spec line 643.
- Tests: snapshot-style assertions that the TUI's rendered string for a known rope matches OperatorView output, not AgentView.

OUT:
- New OperatorView projection shapes (those are mu-dt2 scope — already landed).
- Cache-strategy concerns (mu-ktq orthogonal trait).
- Production renderer adoption in the live agent loop (mu-fb0).

## Acceptance

- TUI pane(s) that previously echoed AgentView content now display the OperatorView projection for SpanKind variants where the two differ.
- Original conversational variants (System / User / Assistant) still render verbatim.
- A drill-down affordance exists (even if minimal) to surface the AgentView content for a collapsed span.
- `cargo test --workspace` green at the closing commit.

## References

- Closed parent: mu-ovl (Agent-view vs operator-view projections from the rope)
- Sibling: mu-dt2 (closed) — projection rules used here
- TUI beads: mu-pex (system_prompt indicator), mu-jb3 (transcript pane), mu-62s, mu-n48
- Spec: \`specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md:614-644\` (agent-view vs operator-view projections); line 643 (drill-down)
- Renderer site: \`crates/mu-core/src/context/renderer.rs:221+\` (FauxProviderRenderer::render_content)

## Notes

mu-ovl's bead body explicitly deferred TUI integration to a follow-on; this is that follow-on. It is independent of mu-fb0 (live-loop adoption) — that path threads the rope through the agent loop's prompt builder; this path threads it through the TUI's display layer.

title: mu-phl v0 Phase E — thread cwd through mu CLI entry points (mu ask)
description:
Step 6 of mu-phl v0. Threads the operator's invocation cwd through to the daemon so recall fires for the right project.

## What

`crates/mu-coding/src/bin/mu.rs` ask path: set `cwd: Some(std::env::current_dir()?)` on the `CreateSessionRequest`. (Similar for any other client entry points that build session-creation requests.)

## Why

Without this, the daemon falls back to its own process cwd, which is wherever `mu serve` was launched from — usually not where the operator is running `mu ask`. Memory recall would key off the wrong project.

## Acceptance

- `mu ask` from any directory threads cwd correctly.
- Daemon log / `ContextAssembly` event for the assembled rope shows recall keyed to the invocation cwd.
- One commit with `## Files` block and `(closes <this-bead>)`.

## Blocked by

- mu-phl v0 Phase D (step 5) — uses the new `cwd: Option<PathBuf>` field on `CreateSessionRequest`.
close_reason: Landed (Phase E) on feat/mu-phl-v0. ask.rs threads std::env::current_dir().ok() through create_session helper to CreateSessionRequest.cwd. 16/3 line change in one file. 769 tests pass; clippy clean. Daemon-side recall now scopes to the operator's actual cwd, not the daemon's process cwd. Phase F (smoke test) is unblocked.

title: sprint-start placed mu-y5hz worker on stale base (#203) while main was #206
description: During the 2026-06-06 overnight goal run, sprint-start mu-y5hz created a jj workspace parented on 58750c5a (Merge PR #203), but origin/main was 1f365ec5 (Merge PR #206). The worker implemented+tested against the stale 489-line cc_data.rs (which lacked the lqqt.2 detail reader, lqqt.3 marks, #204 ai-review panel). Caught only because the first ai-review showed a polluted reverse-diff of #205/#206 as deletions; worker reset via 'jj new main' and redid the work against the real 840-line file. Risk: a worker that didn't cross-check the reviewer's diff description could push a destructive reverse-diff. Investigate whether sprint-start resolves a stale local 'main' bookmark vs the canonical beads DB (cf. memory c295ca3e re: jj-workspace divergent local DB). Likely fix: sprint-start should jj-fetch/rebase the bead workspace onto the freshest main, or fail loudly if local main lags origin/main.

title: Implement mu-037 phase 1: single-daemon peer.hello + mailbox
description:
Spec at specs/mu-037-peer-discovery-mailbox.md (jj ozyprmqn cc380197). Phase 1 only — single-daemon scope.

Two delegate sessions in the same daemon can exchange messages without going through the parent. Wire surface:
- peer.hello / peer.reply with capability advertisement + negotiation
- mailbox.post / mailbox.list / mailbox.consume
- New MailboxMessage event-log entries

Reuses mu-033 Capability entirely — peer handles ARE attenuated capabilities. No new authorization machinery.

Estimated ~1 week. Phase 2 (cross-daemon same-machine via unix socket + filesystem registry) is a follow-up issue.
close_reason: Phase 1 landed via PR #43. Wire surface (peer.hello + mailbox.post/list/consume + session.mailbox_message notification), MailboxMessagePosted/Consumed event-log variants, per-session MailboxState + PeerHandle registry, 6 unit + 3 integration tests. Opaque-token peer handles (intentional divergence from Capability-axis framing). PeerDiscovery trait+impl deferred to follow-up bead. Authorization is handle-required even in same-daemon (no trust-local shortcut).

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Architecture sketch for Phase 1 (2026-05-14, claude-personal session).

## Locked

- **Peer handle = opaque token, not a `Capability`-axis extension.** v1 carries `{peer_session_id, allowed_methods: HashSet<String>, expires_at_unix_ms, max_calls_remaining}`. The spec's "peer handles ARE attenuated capabilities" framing is right at the substrate level but conflates two surfaces: existing `Capability` bounds tool/aws/autonomy invocation; peer handles bound RPC-method invocation against another session. Cleaner to ship the primitive first, unify in Phase 2 when cross-daemon authz makes the distinction load-bearing.
- **`peer.reply` is the response shape for `peer.hello`, not a second RPC method.** Single request/response, no challenge round-trip loop in Phase 1.

## Wire surface (mu-core/src/protocol.rs additions)

- `peer.hello`: `PeerHelloRequest{from, want} → PeerHelloResponse::{Accepted{peer_handle, allowed_methods, expires_at_unix_ms}, Denied{reason}}`
- `mailbox.post`: `{to_session_id, from, kind, subject, body: Value, expires_at_unix_ms?} → {posted, seq}`
- `mailbox.list`: `{session_id, since_seq?, include_consumed} → {messages: Vec<MailboxMessageView>}`
- `mailbox.consume`: `{session_id, seqs: Vec<u64>} → {consumed_count}`

## State model

**Event-sourced.** Two new EventPayload variants in mu-core/src/event_log.rs:
- `MailboxMessagePosted{seq, from_daemon_id, from_session_id, kind, subject, body, expires_at_unix_ms}` — append-only post record
- `MailboxMessageConsumed{seq}` — append-only consumption mark

`mailbox.list` is a projection: scan event log, return posted-minus-consumed. Same pattern as session-status derivation in serve/discovery/mod.rs. No state duplication.

**Per-session registry additions** in serve/sessions.rs::SessionState:
- `mailbox_next_seq: Arc<AtomicU64>` — monotonic seq generator (write coordination)
- `peer_handles_issued: Arc<Mutex<HashMap<String, PeerHandle>>>` — handles this session has handed out, keyed by token

## Dispatch wiring (serve/dispatch.rs, 4 new arms)

- `peer.hello`: target session policy (v1 hardcoded: accept same-daemon peers with `allowed_methods = {mailbox.post}`); issue token; return Accepted.
- `mailbox.post`: append `MailboxMessagePosted` with next seq.
- `mailbox.list`: scan target's event log; project posts minus consumed.
- `mailbox.consume`: append `MailboxMessageConsumed` events.

## Open (my leans, not locked)

- **Single-daemon trust.** Phase 1 trusts the local daemon — `mailbox.post`/`.list`/`.consume` don't require a peer handle for in-process callers. `peer.hello` is mostly ceremonial in this phase, exercised only by the demonstrator test. Real teeth in Phase 2 (cross-daemon).
- **Wire notification on post.** Lean: emit `session.mailbox_message` notification from the forwarder when `MailboxMessagePosted` lands. ~10 LOC; lets Phase 4 TUI subscribe without a follow-up wire spec.

## Acceptance demonstrator

Two delegate sessions A and B in the same daemon: A → `mailbox.post` to B; B → `mailbox.list` sees the message; B → `mailbox.consume`; B re-lists with `include_consumed=false` → message gone.

## Composition

- Builds on mu-031 (delegate sessions provide the two-session-in-one-daemon scenario)
- Builds on mu-iwq Phase D's event-log + tracker patterns (sessions registry mutations)
- Does NOT consume mu-033 Capability for peer handles in v1 — see "Locked" #1
- Phase 4 (F9 mailbox view) unblocked by the wire notification lean above

comments.author: tcovert

title: mu-clip-tui-deadcode: address 3 dead_code lints in mu-tui (pre-existing, surfaced by CI -D warnings)
description:
Three dead_code warnings in mu-tui that block CI under -D warnings. All look like placeholders for in-progress TUI work; per-item decision needed (suppress with #[allow] + reason comment, delete, or wire up).

## Items

1. **`ProviderStatusSnapshot.bytes_received`** field (`crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs:140-143`) — derived Clone/Debug but field is never read. Probably for the metrics framework (mu-pex). Decision: likely `#[allow(dead_code)]` with a comment pointing to mu-pex.
2. **`MuClient.child`** field (`crates/mu-tui/src/mu_client.rs:55`) — child process handle held for Drop semantics (subprocess kill on owner-drop). Decision: likely `#[allow(dead_code)]` with a Drop-discipline comment; the field IS used (held for RAII), clippy just can't see it.
3. **`MuClient::ping` and `MuClient::close`** methods (`crates/mu-tui/src/mu_client.rs:289-295`) — placeholder for protocol features. Decision: likely `#[allow(dead_code)]` with a TODO pointing to the relevant feature bead, OR delete if truly not coming.

## Acceptance

- All 3 dead_code warnings in mu-tui addressed (suppressed with comment OR deleted OR wired up)
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` advances past mu-tui
- cargo test --workspace green
- One commit closes this bead
close_reason: Landed in jj revision omulvzmu. 3 #[allow(dead_code)] annotations with rationale: bytes_received (mu-pex prep), child (RAII handle for close), ping+close (planned TUI exit-path wire-up). 361 tests green; warnings 10 → 5.

title: agent loop: SwitchProvider buffered during a stream is silently dropped
description: Found during mu-wf5w root-cause work (2026-06-05). handle_invoke_llm buffers AgentInput::SwitchProvider arriving mid-stream (invoke.rs ~line 145) into 'buffered'; plan_post_invoke_llm wraps ALL buffered inputs as Action::External; but the main loop's Action::External(AgentInput::SwitchProvider) arm is an explicit no-op ('Already handled in try_recv/recv above; should not reach the action queue' — mod.rs ~897). Net: a provider switch requested while a provider call is streaming is silently discarded — no event, no error, next ask runs on the old provider. Adversarial verifier on the wf5w fix confirmed this is pre-existing and unchanged by that fix. Fix sketch: either handle External(SwitchProvider) in the arm (apply switch + emit ProviderSwitched, reusing the inline drain logic), or have plan_post_invoke_llm route SwitchProvider specially. Note MaybeFinish's drain DOES handle SwitchProvider inline correctly — only the planner-wrapped path drops it.

title: codex provider: message output-item boundaries discarded — multi-item responses fuse into one jammed text block
description:
SYMPTOM (operator capture from the jg9p.1 mu-solo session, 2026-06-05): assistant message rendered with its opening duplicated mid-message, jammed without whitespace ('...if you want, or hold.No worries. I'll hold on the AI review...'). Preceded by two turns showing 'x provider: codex returned 503 Service Unavailable ... (turn ended, no output)'.

EVIDENCE — THE DURABLE EVENT IS CORRUPTED (renderer exonerated): daemon 2f270bcba43f305d session-1, assistant_message_event id 2515 contains 'No worries' TWICE within the single event. Unlike mu-solo-scrollback-dup-recommit-8hva (renderer-only, events clean), here the SOURCE OF TRUTH carries the duplication.

STRUCTURE OF THE CORRUPT MESSAGE: [Response A: 'No worries. I noticed the PR bookmark didn't move ... finalize the jj change/push if you want, or hold.'] immediately followed by [Response B: 'No worries. I'll hold on the AI review until you point me at the right script/env. Current state: #184 exists...'] — two independently-generated responses to the same prompt, mechanically concatenated (no whitespace at the seam).

MECHANISM HYPOTHESIS: attempt A streamed partial deltas then died (503 mid-stream); a retry (loop-level — the codex provider itself retries only on 401, openai_codex.rs:12,1174) regenerated from scratch; the text/delta assembly buffer was NOT cleared between attempts, so the final committed message = A-partial + B-full. Trace where the loop (crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/) or forwarder accumulates streamed text and what happens to that buffer when a stream errors and the call is retried.

SEVERITY NOTE: P1 because the event log is mu's source of truth — downstream consumers (rehydration, replay, analytics, compaction judges, the web console) all inherit the corruption.

RELATED: mu-wf5w (turn lost — same turn-lifecycle machinery, loss direction; the two '(turn ended, no output)' error turns in this capture may BE wf5w manifestations), mu-solo-scrollback-dup-recommit-8hva (renderer-side duplication, fixed separately — diagnosis discipline identical: event log decides which layer).
close_reason: done

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: RE-DIAGNOSIS (2026-06-05, terrain-checked): the retained-buffer-across-retry mechanism is REFUTED. (1) Both 503s were 'reset before headers' — the SSE stream never opened on the failed attempts; zero tokens existed to retain. (2) events_stream state (accumulated_text) is constructed fresh per stream() call (openai_codex.rs ~770); no cross-call state. (3) The agent loop has NO retry — handle_invoke_llm returns Err(Outcome::Error) and the ask ends (done id 2502/2509 stop=error). (4) The two fused takes answer DIFFERENT messages: take 1 responds to u2497 'l' / u2504 'sorry wrong window', take 2 to u2511 'finding the script you need' — three UMs accumulated unanswered in history because the two errored asks had already pushed theirs. REVISED MECHANISM: the third (successful) response very likely contained TWO message output items (model answering the backlog as two messages — natural for the Responses API); the adapter flattens ALL output_text.delta frames into one undelimited accumulator — OutputItemAdded/Done only handle FunctionCall; message-item boundaries are ignored (assemble_content emits one jammed Text block; the whitespace-free seam '...or hold.No worries.' is the flatten signature). FIX SHAPE: track message-item boundaries per output_index and emit them delimited (separate Text blocks, or join with \n\n); unit-testable against an SSE fixture with two message items in openai_codex tests — no live repro needed. Title's 'retry/leak' framing is stale; scope is now: codex adapter loses output-item boundaries.

title: mu-tui: status-bar glyph legend (sys: indicator and similar)
description:
## Problem

The `sys:` glyph in the top-right of the status bar (✓ when default system
prompt set, · when not — line 1953 of mu-tui/src/main.rs, from mu-62s)
isn't discoverable. User report: "sys: — not sure what it's for."

## Proposal

Surface the meaning. Lightest-touch options:

(a) Press-and-hold key (e.g., `?` over the status bar) to show a legend
    popup explaining each glyph
(b) F1 → "status bar legend" section listing each indicator
(c) Brief hint in `:help` output

A direct contextual expansion would be too noisy in the status bar itself
(e.g., `sys:✓ (default system prompt set; :system_prompt to view)`), but
the legend popup or help entry achieves the same with on-demand access.

## Acceptance

- User can determine what `sys:` (and similar glyphs) mean without
  reading source code
- Discovery path doesn't require knowing the right command in advance

## Cross-refs

- mu-62s (added the indicator)
- Provenance: TUI feedback (mu daemon f3845422 session-2, 2026-05-15)

title: GOAL 2026-05-19: delegate-then-review experiment — multi-line prompt as target for protocol iteration
description:
## Tracking bead for /goal session 2026-05-19 (overnight)

Methodology experiment. The PRIMARY DELIVERABLE is **a working delegation-then-review-then-postmortem loop**, with multi-line-prompt-growing-upward as the example task.

The CODE deliverable is secondary. If the delegation infrastructure produces a usable PR but the methodology doesn't iterate cleanly, the experiment is a partial success. If the methodology lessons are sharp but the PR has bugs, the experiment is still useful.

## Crew

| Role | Backend | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Orchestrator | claude-code (this session) | Sets up, monitors, PR review, postmortem |
| Worker | claude-code via `agent-router --auth claude-oauth` | Implements |
| Reviewer | gpt-5.5-codex via `agent-router --auth codex-oauth --tools read,grep` | Reviews worker's output pre-PR |

(Pot isolation deferred — mu-fpgd / claude-in-pot is still blocked. Working without it tonight.)

## Sub-beads (tagged `goal:2026-05-19:<this-bead-id>`)

1. **Implementation**: multi-line prompt growing upward in F3 inline mode (filed below)
2. **Reviewer dispatch**: gpt-5.5 reads worker's commit + spec, returns approve/iterate verdict (filed below)
3. **Postmortem**: orchestrator reviews PR, writes postmortem on protocol + output, captures protocol-refinement suggestions (filed below)

Each sub-bead has stop criteria + acceptance + budget cap.

## Hypothesis (pre-registered)

- Worker can implement the multi-line prompt within ~$20 budget (~200 LOC at conservative 10:1)
- Reviewer can return a usable verdict within ~$2 budget
- Postmortem will surface 1-3 protocol refinement candidates
- End-to-end wall-clock: ~2-3 hours including the model latency for both worker + reviewer

## Stop criteria (in addition to default seven)

- Worker exceeds budget cap → halt + postmortem fires on partial work
- Reviewer's verdict is 'iterate' but worker hits 2 iteration cycles without convergence → halt + postmortem on the methodology, not the code
- Anything touches files outside crates/mu-tui/src/ → halt (scope creep tripwire)

## Anti-success modes the experiment can detect

- Worker silently produces a HollowCommit (claim mismatches diff) — forensics classifier would catch this; orchestrator verifies via verify-claims gate
- Worker silently produces a NarrativeNoAction (commit but no functional change) — verify-claims + cargo test catches
- Reviewer rubber-stamps without reading — protocol check: reviewer's output must cite file:line; orchestrator audits this

## Post-experiment artifact

`MORNING_2026-05-19.md` at repo root with:
- What landed (PR # + commit list)
- Process timeline (worker dispatch → review → iterations → PR)
- Surprises observed
- Suggested protocol refinements (concrete bullet points; if any, also file as beads against the goal-protocol SKILL.md)
- Cost / turns / wall-clock actuals vs predictions

## Provenance

Operator (2026-05-19, pre-sleep): 'take a task, like the expanding prompt box, and delegate to the work crew, set the goals, stop criteria, success, etc. After gpt reviews the code and says it's ready for PR. You review the PR. Then, you can do a postmortem on the process and output, and think about what you might change to the protocol. And repeat. Science with the system.'

title: Split mu-tui/src/main.rs (4530 LOC) into app/* + render/* modules
description:
## Problem

crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs is 4530 lines. The single App struct has ~35 fields. The App impl handles input, command-palette dispatch, RPC calls, render coordination, AND streaming-text accumulation. Tests at the bottom add ~800 lines. AGENTS.md guidance: '~1000 lines is the split point.'

## Proposed splits (per opus review)

- app/state.rs — App struct + field initializers
- app/input.rs — key handlers grouped by mode (Normal, Picker, ApprovalModal, Editing)
- app/notification.rs — handle_notification + per-event variants (session.provider_status, session.assistant_text_finalized, mailbox.delivered, etc.)
- render/header.rs, render/tabs.rs, render/command_center.rs, render/session_detail.rs, render/usage.rs, render/events.rs, render/modal.rs
- mu_client.rs — already a separate file
- tests move to app/tests/ or src/tests/

Target: <600 LOC per file.

## Source

opus-4-7 review event 194, 'What needs work' item 3. Operator awareness: mu-nk3 already did the analogous split for crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
LOC update 2026-05-19: file now 5253 lines on main (was 4530 when this bead was filed); iter-9 fix lands ~5815 after PR #108 merge.

Iter-9 fix did extract one pure helper (`shift_f8_indices_after_eviction`, ~12 LOC), which is the right shape for the eventual refactor — pure index arithmetic separated from the event loop, trivially testable. But the file keeps growing; nothing else got pulled out today.

Per iter-10 reviewer (and prior iter-8 self-review), candidate extraction points worth tracking when this refactor happens:
- F8 events explorer logic: `render_events_explorer` + its cursor/expand/scroll state machinery now spans ~250 lines inline; would slot cleanly into a `render/events_explorer.rs` module with the App fields it owns moved to a sub-struct.
- F3 transcript scroll anchor + inline-emit logic (older, larger surface)
- F5 usage dashboard
- Each F-key view is a candidate self-contained module.

Pure-helpers worth extracting before the bigger split:
- `compute_cursor_visibility_scroll(focused_visual_start, scroll_y, max_top, inner_height) -> Option<u16>` — would enable a unit test for the KEY_ISSUE-1-style "fires-only-on-cursor-moved" gating that iter-9 deferred. ~20 LOC + ~3 tests.
- The `preview_truncate(s, char_count) -> String` char-boundary slicer (~5 LOC) — would let the UTF-8 panic edge case have a direct test instead of being implicit in the render fn.

Cross-refs:
- PR #108 (mu-2ft part 1, merged 2026-05-19, merge sha edcfd53589e0): iter-7 worker + iter-9 orchestrator fix + iter-10 external review
- Sibling bead mu-hgrj: same refactor pattern for mu-coding/src/serve/forwarder.rs (1261 LOC)

title: mu-cap-aws follow-on: intersect_aws_sets HashMap O(n+m) + set-level INV-1 regression test
description:
Two small follow-on hardenings to mu-f5o (commit 773f0f64):

1. **`intersect_aws_sets` O(n+m) optimization** — pre-index the right-hand set by name into a HashMap for O(1) lookup instead of the current O(n*m) nested-loop scan. Practical N is small but the cleaner algorithm is easy and obvious (operator preference: sound-engineering > waiting-for-it-to-be-a-problem).

2. **Set-level INV-1 regression test for both-Some-Some drop path** — assert that when same-name caps have both-Some session policies (deferred to None per AwsCapability::intersect), the cap is dropped from intersect_aws_sets' output AND the set-level inclusion property holds (result ⊆ a AND result ⊆ b by name). Peace-of-mind: catches a future regression that might "fall back to keeping the original cap" on None.

## Scope

IN:
- Replace the nested loop in `intersect_aws_sets` (`crates/mu-core/src/capability.rs:181-195`) with HashMap-indexed lookup.
- Add one test `intersect_aws_sets_preserves_inv1_even_when_some_pairs_drop` adjacent to the existing `intersect_aws_sets_drops_unmatched_names` test.

OUT: anything else — no spec change (algorithm change doesn't alter the documented semantics; INV-1 property already documented).

## Acceptance

- intersect_aws_sets uses HashMap<&str, &AwsCapability> for O(1) name lookup
- Existing tests (intersect_aws_sets_drops_unmatched_names, intersect_aws_sets_is_narrowing_only_property) still pass
- New test asserts: set with both-Some-Some pair drops that name, output names ⊆ a.names AND ⊆ b.names
- cargo test --workspace green
- One commit closes this bead
close_reason: Landed in jj revision lnytzpuu. intersect_aws_sets pre-indexes the right-hand HashSet by name → O(n+m). New regression test intersect_aws_sets_preserves_inv1_even_when_some_pairs_drop asserts set-level INV-1 holds across the both-Some-Some drop path. cargo test --workspace green at 361 tests.

title: mu-solo: fullscreen transcript mode with Codex/cc-like parts (event-log projections)
description: Fullscreen transcript mode for mu-solo with the parts other agents have (Codex/cc), built as PROJECTIONS of the first-class event log (design: agent memory 0329a8f1; inline insights: 3407c55c). Sub-beads render via render_transcript_lines over transcript_events_by_sid into the F3 SessionDetail / ViewportMode::Fullscreen surface, and MUST stay OFF viewport.rs inline commit/scroll-region core (the part that broke mu-solo last spike). Worklist: paging, dump-on-flip, live->transcript toggle, expand-in-fullscreen, plus mu-wf5w foundation bug. Goal complete = all sub-beads closed AND just ci-aipr green. Verify via mu-vt-probe grid assertions.

title: ContextAssembly v2: per-segment source-map
description:
Per specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md. ContextAssembly v1 records counts + provider info; v2 records the full source-map: for each message in the assembled prompt, which source event(s) it came from and what range.

Enables 'why was this in context' (the F4 Context Inspector view from mu-ui-mockups-2026-05-10.md).

Bigger lift — needs schema work + agent loop participation to actually track the source-event mapping during assembly. Write when the inspector UI work creates real demand.

title: mu-file-watch: implement rope source-change detection via kqueue/inotify (watchman as candidate impl)
description:
The spec amendment at `specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md` (section 'Span source-change detection via OS file watches', lines 681+) describes the design: spans that reference file content (`FileLoad` SpanKind, landed in mu-1n3) should be invalidated/rehydrated when the underlying file changes. No implementation bead existed; this fills the gap.

Operator preference (2026-05-14): use libevent/kqueue/epoll directly for the primitive. If we later need cross-platform with reliability beyond what direct kqueue gives, **watchman** (Facebook's, used by jj) is the candidate higher-level abstraction.

## Scope

IN:
- Per-FileLoad-span watch registration when the span enters the rope
- OS-native watcher: kqueue on FreeBSD/macOS, inotify on Linux, ReadDirectoryChangesW on Windows
- Watcher emits change events; rope handles via the policy/signal separation already specced (lines 701-707)
- Optional: watchman backend behind a feature flag for environments where the OS-native path has issues

OUT:
- Eager rehydration (re-read every changed file immediately). The signal/policy separation says: watcher emits 'changed' signal; policy decides whether to rehydrate (could be immediate, debounced, or lazy-on-next-projection).
- Watching directories or globs. v1 watches specific files referenced by FileLoad spans.
- Cross-machine file sync (NFS quirks etc.).

## Acceptance

- New FileLoad span registers a watcher on its source path
- File modification fires a 'changed' signal on the rope
- Test: write a file, FileLoad into rope, modify the file, observe the rope emits the change signal
- Policy stub (initially: log + mark stale; full rehydration in a follow-on bead)
- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings green

## Open design questions

1. **Watcher lifetime**: one global watcher per session, or per-rope, or per-span? Lean: per-session, multiplexing all file watches; cheaper than per-span.
2. **Watchman vs direct kqueue**: start direct; switch to watchman only if scale problems surface (watchman has its own state, runs as a daemon; direct kqueue has zero infrastructure cost).
3. **Cross-platform abstraction**: probably the `notify` crate from crates.io (well-maintained, abstracts kqueue/inotify/Windows). watchman as opt-in fallback.

## References

- Spec: `specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md` lines 681+ ('Span source-change detection via OS file watches')
- mu-1n3 / mu-ovl: FileLoad SpanKind variant (landed)
- watchman: https://facebook.github.io/watchman/
- notify crate: https://crates.io/crates/notify
- Operator note 2026-05-14: 'I'm fine with libevent/kqueue/epoll for monitoring the files; if we find we have more to use, watchman is a tool that seems to work quite well for jj.'

title: AuthState mutex poisoning produces lying Accepted response
description: On poisoned Arc<Mutex<AuthState>> lock in outcome_to_response (dispatch.rs:1438-1451), AuthExchangeResponse::Accepted is returned while AuthState stays Unauthenticated. Liar response. Harmless under mu-yox (no protected RPC consumes AuthState yet); becomes operationally ugly under mu-fnn enforcement. Fix: switch Arc<Mutex<>> to parking_lot::Mutex (non-poisoning) or treat PoisonError as auth-failure. Surfaced by mu-yox implementer envelope notes; confirmed by codex review (minor M2). Provenance: codex-review-mu-yox-output.txt.
close_reason: Merged in PR #67 — handles poisoned-lock case in outcome_to_response; returns Denied{MalformedExchange} instead of lying Accepted.

title: console: prefill mark form note with current note on re-mark
description: Operator-reported 2026-06-06: re-marking to change a rating clears the note — the form's note input is empty, so the new (complete) OperatorMark event carries note=None and the header's latest-wins projection loses the text. Fix: prefill the note input's value with the current mark's note (rating select already preselects). Keeps every event complete — no partial-edit semantics; the old note still rides in history. CLI unaffected (explicit args).
close_reason: PR #201 merged 2026-06-06: note prefill via esc_attr'd value attribute, as_ref() fix for double-consume. Director-reviewed, bot-approved over disputed gpt-oss REJECT (false-premise non-Copy claim). Panel bead mu-ai-review-panel-lrwq absorbs the failure mode.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: ci-aipr REJECT (gpt-oss:20b) — verdict disputed: blocking claim is a hypothetical non-Copy compile failure on a u8 field, directly contradicted by the green just-ci compile in the same recipe run; secondary 'selected unused' claim false (drives option preselection). Escalated to operator per gate convention rather than self-overriding. Corpus note: procedurally the gate worked (blocked + escalated); substantively the verdict is wrong on both findings — a data point for the reviewer-model question (gpt-oss review quality vs qwen3-coder default).

title: mailbox.read RPC: split full body retrieval from mailbox.list
description: mailbox.list should return metadata/subject only by default. New mailbox.read returns full body on demand. Prevents context stuffing and prompt injection from peer mail.

Returns full MailboxMessageView for a single seq, null for nonexistent. Smoke test extended with read + nonexistent-seq cases. 843 workspace tests pass.

title: MCP mailbox adapter: bridge MCP tools over mu native mailbox RPCs
description: Expose mu mailbox as MCP tools (mu_peer_hello, mu_mailbox_post, mu_mailbox_list, mu_mailbox_read, mu_mailbox_consume). MCP clients see normal tools; mu stores messages in its event log. NOT a separate mailbox system.
close_reason: MCP mailbox adapter implemented and tested end-to-end. Unix socket at ~/.local/share/mu/mcp.sock, auto-starts with mu serve. 5 tools (mu_peer_hello, mu_mailbox_post, mu_mailbox_list, mu_mailbox_read, mu_mailbox_consume) dispatch to existing mailbox handlers via shared Sessions state. Full round-trip verified: hello→post→list→read→consume→re-list-empty. Protocol: initialize, tools/list, tools/call, ping. Zero new test failures (843 pass).

title: bash tool: add B-7 (output-cap) test — currently missing from bash.rs tests
Session persistence across daemon restart
description:
Today sessions live in mu serve's in-memory Sessions map. When mu serve exits (TUI quits → stdin closes → transport exits → sessions drops), every session and its event log are lost.

For a daily-driver TUI that's used across many work sessions, that's a real limitation: you can't 'come back to' the conversation you had yesterday.

What's needed:
  - Event-log persistence (SQLite or JSONL — the event_log.rs design doc references both)
  - SessionDiscovery (mu-038) backend that reads the persisted logs on startup, resurrects SessionInfo entries (the agent loop doesn't need to be re-spawned for a 'cold' session — it spawns on the next ask_session)
  - Cap on retention (per-session age limit? per-session size limit? user choice)
  - Capability state persistence (a session with attenuated capability should keep that on cold reload)

Mentioned as deferred work in mu-036 spec (autonomous loops). Write its own spec when implementation pressure builds — likely after mu-035 Phase B/C/D and mu-037 phase 1 are done.

Workaround for now: keep mu serve running detached; have the TUI connect-to-existing rather than spawn-new. That's not implemented but is a much smaller piece of work than full persistence.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: DESIGN RATIONALE (operator riff 2026-06-06, memory rehydrate-vs-attach-duality ddeb2944): session persistence across daemon restart is the seat of a bigger payoff — live restart/upgrade (stop old binary, start new, rehydrate, resume; the session never participates). Requirements that fall out: (1) deterministic context reassembly from the log — SHARED with the replay-probe canary's byte-identical-prefix need; (2) split guarantee: conversation-history rendering versioned/append-stable (exact), ambient spans (tools/system) explicitly upgrade-mutable; (3) drain inventory = un-evented process state: parked wakeups already survive (durable AutonomousScheduledWakeup, PR #209); watches need mu-dvmu; anything else found = file it.

## Discipline

title: specs/architecture/seams.md — canonical dataflows + must-not-bypass guardrails + contract test map
description:
Add a new architectural spec documenting mu's seams — the integration points where one subsystem's output feeds another's input — with canonical dataflows, "must not bypass" guardrails, contract-test names, and per-seam auditor checklists. Becomes the shared external map so the operator doesn't have to hold the integration graph in their head; every agent task that touches a seam cites this spec.

Surfaced by gpt-5.5 during daemon ebccc9 session-2 (2026-05-20) as the foundational artifact for distributing code review across multiple specialized auditors.

## Initial seam inventory to document

Each row: canonical dataflow → must-not-bypass patterns → required contract test name.

1. **Provider request context** — rope → ContextAssembly event → ProviderRenderer → wire bytes. Must not: assemble ad-hoc `Vec<AgentMessage>`, append raw memory strings into the prompt. Test: `provider_request_uses_rope_projection_not_message_vec()`.
2. **TUI transcript** — event-log projection (read-only over the durable log). Must not: maintain a separate mutable transcript buffer that can drift. Test: `tui_transcript_is_event_projection_not_mutable_buffer()`.
3. **Memory injection** — memory recall → context spans with provenance (memory_id, score, timestamp) → rope inclusion. Must not: append opaque strings to the prompt with no source-tracking. Test: `memory_injection_uses_spans_with_provenance_not_opaque_strings()`.
4. **Tool authority** — `Capability`/Biscuit check as single source of truth. Must not: tool-local allow flags that contradict the capability; widening operations of any kind. Test: `delegation_cannot_widen_tools()`.
5. **Session lifecycle (archive/hide/pin)** — events drive state; rehydration filters by event projection. Must not: ad-hoc runtime maps that bypass the event log. Test: `archived_session_hidden_after_restart_but_events_remain_queryable()`.
6. **Immutability content storage** — `Arc<str>` for all immutable-at-creation fields (Span.content, Span.id, ProviderMessage.content, ContentBlock::Text { text }). Must not: re-introduce `String` for these fields. Test: `span_content_clones_share_underlying_bytes()`. (Cross-ref mu-yqeq.2 — the Arc<str> migration bead.)

## Document structure

- **Header**: status, related beads (mu-yqeq, mu-7lwn, the auditor-roles bead this blocks)
- **Why**: 1 paragraph — integration accounting at multi-agent velocity requires shared external map.
- **Seam table** as above.
- **Per-seam sections**: dataflow diagram (before/after when there's an active migration), bypass examples, required tests.
- **Auditor checklists**: cross-link to the auditor-roles spec (separate bead).
- **"Questions the event log must answer"** — living checklist (model/context, tools/authority, runtime, TUI/UX, memory paths).

## Acceptance

- File exists at `specs/architecture/seams.md` with all 6 initial seams documented.
- Each seam has a canonical dataflow, must-not-bypass guidance, and a contract-test name (the test may not exist yet — listed as required follow-on).
- PR-template / agent-task guidance is updated to cite this spec when touching any seam.

## References

- Source: daemon ebccc9 session-2 turn-by-turn proposal by gpt-5.5
- Memory: `mu_posiwid_cat_for_agents_2026_05_20` (auditor-readability framing — this spec is the artifact that makes auditor-readability mechanical)
- Memory: `substrate_thinking_architectural_moves_2026_05_20` (substrate properties that "fall out for free" are exactly what each seam protects)

title: Decompose mu-d04a (mu-solo live-viewport streaming refactor) into right-sized implementation sub-beads
description:
Plan-style (read-only analysis -> filed beads). mu-d04a is a multi-hour refactor (live mutable viewport region redrawn per tick from per-session buffers; insert_before into scrollback only on assistant_text_finalized; demux concurrent streams by session_id; commit ordered by START ts). Too big for one /goal.

Produce 3-5 sub-beads, each: self-contained (ideally one module/file), independently testable, sized to do in one focused session without thrash/hallucination, with blockedBy edges expressing order and a shared-contract-first staging plan (types/state landed before the parallelizable pieces). Add a short decomposition note (in this bead's comments or a specs/ stub). Map the two rooted bugs to sub-beads where they fit: mu-304g (F3 inline render stalls ~20 lines in) and mu-926g (empty/unclosed assistant header artifacts). Source of truth: mu-d04a body + the mu-solo streaming state machine (crates/mu-solo/src/app.rs handle_notification; streaming_text / streaming_header_open / streaming_chars_emitted / pending_close / streaming_route; DynamicViewport). Companion bug already filed: mu-wf5w (missing done-terminus) — link, don't duplicate.

DONE: >=3 sub-beads filed with scope + blockedBy edges + note; each independently testable; no code changed (planning only).
close_reason: Decomposed mu-d04a into 4 right-sized, edge-linked, independently-testable sub-beads (mu-hsyt P2, mu-jnbh P3, mu-33o1 P4, mu-p5pd P5) + a decomposition note on mu-d04a. Found Phase 1 already landed (Turn model + render.rs + commit-on-finalize; fixes mu-304g/926g in mu-solo). >=3 sub-beads filed; planning-only, no code changed.

title: Config-backed provider/model/favorites catalog
description:
Move provider/model quirks and model-specific limits out of hardcoded Rust tables into a layered Figment/TOML catalog.

Motivation:
- qwen3.6:35b-a3b-q8_0 needs max_output_tokens bumped without code changes; current output_limits.rs hardcodes model families.
- RouteCatalog is already the frontend-facing provider×model source of truth; it should expose aliases, labels, quirks, output limits, and favorites for eventual UI selection.
- Provider quirks (usage semantics, auth, paths, tool dialects) and model quirks (context, reasoning, max output) should be configurable independently, then joined into favorites/routes.

Design sketch:
- Built-in TOML catalog embedded with include_str!, overlaid by ~/.config/mu/models.toml and optional env via Figment.
- Sections: [providers.*], [models.*], [model_rules.*], [favorites.*].
- Use maps for keyed deep merge; arrays may replace unless later modeled as bool maps.
- After Figment extraction, validate refs and build alias indexes; dynamic Ollama discovery enriches discovered models by exact model or rule.

Acceptance:
- A user TOML override can set qwen3.6/qwen3.6:* max_output_tokens=16384 without changing code.
- OpenAI-shaped and Anthropic request builders use catalog-derived max_output_tokens with current hardcoded fallback preserved.
- daemon.list_routes/RouteEntry exposes enough metadata for UI selection: aliases/label/quirks/max_output_tokens/favorite info or equivalent.
- Existing behavior remains when no user config exists.
close_reason: done

title: mu ask/serve --bare: first-class flag for hermetic sessions (no recall/startup injection)
description: Operator request 2026-06-05. MU_NO_RECALL=1 already force-disables session-start recall injection (config.rs env_disables_recall), but it's env-only folklore. Add --bare to mu ask and mu serve (forwarded like --ephemeral): sets recall off for the session, making 'hermetic session' an explicit CLI contract. Consumers: ai-review.sh (currently sets MU_NO_RECALL=1 inline — switch to --bare once available), benches (agentic-bench measured models WITH the ~28KB kernel injected; --bare enables a clean re-run), delegated workers that should not inherit operator identity. Consider whether --bare should also skip the discovery bootstrap (compose_system_prompt) or only recall — probably recall+bootstrap both, tool schemas stay.
close_reason: done

title: mu-console: web operator console for session/event forensics
description:
Build a web operator console as a projection over mu events/analytics. Goal: keep mu-solo as the keyboard cockpit while the web console becomes the richer reference projection for sessions, event timelines, context assemblies, compaction audits, cost/cache economics, and replay comparisons.

Principles:
- Read-only first; no chat/control surface in v1.
- Event log / analytics are truth; web pages are projections.
- Axum/server-rendered/HTMX is preferred for the first slice unless implementation terrain says otherwise.
- Web views should become examples/oracles for later TUI affordances.
- Operator questions first: what happened, what did it cost, what context did the model see, what was cached, what did compaction do?

Acceptance for epic: first coherent local web console behind nginx-compatible bind/config with session index, session detail, event timeline, cost/cache summary, and at least one context/compaction drilldown.

title: mu-console: compare two replay/session runs

Add a comparison view for two sessions/runs, aimed at replay economics and quality investigations.

title: mu-036: 'accepted: true' means enqueued, not honored — decide RPC semantics for non-autonomous targets
description:
Surfaced by codex/gpt-5.5 review of PR (mu-28u Phase C), 2026-06-06: both session.start_autonomous AND session.schedule_wakeup return accepted:true on input-channel enqueue; the loop may then decline (schedule_wakeup on a non-autonomous session declines with a warning callout). A caller can get accepted:true with nothing scheduled. PRE-EXISTING shape (Phase B established it); Phase C inherited it consistently.

DESIGN DECISION (operator): (a) handler pre-checks session RunMode (racy but honest best-effort), (b) document accepted = 'delivered to session' and add a follow-up notification/event for declined inputs (the warning callout already lands durably), or (c) loop-side ack via oneshot (sync RPC, strongest semantics, most plumbing). Spec mu-036 does not answer it — that is why this is a bead and not a fix.

title: mu stats: session-metrics projection layer (DuckDB over event JSONL) + dashboard
description:
Aggregation + visualization over the durable event logs, so session economics/shape analysis (like the 2026-06-04 replay readout) is repeatable and visual instead of ad-hoc jq.

WHY DUCKDB: in-process OLAP, queries JSONL directly (read_json over ~/.local/share/mu/events/*/*.jsonl) — zero ETL, the event log stays the single source of truth (the mu thesis). Projections are just SQL views: cost per session (join task_telemetry to pricing), context trajectory (context_assembly.token_count_estimate ordered by event id), token_breakdown composition over time, cache hit/expiry detection (gap analysis on telemetry timestamps), compaction events, tool mix.

PHASES: (1) saved SQL views + CLI entry point ('mu stats' or sidecar script); (2) dashboard page — static HTML + vega-lite fed by query JSON, or mu-native 'mu stats serve'. Operator explicitly wants visuals/navigable projections ('you may be able to keep it all in your head, having visuals will help me').

REFERENCE QUERIES: the analyses in ~/.claude-personal/experiments/replay-economics-2026-06-04.md (trajectory, TTL simulation, composition at first/peak/last, eviction breakdown) are the spec — each should become a view.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
SESSION-QUALITY MARKS + CC-SESSION UNIFICATION (operator-ratified 2026-06-05 evening; context: postmortem-2026-06-05-relitigation-session + the weekend-degradation hypothesis — terrain: incident-flavored task_log entries 16.5% Sun-UTC vs 4-6% weekday baseline; mu-on-API-key showed the same ailment as claude-code the same night, so the billing-path discriminator is dead).

MOTIVATION: event logs persist but carry no operator judgment — without a mark we can't label which sessions were degraded, so they can't feed analysis or the replay-probe canary (byte-identical degraded-session prefixes re-run across days/times, N samples, scored for relitigation/contradiction-of-context — difficulty controlled exactly, time the only free variable).

PROJECTION-LAYER WORK (this bead, phase-2 scope):
1. cc_sessions view in mu_stats.sql — decode claude-code transcripts (~/.claude-personal/projects/*/*.jsonl) with the SAME read_ndjson_objects pattern t4c_tool_inventory.sql already proves (tools.html decodes cc today; the Sessions page doesn't yet). Pull session id, model, timestamps, usage. Sessions page shows both corpora.
2. session_marks view — UNION of (a) mu OperatorMark events from the event logs (see split-out mu bead for the event/console/CLI side) and (b) cc-session marks stored as task_log rows: tag session-mark, session_ref = cc session id (column already exists in task_log schema, currently unused). DuckDB reads task_log.sqlite via sqlite_scanner.
3. Dashboard: mark-rate by day-of-week alongside the existing charts (the oxygen-hypothesis instrument); marked-bad list = the replay-fixture queue by query.

CAPTURE DOCTRINE: mu marks live IN the event log (single-source-of-truth thesis, immutable, re-mark = newer event); cc transcripts are app-owned so cc marks live in the task_log sidecar, never appended to claude's jsonl.

First fixtures already identified: mu session 3dd40c3c1f84f278 (2026-06-05 21:23, dismissed unimpressed) and the 2026-06-05 claude-code relitigation session.

title: mu-tui-markdown: render assistant responses as styled Markdown (termimad or equivalent)
description:
Render assistant Markdown responses in mu's TUI with proper styling: syntax-highlighted code blocks, tables, lists, emphasis, links. Today the TUI renders as plain text. Toggle between rendered and raw views for cut/paste workflow.

## Motivation

Assistant responses ARE Markdown — code blocks, lists, tables, headers, inline code, etc. Rendering them as plain text loses substantial information density. Concrete UX upgrade.

Operator note 2026-05-14: provider/model switching now working end-to-end with streaming updates in both F1 windows. Next-most-impactful TUI win is structured markdown rendering. Operator explicitly requested a raw/rendered toggle so cut/paste can grab the raw source (not the styled-but-unselectable-for-pipe rendering).

## Approach

Rust analogs to Charmbracelet's Glamour (Go):
- `termimad` (https://github.com/Canop/termimad) — most mature Markdown-in-terminal renderer in Rust. Used by broot. Direct dependency candidate.
- `pulldown-cmark` + custom renderer — finer control but more code; only worth it if termimad's defaults don't fit.

Termimad outputs styled text that can be embedded in ratatui via `ratatui::text::Text` / `Line` / `Span`. Compatibility: termimad uses crossterm under the hood, same as ratatui, so colors compose.

## Scope

IN:
- Add `termimad` (or chosen lib) to mu-tui's Cargo.toml
- Render assistant message body through termimad before display
- **Toggle between rendered and raw views** — operator-controlled, per-session. Suggested binding: `R` in transcript view (next to existing F-key family). Default = rendered. Toggle should not lose scroll position or re-flow disruptively.
- Test rendering with: code blocks (multiple languages), tables, nested lists, inline code, emphasis, links
- Theme alignment with mu-u8a's color polish work (moonfly/github_dark family)

OUT:
- Streaming Markdown rendering (rendering partial Markdown as deltas arrive — termimad expects complete blocks). v1: render on session.done event; show raw during streaming. Cleanly avoids the half-rendered-table flicker problem.
- Inline images / VHS-style embedded media

## Design notes

Streaming + raw/rendered interaction: the simplest mental model is "raw while streaming, rendered when done." That gives the operator a stable visual transition (yellow streaming block stays raw text → settles to white styled markdown when complete). The toggle then lets the operator force raw on a completed message for cut/paste. Two states × two phases = matrix to test:

| Phase | Toggle=rendered | Toggle=raw |
|---|---|---|
| Streaming | raw (still streaming) | raw |
| Done | rendered | raw |

This avoids needing partial-markdown rendering altogether and matches what an operator actually wants ergonomically.

Cut/paste compatibility: terminal selection grabs whatever cell content is in the cells. termimad renders styled-text into cells; the *displayed text* is the markdown source minus syntax markers (e.g. `**bold**` → `bold`). For accurate cut/paste of the raw markdown source, toggle to raw first. Document this in the binding's help text.

## Acceptance

- Assistant messages render with at minimum: syntax-highlighted code blocks, formatted tables, styled lists/emphasis
- `R` toggle works in transcript view; default state = rendered
- Toggle preserves scroll position
- Streaming-phase behavior: raw text shown live; transition to rendered (or stay raw if toggle=raw) on session.done
- `cargo test --workspace` green
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` green
- One commit closes this bead

## References

- Charmbracelet's Glamour (Go): https://github.com/charmbracelet/glamour — the design reference
- termimad (Rust): https://github.com/Canop/termimad
- pulldown-cmark (Rust): https://github.com/raphlinus/pulldown-cmark
- Theme coordination: mu-u8a (TUI color polish — moonfly/github_dark family)
- Pattern shelf: mu-zh1 (TUI references including Charmbracelet design language)

## Notes

Filed 2026-05-13 after Charmbracelet survey. Updated 2026-05-14 with raw/rendered toggle requirement after operator UX feedback. Concrete adopt-the-crate task, smaller-scope than full Glamour reimplementation. Sibling to mu-di4 (throbber).

title: Thread system_prompt from CreateSessionRequest to Provider::stream
description:
## Why

`CreateSessionRequest.system_prompt: Option<String>` exists at the wire protocol layer (crates/mu-core/src/protocol.rs:66) but is **accepted at the API boundary and silently dropped**. The Provider::stream trait signature only takes (messages, tools); no system prompt parameter. So clients can send the field expecting customization and get no effect.

Surfaced 2026-05-11 while implementing mu-i6j (cache_control markers on anthropic provider request). The implementation comments explicitly note this gap:

> System prompt is the other classic cache target but mu doesn't currently thread `system_prompt` from CreateSessionRequest through to Provider::stream — see protocol.rs:66 for the wire field; the body construction does not include a `system` key. When that gap is closed, add a second cache_control marker on the last system content block.

See commit ykumwozs 77bb0ce4.

## What

1. Plumb `system_prompt: Option<String>` from the session manager into AgentLoop / Provider invocations. Existing call sites: dispatch::handle_create_session, sessions storage, agent loop's call-out to the provider.
2. Extend Provider::stream trait signature with system_prompt parameter, OR store it on the AgentConfig (preferred — keeps the trait stable and lets each provider decide how to render it).
3. In anthropic provider's build_request_body: if system_prompt is Some, include a top-level `system` field with a content-block array containing the prompt + cache_control marker (mirroring how tools are cached in mu-i6j).
4. Other providers (openai_codex, openrouter, faux) get equivalent treatment.

## Acceptance

- A session created with system_prompt: Some("...") sees the prompt reach the LLM (verifiable by asking 'what's your system prompt' and seeing the contents).
- For anthropic_api, the F5 dashboard's cache% column rises further (system prompt joins tools as a cached prefix).
- Workspace tests still pass; new tests assert the wire-level translation.

## Out of scope

- Designing what mu's default system prompt should be. (Today's behavior is "no system prompt"; this bead preserves that as the default by treating None == today's behavior.)
close_reason: done

title: t4c source: project-local capabilities — parse justfile -> project.just.*, plus Cargo/.beads/AGENTS/CLAUDE as capability/context sources. New RegistrySource(s); provenance tags them (mu-kex4.6.8). Split from mu-kex4.6.8.
Skills and tool schemas as rope spans (eliminate parallel loading mechanisms)
description:
Refactor skill activation and tool registration to emit spans into the rope rather than maintain separate loaded-state structures.

## Scope

In: SkillManager emits skill.activated { skill_id, span_refs } events; ToolRegistry represents each tool's schema as an addressable span; capability attenuation becomes a pointer-set filter; RetainedRope API gains activate_skill / deactivate_skill / filter_tools / provenance.

Out: span granularity policy (start per-file, refine later); skill activation triggers (keep current); operator-view UI changes (handled in mu-NEW-4).

## Why

Today skill loading and tool registration are separate code paths from context management. Unifying them: activation/deactivation IS pointer-set membership change. Subagent capability filtering reuses existing rope machinery; provenance built in.

## Acceptance

Skill-activation behavior preserved; tool schemas render identically at agent-view; capability attenuation produces observable pointer-set diff; provenance lookup works; tests for activate/deactivate, filtered tool sets, span provenance; workspace tests green.

## References

Full draft: ~/.claude-personal/drafts/mu-bead-3-skills-tools-as-spans.md
Blocked-by: mu-qk8, mu-ktq
close_reason: Merged in PR #13 as c8a307f

title: Mine existing claude-code session logs to seed adapter + wire-format
description:
Instead of running fresh sessions to discover claude-code's protocol shape, mine the ~2 months of existing logs across three accounts (claude, claude-personal, claude-openrouter). Build a catalog of message/event shapes and version-vs-shape comparisons.

## Inputs (data sources)

- `~/.claude/projects/<project>/<session-id>.jsonl` (work account)
- `~/.claude-personal/projects/<project>/<session-id>.jsonl` (personal account)
- `~/.claude-openrouter/projects/<project>/<session-id>.jsonl` (openrouter account, if logged)

Each session JSONL contains the same events claude-code emits via --output-format=stream-json (or very close to it). Version metadata is in the session file or derivable from timestamps + binary upgrade history.

## Output: protocol catalog artifact

A document mapping:
- All distinct event types encountered across the corpus
- Per-event type: schema (field names + types), example payloads, frequency
- Per-version: which event types existed, which were added/removed
- Schema-evolution diff: 'Between v2.0.x and v2.1.x, X event added Y field'

## Why this matters

- Seeds the exo-adapter (mu-p49v) with golden fixtures — bootstrap verification has ground truth instead of needing to generate it
- Validates the wire-format design (mu-nt7j) against real-world events instead of speculation
- Surfaces edge cases: rare event types, weird payloads, version skews
- Is itself research — answers 'how stable is claude-code's stream-json across versions' empirically

## Mechanics

1. Inventory: count session files per account, per version, per project
2. Sample: parse all session files, extract distinct event-type schemas
3. Catalog: write the artifact (likely `specs/architecture/exo-protocol-catalog.md` + structured JSON sidecar)
4. Versioning: tag the catalog with the corpus snapshot date — reruns extend it

## Goal-session candidate

This is a strong candidate for autonomous goal-session execution. Read-only on logs, well-defined output, parallelizable across log files. Lower risk than code-writing goal sessions.

## Dependencies

Strictly blocks mu-p49v's smoke-fixture set, soft-input to mu-nt7j schema design. Both can proceed without this in early phases — but adopting this output sharpens both substantially.

title: Expose structured capability help through discover
description: Follow-up from discussion of t4c/discover integration. t4c already exposes rich --help-ai --json / --schema metadata for capabilities, while mu's in-harness discover tool currently provides the ranked first-hop list with terse descriptions. Add an internal second-hop discovery surface so agents do not have to shell out to t4c for structured capability metadata. Desired shape: discover(intent) returns ranked capability IDs; a follow-up capability_help(id) or discover(id, mode=schema) returns the structured AI-help/schema payload (purpose, when_to_use, commands/args, preconditions, examples, risk/load policy where available). This supports shrinking startup context: boot only the invariant 'use discover first', then let the agent self-discover tools/skills at point of use. t4c remains the CLI/export/debug surface for proprietary/non-mu harnesses.

title: mu-core: split agent/loop_.rs into orchestrator + extracted state machines (invoke / execute_tools / autonomy / compaction_integration)
description:
## Problem

`crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs` is the file where everything meets:

- queue / action handling
- message history management
- provider streaming integration
- tool execution
- approval waiting (input_required)
- retry policy
- capability checks
- provider status ticks
- autonomous mode
- compaction integration
- context assembly events
- cancellation semantics

The pure-planner extraction already underway is the right direction.
The loop should become an orchestrator around smaller, testable state
machines rather than the place where everything meets.

Heavy in-file comments are currently doing invariant-documentation
work that should be code structure (the dense comments are necessary
because the control flow is too dense; smaller modules with typed
interfaces would let the structure speak for itself).

## Proposal

Refactor `agent/loop_.rs` into a thin orchestrator with extracted
modules:

```text
crates/mu-core/src/agent/
  loop_.rs                 // orchestrator only: top-level state, action queue
  invoke.rs                // provider invocation + streaming integration
  execute_tools.rs         // tool dispatch, approval waiting, retry policy
  autonomy.rs              // autonomous-mode wakeup + decision logic
  compaction_integration.rs // when/how to trigger compaction
  planners.rs              // already-extracted planner code centralized
  tool_history.rs          // tool call/result tracking and provenance
```

Each extracted module should have a small, well-typed surface;
unit-testable in isolation; document its invariants explicitly.

## Acceptance

- loop_.rs is significantly smaller, focused on orchestration
- Each extracted module has unit tests for its state machine
- Existing agent-loop integration tests still pass
- `cargo clippy -D warnings` clean
- Dense control-flow comments in loop_.rs get replaced by smaller,
  focused modules where the structure documents itself

## Why this matters

Biggest risk in agent-edited codebases is gravity-well files where
local reasoning becomes impossible. agent/loop_.rs is currently the
worst offender. Splitting it makes future agent-assisted modifications
safer because changes can be reasoned about within smaller, typed
boundaries.

## Cross-refs

- AGENTS.md "no 27k-line files" rule
- gpt-5.5 code review of mu, daemon f3845422 session-4 (2026-05-15)
- Related modularization: protocol.rs split + dispatch.rs split (filed
  same session)
- Touches future work: mu-kgu compaction beads, autonomy beads
close_reason: Merged in PR #65 — loop_.rs split into 5 cohesive modules, mu-wk2 integrated, 363+ tests pass, gate-validated ## Files block

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Additional refactor item from gpt code review (mu daemon f3845422 session-4, 2026-05-16): introduce explicit typed state structs for per-ask state and per-session state. Currently agent/loop_.rs uses ad-hoc state passing across the orchestration loop. After the file split lands, define typed structs that make the state-passing invariants explicit at the type level rather than at the comment level. This is gpt's highest-value-cleanup item #7 and is tightly coupled to this split — not worth a separate bead, just include in scope when this bead is picked up.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
**Recovery candidate: jj change vnmssvsw (worktree-mu-nk3-split@origin)** — surfaced 2026-05-16 during jj loose-head audit.

The real refactor work exists here, distinct from the fictional kolvssqm/PR #52 commit that operator closed. Same Haiku 4.5 worker produced both — one actually did the split, the other hallucinated it. Only the fictional one got submitted.

Actual contents of vnmssvsw (6 file changes, 586 insertions, 863 deletions):
- crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/autonomy.rs (+27 LOC, new)
- crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/compaction_integration.rs (+12 LOC, new)
- crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/execute_tools.rs (+377 LOC, new)
- crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/invoke.rs (+146 LOC, new)
- crates/mu-core/src/agent/{loop_.rs => loop_/mod.rs} (trimmed -887 LOC)
- crates/mu-core/src/agent/{loop_tests.rs => loop_/tests.rs} (rename only)

Current main.loop_.rs is 2059 LOC (vnmssvsw's pre-refactor baseline was 2038, so only ~21 LOC of subsequent drift — rebase should be mostly mechanical).

**Recovery options:**

1. **Rebase + new PR.** `jj rebase -r vnmssvsw -d main`, resolve any 21-LOC drift, run `cargo test --workspace && cargo clippy -D warnings && cargo fmt --check`, push fresh PR. Lowest-effort path if the rebase is clean.

2. **Use vnmssvsw as reference, re-do.** Treat vnmssvsw as a worked example, redo the split by hand on current main. Slower but guarantees the result is reviewed against the new mu-b5kl verification gate (which would have caught the fictional kolvssqm submission).

Caveat for either path: the same worker fingerprint produced both real and fictional commits. Review the actual diff carefully — don't trust the commit description by itself. Run `jj show vnmssvsw --stat` and `jj show vnmssvsw` to inspect before promoting.

**Cross-refs:**
- mu-b5kl (new bead) — worker verification gate that would have caught the fictional submission
- jj loose-head audit script: ~/.local/bin/jj-orphan-audit

title: HANDOFF wire-format spec — JSON-canonical artifact for mu/exogenic interop
description:
Define the on-disk format for HANDOFF_<datetime>.{md,json} artifacts. Used at the mu/exogenic boundary ONLY — native mu uses event log + delegate + internal compaction for everything else.

## Use cases (after the discussion narrowing)

1. User-initiated cross-runtime move (mu → claude-code, claude-code → mu, mu → mobile-claude-app)
2. Cross-account / cross-config moves (claude-personal ↔ work — same runtime, different config)
3. mu's foreign-runtime-wrapper producing input for claude-code restart
4. Human-readable durable record (archive, postmortem reference, audit)

Native mu compaction, mu→mu delegation, mu→mu session resume, and mu→mu crash recovery do NOT use this format — they use event logs.

## Format: JSON-canonical + prose-rendered companion

`HANDOFF_<YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSSZ>.json` is the canonical artifact. Mu native producers emit it directly from internal state. The prose render (`HANDOFF_<datetime>.md`) is derived from the JSON — same content, different audience (foreign runtimes that consume as prompt text + human readers).

## JSON schema (sketch)

```json
{
  "format": "mu.handoff",
  "version": 1,
  "produced_by": { "runtime": "mu", "version": "0.1.0", "session_id": "..." },
  "produced_at": "2026-05-23T16:30:00Z",
  "working_dir": "/home/tcovert/src/public_github/mu",
  "repo_state": {
    "vcs": "jj",
    "current_rev": "tvvszlvl",
    "branch": "main",
    "uncommitted": [{"path": "...", "status": "M"}]
  },
  "open_beads": [{"id": "mu-...", "title": "...", "status": "in_progress"}],
  "session_modes": { "effort": "high", "focus": "on", "provider": "anthropic", "model": "claude-opus-4-7" },
  "recent_tool_calls": [/* last N */],
  "focused_files": [/* recent reads, top consumers */],
  "memory_anchors": [{"id": "...", "description": "..."}],
  "standing_policy": "...",
  "next_task": "...",
  "do_not_redo": ["..."],
  "resume_instructions": "cd ...; agent memory context; paste this file's prose render"
}
```

## Naming convention

- Files: `HANDOFF_<YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSSZ>.{json,md}` (datetime — multiples per day are normal)
- Renames MORNING_<date>.md from goal-protocol skill (separate bead handles the rename across goal-protocol + mu specs + jj-orphan-audit script)

## Validation

Schema validator in mu-core. `mu handoff validate <path>` CLI for one-shot check.

title: Phase B: ASCII OS-memory-map renderer for mu-u6hc context-window prototype
description:
Phase B of mu-u6hc. Consumes ContextAttribution from Phase A and renders the OS-memory-style region map (sketch in mu-u6hc body).

## Scope

Pure rendering function: `render(attribution: &ContextAttribution) -> String`.

## Output format (target)

```
Context window: 200,000 tokens  [████████░░░░░░░░░░░░░] 87,400 / 200,000 (43.7%)

┌─ System prompt          ████████          4,200 tokens   (2.1%)
├─ CLAUDE.md (global)     █                   320 tokens   (0.2%)
├─ CLAUDE.md (project)    ███               1,800 tokens   (0.9%)
```

title: Docs↔reality realignment + receipts rebrand (README + committed assets)
description:
Goal session goal:2026-05-31. Bring mu's docs into line with what actually shipped, reconcile beads against code, pilot AsciiDoc transclusion where it pays, then recover the settled 'receipts' brand identity into a rewritten README + committed assets.

Meanwhile the real identity (settled ~May 22) never landed: hero 'mu — for those who demand receipts', triple meaning (mu / mu / wu), accountable-work-runtime north star, cow-in-cowboy-hat logo + 23 assets sitting untracked in ../mu_images/.

SCOPE (4 phases, fully autonomous, single end-of-run briefing):
- P0 reconcile: close verified-done beads after re-checking code.
- P1 triage: status convention, archive MORNING logs via git mv (never delete), reclassify aspirational arch docs, build specs/INDEX.md.
- P2 adoc: pilot include:: transclusion on code-quoting arch docs + CI include-check (partial mu-0y8o); stable mu-0NN specs stay markdown.
