Experiment doc: ~/.claude-personal/experiments/goal-2026-05-31-mu-docs-realign-rebrand.md

title: mu-tui: F3 prompt grows upward; transcript scrolls off the top (multiplexer-friendly)
description:
## Goal

Make F3 (session detail) behave like claude-code and pi:

- **Info bar at the bottom** (status, keys, mode indicator).
- **Prompt input area just above it**, growing **upward** as the user types multi-line content. Long prompts (like a paragraph of design discussion) expand the input region without forcing the user to scroll or losing visibility of what they've typed.
- **Transcript on top, open-ended**: text scrolls off the top of the visible region (no bounded scroll-back pane). Lets terminal multiplexers (zellij, tmux) use their own scrollback to navigate history, and lets zellij's 'open buffer in editor' feature capture the transcript directly.

Today the F3 transcript is rendered inside a bordered scrollable pane (mu-dhr) — which works on its own but defeats multiplexer scrollback and editor-capture flows. The bordered pane should go away on F3; the transcript should print straight to the terminal as line-oriented output and let mux/scrollback do the rest.

## Why

Operator (2026-05-18): claude-code and pi both work this way and it composes correctly with zellij. With mu's bordered F3 pane the operator has to choose between mu's keys and zellij's keys for scrolling history; ideally there's only one model (zellij owns scrollback).

## Behavior

### Layout (bottom-up)

```
... session.cost / model / status / mode keys                          ← info bar
[your-prompt-here-expanding-as-you-type ↩                              ← input area
 continuing on this line ↩                                              (grows up)
 and this one ↩]                                                      
─────────────────────────────────────────                              ← thin separator
> previous user msg                                                    ← transcript
< assistant reply (long)                                                 (open at top,
... wraps and scrolls off the top of visible region ...                  scrolls off
```

### Input area

- Multi-line edit with the existing mu-wd2/mu-h04 readline-style affordances.
- Alt-Enter inserts newline; Enter submits (existing convention).
- Region's height auto-adjusts to fit content, capped at e.g. 1/3 of terminal height. Beyond that, scroll within the input region.
- Ctrl-X Ctrl-E into $EDITOR remains available (existing mu-82l).

### Transcript area

- Lines append to bottom of transcript region as events arrive.
- Old lines scroll off the top.
- No internal mu scrollbar / no PageUp/PageDown inside F3 transcript.
- Operator uses zellij's scrollback (mod-s) or terminal mouse-wheel to read history.

### Other F-views unchanged

F1, F2, F5, F8 keep their existing bordered-pane behavior. This is specifically a F3 (session detail) redesign because F3 is the interactive read+write surface; other views are reporting/dashboard views where bordered panes are appropriate.

## Acceptance

- F3 renders without a bordered transcript scroll pane
- Multi-line input region grows upward as user types; collapses on submit
- zellij scrollback (mod-s) navigates the full transcript history when mu-tui is running in a zellij pane
- zellij 'capture pane to editor' (E in scroll mode) captures the transcript correctly (visible region + scrollback)
- info bar at the bottom shows existing status data
- F3 keys (Enter, Alt-Enter, Ctrl-X Ctrl-E, Esc, etc.) all still work
- Tests / fixtures updated where the bordered-pane shape was assumed

## Out of scope

- Cross-platform terminal scrollback (Windows ConPTY, etc.) — focus is FreeBSD + zellij/tmux + alacritty/wezterm
- Re-architecting F8 events firehose (separate concern; mu-2ft covers F8 ergonomics)
- Mouse selection / mouse-driven scroll inside the input region — operator uses keyboard

## Composes with

- mu-wd2 / mu-h04 (readline-style prompt editing) — input region behavior layers on top
- mu-82l ($EDITOR via Ctrl-X Ctrl-E) — preserved
- mu-diz (universal scroll-wheel + popup ergonomics) — F3-specific changes here may inform diz's broader patterns

## Provenance

Filed 2026-05-18 from operator observation while using the newly-shipped TUI: 'I want to make F3 more like claudecode and pi. In particular, info at the bottom of the screen the prompt should expand up to contain large prompts. The top should be open: because on claudecode and pi, the buffer just scrolls off the top of the screen. This allows buffer managers, or something like zellij/tmux to use it's own scroll to go up. And then I could also use the open buffer into editor feature that zellij has.'
notes:
## Concrete layout decisions (2026-05-18, from screen-capture review)

After comparing pi_ts and claude-code captures and inventorying mu's current F3 loop primitives, settled on:

### Transcript region

- **No outer pane border or scroll indicator.** Today's `┌ Transcript (scrolled up 150/738 · End to bottom · Home to top) ───` border + title go away. Transcript becomes bare line-oriented terminal output. Mux scrollback owns history.
- **Per-turn loops preserved verbatim.** The existing inline boxes are the per-turn structure and survive the redesign unchanged. Catalog:
  - `┌─ you` — user input
  - `┌─ assistant` — assistant text
  - `┌─ tool: <name>` — tool call (multi-field body: `args:`, `→ ok:`, optional `elapsed:`)
  - `┌─ ERROR` — provider/tool failure with raw error inline
  - Speculative: `┌─ tool: <name> · DENIED` — capability-denied tool call (no live example yet; refine when one fires)
- **Inline turn-boundary dividers preserved**: `─── done · end_turn · 229779ms ───` between loops, as today.
- **Elision policy: keep + add expand-key.** Long content still elides with `…` so a multi-KB tool result doesn't dominate the visual flow. Per-loop expand key (likely Enter on focused loop, or a chord like `Ctrl-E`) toggles full content. Persistent — re-collapse with same key. Mux scrollback still navigates the elided form.

### Input region (above footer)

- Bordered open area with thin `─────` rules above and below the editable region (matches pi/claude shape).
- Grows upward as multi-line content is entered; capped at ~1/3 of terminal height. Beyond the cap, scroll within the input region.
- Existing readline + Alt-Enter + `$EDITOR` (Ctrl-X Ctrl-E) affordances preserved.

### Footer (two lines)

Line 1 — financial/runtime status (relocate today's header data):
```
$X.XX · IN→OUT tok · model · provider · session-id
```

Line 2 — mode + single rotating hint:
```
command mode · ? for help
```

Specific tokens to include and their order: derive from existing header rendering at `main.rs:~1962-2006`. Move spans down; don't reinvent.

### `?` help overlay

- Single key (`?`) opens a modal overlay with the full key reference.
- Sections: navigation (F1-F9, j/k), interaction (Enter, Alt-Enter, i, n), session lifecycle (a archive [pending mu-770], y yank), palette/system (:, q, Esc), prompt edit (Ctrl-X Ctrl-E, Ctrl-X Ctrl-P).
- Dismiss with `?` again, `Esc`, or `q`.
- Overlay does NOT take over scrollback — closing returns to whatever transcript position the user was at.

### Color

Deferred. Today's color rendering at the loop borders + status dividers stays as-is; refine after layout lands. mu-62s palette (system_prompt) gives us the infrastructure when we come back to this.

### Out of scope (this bead)

- Color refresh
- F1/F2/F4-F9 redesigns (this is F3-only)
- mu-cha (F1+F2 unification) — separate bead
- Rejected-tool-call variant content — design when first example fires

### Acceptance (refined from original)

- F3 renders without outer transcript pane border
- Per-turn loops + dividers intact
- Multi-line input grows upward; collapses on submit
- Two-line footer present with relocated header data + ? hint
- `?` opens help overlay; closes returning to prior scroll position
- Mux scrollback (zellij mod-s, tmux Ctrl-b `[`) navigates full transcript history
- Long content elides with `…`; expand-key on focused loop toggles full content
- Existing F3 key bindings (Enter, Alt-Enter, Ctrl-X Ctrl-E, Esc) all still work
- Tests / fixtures updated where bordered-pane shape was assumed

title: flaky test: intermittent cargo test --workspace failure (timing/concurrency race in transport tests) — undermines just ci / ci-aipr / PR CI
description:
SYMPTOM: cargo test --workspace intermittently fails one lib target then passes on rerun. Observed 2026-06-01: mu-core --lib failed once during a just ci-aipr run (passed on immediate rerun); mu-coding --lib did the same during mu-818c. Roughly 1 in 3 full-CI/just-ci runs. NOT reproducible: 8 idle full-suite reruns + 40 loaded isolated-test reruns + 5 loaded full-suite reruns all passed. So it is a rare load/timing flake that needs intra-process parallel-test contention (cargo test --workspace running hundreds of async tests in one tokio runtime), not external CPU load.

STRONG SUSPECT (by inspection, NOT reproduced-to-confirm): concurrent_dispatch_returns_second_response_first (crates/mu-core/src/transport.rs ~:382). It sends a slow request (id=1) gated by a fixed tokio::time::sleep(50ms) and a fast request (id=2), then asserts id=2 response arrives FIRST. Under parallel-test contention the fast dispatch can be starved past 50ms and the order flips -> assert fails. Classic timing-ordering flake. SECOND suspect: eof_terminates_serve_after_draining_response (transport.rs ~:409) — serve EOF-drain-terminate, the shutdown lifecycle.

HYPOTHESIS (tcovert): related to the concurrency/exit-code/unfinished-process work (mu-qc08). Partially supported — the suspects are in the serve/dispatch lifecycle layer — but the leading suspect is an ordering-by-sleep race, not an exit-code bug per se.

FIX DIRECTION: make ordering deterministic — gate the slow handler on a signal that the fast response was actually emitted (barrier/oneshot), or assert set-membership rather than strict order; drop the 50ms sleep. Audit async tests for sleep-based timing assumptions. Principle: we must be able to TEST our tools — ci/ci-aipr/PR-CI reliability depends on a deterministic suite.

NEXT: confirm the specific flaky test by capturing a real failure (e.g. run cargo test --workspace in a CI loop with --test-threads stress, or instrument the ordering assertion to log timings) before fixing, since identification here is by inspection only.

title: mu-solo: tool-result lines truncate at viewport width instead of word-wrapping (bash allowlist rejection unreadable)
description:
Operator capture 2026-06-04 (live-fire session for goal mu-ac5k): a long single-line tool result renders truncated at the viewport edge, not wrapped:

  | ● Bash(rm /tmp/livefire-marker)
  |   ⎿  bash: command ["rm", "/tmp/livefire-marker"] is not in the strict-mode allowlist. Currently allowed prefixes: git status, git log, git diff, git show, git branch, git remote, git rev-parse, l

The line cuts mid-word ('l...' = 'ls') and the rest of the allowlist is lost — exactly the content the operator/model needs to act on the rejection. Assistant prose in the same block wraps fine, so the gap is specific to the tool-result ('⎿') line rendering path (likely a truncate_at_word/single-line Span where a wrapped Paragraph is needed; compare the prose path). Long error strings are common (allowlists, compiler errors), so result lines need wrap + continuation indent under the ⎿ gutter.

Repro: any strict-mode bash rejection in a normal-width terminal.

title: In-session memory_recall tool — recall-on-demand for the demoted tail
description:
With the injection shrunk to the identity kernel, everything else must be discoverable mid-session or the change is memory amputation. Add a memory_recall tool to mu's tool set: shells out to 'agent memory recall <query> --k N --json' (labeled testimony fields incl. the rendered trust string, shipped in agent_tools PR #8/at-baj) and returns results to the agent. Results enter the rope as tool_result spans at the tail (Hot) — they age out under normal compaction by construction (mu-tlri drops from the tail only).

Design notes: follow an existing simple tool as the template (grep/read shape); read-only, no approval needed (PermissionLevel allow); spec-amend note per repo rule #9 if the tool list is spec-documented. The 'one discover surface, three corpora' t4c phase-3 direction is the eventual home — this is the memory corpus slice of it, not the unified surface.

Acceptance: in a live session the agent can recall a demoted memory by topic and the result carries the testimony label; tool listed in tool registry with tests per existing tool-test pattern.
close_reason: Merged PR #171 — memory_recall tool live, default-on in mu-solo; testimony labels pass through; tail-span economics by construction

title: mu-7rk-g: Multi-step auth exchange state registry (defer until 2nd mechanism)
description:
**mu-7rk decomposition phase G — DEFERRED.** Multi-step auth exchange state registry. Only needed once a 2nd auth mechanism (GSSAPI, SASL OAUTHBEARER, etc.) lands. Filed now so the design isn't lost.

## Why

Codex important #5 on original mu-7rk:

> "peer.auth_response has no in-flight challenge-state registry. It loops over all handlers and asks each to process an arbitrary server_state_id. This is unsafe for future multi-step mechanisms: state IDs are not bound to a mechanism, connection, expiry, or nonce."

BEARER (v1) is single-step — `step_initial` always returns `Done` or `Denied`. The `Challenge` outcome and `peer.auth_response` RPC exist as scaffolding for multi-step mechanisms but have no state machine yet. This bead adds the state machine **when needed**.

**Defer triggers:** any bead proposing GSSAPI / SASL OAUTHBEARER / TLS-cert mechanism implementation MUST land mu-7rk-g first.

## Scope

### In

- Per-connection pending-auth state registry:
  - Keyed by **cryptographically-random `server_state_id`** (e.g., 128-bit, generated via `rand::thread_rng()` over `getrandom`)
  - Stores: `mechanism: AuthMechanism`, `handler_state: Box<dyn AuthHandlerState>`, `created_at: Instant`, `single_use: bool`
  - Single-use semantics enforced by removal-on-lookup (`take`, not `get`)
  - Expiry via `created_at + AUTH_CHALLENGE_TTL` (default 30s); expired entries garbage-collected on next access OR via background task
- `AuthMechanismHandler` trait extension: `step_response` receives the `handler_state` from the registry, NOT just the wire `server_state_id`
- `peer.auth_response` dispatch handler:
  - Looks up state by `server_state_id`
  - Reject if: not found, expired, mechanism mismatch (state was for mechanism X, response claims mechanism Y), connection mismatch (state created on connection A, response on connection B)
  - All rejections return `Denied{MalformedExchange}`
- Tests:
  - Multi-step happy path (synthetic test handler that does `Challenge → Done`)
  - Replay attack: same `server_state_id` used twice → 2nd rejected
  - Cross-mechanism: state for mechanism X, response claims mechanism Y → rejected
  - Cross-connection: state created on A, response on B → rejected (depends on connection identity being trackable)
  - Expiry: state created, wait 31s, response → rejected as expired

### Out

- Implementing GSSAPI / SASL OAUTHBEARER / TLS-cert handlers (each is its own bead)
- Persistence of pending-auth state across daemon restarts (in-memory only; restart invalidates)

## Acceptance

- All five test cases above pass
- `cargo test --workspace` green; BEARER (single-step) flow continues to work without entering the registry
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean
- New workspace dep candidates: `rand` (likely already in tree); `getrandom` (likely already in tree). Verify both before adding.
- Audit: grep that `peer.auth_response` cannot succeed without going through the state registry lookup

## References

- Codex review: `~/.claude-personal/experiments/codex-review-mu-7rk-output.txt`
  - important #5 (multi-step state registry)
  - design_concern #3 ("future multi-step design is incomplete")
- Provenance: deferred from original mu-7rk because no 2nd mechanism is in flight; filed in decomp to prevent loss
- Composes with mu-7rk-c (gate) — the registry only matters for protected RPCs the gate enforces

title: qwen3-coder:30b tool calls degrade to text dialect through mu serve ollama provider
description: Smoke test 2026-06-04 (agentic bench prep): mu ask --provider ollama --model qwen3-coder:30b --tools read,grep emits the tool call as raw text in qwen's training-native XML dialect (<function=grep><parameter=pattern>...) instead of executing it — the loop ends with markup as the final answer. Control: raw POST to 10.1.1.143:11434/api/chat with a native tools array returns a proper structured message.tool_calls object for the same model, so ollama's chat template is fine. gpt-oss:20b and qwen3.6:35b-a3b-q8_0 work correctly through the identical mu path. Suspect mu's ollama provider endpoint choice or tool-schema formatting flips qwen3-coder into its text dialect. Repro: cd mu && mu ask --provider ollama --model qwen3-coder:30b --tools read,grep 'What is the resolver value in root Cargo.toml?'
close_reason: done

title: interactive cache viewer: web UI for context window visualization
description:
Web-based visualization of mu's context window layout, inspired by the Anthropic cache viewer but interactive and backed by real session data.

Core features:
- Proportional block display showing cached regions (up to 4 cache breakpoints)
- Color-coded by span kind (System, ToolSchema, User, Assistant, ToolCall, etc.)
- Click a block to zoom into its spans — see individual tool schemas, messages, compaction decisions
- Show cache hit/miss boundaries, evicted vs surviving spans after compaction
- Read from JSONL event log (same source of truth as everything else)

Stretch:
- Live mode: watch compaction happen in real time as context fills
- Diff mode: before/after compaction side-by-side
- Cost overlay: show per-region pricing (cached vs uncached token rates)

Implementation: localhost web server (could be a simple axum or warp binary in mu workspace), static HTML/JS frontend. TUI would be painful for click-to-zoom interaction.

Not a 6/15 item — observability/debugging tool for tuning compaction policy.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Friction B (folkloric capabilities) is the single biggest friction named across the c072d6d7 and 2026-05-30 sessions, and this bead is its fix. A concrete high-value instance — code-index/code_recall reachable only via token-expensive grep, not an in-loop tool — is filed as companion bead mu-re0s. See Sequencing review in agent-experience-frictions.md.

title: TUI: suppress empty 'assistant' block on tool-only turns
description:
Currently when the model emits a turn that is entirely tool calls (no text content blocks), the transcript renders a 'assistant — (no text in this turn)' block above the magenta tool block. That's cosmetic cruft.

Fix in crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs::render_transcript_lines: when an 'assistant_message_event' has empty joined text AND there's a 'tool_call' or 'tool_result' event in the same logical turn (i.e. next significant event in the log is a tool_call before the next done), skip rendering the empty assistant block entirely. The tool block itself is the visible record of what the model did this turn.

Discovered in mu-7n3 verify screenshot (2026-05-11).
close_reason: done

title: OperatorMark session-quality event + console rating capture + mu mark CLI
description:
Session-quality marks for mu sessions, stored in the event log per the single-source-of-truth thesis. Companion to mu-mucm's projection-layer work (cc_sessions / session_marks views + dashboard) — field names to be kept in sync with the session_marks view there.

WHY: event logs persist every session but carry no operator judgment; without a mark, degraded sessions can't be labeled, queried, or queued as replay-probe fixtures (see mu-mucm comment 2026-06-05 + postmortem-2026-06-05-relitigation-session in ~/.claude-personal/skills/postmortem/examples/). The moment of disgust is quit-time — capture must be one command or one click.

SCOPE (three seams, mapped 2026-06-05):
1. mu-core/src/event_log.rs: EventPayload::OperatorMark { rating: u8 (1-5), note: Option<String> } appended via the existing SessionEventLog::append(). Immutable; re-marking = newer event wins at projection time. Actor = User.
2. mu console (mu-coding/src/console/): first write path — POST /sessions/{daemon_id}/{session_id}/mark (form: rating + optional note) + render current mark in the session header (views.rs) + small form snippet (html.rs). Console is pure-GET today (mod.rs has 7 GET routes) — keep the write path minimal and obviously bounded.
3. mu mark <session-id> <rating> [note] CLI subcommand (mu-coding/src/bin/mu.rs) for quit-time capture without a browser; resolves daemon_id/session_id the same way the console scanner (console/data.rs scan_sessions) does, accepting unambiguous session-id prefixes.

OUT OF SCOPE: cc-session marks (task_log sidecar, session_ref column) and all projection/dashboard work — that's mu-mucm.

ACCEPTANCE: mark a real past session from CLI and from console; event appears in the session's jsonl; console header shows it; re-mark supersedes; mu-mucm's session_marks view can read it (coordinate field names before landing).
close_reason: done

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
IMPLEMENTED + smoke-verified 2026-06-06 (workspace mu-mu-operator-mark-5mwr, actor cc-personal-cff69449). Two commits, just ci green (honest exit code — first run's fmt-check failure was masked by a tail pipe, caught and fixed):

1. vztywqyl mu-core: EventPayload::OperatorMark { rating: u8, note: Option<String> }, serde kind=operator_mark, note skipped when absent; no-op arms at the 5 exhaustive matches; roundtrip + wire-shape test (field names = the mu-mucm session_marks contract).
2. rztupmsr capture layer: console::mark module (prefix resolution refuses ambiguity; append via from_jsonl + attach_disk_writer; RE-READS to verify durability since daemon append is best-effort), 'mu mark <session> <rating> [note] [--events-dir]' CLI, console POST /sessions/{d}/{s}/mark (first write route, path-segment guard) + header stars/note + re-mark form.

E2E smoke on a scratch COPY of the real degraded session 3dd40c3c (provider_kind=anthropic_api model=claude-opus-4-8 — corroborates the same-night API-path observation): CLI mark -> event 367 durable; console POST -> 303, header '★★★★☆ 4/5', re-mark superseded by id; rating=9 refused; ../ traversal refused. title: mu-tui: archived session restore picker (fzf+rg+jq enhancement on mu-770)
description:
## Problem

mu-770 covers archive/restore mechanics (session.remove, archive, restore,
list_archived RPCs + TUI affordances). The UX for restoring an archived
session is open — could be a basic list, or richer picker.

## Proposal

Implement restore-picker as fzf-style fuzzy-search popup with rg + jq
integration for filtering/preview:

- Open via shortcut from F3→F3 (e.g., `:restore` or key binding)
- Fuzzy-search across archived session names, last prompts, dates, models
- Live preview of highlighted archived session's last few turns
- Enter to restore, Esc to cancel

Builds on whatever basic restore UI mu-770 lands. If mu-770's TUI scope
already includes this, treat as a follow-up enhancement.

## Acceptance

- Picker opens on key/command from F3 session list
- Type-to-filter narrows the list
- Preview pane shows enough to identify the right session
- Restore action moves session back to active list

## Cross-refs

- mu-770 (session-lifecycle: archive/restore mechanics this builds on)
- Provenance: TUI feedback (mu daemon f3845422 session-2, 2026-05-15)

title: Context span elision with rehydration handles (lossless tool-result eviction)
description:
Old tool results in live context shrink to head + span-hash pointer; a rehydrate tool (context_recall) pulls any elided span back verbatim from the durable event log on demand.

WHY: at peak, tool_result was 73-85% of context in the 2026-06-04 baseline/replay sessions — context IS tool output. Elision is LOSSLESS (event sourcing already persists every result durably; the rope/source map already tracks span identity by hash) and costs zero model tokens, unlike summarization which risks paraphrased-code errors.

DESIGN SKETCH: after a tool result is N model-calls old (or below a relevance heuristic), renderer emits '[elided span <hash>: first 2 lines... | rehydrate via context_recall]'. context_recall(span_hash) returns the verbatim content as a fresh tool result. Generalizes the 'hash' half of HashAndSummaryPolicy (mu-8bkf) beyond compaction: same span-hash identity, applied continuously rather than at threshold.

RELATED: mu-8bkf (judge-backed compaction not wired), mu-zr9k (file_load injection cost), tool-result ingestion hygiene bead (same family, other end of the pipe).

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
FROZEN DESIGN (Spike C, operator + claude, 2026-06-05 evening — all four forks operator-ratified):

1. CADENCE — BATCHED AT CACHE-COLD MOMENTS. Elision rewrites rendered history, so each elision invalidates the prompt-cache prefix from that span onward. Continuous per-call elision (original sketch) pays that invalidation repeatedly and can cost MORE than the rent saved. Elide eligible spans in batches, timed to moments the cache is already cold: ask-after-gap > cache TTL (f1a0 measured 74% of interactive cache writes were gap-expiry rewrites — these moments are frequent), provider/model switch, and compaction-threshold crossings. Net effect: elision rides cache invalidations that were happening anyway.

2. EVICTION (v1) — DETERMINISTIC AGE+SIZE. Elide tool-result spans older than N model-calls AND larger than X tokens (constants first, config later; suggest N=8, X=1500 tokens as starting points to tune via telemetry). NO relevance scoring in v1: similarity-based relevance inherits the confirmation shape measured in the 611fbb3f autopsy (similarity to recent context keeps what agrees with where the conversation is going). Reference-checking and relevance are v2 candidates on top, judged by telemetry.

3. READ SPANS ARE ELIDABLE + EDIT GUARD. File reads are the bulk of the 73-85% — exempting them guts the feature. Handle text warns; ADDITIONALLY the edit tool fail-softs when its target file's read-span is currently elided ('the read of this file was elided — rehydrate <hash> or re-read before editing'). Rationale: 2e0h showed models edit from belief, not bytes; rehydration makes elision lossless only if the model actually rehydrates, so the edit tool enforces the rehydrate-before-edit invariant at the point of harm.

4. REHYDRATION — APPEND-ONLY. context_recall(span_hash) returns the verbatim content as a FRESH tool result appended at the live end (cache-correct: append-only context never invalidates prefix). The original handle remains as a ~2-line tombstone. Rehydrated copies re-age and re-elide to the SAME span hash — no new identity, no state growth. Splice-in-place rejected: every splice would pay the invalidation elision exists to avoid.

HANDLE FORMAT: '[elided <span_hash>: <first 2 lines of content> … (<N> lines, <M> tokens) — context_recall("<span_hash>") returns it verbatim]'. Span hash = the existing rope/source-map span identity (same hash family as HashAndSummaryPolicy / prefix_span_hashes in ContextAssembly — one identity everywhere).

LAYERING: elision is a PROJECTION choice; the durable event log is untouched (same log-vs-projection grain as the renderer journal and the 2e0h seam). Elision = first line of context defense (cheap, lossless, continuous-ish); judge-backed compaction (8bkf) = second line (threshold, lossy-by-policy); compaction must treat elided handles as already-minimal (skip, never re-process). 2e0h TIER-2 UNBLOCK: sidecar distillation routes logs/test-output; elision routes code-bearing results — the guardrail split 2e0h needed.

TELEMETRY FIRST-CLASS: count spans elided / tokens saved / rehydration rate / edit-guard trips, into ask_telemetry + duckdb views (mu_stats pattern). The N/X constants get tuned by measurement, not vibes.

title: or85.1: span age/size tracking + batched elision in the projection (cache-cold triggers)
description: Core of the frozen or85 design (see or85 comment 2026-06-05): track tool-result span age (model-calls) and size; at cache-cold moments (ask-after-gap > TTL, provider switch, threshold crossing) elide eligible spans (age > N=8 AND size > X=1500tok, constants to tune) in one batch. Handle format per frozen design. Projection-only — durable log untouched. Compaction treats handles as already-minimal.

title: or85.2: context_recall tool — span_hash to verbatim append-only rehydration
description: context_recall(span_hash) pulls the elided span verbatim from the durable event log and APPENDS it as a fresh tool result (never splices — cache-correct per frozen design). Same span-hash identity as rope/source-map. Rehydrated copies re-age and re-elide to the same hash. Register on daemon tool surface; tool description must tell the model when to use it (the handle text points here).

title: or85.3: edit-tool guard — fail-soft when target file's read-span is elided
description: Per frozen design fork 3: edit on a file whose read-result span is currently elided fail-softs with 'rehydrate <hash> or re-read before editing'. Enforces rehydrate-before-edit at the point of harm (2e0h lesson: models edit from belief, not bytes). Needs a cheap projection-side index: file path -> elided read-span hashes.

title: or85.4: elision telemetry — spans/tokens saved, rehydration rate, edit-guard trips
description: ask_telemetry fields + duckdb views (mu_stats pattern) for: spans elided per batch, tokens saved, rehydration rate (handles pulled back / handles created), edit-guard trips. The N/X eviction constants get tuned from this, not vibes. Also informs whether v2 relevance scoring is worth it.

title: Agent-view vs operator-view projections from the rope
description:
Introduce ProjectionTarget enum (AgentView | OperatorView) and two distinct rendering paths from the same retained pointer set. ProviderRenderer takes a target.

## Scope

In: ProjectionTarget enum; OperatorViewRenderer trait; per-span-kind operator-view rules (tool result -> structured table; skill activation -> one-line badge; tool schema -> name; memory injection -> collapsible); TUI changes (mu-pex / mu-jb3) consume operator-view rendering; tests for both targets.

Out: drill-down UI for inspecting agent-view at specific turn; operator-view variations (compact/detailed/debug) — start with one canonical view.

## Why

LLM tools conflate agent view and operator view. Humans and models have different attention budgets and relevance criteria. Separating projections: operator gets usable surface (skimmable tool results, collapsible context); agent gets full fidelity; debugging becomes possible (AgentView at turn N).

## Acceptance

AgentView produces output identical to current agent context rendering; OperatorView produces distinct usable rendering; TUI integrates (at least mu-pex system_prompt indicator sources operator-view); provenance preserved for span-click drill-down; workspace tests green.

## References

Full draft: ~/.claude-personal/drafts/mu-bead-4-projection-targets.md
Blocked-by: mu-ktq
Existing TUI: mu-pex, mu-62s, mu-jb3, mu-n48
close_reason: Landed in xszxyzyy 8920297c — per-SpanKind OperatorView rules: name/badge for tool/skill, collapsible for memory, summary for tool-result/compaction, byte-count for file-load. AgentView verbatim. Acceptance tests: AgentView unchanged for original 4, AgentView != OperatorView for 6 enriched kinds (>= 4 required). cargo test --workspace green; mu-core 136 → 141. mu-ovl closes; mu-ovl-tui follow-on bead filed separately.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Tracking bead for experiment 4 / 2026-05-13d. Sub-beads: mu-1n3 (mu-ovl-A SpanKind enrichment) blocks mu-dt2 (mu-ovl-B rendering rules); both block mu-ovl close. Experiment doc: ~/.claude-personal/experiments/goal-2026-05-13d-mu-ovl.md. Stretches: spec cross-ref, mu-ovl-tui follow-on bead, design memo. Out-of-scope: TUI integration, live agent loop adoption (mu-fb0).

Runner stdout/stderr capture should be bounded/truncation-aware so a noisy runner cannot cause unbounded memory growth.
