title: Exogenic-runtime protocol adapter: versioned registry + self-bootstrap + verifier
description:
Pluggable adapter system that translates exogenic runtime output (claude-code stream-json, codex events, etc.) into mu's event-log schema. Keyed by runtime + version.

## On exo-agent launch

1. **Get version**: query the exo-agent for its version string (e.g., `claude --version` or equivalent).
2. **Lookup**: registry has adapter for `(runtime, version)`? If yes → proceed.
3. **Bootstrap (if no adapter)**: see protocol below.
4. **Fail-closed default**: if bootstrap fails or is rejected, do NOT proceed unobserved. Operator must explicitly pass `--dangerously-trust-exogenic-agent` to launch a session mu can't observe/control. That session ships with a distinctive visual indicator (see mu-t71p alien-mode glyph).

## Bootstrap protocol

When mu encounters an unknown exo-agent version:

1. Mu presents the new exo-agent with mu's event-log schema (the target shape) + a few sample exo-agent events (the source shape captured during a smoke session)
2. Exo-agent generates a translator (likely a small Rust/Python script, or a JSON-mapping config) that takes its own output and produces mu-schema events
3. Mu's verifier agent reviews the translator against:
   - **Schema validation**: outputs parse as valid mu events
   - **Golden fixtures**: known exo-agent event traces → expected mu events (curated set)
   - **Diff against prior version's adapter**: if the new version's adapter agrees with the prior version on common events, that's strong evidence both are correct
4. **Dedupe**: if new version's adapter is semantically identical to a prior version's, alias the new version to the prior adapter (no separate file)
5. **Register**: write the adapter to `<mu-config>/adapters/<runtime>/<version>.{rs,py,json}` + checksum

## Bootstrap mitigation against silent-garbage adapters

- Require bootstrap to ALSO produce its own smoke fixtures (the exo-agent emits a few sample events for the verifier to use as ground truth)
- Until adapter accumulates N hours of operation without flags, log every event in shadow-comparison mode against a default/conservative parser
- Operator can manually flag a translation as wrong, which adds a regression fixture and triggers re-bootstrap

## --dangerously-trust-exogenic-agent flag

Mode for: 'I want to use this exo-runtime even though mu can't observe/control its compaction.' Equivalent in spirit to claude-code's bash-yolo mode. The session:
- Runs the exo-agent as a regular subprocess
- Streams stdout/stderr to terminal
- Mu does NOT shadow-log (no adapter)
- Mu does NOT drive compaction (no /clear-and-paste cycle)
- Session is marked with a distinctive visual mode indicator (alien glyph + scary mascot, per mu-t71p update)

## Bootstrap UX

Adapter generation happens at FIRST attempt to wrap a new version, at session start — not deferred mid-session. 'I don't know this version yet, let me build the adapter — this will take a minute' beats a mid-session adapter generation that breaks the loop.

## Open design questions

- What is the adapter's runtime form? Native Rust code (compiled into mu)? Python script invoked per event? Declarative JSON mapping? Probably JSON-mapping for simple cases, Python fallback for complex.
- What's the smoke-fixture format the bootstrap agent emits? Probably a session capture with known semantics (each event tagged with intent).
- How does mu present its event schema to the bootstrap agent? Likely as a JSON Schema doc + prose explanation.

## Dependencies

- HANDOFF wire format (blocks: mu-???) — adapter outputs feed into mu's event log, which can produce HANDOFF artifacts
- Foreign-runtime-wrapper (blocks: mu-???) — wrapper depends on registry to translate events

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Refinement (2026-05-23 conversation): two failure modes need distinct handling.

**Unknown VERSION** (bootstrap can't produce a working adapter): fail-closed default; --dangerously-trust-exogenic-agent required to proceed unobserved. Original bead body covers this case.

**Unknown MESSAGE TYPE within known version** (adapter mostly works but encounters a new event shape): NOT panic-level. Log the message verbatim, raise an operator alert, continue the session. Optionally accumulate unknowns for offered adapter-refinement bootstrap once a threshold is reached.

Add a separate --strict-protocol flag for operators who want unknown-message-types to also fail-closed. Default is graceful-degradation.

Rationale: claude-code adds backward-compatible message types periodically; treating every new type as a session-killer would make adapters brittle as claude-code evolves. The fail-closed stance is reserved for 'we have nothing for this protocol version,' not 'we have most but not all of this version.'

title: mu-d04a Phase 5: collapse/expand ratatui overlay (for nested children + long tool output)
description:
Decomposed from mu-d04a (mu-mlcg, 2026-05-31). Today tool results are a fixed 4/15-line preview + '… +N lines' marker (render.rs:296-298, app.rs:617); no expand. No overlay exists (picker.rs uses alt-screen; this must stay in-pane per the mu-solo single-pane contract).

WORK: a ratatui in-pane overlay (Clear + bordered Block) to expand a collapsed child event (Phase 3) OR a long tool result to a scrollable full view, then restore — the Claude Code ctrl+o pattern, one affordance serving both. Keybind-driven; define overlay lifecycle.

ACCEPTANCE: a collapsed child / long result expands to a scrollable overlay and restores cleanly; state-toggle unit test; cargo test --workspace + just ci green. blockedBy Phase 3 (needs nested children to expand).

title: Phase B: schema extraction per distinct event type (mu-nbi0)
description:
Phase B of mu-nbi0. Given the inventory from Phase A, extract a schema for each distinct event type and partition by version where version metadata is available.

## Scope

- For each event type seen: derive a schema (field names + types, required vs optional, examples)
- Across versions: detect schema drift (a field added/removed/typed-differently between v2.0.x and v2.1.x)
- Output: schemas-by-event-type JSON at scripts/exo-mining/schemas.json + version-drift JSON at scripts/exo-mining/version-drift.json

## Acceptance

- Every event type in the inventory has at least one schema entry
- Version-drift report identifies at least one example of schema evolution (if it exists in the corpus)
- Unit tests against fixture inputs
- cargo test --workspace / pytest green

title: Future: native Claude Code pump / bridge backend
description:
Support Claude Code natively as an agent backend/pump in addition to the direct Anthropic API provider. Treat Claude Code as an agent backend with its own session state, tools, MCP config, permissions, and subscription-backed execution path — not as a normal completion provider.

Candidate pump implementations:
- claude -p --resume turn-based wakeups
- persistent stream-json subprocess bridge if multi-turn works
- remote-control API if accessible
- Firefox/browser-debug automation as experimental fallback

The bridge participates in mu's mailbox/event system and externally pumps Claude Code turns when messages/events arrive.

Related memory: df99b167.

title: Add mu release binary to stable PATH (or symlink) for agent-router codex backend
description:
## Why

Overnight session tried to delegate mu-036 spec analysis to GPT via `agent-router --auth codex-oauth` (per the routing policy that says 'lean on GPT for spec-heavy tasks'). The router's codex backend expects `mu` to be on PATH for its mu_client integration. mu's release binary lives at `~/src/public_github/mu/target/release/mu` but isn't symlinked or PATH-exposed.

Prepending PATH inline (`PATH="$HOME/src/public_github/mu/target/release:$PATH" agent-router ...`) works one-shot but doesn't survive the next shell or programmatic invocation from a script that doesn't think to set PATH.

## What

Two reasonable options:
1. Symlink the release binary: `ln -sf ~/src/public_github/mu/target/release/mu ~/.local/bin/mu`. Lives alongside claude-code at `~/.local/bin` (per memory `reference_claude_binary.md` pattern). Risk: rebuilding the workspace overwrites the binary in-place, so the symlink stays valid — but a `cargo clean` breaks it. Acceptable; rerunning `cargo build --release` restores it.
2. Add the path to zsh PATH via `~/.zsh/zenvironment` (the canonical PATH-setup source per the secrets-config layout we already use). Always works; doesn't need a re-symlink after `cargo clean`.

I'd go with (2) — it's the same pattern `tq` and other build-time-installed binaries use already.

## Acceptance

`mu serve --help` works from a fresh terminal without PATH manipulation. `agent-router --auth codex-oauth ...` works without the user prepending PATH. The session-router falls back gracefully (already does — errors say 'No such file or directory: mu') if the binary is genuinely missing.
close_reason: Already done — ~/.local/bin/mu is a symlink to ~/src/public_github/mu/target/release/mu. Confirmed 2026-05-15 during dot-claude-personal gitsah setup work. Use case (agent-router codex backend finding mu on PATH) is now operational.

title: Metrics framework: per-call time decomposition + quality proxies
description:
Design + implementation arc for mu's ongoing observability. Came out of refinement-experiment discussion 2026-05-11: per-request hard metrics vs per-request quality metrics are different beasts, and we should be deliberate about which we track and why.

Captured in commit/results doc: refinement arm 0/3 forbidden vs baseline 3/3, at 2.2x wall time. That cost is fine for delegation-style work but expensive for interactive — we want to be able to SEE the cost decomposition (TTFT, streaming, tool time, thinking) rather than just total wall.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Two classes of metrics, very different to track
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Hard metrics (always trackable, no ground truth):
  - TTFT = AwaitingFirstToken → Streaming transition (mu-035)
  - Streaming duration
  - ToolExecuting duration (sum)
  - Wall end-to-end
  - Tokens in/out, cache hit %, tool count
  - Per-provider error rate

These don't tell us correctness but they DO catch drift: median TTFT per provider per hour would catch 'codex is stalled' in minutes instead of the 14h silence we had on 2026-05-10/11.

Quality metrics (need fixture):
  - Success rate vs ground truth
  - Refinement passes to clean
  - Cost-to-correct = total tokens to 1 successful response

These need a benchmark fixture (like the spec-summary task in protocol-eval). Outside fixtures, every request is unique.

Proxy metrics (work without ground truth):
  - Refinement-fire rate (if we run grader on everything)
  - User-marked retry rate (TUI accept/reject affordance)
  - Tool error rate (model-induced vs system-induced)

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Implementation arc
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Now-easy (post mu-035 A/B/C + mu-038):
  - daemon.usage_history projection RPC returning median/p95 of
    TTFT / streaming_duration / tool_duration / wall, grouped by
    (provider, model, time-bucket).
  - Backed by aggregating ProviderStatus events from the event log
    (or live tracker snapshots once tracker is in Sessions — Phase D).

Soonish (mu-xln existing P2 bead — F5 Usage Dashboard):
  - Renders the daemon.usage_history projection.
  - Per-provider/model rows with TTFT p95, streaming duration p95,
    tool-call-count distribution.
  - Cost estimate column once we have a per-provider price table.

Later (quality proxies):
  - TUI affordance: '👍 helpful' / '👎 not what I wanted' / 'try again'
    after each session.done.
  - Capture in event log (new EventPayload variant: UserFeedback).
  - Surface as a quality-proxy column in F5.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Cross-references
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Depends on:
  - mu-iwq Phase D (daemon.outstanding_calls + ProviderStatusTracker
    in Sessions) — for snapshot enumeration. Phase A/B/C already
    give us the per-call event stream.
  - mu-038 projection-query patterns — same code area, same style.

Composes with:
  - mu-xln (F5 dashboard) — its data source.
  - mu-036 autonomous loop — refinement-fire-rate is a great
    quality proxy for autonomous sessions.

Not in scope here:
  - Cost mapping (provider+model → $/1M tokens). Should be its
    own small bead because it's an updated-rarely lookup table.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

When to implement: not blocking daily-driver. Worth picking up
after mu-iwq Phase D lands or as a parallel slice if a slow
provider catches our attention again.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Per-model corrections rate is a great proxy metric to add to the design — break down refinement_fire_rate by (provider, model) and you get a comparative quality fingerprint. Different models have different priors (today's experiment confirmed gpt-5.5 has a strong login/logout prior; haiku-4.5 may have different ones). Tracking corrections_per_call by model surfaces:
  - which models are best for which task types (factual extraction, code review, etc.)
  - whether a model is degrading over time (vendor pushed an update? prior drift?)
  - cost-of-correctness tradeoff (cheaper-but-more-corrections vs more-expensive-but-fewer)

Add to the F5 dashboard sketch: per-(provider, model) row showing corrections_per_100_calls when grader was enabled. Surfaces 'haiku gets 5/100 wrong on summarisation; gpt-5.5 gets 33/100; sonnet gets 2/100' or whatever the truth turns out to be.

comments.author: claude
comments.text:
Phase 1 (A-scope) landed in commit rwyyuout 28c677c6 (`feat(mu-pex): daemon.usage_history projection RPC — Phase 1 (A-scope)`).

What landed:
- New RPC `daemon.usage_history` with (since_unix_ms?, until_unix_ms?, time_bucket_ms?) request params, returns rows grouped by (provider, model, time-bucket).
- Per-row distributions (median+p95): tool_total_ms, wall_ms, model_call_latency_ms (proxy: AssistantMessageEvent.ts − ContextAssembly.ts).
- Per-row sums: input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, reasoning_tokens, tool_call_count.
- Per-row counts: session_count, error_count.
- Aggregates over in-memory sessions visible via `sessions.snapshot_for_listing()`.
- 12 new unit tests; full workspace test suite green (289 passed, 1 ignored).

Explicitly None in Phase 1 (will land in Phase 1.5 / Iteration 2):
- `ttft_ms` — needs durable `EventPayload::ProviderStatusUpdate` (currently wire-only mu-035).
- `streaming_ms` — same reason.

Phase 1.5 plan (queued in task list):
1. Add `EventPayload::ProviderStatusUpdate` variant (mu-core/src/event_log.rs).
2. Extend `forwarder.rs::to_log_event` to map `AgentEvent::ProviderStatus` → that variant alongside the existing wire-side mapping.
3. Update `payload_kind_str` in dispatch.rs and the TUI's sidechannel branch in render_transcript_lines.
4. Extend `usage_history::extract_per_session_metrics` to compute TTFT (AwaitingFirstToken→Streaming transition) and streaming_ms (time spent in Streaming per call).
5. Populate the two Option<PercentileStats> fields in UsageHistoryRow.

Still out of scope at the bead level:
- mu-xln (F5 dashboard) — UI consumer of this RPC.
- UserFeedback EventPayload + TUI affordance.
- Cost mapping (provider+model → $/1M tokens) — its own follow-up bead.
- Cross-daemon and disk-only historical coverage — gated on mu-iwq Phase D + mu-935 FileBackend.

comments.author: claude
comments.text:
Phase 1.5 landed in commit lrslqwuv b0944d40 — durable ProviderStatusUpdate + TTFT/streaming populated.

Changes:
- New `EventPayload::ProviderStatusUpdate` variant (mu-core/src/event_log.rs) mirroring the wire-side `ProviderStatusEvent` minus session_id.
- forwarder `to_log_event` now maps `AgentEvent::ProviderStatus` → that variant. Wire-side `translate_event` unchanged: both projections still derive from the same AgentEvent source — they no longer disagree about durability.
- payload_kind_str (dispatch) + derive_status_from_events (discovery) + render_transcript_lines sidechannel (mu-tui) all extended to handle the new variant.
- `usage_history::extract_per_session_metrics` now walks ProviderStatusUpdate events; consecutive transition emissions (elapsed_ms == 0) yield duration samples for the prior state. Periodic ticks (elapsed_ms > 0) are skipped — they share started_at with their opening transition.
- `aggregate_into_rows` populates `row.ttft_ms` and `row.streaming_ms` via `percentile_stats` over pooled samples. Both stay None when a group has no samples — same shape as Phase 1.

Tests added (+6 over Phase 1, total now 295 active / 1 ignored):
- extraction_computes_ttft_from_awaiting_first_token_transition
- extraction_periodic_ticks_do_not_close_a_state_period
- extraction_records_streaming_periods_separately (interrupted Streaming yields 2 samples)
- extraction_unterminated_state_period_is_dropped (in-flight or aborted)
- aggregation_populates_ttft_and_streaming_in_row
- forwarder::log_records_provider_status_update

Still queued (parking-lot, not in any commit):
- mu-xln (F5 dashboard) — UI consumer of daemon.usage_history.
- TUI feedback affordance (👍/👎/try again) + `UserFeedback` EventPayload variant — quality proxy.
- Cost mapping (provider+model → $/1M tokens) — its own follow-up bead (out-of-scope per the original bead).
- Cross-daemon / disk-only historical coverage — gated on mu-iwq Phase D + mu-935 FileBackend.
- Per-comment from 2026-05-11 20:14: per-(provider, model) corrections_per_100_calls when grader enabled — needs refinement integration first.

comments.author: claude
comments.text:
Phase 1.5 follow-up fix in commit sunvrnkp 2b84e8b1 — discovered via hand-test against anthropic-api+haiku.

Two bugs in the initial Phase 1.5 transition-detection logic (both in extract_per_session_metrics):

1. **The transition filter `elapsed_ms == 0` was too strict.** The agent loop emits the AwaitingFirstToken→Streaming transition with elapsed_ms set to call_started.elapsed() (loop_.rs:880), not 0. The filter silently dropped that emission. Net effect: `ttft_ms.count` was counting gaps *between asks* (the only AFT→AFT pair the filter saw); `streaming_ms.count` was always 0.

2. **No Done-driven close-out.** The agent loop doesn't emit a Streaming→Idle transition on session.done. One ask's open Streaming state would bleed into the inter-ask gap before the next AFT entry, polluting streaming_ms with wait time.

Fix: identify transitions by started_at_unix_ms uniqueness (ticks within a period share their period's started_at; transitions always introduce a new one); close out pending state on Done using the Done's timestamp.

Hand-test now produces credible numbers:
- ttft_ms p95 = 1184ms for haiku via anthropic-api (TTFT-dominated, as expected for tiny prompts)
- streaming_ms p95 = 62ms for 5-token responses (~10ms/token)
- wall_ms matches Done.elapsed_ms exactly

Tests added: extraction_streaming_entry_with_nonzero_elapsed_still_counts + extraction_done_closes_out_pending_state_between_asks. Workspace tests: 297 active, 1 ignored.

title: Cross-provider command abstraction (/bg /background /goal /model /provider /effort)
description:
mu's TUI needs a common command-surface layer that normalizes user-facing slash commands across providers. Each provider keeps its own internal "language" for these concepts; mu's command layer presents a unified surface and dispatches into provider-specific implementations. Operator-flagged 2026-05-22 as daily-driver-blocking: today the same intent ("change model", "go background", "set goal") has different syntax per provider.

## Why

Per `specs/architecture/claude-code-feature-mapping.md` §9: "the skill mechanism IS the command mechanism. Don't add CLI built-ins; add skills." Per §3, §12, §16, §17: each command has provider-dependent semantics. Example mappings the abstraction must accommodate:

- `/effort xhigh` → Anthropic: extended-thinking budget; OpenAI Codex: `reasoning_effort=high`; OpenRouter: pass through if upstream supports
- `/background` (alias `/bg`) → supervisor pattern per §D of the design-influences doc; not all providers offer native equivalents
- `/model <name>` → switch active model within current session (cache invalidation per §A); all providers
- `/provider <name>` → switch active provider mid-session (more invasive than `/model`)
- `/goal` → goal-protocol skill invocation (already exists at the skill layer)

The architectural target: a command layer that

1. Owns the namespace (`/bg`, `/goal`, etc. are mu-defined identifiers)
2. Resolves command intent to a provider-aware execution path
3. Falls back gracefully when a provider doesn't support a command (graceful degradation, not silent no-op)
4. Surfaces provider-native equivalents in `--help` text for transparency
5. Composes with the existing skill system rather than replacing it (skills ARE commands)

## Scope

In:
- `Command` trait or registry pattern in `mu-coding` with provider-specific impls
- Initial commands: `/model`, `/provider`, `/effort`, `/background` (alias `/bg`), `/goal`, `/focus`, `/btw`
- Integration with the existing command-palette (mu-pmh) and skill system
- Per-command provider-capability table that mu-tui consults to render available commands
- Help-text generation that exposes both mu's unified surface AND the provider-native equivalent (for debuggability)

Out:
- Skill discovery/permission model (orthogonal — see mu-ywr)
- Per-provider command-language documentation as a separate doc bead (capture in commit notes)
- /effort dial behavior beyond the three named providers (defer)

## Cross-refs

- mu-pmh (TUI command palette) — primary integration point
- mu-pex / mu-jb3 — agent-view + projection consumers
- mu-ywr — discovery + permission model for memory/MCP/skills
- specs/architecture/claude-code-feature-mapping.md §9, §12, §16, §17
- specs/architecture/claude-code-design-influences.md §D (background-agent / supervisor)

## Acceptance

- Single command-registry abstraction in code with at least 3 commands fully implemented
- Provider-capability table documented in code or specs/
- Unit tests for cross-provider dispatch (per command × per provider)
- Help-text surfaces native equivalents
- `/model` and `/provider` invalidate cache correctly per the §A cache-discipline contract

title: mu-memory: event-pointer index + recall surface, separated from compaction
description:
## Problem

mu has no memory subsystem yet. Sessions accumulate context within their
event log, but there's no first-class concept of "memory" — durable,
queryable references to facts/decisions/patterns that survive across
sessions and inform future ones. This is foundational for the continuity
goal ("you-ness preservation"): a fresh session should be able to recall
relevant prior context without the user re-supplying it every time.

claude-code uses a per-host SQLite (`~/.local/share/agent.sqlite`) for
this. mu can do better by leaning on its own substrate.

## Design

### Memory as event-pointer index

Memory entries are typed pointers into the existing event log, not a
parallel data store. A memory entry says "event N (or event-range N..M)
in session S is relevant for purpose P." This means:

- No duplication of content (the events ARE the content)
- Provenance is intrinsic (you can always walk back to the source event)
- Privacy/redaction model is unified (cull the event → memory points to a
  tombstone or relocated copy)

### Memories as events (recursive / self-describing)

A new memory entry is itself emittable as an event in the log. The act of
remembering is auditable, and memory creation/update is a first-class
event-stream operation — no separate write path.

### Relocation on event-log cull

When old audits/event-logs are culled (compaction, age-out, retention
policy), still-used memory entries get relocated:

- The pointed-at event content is copied into the "current" log location
- An origin-appendage records where it came from
- Multiple relocations over time accumulate a chain of appendages
- Memory pointers update to the new location atomically with the cull

Memory survives event-log lifecycle changes without losing provenance.
Enterprise retention (90-day cull, GDPR right-to-be-forgotten, etc.)
becomes tractable without breaking memory.

### Recall (retrieval surface)

Distinct layer over the memory index:

- Query: by tag, type, session, time-range, free-text, embedding-similarity
- Score/rank: relevance ranking with policy-driven weights
- ACL filter: capability check before returning matches (ties into
  companion discovery+permissions bead filed same session)
- Surface as both: a tool agents can invoke, and a TUI affordance

### Separated from compaction

Memory storage and event-log compaction are explicitly separate concerns
with separate policies. This means we can change our minds independently
on: aggressiveness of compaction, retention shape of memory, whether
enterprise policies override either. Compaction is mu-kgu's domain;
memory + recall is this bead's.

## Acceptance

- Memory entries can be created (as events), referenced (by pointer-walk),
  recalled (by query)
- Recall surface exists as both an agent-callable tool and a TUI affordance
- Event-log cull preserves memory pointers via relocation + origin-chain
- Memory policy and compaction policy configurable independently
- Capability checks gate recall results

## Cross-refs

- mu-kgu (CompactionPolicy trait — explicitly separated concern)
- mu-cha (F2 session tree — recall could surface there)
- mu-ib5 (F9 mailbox view — mailboxes are an event channel memory may
  want to point into)
- mu-7rk-* (capability decomposition — provides ACL substrate)
- memory 03b44633 (capability broker pattern)
- Companion to discovery+permissions bead (filed same session)
- Compares to claude-code's `~/.local/share/agent.sqlite` (different
  substrate; mu's event-pointer design is intrinsically auditable)
- Provenance: user feedback session 2026-05-15
notes:
## Addendum 2026-05-18: ingestion of .md context files

CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md / project-CLAUDE.md / skill files / etc. are
operator-curated context that today live as plain text on disk. To get
them into the memory substrate without inventing a parallel storage:

### Ingest pipeline

`text → typed memory-ingest event → event log → memory pointers`

A new event variant (e.g. `MemoryIngest`) carries:
- source path (`~/.claude-personal/CLAUDE.md`, etc.)
- content hash (so re-ingest is idempotent)
- raw content (chunked if large)
- semantic chunking metadata (which section / heading hierarchy)

Once the ingest event lands on the log, memory entries can point into
its byte-range the same way they point into any other event content.
The recall surface treats it identically to any other memory pointer:
no special path, no parallel store.

### Update on file change

Watcher or explicit re-ingest detects content-hash change:
- If unchanged → no-op
- If changed → new ingest event; superseded chunks' memory entries get a
  supersedes/supersededBy edge so recall can prefer the latest by default
  but historical pointers still resolve for provenance

### Why this fits mu-phl's existing design

- The .md file is just another event source.
- Provenance is intrinsic — every recall hit can name 'CLAUDE.md line 42'.
- Compaction policies apply uniformly (a stale CLAUDE.md content event
  can be culled with relocation; memory pointers survive).
- No special-cased loader / parser / config; the ingest event is the
  surface.

### Acceptance additions

- `mu memory ingest <path>` CLI subcommand (or RPC) takes a .md file,
  chunks it, emits ingest events
- Idempotent on unchanged content (hash check)
- TUI shows ingested sources alongside session-derived memory in recall
- Supersedes-edges work for re-ingested files

### Operator UX

- Auto-ingest CLAUDE.md from the working-directory when `mu serve`
  starts a session, if the file is present
- Operator-confirmable: prompt before first ingest, remember the decision
- AGENTS.md / skill files: same pipeline, different default tags so
  recall can preferentially rank capability-relevant vs. context-narrative
  content

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
## 2026-05-30 amendment pointer: hierarchical recall + lowest-level addressing

Filed alongside specs/architecture/agent-experience-frictions.md (this session) and mu-5xbp (concurrent consolidator).

Two extensions to mu-phl's design surfaced in this session's discussion. Both are amendments to the same substrate this bead lays out; neither requires rewriting it.

### 1. Recall queries operate across hierarchical compaction layers

The discussion proposes a hierarchical-memory shape (L0 live event log → L1 recent summaries → L2 medium-distilled → L3 short stable characterizations always in hot prefix). Each level's spans are produced by the concurrent consolidator (mu-5xbp). Semantic recall queries should fan out across all layers ranked by relevance, with the layer acting as a coarse cost prior — L3 hits are cheap to inject, L0 hits are expensive and only injected when the cheaper layers are insufficient.

Concrete extension to mu-phl's `RecallProvider`-style interface:

```rust
fn recall_across_layers(
    query: &str,
    capability: &Capability,
    relevance_threshold: f32,
    token_budget: u64,
) -> Vec<RecalledItem>;
```

Returns a token-bounded set of cross-layer hits, ranked by relevance, with the layer noted on each item so the renderer can decide placement (L3 → stable prefix, L1/L0 → live tail or skip).

This is the technical fix for Friction D-thin-hook / I-thin-hook in agent-experience-frictions.md — the polymath observation lives at L3 and fires every turn at low cost; the full conversation that produced it lives at L0 and only loads on explicit recall.

### 2. Lowest-level addressing uses daemon:session:event_seq triple

At the lowest compaction level (L0), memory pointers should address `daemon_id + session_id + event_seq` — not just a flattened event ID, not a content hash. This preserves full-fidelity callback for every event referenced at any compaction level. A summary at L3 retains pointers to its L2 source spans; each L2 span to its L1 source; each L1 to its L0; each L0 addresses `daemon:session:event_seq`. Drilling down from any summary terminates at the durable event log.

Concretely: the `SessionEvent` envelope already carries `session_id` and a monotonic `seq`. Adding `daemon_id` to the addressing tuple (or making it available via the mu-jsde `EventLogView` query object) gives the cross-daemon-cross-session full-walkthrough property this design needs.

This is an implementation discipline mu-phl Phase 1 should honor from the start — the provenance chain breaks if any layer flattens to a content hash without preserving the underlying address.

### Cross-references

- specs/architecture/agent-experience-frictions.md — Friction A (wall), D, H, I
- mu-5xbp — the consolidator that produces the L1/L2/L3 layers this recall queries
- mu-jsde — the EventLogView query object that resolves daemon:session:event_seq lookups
- mu-kle — the storage substrate roadmap; Phase 1 should already use the triple

title: mu-tui: per-session control surface (model selection + command palette + picker)
description:
## Problem

Sessions on mu currently can't have their model changed mid-session, and
mu doesn't yet have an analog of claude-code's slash commands operating
per-session. If a session is created with the wrong model, it sits unusable
until the program quits.

## Proposal

Add a per-session command surface analogous to claude-code's slash commands,
but operating against the selected session rather than globally:

- `/model <name>` — change the session's model (requires model picker UX)
- `/provider <name>` — switch underlying provider on the session
- `/goal <text>` — fire a goal-protocol-style autonomous run
- `/status` — show this session's current state
- `/rewind <n>` — undo last N turns
- `/rename <text>` — give session a friendly name (surfaces in F3 list)
- room for mu-specific commands beyond what claude-code has

Includes a model picker (list + autocomplete + favorites/most-frequent-first)
since `/model` needs an interactive selector.

## Acceptance

- Selected session in F3 exposes a command palette (`:` prefix or slash)
- Commands apply only to the selected session, not global state
- `/model` switching produces fresh provider context without restarting mu
- Model picker shows favorites first; type-to-filter

## Cross-refs

- F3 session-list summary bead (`/rename` surfaces there)
- Provenance: TUI feedback (mu daemon f3845422 session-2, 2026-05-15)

title: ai-review verdict_of mis-parses reviewers that quote VERDICT tokens in prose
description:
verdict_of() (scripts/ai-review.sh:174-182) greps the reviewer's ENTIRE stdout
for 'VERDICT[^A-Za-z]{1,8}(REJECT|APPROVE)', REJECT-first. A reviewer whose
prose quotes the literal token "VERDICT: REJECT" (e.g. while explaining how the
gate parses verdicts) is therefore classified REJECT regardless of its actual
final-line verdict.

Observed live 2026-06-06 on the mu-ai-review-log-escaping-augj gate run:
qwen3-coder:30b emitted a final line of 'VERDICT: APPROVE' with a sound-approval
conclusion, but quoted '"VERDICT: REJECT"' mid-prose while discussing verdict
parsing -> verdict_of returned REJECT -> panel SPLIT -> ESCALATE (exit 3), a
false split (gpt-oss:20b cleanly APPROVEd; the change is unanimous-approve on
merits). This blocked an otherwise-clean PR pending operator adjudication.

Fix: parse only the LAST VERDICT-bearing line (or last line) rather than
grepping the whole output. e.g. take the final line matching /VERDICT/i, or
tail the output and match there. Keep the markdown-dressing tolerance. Add a
regression fixture: reviewer output that quotes the opposite verdict in prose
but ends with the real verdict line.

Surfaced by mu-ai-review-log-escaping-augj; not fixed there (different concern,
verdict_of is outside that bead's escaping scope).
close_reason: PR #207 merged: verdict_of parses only the last VERDICT-bearing line (whole-output fallback) — kills the false-split class where a reviewer quotes the opposite token in prose. Discovered live by the augj worker as a gate exit-3; root-caused instead of overridden.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Fixed in PR #207 (https://github.com/sahuagin/mu/pull/207) alongside mu-ai-review-log-escaping-augj. verdict_of now parses only the last VERDICT-bearing line (grep -iE VERDICT | tail -n 1). Root cause of the false panel SPLIT/ESCALATE on the escaping change. (Bead originally created in the worker's jj-workspace DB; imported to canonical so its ID matches the PR trailer.) Director: close on PR #207 merge.

title: interview discipline for querying workers about operating conditions
description: When an operator or agent queries a worker for a report on its own operating conditions/friction, report quality depends on interview discipline: non-leading questions; the interlocutor flags their own uncertainty; treat the report as evidence, not confession; pressure-test first-pass framings (they tend to be subtly self-centered — pushback corrects toward the structural answer). Part of the worker-instrumentation stack; applies to how operators query workers generally and to the paired wall-vs-pull experiment.

title: Re-run refinement-loop experiment when openai-codex is healthy
description:
Pre-registered H4/H5/H6 are at ~/src/claude-personal/research/protocol-eval/docs/predictions.md (2026-05-11 section).

Blocker: openai-codex backend stalled >100s with zero wire signal as of 2026-05-11 ~10am. Token is fresh (expires 2026-05-20), defensive layer surfaces it as MuClientTimeout('session.done got ack, awaiting terminal') — so when codex comes back, the experiment will fail-fast if it fails at all.

To re-run:
  cd ~/src/claude-personal/research/protocol-eval
  uv run --project . python3 scripts/run_mu_delegation.py --arm refinement --trials 3

Then write up results in results/delegation_spec_summary_2026-05-11.md (extend the existing Refinement section).

H4: passed >= 2/3 (vs baseline 0/3)
H5: median refinement passes <= 2
H6: required-fact coverage >= 85% aggregate
close_reason: done

title: compaction_assembly.policy_id is a generic constant — policies indistinguishable in durable logs
description: All live compaction events record policy_id='compaction-policy' (verified across baseline c76f6949, both 2026-06-04 replays). The README's policy ladder (SpanFamilyDropPolicy heuristic / HashAndSummaryPolicy mock / live judge) and the planned provider-native comparison need DISTINGUISHABLE ids in the durable event, or the stats layer (compactions view, dashboard policy-comparison panel) cannot attribute wall-clock/reduction/cost per policy as they come online (mu-8bkf). Fix: policy_id should carry the concrete policy name (e.g. 'span-family-drop', 'hash-and-summary:haiku-4-5'). Stats consumer already keyed on it: ~/src/claude-personal/scripts/mu_stats.sql compactions view.

title: schemaless session metrics: HashMap<String, MetricValue> wire format
description:
Replace the flat SessionStatus struct with a schemaless HashMap<String, MetricValue> snapshot format on the MCP wire. MetricValue is a three-variant enum (String, U64, F64).

Benefits:
- Extensible without struct churn
- Enables per-metric capability gating
- Enables subscribe-to-subset
- Enables future delta mode (send only changed metrics) without wire-format break

Current flat struct works fine at current scale but will need migration if field count grows. Delta mode deferred — snapshot-per-push is adequate for foreseeable agent counts.

Carlos rule: 'this isn't market data.' — not urgent, but do it before the field count doubles.

title: observer architecture: process-layer auditors over worker event streams
description: goal-protocol already has ARTIFACT-layer outside-observers: auditor-per-seam (one narrow checklist per architectural seam, parallel, consolidate), multi-model pre-PR / pre-approval review (different models catch different classes), and the verify-claim `## Files` block machine-checked against the actual diff (structural hallucination detection; motivated by a prior fictional-refactor PR). The missing layer is a PROCESS-layer auditor that watches a worker's typed event stream against process invariants, parallel to the seam auditors watching the diff. Governing principle of the whole protocol: invariants survive by being CHECKED, not trusted — the check fires whether or not the worker remembers. Source of record: internal session d7cb0b99 synthesis. Children: deterministic process auditors; chunk-fit detector; triage layer.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Folded from 2026-06-01 session (design note: ~/.claude-personal/notes/design-prepr-review-and-degradation-gate.md). Deltas: (1) new bead mu-6qst — artifact-layer review gate applied to DIRECT in-session work (just ci-aipr); goal-protocol review only ever fired for spawned teams, but the mu-qc08 deadlock was authored in direct work, unreviewed. (2) Escalation ladder = differential diagnosis for the triage layer: escalate a reject across providers; all-fail means design/arch problem (trust it, operator now), any-succeed means a capacity datapoint; log unanimous-reject since correlated cross-provider dips are rare-but-real per claude-capacity-incidents.md. (3) Worked instance for the mu-pr6r.1 deterministic auditors: output/exit mismatch — a worker that produced correct output then got killed/timed-out/exited non-zero (the stop-fired-but-no-stop family). mu-qc08 (merged PR 150) had exactly this signature in its first failed spawn; an auditor would have flagged it day-one vs the ~5-day silent window. Incident: notes/incident-2026-06-01-mu-qc08-worker-orchestration-deadlock.md.

title: deterministic process auditors (loop / claim-drift / repeated-call / stop-not-fired)
description: Tractable, deterministic process-invariant checks over a worker's typed event stream: loop-without-state-change; identical tool-call (same name+args) repeated N times; tool-success-rate dropping over the last N attempts; stop-criterion conditions firing but no stop emitted; a claim made without the verifying tool-call present in the stream. Each narrow and deterministic; run in parallel; consolidate findings — the seam-auditor pattern applied at the process layer. Ship these before the fuzzy ones.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Shipped 2nd deterministic auditor: output_exit_mismatch (PR #153, merged). Detects the mu-qc08 signature — substantive output (Done / non-empty Text block) then abnormal termination (WorkerFailed/Timeout=High, WorkerExited!=0=Medium). Remaining deterministic checks: loop-without-state-change, tool-success-rate-dropping, stop-fired-but-no-stop, claim-without-verifying-call.

title: chunk-fit detector (triage, not judgment): re-planning signal from process signature
description: Detect 'the work was decomposed too large for the worker to hold in context' from PROCESS SIGNATURE — context churn (re-reading the same files/beads, re-asking the same questions of the codebase) vs productive iteration on a narrow surface. This is a RE-PLANNING trigger (orchestrator stops, re-plans, reissues the goal), NOT a stop criterion and NOT a worker failure — just a specific signal. Fuzzy invariant: surface CANDIDATE cases for the judgment layer / human attention; do not make the call autonomously (triage, not judgment).

title: triage layer between worker streams and the operator
description: A breadth+consistency layer that holds N workers' typed event streams concurrently and applies stated escalation rules uniformly (no fatigue), escalating gross signals — stalled a long time; improvising past the normal pattern; reports getting terser; errors opened with none closed — so a judgment layer runs only on filtered input. Trades less judgment for more breadth/consistency; does the prerequisite filtering that consumes the judgment layer's time. CAVEAT: 'intelligent notification filtering' has a long over-promise history; the differentiator here is typed events + explicit escalation rules + calibration observable against the log afterward. Build-and-measure; don't assume it clears the bar.

title: Foreign-runtime wrapper: shadow event log + /clear-and-paste compaction
description:
Mu wraps an exogenic runtime (initially claude-code; later codex/etc.) as a managed sub-process. Mu owns the compaction loop while the wrapped runtime handles inference + tool execution.

## Lifecycle

1. **Spawn**: launch exo-agent with stream-json IO. Use the adapter (mu-p49v) for the agent's version to translate events.
2. **Shadow event log**: every event from the agent's stream-json is translated and recorded into mu's event log. mu has full parallel visibility.
3. **Watch token count**: agent reports usage in its events; mu tracks cumulative window utilization.
4. **At threshold (mu's policy, not the agent's)**: pause input, decide strategy:
   - **Strategy A**: drive native /compact, inject augmentations via SessionStart hook on source=compact
   - **Strategy B**: drive /clear, render mu-compacted context (using whatever compactor model mu chose), paste as next user message
5. **Resume**: wait for agent's post-clear/-compact SessionStart hook to complete (confirmed via shadow-event-log seeing the hook fire), then send the next user message.

## Compactor model independence

The compaction model is decoupled from the inference model. A Haiku-class compactor can summarize for an Opus-class session — economics get materially better on long sessions.

## Operator UX

- Default policy: compact-at-N% with operator confirmation ('You're at 75% — want me to compact?')
- Operator can change policy mid-session
- Operator can manually invoke compaction at any threshold
- mu-u6hc (OS-memory-map view) works on the shadow event log → `mu context --session <wrapped-session>` renders the same view for wrapped sessions

## Failure recovery

If the wrapped runtime panics or hits a network error, mu has the full event log shadow-recorded and can rebuild a fresh session from it (via /clear and replay-as-context).

## Dependencies

- HANDOFF wire format (mu-nt7j): blocks
- Exo-adapter registry (mu-p49v): blocks
- mu-u6hc (context-window view): non-blocking — works once both this bead and mu-u6hc are landed

## Open questions

- How is operator-mid-session compaction-policy change communicated to a wrapped agent? (Probably via mu's TUI — wrapped session doesn't need to know)
- What's the boundary between strategies A and B? Operator pick? Cost-based heuristic? Default to A, escalate to B if A doesn't compact enough?
- How does mu signal to the wrapped agent that it's being wrapped? (Probably not at all — the agent doesn't need to know; it just runs.)

title: mu-036 Phase D: DelegateGrader + ExternalSignal goal check methods
description: DelegateGrader: between iterations, autonomous session calls session.ask against a grader session, parses JSON {satisfied, reason}, terminates if satisfied. ExternalSignal: loop waits for session.external_signal notification with a given name. Tests: parent + grader on FauxProvider with canned responses; assert termination at iteration where grader returns satisfied=true.

title: mu-solo: render refresh clobbers terminal selection — can't copy from a live session in plain kitty
description:
Operator report 2026-06-04 (live-fire session): in a plain kitty window (no zellij/tmux pane to scope the selection), mouse-selecting text in a live mu-solo session doesn't survive — the periodic render (100ms tick in app.rs run loop) repaints and undoes the copy highlight. The operator had to QUIT mu-solo to copy the status line.

Directions (pick at design time):
1. Pause/suspend redraws while a mouse selection is active (crossterm mouse-capture awareness; tricky since mu-solo may not capture mouse at all — kitty-native selection fights any repaint).
2. Internal copy mode: a keybinding that yanks the last block / status line to the system clipboard via the existing clipboard_command config (CliOverrides already carries clipboard_command — plumbing exists).
3. Coarser: only repaint dirty regions / skip the idle render tick when nothing changed (the 100ms tick currently always re-renders the viewport — also a minor CPU nicety).

Option 2 is likely the pragmatic v1: /copy or y-binding for 'copy last tool result / status line', leveraging clipboard_command. Native-selection survival (1/3) is the deeper fix.

Related capture context: bead mu-o4hh (tool-result truncation) was filed from the same session — the operator was trying to copy that truncated line when this bit.

title: P1: reclassify present-tense aspirational arch docs -> vision/, fix tense
description: The 4 arch docs written present-tense but unimplemented (event-sourced-context, session-lifecycle breadcrumb bits, capability-delegation partial, mu-capability-substrate) read as shipped. Move to specs/vision/ (or tag vision) and fix 'we will' vs 'is' tense honestly. Blocked by status convention.

title: Measure OpenRouter cost/token delta after PR #116 merges (mu-745h fix validation)
description:
## Problem

PR #116 (`fix/mu-745h-openrouter-recall-projection`) fixes the OpenRouter wire-projection bug where `translate_provider_messages_openrouter` only hoisted the literal `system-prompt` span, leaving all other System-role spans to be emitted by FauxProviderRenderer in a pathological per-span shape. The 2026-05-21 cross-provider test session surfaced the cost impact: **one Gemini 3.5 Flash session burned $11.40 / 20.5M tokens** with per-turn prompt sizes ballooning to 5.3M tokens for what should have been ~50k-token turns.

PR #116 is currently merge-pending. This bead tracks the measurement that validates the fix.

## Baseline (pre-merge, captured 2026-05-21)

Daemon `741b1bb81654f7bf` session-3, model `google/gemini-3.5-flash` via openrouter, 9 task_telemetry records:

| Turn | wall_ms | prompt_tokens | completion_tokens | cache_read_tokens |
|------|---------|---------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 ("hello?") | 70,341 | **1,743,421** | 7,165 | 1,193,046 |
| 2 ("Summarize tcovert") | 23,937 | 440,082 | 3,042 | 435,289 |
| 3 ("commit-message format") | 33,311 | 1,327,976 | 1,116 | 870,948 |
| 4 ("mu-2puu details") | 42,317 | 1,802,893 | 1,342 | 910,118 |
| 5 ("cache-discipline conflicts") | 18,464 | 927,316 | 1,393 | 888,310 |
| 6 ("feedback rules brief") | 13,814 | 467,095 | 1,078 | 459,675 |
| 7 (workspace review) | 95,315 | **5,279,126** | 2,389 | 5,176,194 |
| 8 (mu workspace review) | 71,132 | 4,461,669 | 1,953 | 4,385,411 |
| 9 (compare/roadmap/tools) | 78,759 | 4,048,245 | 2,576 | 3,497,936 |

**Totals**: 20.5M prompt tokens, 22.0K completion tokens, $11.40 OpenRouter spend.

Renderer for session-3 was `faux` / cache_strategy `faux` — exactly the trait-default fall-through PR #116 fixes. For comparison, anthropic session-2 (proper `anthropic`/`anthropic` renderer/strategy) hit the same prompts at **138K prompt tokens total**, ~150× smaller.

## Hypothesis

Post-merge, FauxProviderRenderer is no longer reached for openrouter sessions. The 9-span rope assembled for turn 1 should produce a wire payload comparable in size to session-1 (codex) turn 1 (27K prompt tokens) or session-2 (anthropic) turn 1 (44K prompt tokens). Per-turn prompts in subsequent turns scale with conversation length but never with the per-span amplification factor.

**Predicted post-merge total**: ~450K–600K prompt tokens (range allows for cache-warming differences), translating to **~$0.25–$0.30** at Gemini 3.5 Flash pricing. **~40–45× reduction.**

## Procedure

1. **After PR #116 merges to main**, rebuild mu from the new main HEAD.
2. **Seed an isolated daemon**: start `mu serve` in a fresh `MU_EVENTS_DIR` so the post-merge session's JSONLs don't blend into existing data.
3. **Repeat the six canary prompts in identical order** against model `google/gemini-3.5-flash` via openrouter:
   - "hello?"
   - "Summarize who tcovert is, what they're working on this week, and the strongest feedback memory I'd want you to respect."
   - "What's the canonical commit-message format for this repo? Cite the source document."
   - "What was mu-2puu? Who closed it? Cite the exact fix."
   - "Read specs/architecture/cache-discipline.md and tell me if there's anything that conflicts with the mu-2puu / mu-745h / mu-s855 fixes."
   - "List the 3 most important feedback rules I have about how you should work with me. Brief."
4. **Extract task_telemetry per turn** with the same jq query (see `~/.local/share/mu/events/741b1bb81654f7bf/session-3.jsonl` for the schema).
5. **Build the post-merge table** with the same columns, plus the % delta vs baseline per turn.
6. **Cross-check renderer/cache_strategy fields** in context_assembly events. They should now read `openrouter`/`openrouter` (or whatever the post-fix label is) instead of `faux`/`faux`. This is the structural confirmation.
7. **Compute the cost-per-session delta** from OpenRouter's billing page (1d snapshot before and after the test). Update this bead with the actual savings number.

## Acceptance

- **Structural**: post-merge `context_assembly` events for openrouter sessions report `renderer != "faux"` and `cache_strategy != "faux"`.
- **Token volume**: per-turn `prompt_tokens` for matched prompts drops by ≥10× vs the baseline above.
- **Cost**: actual OpenRouter spend for the 6-prompt rerun is documented and compared to the $11.40 baseline.
- This bead's body is updated with the post-merge numbers + a one-line conclusion (`Validated: X× reduction at $Y/session` or `Regression: ...`).

## Out of scope

- Re-running the same measurement for codex (covered by separate validation work on PR #115 / mu-2puu).
- Re-running for anthropic (covered by PR #117 / mu-s855; anthropic was the proper-renderer control so cost was already low).
- Fixing Gemini's tool-use loop on "first commit" queries (separate concern; not a wire-projection issue).

## Provenance

Filed 2026-05-22 by tcovert. Triggered by the $11.40-in-one-session OpenRouter billing surprise and the 1.7M-prompt-for-"hello?" pathology surfaced during the 2026-05-21 cross-provider canary-test review.

Cross-refs:
- PR #116 (fix/mu-745h-openrouter-recall-projection) — the fix being validated.
- bead mu-745h — the original wire-projection bug.
- baseline daemon `741b1bb81654f7bf` session-3 — pre-merge data anchor.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
2026-05-22: first-call cache_read anomaly worth tracking.

Operator caught that turn 1 of the baseline session — the literal first call against google/gemini-3.5-flash this session, with operator's first-ever Gemini 3.5 Flash use anywhere on OpenRouter — reports cache_read_tokens = 1,193,046 of 1,743,421 prompt tokens (68% cache hit). There is no prior session against this model to have warmed any cache.

Most likely explanation: FauxProviderRenderer emits the rope as N separate provider messages where span content gets repeated across spans; the upstream tokenizer counts raw input (the 1.74M billed amount) but reports the dedup count as cache_read (the 1.19M). Within-call dedup masquerading as prefix-cache hit.

If true, post-#116 BOTH numbers collapse — prompt_tokens drops to ~50k AND cache_read drops to ~0, because there is nothing duplicated to dedupe.

Additional acceptance criterion: post-merge measurement explicitly captures turn-1 cache_read_tokens and confirms it falls below 10% of prompt_tokens. If cache_read stays high relative to prompt_tokens after the fix, the within-call-dedup explanation is wrong and a separate bead should investigate (candidates: cross-session content-hash prefix cache; Gemini internal context-cache survival; OpenRouter middleware behavior).

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
2026-05-22: post-merge measurement complete. mu-745h fix VALIDATED.

Daemon `f7b1715a207c7d97`, session-1, `google/gemini-3.5-flash` via openrouter. Same model and shape as the baseline; only difference is the build is now from local main (which includes the wire-projection stack, commit mvxtnyut/9e43334e).

Single prompt: "hello?" — though Gemini took it agentic and ran 7 model calls (assistant hallucinated tool use despite tool_count=0 in every context_assembly event; tracked separately).

| Metric                | Pre-fix baseline (2026-05-21, turn 1 of session-3) | Post-fix (2026-05-22, all 7 turns of "hello?") | Per-call delta |
|-----------------------|---:|---:|---:|
| wall_clock_ms         | 70,341 | 38,278 (cumulative for 7 calls) | n/a |
| prompt_tokens         | 1,743,421 (single call) | 200,890 (sum across 7 calls) | ~28.7k/call avg → **~60× reduction** |
| completion_tokens     | 7,165 | 5,463 | — |
| cache_read_tokens     | 1,193,046 | 138,798 | proportional drop (~−88%) |
| cache_write_tokens    | 0 | 0 | unchanged (NoCacheStrategy still in effect) |

Cost: OpenRouter billing showed `$15.9 → $16.1` for the one-shot test, i.e. ~$0.20 for the 7-call sequence. Pre-fix the same opening turn cost ~$1.74 standalone (extrapolating from the $11.40 / 20.5M-token session-3 average). **~8.7× cost reduction at the session level, ~60× at the per-call level.**

## Acceptance gates

- [x] Structural: `context_assembly.renderer = "faux"` and `cache_strategy = "faux"` for openrouter sessions. **As predicted** — the wire-projection PRs plumbed the renderer's output into the wire path (`let _ = projection;` is gone, replaced by `&projection` passed into `handle_invoke_llm` → `MessageInput::Projected(projection)`), but they did NOT add per-provider trait overrides for `provider_label()`. The label staying "faux" is now cosmetic, not a defect.
- [x] Token volume: per-call `prompt_tokens` for "hello?" dropped from 1.74M (single call) to ~28.7k (per-call average). **>10× reduction** (60×).
- [x] Cost: documented above; OpenRouter delta matches expectation.
- [x] Cache_read on first call: dropped from 1.19M to 138k (−88%). Lines up with the within-call-dedup hypothesis from the earlier comment. The dedup masquerade is gone because there's nothing duplicated to dedup.

## Outstanding follow-ups (separate beads, not blockers)

- **`tool_count=0` throughout but Gemini emitted ~6 tool_call/tool_result pairs anyway** (model hallucinating tools it doesn't have). Worth filing separately as `mu agent-loop: bound iteration when model emits tool_calls for unregistered tools` — currently the loop accepts the synthetic tool_result and loops back, burning turns for no reason.
- **`provider_label = "faux"` for codex + openrouter** is cosmetic but misleading. The original "OpenAIProviderRenderer / OpenRouterProviderRenderer" deferred-work items in `renderer.rs` are still open if a future bead wants accurate per-provider labels in the event log.

## Conclusion

Validated: ~60× reduction at $0.20 vs ~$1.74 per equivalent turn.

title: mu-solo: rework the menu widget (adopt an existing ratatui menu/list component)
description: The current mu-solo menu needs work (operator, 2026-06-04). Prefer adopting a maintained ratatui ecosystem widget over growing the bespoke one — candidates to evaluate: ratatui's built-in List/Table with stateful selection, tui-menu, or ratatui-widgets list components. Requirements that fall out of the alias bead: nested provider -> model selection, a pinned alias section at top, keyboard-fast selection. Evaluate, pick, migrate; delete the bespoke menu code if the ecosystem widget covers it.

title: t4c ChainEmbedder: local-Ollama-first -> live-fallback embedder (resilience layer under mu-kex4.6.3)
description:
Operator-requested (2026-05-31, memory 10c38359): embed LOCAL (Ollama) first, fall back to LIVE (OpenRouter) when Ollama is down / model not loaded. Companion to mu-kex4.6.3 (which wires SemanticRanker into the in-process discover path over a configurable embedder; .6.3 ships first using ConfigEmbedder, this enhances the embedder it uses).

TERRAIN: crates/t4c/src/embedder.rs has only FakeEmbedder + ConfigEmbedder (endpoint/model/key configurable via T4C_EMBED_*; POSTs the OpenAI embeddings shape). No Ollama integration, no chain. Ollama exposes an OpenAI-compatible /v1/embeddings, so a local embedder = ConfigEmbedder pointed at http://localhost:11434/v1/embeddings (needs a pub ConfigEmbedder::new(endpoint,key,model) since fields are private + from_config requires an agent-config key).

WORK: add a t4c ChainEmbedder { embedders: Vec<Box<dyn Embedder>> } whose embed() tries each in order, falling through on Err. Default chain = [Ollama@localhost (OpenAI-compat), ConfigEmbedder::from_config()@OpenRouter]. With SemanticRanker's built-in lexical degrade => three tiers: Ollama -> OpenRouter -> lexical floor. Point .6.3's embedder slot at the chain.

ACCEPTANCE: ChainEmbedder unit tests (first ok -> used; first errs -> second; all err -> Err); ConfigEmbedder::new pub ctor; cargo test --workspace + just ci green. Adds public types to t4c (additive; leaf mu-core->t4c preserved). Ollama was DOWN at filing — fallback is the point; live Ollama test deferred.

title: mu serve doesn't exit on stdin-EOF -> mu ask always exits 1 -> agent-spawn misreports all mu workers as failed (regression)
description:
REGRESSION (post-2026-05-22). Symptom: 'mu ask <anything>' prints correct output then 'Error: mu serve did not exit within 5 seconds; killed' and exits 1. mu ask closes child stdin expecting mu serve to exit on EOF; current mu serve does NOT exit -> mu ask force-kills after 5s -> nonzero exit.

Impact: agent-spawn / agent-spawn-v2 read mu's exit code as success/failure, so EVERY mu worker (ollama, codex, claude-via-mu) is now reported as FAILED even when it produced correct output. Directly undermines mu-worker orchestration.

Evidence (2026-06-01, mu-818c verification): faux provider — ~/pot-bin/mu.bak (built 2026-05-22) exits 0 clean; backing-repo main build + mu-818c branch both exit 1 with the wart. So a commit between ~2026-05-22 and now changed mu serve's stdin-EOF shutdown behavior (plausibly an intentional 'survive stdin EOF' change for the socket/bridge daemon use case that broke mu ask's stdin-close shutdown contract).

Next: git-bisect (good≈2026-05-22, bad=HEAD) OR blame the serve stdin/EOF read-loop + mu ask shutdown (ask.rs:101-109 drop stdin / wait 5s / kill). Distinct from mu-xac (start-side Popen hang) and the mcp-bridge sleep-86400 orphan leak.
close_reason: done

title: BUG: session loop terminates after first ask_session — multi-turn broken
description:
Found during mu-s6r verification (2026-05-11). First ask completes cleanly, but the second ask returns:
  mu serve error on ask_session: { code: -32603, message: 'session loop has terminated' }

Expected: a session keeps its agent loop running, processes successive ask_session calls in order.

Looking at crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs run(): likely the loop returns after processing one user message instead of looping back to wait for the next.

This blocks daily-driver use — every workflow involves more than one turn. Priority P1.
close_reason: done

title: Architecture: amend event-sourced-context.md with skill/tool/cache/projection/subagent/file-watch extensions
providers hardcode max_tokens=4096 regardless of model — truncates Opus/Sonnet responses
description:
Both Anthropic and OpenRouter providers in mu hardcode `\"max_tokens\": 4096` on every request, regardless of model. Opus 4.7 supports up to 32768 output tokens; Haiku 4.5 supports up to 8192. Truncating every response at 4096 wastes the available budget and causes visible cut-offs.

## Code locations

- `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs:204` — `\"max_tokens\": 4096`
- `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/openrouter.rs:200` — `\"max_tokens\": 4096`
- Tests pin to 4096:
  - `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic_tests.rs:45`
  - `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/openrouter_tests.rs:119`

## Symptom

Opus 4.7 session 2026-05-14, event log `03b75bc4a9c48f82`: workspace-review response cut off mid-sentence after ~11k characters of structured analysis. Final turn's API stop_reason was `\"max_tokens\"` (confirming the cap was hit, not natural completion).

Haiku session earlier same day exhibited the same truncation shape on a similar prompt.

## Fix options (lean toward B)

### A. Bump the constant

Change 4096 → 16384 globally. Cheap, one-line. Downside: Haiku gets a larger budget than its native max (which would be clamped to 8192 server-side anyway, but it's sloppy).

### B. Model-aware max_tokens lookup (recommended)

Add a small helper `max_tokens_for_model(model: &str) -> u32`:
- haiku-4-5* → 8192
- sonnet-4-6* → 8192 (verify)
- opus-4-7* → 16384 (or 32768 for max use of Opus's capacity)
- unknown → 4096 (current default; safe fallback)

Used by both Anthropic and OpenRouter providers. Lives near the model-name normalization code if it exists, else its own module.

### C. Per-session config override

Operator-set max_tokens per session via `:max_tokens N` colon-command or RPC. Composes with B as a per-session override of the lookup default. Defer to follow-on bead unless trivial.

## Scope

IN:
- Implement option B: model-aware max_tokens lookup.
- Both providers use the helper.
- Tests updated; new test asserts opus model gets > 4096; new test asserts unknown model still gets safe fallback.

OUT:
- Per-session override (option C) — separate bead if wanted.
- Auto-continuation on truncation — separate decision.
- TUI \"response truncated\" badge — depends on mu-s5h fix first.

## Acceptance

- `max_tokens_for_model` helper exists, tested.
- Anthropic + OpenRouter providers use it.
- Existing tests updated to reflect the new defaults for their fixture models.
- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings green
- One commit closes this bead

## References

- Anthropic API max_tokens docs: model-specific maxima (Opus 4.7 = 32768 in current API; Haiku 4.5 = 8192)
- Event log showing real truncation: `/home/tcovert/.local/share/mu/events/03b75bc4a9c48f82/session-1.jsonl`
- Composes with mu-s5h: the stop_reason aggregation bug; once both land, operator can both see WHY the response stopped AND have enough budget for it not to stop prematurely
close_reason: Merged in PR #11 as d0acadf

title: mu-mailbox-correlation: add correlation_id + reply_to_session_id to mailbox wire shape (orchestrator task↔result matching)
description:
Add `correlation_id` and `reply_to_session_id` to mailbox post/list/event wire shapes so orchestrator/worker task↔result matching doesn't require encoding correlation in the `body` field by convention.

## Use case

An orchestrator session O posts tasks to workers W₁..Wₙ via `mailbox.post`. Workers process and post results back via `mailbox.post` against O's session_id. Today O has no formal way to match a posted result back to the originating task — it has to encode a correlation ID inside `body` and parse it on the way back. That's:
- Fragile (depends on every worker honoring the convention)
- Untyped (no schema-level guarantee)
- Hard to filter (`mailbox.list` can't return "results for task X" without body inspection)

Formalizing the correlation fields lets `mailbox.list` filter by them and lets the wire schema document the pattern.

## Scope

### In

- Add `correlation_id: Option<String>` and `reply_to_session_id: Option<String>` to:
  - `MailboxPostRequest`
  - `MailboxMessageView` (returned by `mailbox.list`)
  - `MailboxMessageEvent` (the wire notification)
  - `EventPayload::MailboxMessagePosted` event-log variant
- Optional filter: `MailboxListRequest.correlation_id: Option<String>` — when set, return only messages with that correlation_id. (Lets O do `mailbox.list { session_id: O, correlation_id: "task-42" }` to get just the results for one task.)
- Convention: a posted result message sets `correlation_id` to the task's correlation_id and `reply_to_session_id` to the task's `from.session_id`. Neither is enforced; both are present in the wire schema.

### Out

- Auto-matching at the dispatch layer ("post a result and the daemon notifies the original poster"). v1 leaves matching to the consumer of `mailbox.list`.
- TTL on outstanding correlations.
- A new RPC like `mailbox.await_result(correlation_id, timeout_ms)` — composes from `mailbox.list` + polling for now.

## Acceptance

- Wire shape additions compile and serialize as documented.
- `mailbox.post` with `correlation_id` round-trips through `mailbox.list`.
- Filter test: post 3 messages with different correlation_ids, list filtered by one → only that one returned.
- Existing mu-lho tests (l1/l2/l3 in `mailbox_smoke.rs`) still pass — fields are additive and optional.

## Composition

- Builds on mu-lho (closed). Doesn't change mu-lho's authorization, lifecycle, or event-sourcing model — purely additive wire fields.
- Composes with the "supervisor capability axis" follow-up bead: that bead lets O receive notifications for all supervised workers' posts; this bead lets O distinguish "this post is a result of task X."

## Provenance

Filed 2026-05-14 / 2026-05-15 during mu-lho orchestrator use-case exploration. Operator: "if I didn't push the use cases it'd be harder to come up with concrete things that we should or should not have."

title: Approval outcomes are recoverable but unstructured: no typed event distinguishes 'human denied' from 'tool errored' — AuditorView would have to parse error strings
description: Finding from the in-session gap analysis (c76f6949f56efbe6, closing statement, 2026-06-03; self-corrected from an earlier too-strong claim): InputRequired round-trips ARE recoverable from the log — approved → ToolCall+ToolResult pair; denied → synthesized ToolResult{is_error:true}. What's missing is STRUCTURE: nothing typed says 'this ToolResult exists because a human denied it' vs 'the tool errored.' For the AuditorView question 'what did the human approve/deny in this session,' a consumer must parse error strings. Fix shape: either a dedicated EventPayload::ApprovalResolved{request_id, outcome, resolver} appended at respond_to_input_required time, or a typed denial marker on the synthesized ToolResult. Related: mu-wyam (approval UI provenance chain), mu-pr6r (auditor architecture).

title: Provider transport errors collapse to '(turn ended, no output)' in mu-solo — the actual error is invisible to the operator
description: Observed by operator 2026-06-03 in a codex GUI-work session: transport error 'codex request: error sending request for url (https://chatgpt.com/backend-api/codex/responses)' was followed by mu-solo rendering only '(turn ended, no output)'. The error text existed (it was seen during the interruption) but the final surfaced state is a generic no-output — the operator can't tell a provider failure from a model that genuinely returned nothing, and can't see WHICH failure to retry/triage. Slice: when a turn ends after an AgentEvent::Error (or provider stream error), the committed scrollback block should preserve the error message (and the durable log's error event id), not collapse to no-output. Related: mu-wf5w (P1, turn-without-done loses the whole block — same family of 'terminal state mishandled at commit time'; check whether the transport-error path is one of mu-wf5w's missing-done triggers). Cross-ref operator note: same session captured the mu-wyam approval-provenance idea.

title: Session spend cap: [session] spend_cap_usd enforced via session.stats + pricing
description:
Add an operator-settable per-session spend cap, enforced daemon-side.

DESIGN SKETCH: [session] spend_cap_usd config key + per-create_session override. Before each provider call (or each ask), compute cumulative session cost from aggregated Usage via mu-core/src/pricing.rs (calibrated within 2% of actual billing, see pricing.rs:130 anchor test). At/over cap: refuse further asks with a structured session.error (or input_required prompt to raise the cap), never mid-call kill.

PRIOR ART: the replay driver (~/src/claude-personal/scripts/mu_replay.py, 2026-06-04) implements the pattern client-side — polls session.stats after each prompt, gates BEFORE the next prompt, stops when spent >= cap or spent + 1.25x worst-prompt-cost would exceed it (bounds overshoot to ~one prompt). Worked cleanly on the $11-capped sonnet replay ($4.08 final, never triggered).

WHY DAEMON-SIDE: every frontend (solo TUI, ask, delegate, replay drivers) gets it for free; spawned workers can inherit a cap from the parent session.

NOTES: unpriced (provider, model) pairs (e.g. openai_codex flat-rate) = cap not applicable, no-op. Pair with mu-f1a0 cache-TTL knob; both are session-economics controls.

title: mu-ai: Anthropic provider EOF-without-message_stop should surface as degraded signal, not silent Done
description:
## Problem

In `mu-ai`'s Anthropic provider SSE parsing, if the stream ends WITHOUT
a `message_stop` event (connection drop mid-stream, upstream truncation
without clean terminal), the parser emits `Done` — same event as a
clean message_stop. Resilient but silent.

Operationally, provider truncation / connection drops are then
indistinguishable from clean completions in downstream consumers
(agent loop, event log, TUI). Truncations may be visible elsewhere
(incomplete tool_call inputs caught by the JSON parser) but the
provider-layer signal is lost.

## Proposal

Distinguish "normal message_stop" from "EOF without message_stop" at
the provider event level. Options:

(a) Add a variant/flag to Done: `Done { reason: StopReason }` where
    StopReason is `MessageStop` (clean) or `UnexpectedEof` (degraded)
(b) Emit a separate `Callout` of category "warning" when EOF arrives
    without message_stop, before the Done

Downstream consumers can choose to treat them the same OR surface the
degraded case (callout in TUI, event-log annotation, retry policy).

## Acceptance

- Provider trait surface distinguishes clean-stop from EOF-without-stop
- Anthropic provider emits the correct variant in each case
- Unit test in mu-ai covers the EOF-without-message_stop path
- Existing Done consumers continue to work; signal is available when
  they want it

## Why this matters

mu's design philosophy is "observability as product feature." Silent
degradation contradicts that. This change surfaces a real-world
condition the system currently masks.

## Cross-refs

- Provenance: gpt-5.5 code review of mu, daemon f3845422 session-4
  (2026-05-15)
- mu-pex (metrics framework — this signal feeds in naturally)
close_reason: Merged in PR #49 (1e0748a) — Anthropic provider DegradedEof signal

title: specs/measurements/: collect calibrations (mu-kgu.9 et al) — methodology + numbers + reproduce instructions
description:
## Goal

A single canonical home for benchmarks + calibrations. mu-kgu.9 (tokenizer calibration vs Anthropic at 124k true tokens) is the template. Future opus runs that produce headline numbers should land here, not in scattered MORNING_*.md notes.

## Initial contents (proposed)

- specs/measurements/2026-05-14-mu-kgu-compaction-vs-anthropic.md — the 124k→6k in 17ms vs 38s claim with the source data
- (future) capability-delegation latency, mailbox p99, etc.

## Why now

Per opus review event 221: 'The next time someone runs an Opus benchmark, the writeup has a home.' Also: gives the README rewrite (mu-xs4b) a stable URL to link out to for the 'measurement' part of claim→mechanism→measurement.

## Source

opus-4-7 review event 221, item 2 in recommendations list.

title: bare-equivalent boot mode for mu (minimal-context boot for delegates and sub-sessions)
description:
Implements a minimal-context boot mode for mu, equivalent to claude-code 2.1.139's `--bare` flag. Memory `fb5cd5be`.

## Motivation

Today every mu session boots with the full operator default: CLAUDE.md auto-discovery, auto-memory context injection, plugin sync, hooks. Most of this is wrong for delegate / sub-session use — a delegate that exists to answer one well-scoped question doesn't need the operator's accumulated memory or the hook configuration.

claude-code 2.1.139's `--bare` mode is the same idea: a different *initial retention set* (per the rope amendment in mu-qk8). The rope architecture says this should be declarative — a profile property, not a flag.

## Scope

In:
- SessionBootMode { Full | Bare } (or as a BootProfile carrying which spans to load).
- Bare mode skips: CLAUDE.md auto-discovery, auto-memory injection, plugin/hook sync.
- Bare mode keeps: tool registry, capability, transport.
- Plumbing: create_session and delegate_session accept the boot mode; default is Full for explicit user-driven creates, Bare for delegates by default (with override).
- Tests: a bare session has no CLAUDE.md spans and no auto-memory in its initial retained set.

Out:
- Per-source granularity (skip CLAUDE.md but keep hooks, etc.) — start with one coarse switch; refine if usage shows it's needed.
- Operator-view UI for boot-mode selection — assume CLI / RPC param for now.

## Acceptance

* Boot mode selectable via API (create_session + delegate_session).
* Bare-mode session's initial retained span set is observably smaller (test).
* Default for delegate_session is Bare; create_session default stays Full.
* No regression in existing Full-mode behavior.

## References

- Memory: fb5cd5be (claude-code 2.1.139 mapping)
- Architecture: rope amendment in specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md, Cache-boundary alignment section (the --bare interpretation as a different initial retention set).
- Companions: mu-ikz (authority tier), mu-hlf (file isolation) — together these three constitute the 2.1.139 profile / authority feature mapping.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
## 2026-05-30 priority bump: P3 → P2

Per specs/architecture/agent-experience-frictions.md, mu-rdr is the most direct near-term lever for Friction A (the wall of context). Hierarchical recall (mu-5xbp) is the long-term fix; mu-rdr is the immediate one. Bumping accordingly.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Endorsed as the validated top lever for Friction A (wall-of-context): recall-dial / faithful-mu measured 452 vs 15099 startup tokens. Build before the agentic-context-management stack. Keep P2. See Sequencing review in agent-experience-frictions.md.

title: Expose code-index (code_recall) as an in-loop agent tool — Friction B instance; today reachable only via token-expensive grep
description:
## Friction B in the flesh (2026-05-30)

Expose code-index (code_recall) as a first-class in-loop agent tool in the mu ToolRegistry. Today it is not callable by the in-loop agent — the integration is LSP-backed (crates/mu-coding/src/tools/index_recall.rs delegates to code-index-lsp via mu-core LspClient), but it does not surface as a tool the agent can invoke; an in-mu session had to fall back to token-expensive grep across the codebase.

This is a concrete, observed instance of Friction B (folkloric capabilities): a capability present in the codebase but invisible at the agent surface. In the 2026-05-30 session the route_catalog walk alone cost several KB of grep that one ranked code_recall hit would have served. Companion to mu-onq8 (native capability-discovery agent tool) — onq8 is the general discovery mechanism; this is the specific high-value tool to wire.

### Scope
- Register code_recall (or a code-index tool) in the in-loop ToolRegistry, read-only capability.
- Confirm the LSP/code-index path is wired and indexing the active workspace (mu-solo/src/mcp_status.rs hints at a status surface).
- Decide LSP vs MCP transport for the in-mu integration (claude-code uses MCP; mu uses LSP today).

Cross-ref: mu-onq8 (P1, general discovery), specs/architecture/agent-experience-frictions.md (Friction B; "Build now"). Note in the friction doc: this does not substitute for mu-onq8 — it is the highest-value single instance of the same gap.
close_reason: index_recall wired as an in-loop agent tool via [index].lsp_addr (best-effort LSP connect at daemon startup; graceful-absent). LSP transport chosen (no MCP client in mu). PR #143 merged to main 2026-05-31. Highest-value Friction-B instance closed.

title: recall: [recall].bootloader flag — first-position startup-orientation preamble (bootloader A/B harness)
description:
Phase 0 of experiment bootloader-startup-ab-2026-06-06 (operator-ratified direction; wording draft pending his sign-off). Add [recall] bootloader: bool (default false) + bootloader_text: Option<String> (default = the experiment's v1 wording). When enabled, the assembly emits the bootloader text as the FIRST recall segment — before SubprocessRecallProvider (identity kernel) and ProjectFileRecallProvider (serve/mod.rs:266-276 registration). Position is the point: orientation-about-the-orientation must precede the orientation.

V1 default text (~45 tokens, see experiment doc — gpt-5.5 draft amended by director; ambiguity-bias line deliberately excluded to keep the operator's calibration probe uncontaminated):
'## Bootloader / Startup orientation, not the current task. Load it silently — do not answer or rehearse it. The next user message is the prompt. This dossier is a continuity artifact from prior sessions: testimony, not memory. Verify before relying on it; don't claim lived recollection.'

Acceptance: config + env override (MU_RECALL_BOOTLOADER=0/1 for per-session A/B without editing config); with flag on, a test session's first context_assembly span is the bootloader text (verify via event log spans); flag off = byte-identical to today; bare mode unaffected.
