Panel PASS unanimous. Experiment harness complete.

title: Status-bar context tokens double-count for codex/OpenAI: live_usage() sums input+cache_read+cache_creation, but OpenAI input_tokens already includes cached
description: Operator-observed (2026-06-03, session 6ee4d6229660ba68): mu-solo status showed context '93k' at session end; actual last-call context was 55,577 tokens. 93,209 = 55,577 (input) + 37,632 (cache_read) — exact match for the double-count. Site: crates/mu-core/src/event_log.rs:604-606 live_usage(): total_input = input_tokens + cache_read + cache_creation. That formula is correct for Anthropic accounting (disjoint buckets) and wrong for OpenAI/codex (cache_read is a SUBSET of input_tokens). Flows into SessionStatus.last_call_context_tokens and context_pressure_pct (forwarder.rs:387-393) → mu-solo status line {used}/{window} and the 70%/90% pressure colors fire early on codex. Same bug class as mu-3b5c (deep-analyze.py) — third sighting of this trap today (also the invalid-premise compaction bead). FIX OPTIONS: (a) provider-aware branch in live_usage (needs provider_kind threaded in, or read from log's SessionCreated); (b) normalize Usage semantics at the adapter boundary — define mu's canonical input_tokens as TOTAL prompt tokens (OpenAI convention), make the Anthropic adapter sum its buckets into it, keep cache fields informational; then every consumer (live_usage, analytics, deep-analyze) gets honest numbers for free. (b) is the systemic fix; audit all Usage consumers if taken. Cross-ref: mu-3b5c, memory 42577731.
close_reason: Merged in PR #164: UsageSemantics self-declaration + registration-time stamping + live_usage interpretation. mu-tui cache-hit% follow-up noted on the closing comment trail.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Operator design direction (2026-06-03): put usage interpretation ON THE PROVIDER INTERFACE, consistent with how renderer/cache_strategy/compaction already work (each provider self-declares its pair, mu-fb0 idiom). Sketch: Provider gains fn interpret_usage(&self, raw: &Usage) -> ContextAccounting, where ContextAccounting has Option fields for the component parts — prompt_total, fresh_input, cache_read, cache_write, reasoning, output. Each adapter maps its wire semantics into the canonical fields: codex → prompt_total = Some(input_tokens) (already total), fresh = input - cached; anthropic → prompt_total = Some(input + cache_read + cache_creation); faux/unknown → None for unsupported parts, consumers display what's populated and infer or omit the rest (no arithmetic in consumers, ever). Kills the bug class at all three sites: live_usage()/status bar, deep-analyze (mu-3b5c), and any future consumer. live_usage() then needs the provider handle or the interpretation done at event-append time (interpreted accounting could be stamped INTO the assistant_message_event/done payloads — durable, provider-correct at write time, consumers never re-derive). Stamping-at-write is the most event-sourced answer and survives provider switches mid-session (each event interpreted by the provider that produced it).

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Implemented in PR #164 per operator direction (registration-time stamping, not per-event): UsageSemantics on ProviderCapabilities (Option<bool> fields, prompt_total/fresh_input as the single shared arithmetic, None-not-guess when unknown+material); stamped on SessionCreated + ProviderSwitched (all 3 switch sites); live_usage folds convention forward. Regression test pins the incident numbers (55,577/37,632 → 55,577 not 93,209). FOLLOW-UPS not in PR: (1) mu-tui main.rs:4314-4320 cache-hit %% uses additive denominator — same class, display-only; (2) mu-3b5c deep-analyze.py can now read the stamp from session_created/provider_switched instead of guessing by provider_kind.

title: mu-context-ulimit: soft/hard context caps with judge-mediated raise requests
description:
Exploration bead. Treat context size as a **bounded capability with soft/hard thresholds**, where the agent must request elevation past soft, and an independent **judge** (not the agent) evaluates whether elevation is warranted.

## Motivation

Context rot is empirical: model performance degrades well before nominal max context, and the degradation isn't graceful. Anthropic's auto-compaction target of ~150k for Opus reflects this; past that point attention quality drops measurably. Today mu compacts at a threshold (mu-kgu.4) but treats context size as a *budget* managed silently. Reframing it as a *capability with soft and hard caps* surfaces the cost and creates a decision point.

## Design sketch (ulimit-style)

- **soft cap** — per (provider, model) calibrated to the degradation knee (e.g. ~150k Opus, ~80k Sonnet, ~40k Haiku). Hitting soft triggers compaction policy (the mu-kgu integration point).
- **hard cap** — absolute ceiling (nominal max minus safety margin). mu refuses to grow context past hard regardless of agent request.
- **raise request** — between soft and hard, the agent may emit `session.request_context_raise { current_tokens, requested_cap, claimed_reason }`. The supervisor / policy decides.
- **judge-mediated decision** — the request is NOT decided by the agent's self-assessment. Instead, a separate judge call (reusing the mu-kgu.3 HashAndSummaryPolicy + mu-kgu.11 ProviderJudge plumbing) independently evaluates: "given the current rope, is the request justified, or is X tokens droppable instead?"

### Judge verdict shape

```
enum CapRaiseVerdict {
  Approve { recommended_cap: usize, reason: String },
  Deny { recommended_compact: CompactionDirective, reason: String },
  ApproveAndCompact { recommended_cap: usize, also_drop: CompactionDirective, reason: String },
}
```

The third option (compact-and-raise) is the most honest answer when context genuinely contains both load-bearing and droppable spans.

## Composition with existing substrates

- **mu-kgu (compaction substrate)** — soft cap triggers the existing CompactionPolicy. Same machinery. New input: the judge can be asked "is this compactable?" separately from "compact it now."
- **mu-fvy0 (accountable runtime)** — \"raise cap\" is a CapabilityKind axis. Operator can grant blanket raise authority to a session, or require per-request approval. Judge verdict is evidence; capability holder decides.
- **mu-035 (provider_status)** — a new `ProviderStatusKind::JudgeEvaluatingCapRequest` sub-kind so the operator sees the evaluation happening in real time.
- **mu-fvy0-telemetry (mu-5g7i)** — every (request, judge verdict, operator decision, downstream outcome) is a TaskTelemetry datapoint. Over time the analytics layer (mu-8ypx) measures whether judge-approved raises correlate with better task outcomes vs judge-denied + compacted, vs no-request baselines. Empirical loop for tuning the policy.

## Open questions (decide before implementing)

1. **Per-model soft caps**: yes (Opus 4.7 ≠ Sonnet 4.6 ≠ Haiku 4.5 ≠ GPT-5.5). Where do calibrated values live — config file, hardcoded const, telemetry-derived? Probably a config table seeded with vendor defaults, refined from observed telemetry.
2. **Hysteresis**: once raised, when do we downgrade? Auto-downgrade when usage drops under 0.8×soft for N turns? Sticky-for-session? Decide based on telemetry of how often raised sessions stay near the new ceiling vs settle back down.
3. **Judge model selection**: cheap-small (Haiku 4.5, ~$0.005/eval) is ideal — fast, focused, parallel to the main turn. But the judge's accuracy matters for the loop. Calibration question.
4. **Per-request capability vs blanket capability**: per-request is more accountable, blanket is less ceremony. Probably both should be possible, with the default being per-request and operators able to grant blanket per-session.
5. **Failure modes of the judge**: what if the judge itself hallucinates? Falls back to default-deny? Default-approve? The hollow-commit / lying-state classifier (mu-8alb) gives us a way to detect judge errors retrospectively but not prevent them in-flight.

## Acceptance (when this becomes implementable)

- Soft/hard cap fields on session config, per-(provider,model) defaults
- `session.request_context_raise` wire event + policy that intercepts it
- `CapRaiseJudge` consumer of existing JudgeAdapter, with a new prompt schema
- Three verdict types correctly produced and respected
- Telemetry envelope captures the full request→verdict→outcome chain
- Spec'd in `specs/mu-NNN-context-ulimit.md`

## Not implementing now

This is an exploration / vision bead. Implementing requires mu-kgu (done), mu-fvy0-telemetry (mu-5g7i — not yet started), and a config-system pass for per-(provider,model) tables. Worth filing as a coherent target so the substrate pieces are designed with this in mind.

## Provenance

Operator observation at 2026-05-16: "was looking at your context ctx:45%... /compact tries to get under 150k... context rot... should LRU keep at 150k? or let it go to max. Then I thought of ulimit and soft and hard caps." Plus follow-up: "we already have a judge function. That might be a good place for an independent assessment."

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
## Refinements 2026-05-16 (operator)

Two structural changes from the initial sketch:

### 1. The raise-request is fire-and-forget from the agent's side

The agent does NOT block waiting for a verdict. It emits `session.request_context_raise` as a signal and continues with whatever context it has. The judge + supervisor process asynchronously. This removes the "synchronous capability gate" complication and matches the mu-kgu.8 background-compaction-worker pattern — the model keeps working, mitigation happens in parallel.

Consequences:
- The agent loop never stalls on a cap request
- The verdict may come AFTER the current turn completes (still useful: informs the NEXT turn's cap)
- The judge can fire background compaction directly when it decides "drop these spans"; the cap-raise flow becomes a unified "context-pressure response" pipeline

### 2. Judge verdicts are recorded, always

Every verdict goes to the event log as a durable EventPayload variant (and into the mu-5g7i telemetry envelope). This is non-negotiable even when nothing acts on the verdict — the recording is what lets the analytics layer (mu-8ypx) measure judge accuracy over time. Without records there is no feedback loop.

### 3. The judge is pluggable

The JudgeAdapter trait from mu-kgu.11 is the extension point. Operators can plug in:
- A cheap-small judge (Haiku 4.5) — default, fast, low-cost
- A same-family judge (Opus 4.7 evaluating its own context) — more expensive, possibly more accurate, possibly more biased
- A domain-specific judge (custom prompt for specific workloads — e.g. preserve all type definitions, drop file contents older than 5 turns)
- A composite judge (multiple judges, vote)
- A null judge (always-approve or always-deny, for testing or specific policies)

Pluggability means experimentation is cheap — operators can A/B different judges and let the telemetry layer compare outcomes.

### Composite picture

```
agent: ...mid-turn, context approaching soft cap...
  -> emit session.request_context_raise (fire-and-forget)
agent: ...continues turn with current context...

(in parallel)
supervisor: receive raise request
  -> invoke pluggable Judge::evaluate(rope, request_envelope)
  -> Judge returns: Approve | Deny | ApproveAndCompact | ScheduleBackgroundCompact
  -> Record verdict in event log + telemetry sink
  -> Act:
     - Approve: raise soft cap for next turn
     - Deny: no action; verdict recorded for analytics
     - ApproveAndCompact: schedule mu-kgu.8 background worker AND raise cap
     - ScheduleBackgroundCompact: dispatch worker, don't raise

agent: ...completes turn, next turn sees new cap and/or compacted rope...
```

The agent never knows it asked. The agent just operates with whatever context shape it has now. The judge's accuracy is empirically measurable retrospectively, which is the only honest way to tune it.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
## 2026-05-30 cross-link: protocol generalization

Filed alongside specs/architecture/agent-experience-frictions.md (this session) and the mu-63sr amendment.

The Request/Decide/Apply event triple introduced in the mu-63sr amendment generalizes the judge-mediated decision pattern this bead lays out. The cap-raise verdict shape (`CapRaiseVerdict::Approve | Deny | ApproveAndCompact`) maps cleanly into the more general `ContextModificationDecision`:

- `Approve { recommended_cap }` → `Decision::Approved { actual_action: RaiseCap { ... } }`
- `Deny { recommended_compact }` → `Decision::Modified { suggested_action: Compact { ... } }`
- `ApproveAndCompact` → `Decision::Modified { suggested_action: RaiseCapAndCompact { ... } }`

This bead doesn't need rewriting — the cap-raise use case is one instance of the general protocol. Implementation can either:

1. Land the cap-raise mechanic as its own first instance of the triple, with `ContextModificationAction::RaiseCap` as the action variant, OR
2. Wait for the mu-63sr amendment to land and then add cap-raise as a second action variant.

Either ordering works. The substrate (judge call, request/decide/apply events, capability gating) is shared. The cross-reference is that any "the agent asks for a capability elevation, an independent judge evaluates" decision flows through the same machinery — context release (mu-63sr), context cap raise (this), and consolidator outputs (mu-5xbp).

See specs/architecture/agent-experience-frictions.md Frictions E (release) and G (pressure) for the unified framing.

title: forensics: add SkippedTest outcome class — detect +#[ignore] in diff
description:
Sub-bead 2 of mu-uvsq (overnight 2026-05-19).

## Scope

Add a new `Outcome::SkippedTest` discriminator to forensics.rs. Detect commits that add `#[ignore]` annotations to existing tests as a means of bypassing test failures rather than fixing them. Per-repo rule #1: `No #[ignore] shortcuts unless explicitly authorized.`

## Discriminator order

Goes AFTER hollow_commit (more severe — hollow is dishonest output, SkippedTest is process violation) but BEFORE CI-based discriminators (a SkippedTest + CI-green commit still classifies as SkippedTest, because the green CI is masking the disabled tests).

## Detection

Heuristic: scan the commit diff for additions matching `+#[ignore]` or `+#[ignore(` (allowing the variant with a justification string). Heuristic — not a parser, but stable enough since `#[ignore]` is a fixed token.

Probable confidence (not Definite — there are legitimate cases like ignoring tests that depend on external services in CI; the discriminator should fire and let operator/analytics decide).

## Inputs

`CommitInfo` already carries `diff_files: Vec<String>`. Need a new field carrying the raw diff text (or a per-file list of added lines) so the discriminator can scan for `+#[ignore]`. Smallest viable: add `added_lines: Vec<String>` to `CommitInfo`. Caller (the analytics/backfill layer) is responsible for populating it from `git diff` output.

If wiring the caller is out of scope: stub the field, gate the discriminator on `added_lines.is_empty()` so it cleanly no-ops until plumbing lands. Sub-bead is still useful because the OUTCOME exists, fixture tests pass, future caller work is unblocked.

## Tests required

- Positive: commit with one `+#[ignore]` line → `Outcome::SkippedTest`, Probable
- Positive with justification: `+#[ignore = "needs network"]` also matches
- Negative: commit with NO ignore annotations → falls through to CI-based discriminators
- Negative: commit removes an `#[ignore]` (i.e., re-enables a test) → does NOT classify as SkippedTest (the deletion is unmark, not skip)
- Multi-line: counts how many tests were skipped (rationale includes the count)

## Acceptance

- `Outcome::SkippedTest` variant added
- `CommitInfo` gets `added_lines` field (or equivalent surface) with serde defaults so existing callers don't break
- Discriminator + tests
- Documentation update in forensics.rs module doc

## Provenance

mu-uvsq scope; mu-yyi (process feedback against `#[ignore]`) is the operator policy this enforces in the analytics layer.

title: mu-phl v0 — memory recall MVP (goal session 2026-05-21)
description:
Goal-tracking bead for the 2026-05-21 implementation of mu-phl v0 — memory recall wire-up. The full design lives at `~/.claude-personal/plans/happy-sprouting-sprout.md` (operator-approved 2026-05-21).

## Goal narrative

Every newly created mu session starts with the project context that claude-code sessions get: a markdown snapshot from `agent memory context --cwd <session_cwd>` plus the contents of the relevant CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md hierarchy for that cwd, injected as `MemoryInjection` and `FileLoad` spans in the stable cacheable prefix of the rope.

This is v0 — a focused 1-day landing that reuses existing infrastructure. The code path is structured so v1's full mu-phl event-pointer port and v1's mu-ywr capability-filtered discovery slot in **without touching the rope-assembly call site again**.

## Architecture (one-line)

`RecallProvider` trait + `SubprocessRecallProvider` (shells out to `agent`) + `ProjectFileRecallProvider` (reads .md hierarchy) → builds `ProjectContext` → attached to `AgentConfig` at session creation → injected as `SpanKind::MemoryInjection` / `SpanKind::FileLoad` spans in the stable program-region prefix of the rope.

## Sub-beads (this goal session)

This bead lists the 7 sub-beads in dependency order. Each is one atomic commit per the mu per-repo goal-protocol bead-per-commit rule.

Tagged `goal:2026-05-21:<this-bead-id>` for queryability.

## Out of scope for v0

Full mu-phl event-pointer architecture; mu-ywr filtered/marked discovery enforcement; mid-session recall; auto-ingest of .md files into mu's event log; TUI surfacing changes. All documented in the plan's "Seams left for v1+" section.

## Parent

This work IS the v0 slice of `mu-phl` (the open P2 architecture bead). Parent: mu-phl.

## Stop criteria additions (this session)

Beyond the seven defaults + mu's three repo-specific (capability invariant; provider/wire-protocol spec amendment; generated-file edits):

- If step 1 (system_prompt String→SpanText migration) surfaces more than 8 construction sites OR touches anything outside the trivial mechanical change, halt and reconsider — that's evidence the migration has a hidden scope larger than v0 should swallow.
- If step 5 (daemon wiring) requires changes to JSON-RPC message shape beyond the documented `pub cwd: Option<PathBuf>` additions, halt and file a spec amendment per mu rule #9 before continuing.

Live mu ask smoke deferred to operator. Side memories saved for Capsicum/Casper (bcb00d60) + libmap+jail_attach (8f7b079c). MORNING briefing at MORNING_2026-05-21.md. title: agent loop hardcodes Done.stop_reason — masks MaxTokens/StopSequence from per-turn stream
description:
The agent loop in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs` always emits `AgentEvent::Done { stop_reason: StopReason::EndTurn, ... }` (or `StopReason::Aborted` for cancellations). It never propagates the provider's per-turn `StopReason` (e.g. `MaxTokens`, `StopSequence`) into the session-level Done event.

## Symptom observed

Opus 4.7 session against mu, 2026-05-14, event log `03b75bc4a9c48f82`:
- Last `assistant_message_event` payload: `stop_reason: "max_tokens"` ← real
- `done` event payload: `stop_reason: "end_turn"` ← misleading projection

The Opus response was cut off mid-sentence (max_tokens cap of 4096 hit). The TUI's "session done" affordance, which reads the Done event, reported it as a clean end_turn — making the truncation invisible to the operator. Same pattern would have appeared with Haiku at the same cap.

## Code locations

All `AgentEvent::Done` emissions in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs`:
- `:645` — iteration cap hit → hardcodes `StopReason::EndTurn`
- `:738` — OutstandingCancelled → `StopReason::Aborted` (correct)
- `:805` — another cancel path → `StopReason::Aborted` (correct)
- `:992` — normal completion → hardcodes `StopReason::EndTurn`
- `:1043` — another completion path → hardcodes `StopReason::EndTurn`
- `:1068` — Outcome::Done helper → hardcodes `StopReason::EndTurn`

The provider returns `AssistantMessage` with a real `stop_reason` (Anthropic provider maps `\"max_tokens\"` → `StopReason::MaxTokens` correctly at `anthropic.rs:344`), but the agent loop discards it.

## Why this matters

Event-sourced context architecture treats projections as derived from the event stream. The `done` event is supposed to reduce per-turn signal into a session-level summary. When the reduction loses information that the source preserved, the projection is wrong. This is exactly the failure mode the architecture is designed to prevent — finding it here means a regression test discipline gap.

Operator impact: a truncated response that looks complete in the TUI. Bigger consequence: any tool/automation downstream of the Done event makes wrong decisions about whether to retry or extend.

## Scope

IN:
- Propagate the final turn's `StopReason` from `assistant_msg.stop_reason` into all `Done` emissions where a turn just completed.
- Hold the latest-known stop_reason in the agent loop's local state, updated each turn.
- For paths where the loop terminates without a final turn (iteration cap, no provider call), keep current behavior (`EndTurn` / `Aborted`) but document why.
- Test: per-turn stop_reason of MaxTokens / StopSequence / EndTurn propagates correctly into the Done event.
- Test: cancellation path still emits Aborted regardless of provider stop_reason.

OUT:
- TUI rendering improvements for the new state (separate bead — operator badge for "response was truncated").
- Auto-continuation policy (\"if max_tokens hit, ask the model to continue\" — separate decision).

## Acceptance

- Done event's stop_reason matches the underlying per-turn provider stop_reason on the natural-completion path.
- New regression tests in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_tests.rs`.
- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings green
- One commit closes this bead

## References

- Provider stop_reason mapping: `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs:344`
- StopReason enum: `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs`
- Symptom event log: `/home/tcovert/.local/share/mu/events/03b75bc4a9c48f82/session-1.jsonl`
close_reason: Merged in PR #11 as d0acadf

title: mu-edit-patch: diff/patch as a first-class editing primitive (complement to line-based Edit tool)
description:
Today, mu's Edit tool is line-based string replacement (old_string → new_string). This works but: (1) multi-hunk edits require many separate tool calls, (2) ambiguous matches force the model to include lots of surrounding context, (3) some patterns are easier to express as unified diffs than as old/new pairs.

Aider's approach: the model emits unified diffs; the harness applies them via patch-like logic. More robust for multi-hunk + structural edits. Worth a complementary primitive (NOT a replacement for Edit; both have their uses).

## Scope

IN:
- New `Patch` (or `ApplyDiff`) tool in `crates/mu-coding/src/tools/`
- Input: unified diff string OR (file_path, hunks: [{old_lines, new_lines, position}]) — pick one
- Application logic: use the `similar` or `diffy` crate from crates.io; fail cleanly when hunks don't apply (return position + context)
- Returns: success/failure per hunk, list of applied hunks, list of rejected hunks with reason
- Same Read-required convention as Edit

OUT:
- Replacing Edit. Both stay; agents pick the right one for the job.
- Conflict resolution (3-way merge). v1 is straight apply-or-reject.
- Cross-file refactors (single-file patches only).

## Acceptance

- New `Patch`/`ApplyDiff` tool registered in mu-coding
- Schema documents the input format choice
- At least three test cases: clean apply, ambiguous apply, rejected hunk with structured error
- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings green

## Design fork (decide at impl time)

Two reasonable input shapes:

A. **Unified diff string** — matches `patch(1)`'s format. Familiar to humans. Model emits text that LLMs are good at generating. Implementation parses the diff format.

B. **Structured hunks JSON** — schema-validated input. Less natural for the model to emit but easier to validate. Implementation skips parsing.

Lean A: aider's experience shows unified diffs work well in practice. The downside (parsing fragility) is mitigated by the `similar`/`diffy` crates.

## References

- Aider's diff approach: https://aider.chat/docs/more-info.html (their edit format docs)
- similar crate: https://crates.io/crates/similar
- diffy crate: https://crates.io/crates/diffy
- mu's existing Edit tool: `crates/mu-coding/src/tools/edit.rs`
- Architecture gap noted 2026-05-14 alongside mu-web-fetch + mu-file-watch

title: anthropic wire-projection: System-role spans beyond system-prompt get dropped + cache_control coverage incomplete
description:
Same pattern as mu-2puu (codex) and mu-745h (openrouter), Anthropic edition. Two interacting issues:

## Issue 1: System-content hoisting (same bug shape as siblings)

translate_provider_messages in crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs:331-381 only captures the FIRST System-role projected message whose source_span_id equals literally "system-prompt" into system_text. Every other System-role projected span — including mu-phl v0's memory-recall:* and project-file:* spans — gets silently dropped. The comment at line 325 even says "assemble_rope produces at most one" — outdated by mu-phl v0.

## Issue 2: Cache-control coverage (Anthropic-specific)

detect_cache_targets at lines 505-527 walks the projection looking for cache_control markers, but only recognizes "system-prompt" and "tool-schema:*" span ids as triggers for system_should_cache / tools_should_cache. Cache markers on memory-recall:* or project-file:* spans (which the AnthropicCacheStrategy may place there post-mu-phl) don't set system_should_cache, so the resulting body.system block won't carry cache_control even though those spans are now contributing to body.system.

## Fix

1. translate_provider_messages: hoist ALL non-tool-schema System spans, concatenate with \n\n into system_text. Mirrors mu-2puu / mu-745h shape.
2. detect_cache_targets: trigger system_should_cache on cache markers attached to ANY non-tool-schema System-role span (system-prompt, memory-recall:*, project-file:*, future kinds). Tool-schema marker still goes to tools_should_cache.

## Regression tests

Mirror mu_2puu_* / mu_745h_* shape:
1. Recall-injected rope concatenates all non-tool-schema System content into body.system block
2. Tool-schema content does not leak into body.system
3. Cache-control: marker on a memory-recall span triggers cache_control on body.system

## Verification

- cargo test --workspace green (including yqeq4_parity_* tests still passing)
- cargo clippy clean
- Live smoke against opus: optional but recommended (costs $0.05-0.20). canary value w11wKFoinTnTRMS already seeded in agent.sqlite (memory d39d153b) — mu ask --provider anthropic-api --model claude-opus-4-7 should surface it.

## Stack

Stacks on PR #116 (openrouter fix), which stacks on #115 (mu-phl v0 + codex fix), which stacks on #114 (feat/mu-yqeq).
close_reason: Fixed in PR #117. Two-part fix: (1) translate_provider_messages now hoists all non-tool-schema System-role spans (concatenated with \n\n) into body.system; (2) detect_cache_targets recognizes cache markers on any non-tool-schema System-role span as system_should_cache triggers, not just on system-prompt. 3 regression tests cover the case (mu_s855_projected_concatenates... + mu_s855_projected_excludes_tool_schema... + mu_s855_cache_marker_on_recall_span_triggers...). 776 tests pass, clippy clean. Live smoke against opus deferred (operator's call; costs $0.05-0.20).

title: TUI: realtime context-window explorer panel (cache + memory state, CC-style timeline)
description:
Implement a TUI panel modeled on Claude Code's `/en/context-window` interactive timeline, showing live cache and memory state for the active session. Operator-flagged 2026-05-22 as a daily-driver win now that mu-phl-v0 memory recall has shipped — F3 needs to surface what's in context, what's cached, and what survives compaction.

## Source

`specs/architecture/claude-code-feature-mapping.md` §1 (Tier 1, "Context-window explorer"). The CC docs ship a fully data-driven interactive timeline. Data model worth modeling exactly:

- **Event record**: `{t: 0.0–1.0, kind, label, tokens, color, vis, desc, link, tip, noSurviveCompact?}`
  - `kind` ∈ `{auto, user, claude, hook, sub, compact}`
  - `vis` ∈ `{hidden, brief, full}` → ●/◐/○ visibility indicator
  - `noSurviveCompact: true` flag on skills (the one source that doesn't reload after `/compact`)
- **11-color legend**: System / CLAUDE.md / Memory / Skills / MCP / Rules / Files / Output / Claude / You / Hooks
- **Gate events**: prompts, `!` bang commands, `/` slash commands, and `/compact` — render as scrub-pause points

## mu fit

Maps onto `RetainedRope` / `ProviderStatusSnapshot` (mu-nat, mu-di4). Rope spans already are an event stream — add `(token_cost, visibility, survives_compact)` tags per span and render.

## Scope

In:
- Extend `Span` metadata with three tags: `token_cost: u32`, `visibility: SpanVisibility`, `survives_compact: bool`
- New TUI panel (likely F4 or extension of F5) rendering the live timeline with the 11-color legend
- Real-time updates as events fire; cache-marker boundary visible (where `AnthropicCacheStrategy` placed it)
- Drill-in keypress to show `desc`/`tip` for a focused span
- Span-kind → color mapping table documented

Out:
- The Wikipedia-style "interactive video playback" affordance — defer; daily-driver needs the now-state first
- Survives-compaction simulator — defer
- Persistence/replay of timelines — defer

## Dependencies

- Span metadata extension lands first; renderer is downstream
- After mu-3aa + mu-6oqm (OpenRouter renderer), cache annotations will be uniform across providers; this panel becomes provider-agnostic

## References

- specs/architecture/claude-code-feature-mapping.md §1 (Tier 1)
- specs/architecture/claude-code-design-influences.md (companion synthesis doc)
- mu-nat (RetainedRope)
- mu-di4 (ProviderStatusSnapshot)
- mu-fb0 (live-loop adoption — integration point)
- CC source: code.claude.com/docs/en/context-window
- Memory ff33f770 (design-influences doc pointer)

## Acceptance

- `Span` carries `token_cost`, `visibility`, `survives_compact`
- Panel renders live during streaming without stalling F3 (cross-ref: mu-304g for F3 streaming-render correctness)
- 11-color legend visible
- Cache boundary marker visible

title: build mu-worker pot capability: Rust template + safe memory/task_log mount strategy + mu-spawn wrapper (enables walk-away goal-protocol runs against mu)
Realtime token ticker: surface mid-stream usage (message_start/message_delta) to mu-solo, reconcile to exact on final usage ('top up or drop')
description: Operator design (2026-06-03): process incoming streams for realtime stats updates in mu-solo; when the provider's authoritative count arrives, top up or drop to the exact number. The data ALREADY flows: anthropic.rs parses usage from message_start (exact input_tokens + cache_read/cache_creation at stream START) and message_delta (running cumulative output_tokens) and merges into AnthropicUsage — but only surfaces it in the final ProviderEvent::Done. Plumbing slice: (1) adapter emits the merged usage as it updates (extend ProviderEvent or AgentEvent::ProviderStatus with optional tokens-so-far); (2) forwarder includes it in the session.provider_status wire notification (tracker already streams elapsed/bytes); (3) mu-solo status line ticks live and snaps to exact when Done usage lands. Per provider: Anthropic = input exact at stream start (also feeds mu-wsgx's feedback predictor with last_actual immediately), output exact-running via message_delta; codex = usage only at response.completed, so tick output via local estimate (cl100k IS the OpenAI tokenizer family — near-exact for codex) and snap at end. UsageSemantics stamps (PR #164) make the displayed interpretation provider-correct. NOTE: do this AFTER mu-9x4j (duplicate notification processing) — a live ticker over a wire path that occasionally double-delivers would double-tick.

'What we just came to is fairly important — it distills what a model/agent is down to its essence.' Home: specs/architecture/ (graduation to README-adjacent later, operator's call). Sources: memories fd8ebb16, ddeb2944, 7c457291; gpt session mu:a0d2e6c477c091a2; beads mu-mh4, mu-dvmu, mu-m7x, nxpo; experiment bootloader-startup-ab-2026-06-06.
