title: Pot template: add tcovert user (UID 1001) + flip mu/pi/claude runtimes to jexec -u tcovert
description:
## Context

Currently the agent-runtime pot template has no tcovert user — only root. agent-spawn invokes runtimes as 'jexec POT_NAME env ... mu ask ...' with no user flag, so mu and pi (and now claude per mu-claude-pot bead) all run as root inside the pot.

Operator preference (stated 2026-05-17): 'I try never to run as root, even in jails.' Operator chose to follow the existing root pattern for the claude integration to avoid scope creep, but flagged this as debt to pay down.

## Acceptance

- agent-runtime template /etc/passwd has tcovert (uid=1001, gid=1001) matching host
- /home/tcovert/ exists in template, owned 1001:1001
- agent-spawn invokes runtimes as 'jexec -u tcovert POT_NAME env ...' for mu, pi, and claude branches
- All three runtimes verified working under the new user
- ~/pot-bin/ mount-in path and other host-bound mounts work without root inside the pot

## Risk

File-ownership inconsistencies if existing pots' on-disk state was written as root and a tcovert-user runtime needs to read it. Probably not an issue for ephemeral pots (each spawn is fresh) but worth checking.

## Snapshot dance required

User add + /home/tcovert/ creation needs the working-YYYY-MM-DD-* snapshot lexically beating the current pin (see freebsd-jails skill).

## Source

Operator 2026-05-17 during claude-in-pot integration.

title: Per-session JSONL event log on disk (write-side persistence)
description:
mu serve writes every SessionEvent to a per-session JSONL file as it's appended to the in-memory event log. No read-back-at-restart logic — that's mu-mh4 Phase 2. Write-only.

Motivation: when sharing TUI test sessions with Claude (or any other agent / human reviewer), an on-disk log is faster, more accurate (no copy-paste truncation), and structurally typed (JSONL parses, terminal screenshots don't).

Scope:
  - Path: ~/.local/share/mu/events/<daemon_id>/<session_id>.jsonl
  - One serde-encoded SessionEvent per line
  - Append-only; no rotation in v1 (mu sessions are bounded by daemon lifetime)
  - Daemon-id directory means concurrent daemons coexist cleanly
  - Write fails do NOT block in-memory append (log fsync delay must not stall the agent loop)

Implementation:
  - SessionEventLog gains an optional disk_writer field
  - serve_with_io creates the file on session insert
  - On append(), write the encoded line; ignore IO errors (best-effort)
  - File opens for append on first event, drops on session removal

Composes with mu-mh4: this is the FOUNDATION for cold-reload. Once write-side is durable, mu-mh4 Phase 2 adds the read-at-startup loop + SessionDiscovery resurrection.

Composes with mu-pex (metrics framework): the per-session JSONL files become a natural source for offline analysis (median TTFT per provider, cost-of-correctness over time, etc.) without needing a live daemon connection.

Also useful for protocol-eval — instead of stitching events back together from notifications, the analysis layer can just walk the JSONL files.

Filed P2; small implementation (30-60 min).
close_reason: done

title: dashboard: Anthropic Usage/Cost Admin API reconciliation panel — mu's computed costs vs billed reality
description:
Operator-ratified 2026-06-06. Add a reconciliation panel to gen_mu_dashboard.py: pull the Anthropic Admin Usage & Cost API (org-level usage_report/cost endpoints) on the hourly cron and render billed tokens/cost per day per model NEXT TO mu's computed virtual costs (harness_costs view), with a divergence line.

PREREQ: an Admin API key (sk-ant-admin…) — operator unsure one exists; check console Settings → API keys / Admin keys; create if absent (operator action, key into the env file the cron sources, NEVER logged or committed).

WHY: validated to the cent 2026-06-06 — mu computed $2.77 for session mu:3dd40c3c1f84f278/session-1 (19 asks, out 23,709); console billed $2.77, out 23,709, exact match. A standing panel catches: pricing-table drift (model price changes), caching regressions (tools-changed spikes show as computed<billed divergence), and billing surprises — without reading bar-chart slivers by hand. Note console quirks observed: hourly ingest lag, UTC day boundaries, some subpages updating unevenly (miss-reason charts stuck at Jun 1 while composition showed Jun 3-6).

SHAPE: ~02ig-sized. One API client function (python, requests already in scripts env), one vega panel + divergence table, degrade gracefully when the admin key is absent (panel renders 'no admin key' note). Daily granularity is enough; reconcile per-model. Acceptance: dashboard shows billed-vs-computed for the trailing 7 days with divergence; missing-key path clean.

Terrain-verified endpoints (cost_report amount in CENTS, no per-model group_by); list-price↔cost_report reconciles to the cent every day in window; three structural divergence classes surfaced (codex excluded, Max5 OAuth subscription-billed invisible to API, UTC bucketing). No-key degradation verified. Secret handling per rules. Director-verified live.

title: mu-fvy0-classify-refinement (overnight 2026-05-19): expand claim parser + add 2 outcome classes
description:
## Tracking bead for /goal session 2026-05-19

Refine the forensics classifier (`crates/mu-core/src/forensics.rs`, mu-8alb / spec mu-041). Currently uses only `exit_reason`; the TaskTelemetry envelope has 13 unused signal fields. Claim parser is narrow on purpose but missing common patterns we routinely commit.

## Sub-beads (tagged `goal:2026-05-19:<this-bead-id>`)

1. **Expand claim parser** — add patterns for `Cargo.toml`, `crates/<crate>/Cargo.toml`, `.github/workflows/*.yml`, `crates/<crate>/(benches|examples)/*.rs`. Each new pattern carries unit tests including a negative case (false-positive shape we don't want to flag).
2. **Add SkippedTest outcome** — new `Outcome::SkippedTest` class. Detect diffs that add `+#[ignore]` to existing tests (mu's per-repo rule #1 against `#[ignore]` shortcuts). Discriminator runs AFTER hollow_commit (which is more severe) but BEFORE CI-based discriminators (a commit can be SkippedTest + CI-green; SkippedTest wins because tests being added-and-ignored is a process violation, not a CI artifact).
3. **Add CapabilityInflation outcome** — new `Outcome::CapabilityInflation` class. Detect when `tools_granted.len()` significantly exceeds `tools_actually_called.len()` (over-provisioned capability grant). Probable confidence; rationale names the unused tools. Threshold: granted ≥ 3 AND used ≤ 50% of granted.

## Out of scope (explicit deferrals)

- Multi-classification (returning Vec<Classification>) — bigger architectural change; separate bead.
- Numeric confidence (0.0-1.0) — current Definite/Probable/Inferred is sufficient for v1.
- New event-stream-aware signals beyond what's in TaskTelemetry envelope — separate beads when needed.
- Integration with the analytics backfill / sink layer — sub-beads only touch forensics.rs.

## Acceptance

- Three sub-beads closed with their commits + tests
- `cargo test --workspace` green at every commit
- Each commit ends with proper `## Files` block
- briefing in `MORNING_2026-05-19.md` with results / surprises / suggested next session

## Stop criteria (per-repo + standing)

Default seven (skill) + mu's additions (#8 capability invariant, #9 protocol-spec drift, #10 generated-file edits). For this session none of those should fire — work is pure inside forensics.rs.

Specific stop addition: **if a discriminator change would alter the existing test fixtures' expected outcomes**, halt — that's a behavior change for downstream analytics consumers and needs operator review.
notes: Deferred 2026-05-19 — operator pivoted to delegation-with-postmortem methodology using multi-line prompt as a more bounded experiment target. Forensics scope is good for a future overnight; the goal-protocol research cadence wants a smaller bounded task tonight to study process not output.

title: hook priority list: evidence-graded start-with and phase-two sets
description:
Prioritized hook backlog from Opus mu-solo session (2026-05-27). Each hook maps to a documented failure in behavioral memories.

## Start-with (week 1-2, high evidence, high leverage)

1. sed -i interception (PreToolUse, blocking) — destroyed app.rs twice. Regex-detect in bash, refuse with Python alternative.
2. Topical recall on prompt submit (UserPromptSubmit, advisory) — highest leverage single intervention. Vector recall against prompt text, surface top 2-3 hits above similarity threshold. Addresses 'I forget to query memory' at exactly the right moment. Budget-capped: never inject more than ~500 tokens.
3. Destructive VCS op confirmation (PreToolUse, prompting) — git reset --hard, push --force, jj abandon, rm -rf. Surface rule, require stated authorization.
4. Host-Python interception (PreToolUse, blocking) — pip install, uv pip, maturin outside pot context. Previously broke work repo.
5. Pre-action capability check for autonomous sessions (SessionStart, advisory) — smoke-test path reachability, surface failures upfront. Prevents $7+ approval-denial loop burns.

## Phase-two (after capability manifest exists)

6. Tool-choice nudges (PreToolUse, advisory) — grep → code_recall/LSP. Requires knowing what's actually loaded.
7. Spline-jail invocation correction (PreToolUse, advisory) — Playwright/cert-harness pattern.
8. PostToolUse verify prompts (PostToolUse, advisory) — 'verify the result before continuing.' Carefully tuned.
9. Decision/memory injection on tool-call (PreToolUse, advisory) — requires decision log.
10. Session-end summary (SessionEnd, action) — requires journal API.

## Speculative (earn it)
11. Cost-budget enforcement
12. Typo correction
13. Performative-signoff suppression (probably-never — wrong layer)
14. Auto-checkpoint (probably-never — violates agency)

Watch for: hook fatigue (threshold tuning), silent enforcement (always name the rule), drift between hook and documentation.

title: c137-dialogue-mcp: clean up target/ pollution in chore(cargo) commit before any further work there
description:
## Block

Before making any further changes to `~/src/c137-dialogue-mcp`, clean up the polluted chore commit from the 2026-05-19 .cargo/config.toml rollout.

## Current state

- Commit `tqultwuwotrv` (3e3760ea123e) has description 'chore(cargo): TMPDIR=target/tmp via .cargo/config.toml' but contains:
  - target/.rustc_info.json
  - target/CACHEDIR.TAG
  - target/debug/.cargo-lock
  - target/debug/.fingerprint/* (many files)
  - target/debug/build/* (more files)
  - .gitignore (with one line: 'target/')
- Commit `qsnttnqqmuuv` (de203ff05b0a, description 'add HTTP transport (--http / MODE=http) — same 3 tools, REST shape') has the actual `.cargo/config.toml` mixed in with HTTP transport WIP work.
- A fresh `.gitignore` containing `target/` was written 2026-05-19 to repo root (uncommitted at the time of cleanup; may have been auto-snapshotted into one of the commits by now).

## Root cause

c137-dialogue-mcp didn't have `.gitignore` for target/ before the rollout. The rollout's `jj new -B @ -m "chore(cargo)..."` invocation auto-snapshotted the dirty working dir (which included target/ build artifacts plus my new `.cargo/config.toml`) into one or both commits depending on jj's internal ordering. The other 7 affected jj repos were squash-clean because they already had target/ in their .gitignore — only c137-dialogue-mcp lacked it.

## Recommended cleanup

1. **Add target/ to .gitignore and commit it standalone** (if not already done): `jj new -m "chore: ignore target/"`, then verify .gitignore content is just `target/` (no other accidental files).
2. **Strip target/ artifacts from tqultwuwotrv**: `jj restore -r tqultwuwotrv` with explicit paths, OR `jj abandon tqultwuwotrv` if no salvageable content (only the target/ junk is in there).
3. **Extract .cargo/config.toml from qsnttnqqmuuv** into its own commit: `jj split -r qsnttnqqmuuv` and put just the .cargo/config.toml in the first half.
4. **Verify**: `jj diff -r <any-non-chore-commit> --stat` should show no target/ files; `.cargo/config.toml` should appear in exactly one chore-shaped commit.

## Why this is filed pre-work-blocker

Operator decision (2026-05-19): pin this so we don't accidentally compound the mess. Before \*any\* commit work in c137-dialogue-mcp resumes, the cleanup has to happen — otherwise new commits land on top of the polluted base and the cleanup gets harder.

## Cross-refs

- [[host_claude_print_dispatch_gotchas_2026_05_19]] — the rollout-tooling lessons
- [[workers_test_not_assume_environmental_limits_2026_05_19]] — sibling rollout finding
- Earlier .cargo/config.toml chore commit landed in PR #107 (merged into mu's main)
close_reason: Source recovered at jj tip (6801f70), .gitignore conflict resolved, builds clean. No GitHub remote yet — will create when actively developing. target/ pollution no longer an issue.

title: Periodic codex smoke-test: validate mu-035 heartbeat catches backend stalls
description:
## Why

mu-035 Phase B added periodic ProviderStatusUpdate ticks while in non-streaming waits specifically so a stalled provider stays visible instead of hanging silently. The classic motivating incident was the 14hr openai-codex stall observed 2026-05-10/11 (see mu-cea). Validating that the heartbeat actually catches a recurrence — and how fast — needs running codex regularly enough to hit a stall in practice.

Filed as a behavioral / monitoring task: not new code, but a habit + a place to record observations.

## What

Use openai-codex as the periodic provider for at least 1 ask/day during normal mu testing. Watch the F5 dashboard:
- ttft p95 for openai_codex should look like other providers' (sub-second to a few seconds typical).
- If a stall is in progress, the periodic ProviderStatusUpdate ticks should keep arriving (visible in F8 firehose) even when the model isn't sending content.
- Wall p95 should be the slowest signal; if it diverges wildly from ttft+stream+tool, that's a stall signature.

If a stall recurs:
1. Reopen mu-cea (or file a new bead) with the date + duration + recovery mechanism.
2. Check whether mu-035's heartbeat fired during the stall (it should — if not, that's a separate bug).
3. Verify session.cancel_outstanding (mu-035 Phase C) works to abort cleanly.

## Cross-reference

- mu-cea: BUG / external — openai-codex backend stalls
- mu-035: provider_status heartbeat
- The heartbeat is the load-bearing safety net here; the only way to know it works is to actually use codex regularly.

title: mu-7rk-a: Auth protocol types — AuthMechanism enum, AuthDenialCode, request/response types
description:
**mu-7rk decomposition phase A** — Auth protocol types only. No server, no client, no behavior change.

## Why

Original mu-7rk tried to land protocol + server + handler + client + enforcement + transport-close + hardening in one bead. Codex review (2026-05-15) rejected it as "security theater" with 4 blockers and 9 important issues. Decomposition splits the work along natural seams; this is phase A: pure wire types.

## Scope

### In

- `mu-core::protocol::AuthMechanism` enum with `Bearer` variant + `Other(String)` extensibility variant (per codex's important issue #4: closed enum makes "future-extensible" claim hollow). Snake_case wire serialization.
- `mu-core::protocol::AuthDenialCode` enum with at minimum: `InvalidCredentials`, `UnsupportedMechanism`, `MalformedExchange`. Snake_case wire serialization.
- `AuthOfferRequest`, `AuthInitiateRequest`, `AuthResponseRequest`, `AuthExchangeResponse` structs. **All with `#[serde(deny_unknown_fields)]`** (per codex important #9).
- `Display` impl for `AuthMechanism` (per codex minor #4: avoid serde_json round-trip just to format the wire name).
- Roundtrip tests for serialization, including: known-mechanism deserializes to `Bearer`; unknown mechanism deserializes to `Other("foo")`; unknown fields rejected; enum casing matches snake_case.

### Out

- Any handler implementations (mu-7rk-b)
- Any dispatcher wiring (mu-7rk-b)
- Any server-side state (mu-7rk-b)
- Any client side (mu-7rk-e)
- Any enforcement (mu-7rk-c)

## Acceptance

- All new types public in `mu-core::protocol`
- `cargo test --workspace` green
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean
- `cargo fmt --check` clean
- No behavior change in existing code paths (no callers yet)

## References

- Original bead: mu-7rk (now closed)
- Codex antagonistic review: `~/.claude-personal/experiments/codex-review-mu-7rk-output.txt` — see "important_issues" #4 and #9 for the design corrections in this bead
- Reference diff (v0 attempt, rejected): jj rev `twztzykv 78978fdb` in workspace `.delegations/mu-7rk-2026-05-15`. The protocol types in v0 are mostly correct shape; this bead extracts them with the codex corrections applied (extensible mechanism enum, deny_unknown_fields).
- Provenance: decomposition discussion 2026-05-15 with operator after codex review.
close_reason: Merged in PR #44 (3032268) — mu-7rk-a auth protocol types. br dep graph treats vha as blocked by yox but actual merge order was vha→yox→m84; deps are reverse-direction.

title: mu-solo: dump-on-flip — Fullscreen->Inline re-renders session slice from event log into native scrollback
description: On ViewportMode Fullscreen->Inline, re-render the session event slice from the durable log into native scrollback via viewport insert_before (Codex flip behavior, whole-session via render_transcript_lines). Read-only projection; reuse insert_before only. Accept: mu-vt-probe asserts post-flip transcript lines present in scrollback above the inline viewport.

title: mu-coding: surface per-tool approval policy at session start (which tools gate, which don't)
description:
## Problem

The bash tool can be configured with `--bash-prompt` (per-call approval
required). Other tools (read, write, edit, grep, glob, ls) have their
own approval policies (or none). The agent has no way to know which
tools gate from inside the session.

This shows up as **model-strategy asymmetry** under approval-gated
daemons:

- gpt's tool-selection prior favors `read`/`grep`/`glob`
- Claude's tool-selection prior favors `bash`
- Same daemon, same tool surface, same prompt → very different friction
  because Claude's bash calls trigger popups and gpt's read calls don't

The agent has no way to know this constraint exists; only the operator
knows which tools are gated. The result is the agent picks tools by its
training prior, and friction emerges as a function of (model_prior x
operator_policy) without the agent being able to adapt.

Empirically observed (2026-05-16): same daemon (f3845422), same review
prompt, claude-opus-4-7 session-9 hit approval modals on every bash
call (timing gaps 800ms–47000ms); openai_codex/gpt-5.5 session-4 hit
zero modals because it used only typed tools.

## Proposal

At session start, surface per-tool approval policy to the agent.
Options (could combine):

(a) **In each tool's description** — e.g., bash's tool schema
    description text could include "requires per-call user approval
    when daemon is in --bash-prompt mode"

(b) **In session_created payload** — extend with an `approval_policy`
    map:
    ```json
    "approval_policy": {
      "bash": "per-call",
      "read": "none",
      "write": "per-call",   // hypothetical
      "edit": "per-call",    // hypothetical
      "grep": "none",
      "glob": "none",
      "ls": "none"
    }
    ```

With (b), the agent could reason about it explicitly ("read doesn't
gate, prefer it for exploration") and the operator could query the
policy without running a tool to discover it.

## Acceptance

- Agent can determine per-tool approval policy without invoking the
  tool (and waiting for the result)
- Policy updates auto-reflect in the surfaced information
- Per-tool policy is per-session (correctly reflects the daemon's
  flags the session inherited)
- The asymmetry observed in the f3845422 incident becomes addressable
  at the agent layer (Claude could choose differently if it knew the
  bash-gating constraint)

## Why this matters

Without legibility, the agent's tool-selection prior collides
invisibly with the operator's policy. Worst case: an agent picks a
heavily-gated tool repeatedly because it has no signal that the
lighter-gated alternative exists. Best case: the agent picks
correctly by luck. Either way, the experience asymmetry is invisible
and the cause is non-obvious (took us most of an evening to diagnose
this exact symptom).

Complements the bash strict-mode policy legibility work (mu-6hl) on a
parallel axis (per-tool approval policy, not bash-specific policy).

## Cross-refs

- mu-6hl (bash strict-mode policy legibility — same observability
  family, bash-policy axis)
- mu-cr4 (PreToolUse hook for JIT tool-conventions injection — could
  be the surfacing mechanism)
- Companion to the approval-lifecycle events bead (filed same session
  — recording the lifecycle is the audit side; surfacing the policy
  is the agent-side)
- Provenance: TUI asymmetry diagnosis, daemon f3845422 session-9 vs
  session-4 (2026-05-16)

title: mu-phl v0 Phase B — substrate seam: recall module + ProjectContext + assemble_rope_with_context
description:
Step 2 of mu-phl v0 (plan at `~/.claude-personal/plans/happy-sprouting-sprout.md`). Substrate seam, no runtime behavior change.

## What

- Add `crates/mu-core/src/context/recall/mod.rs` with the trait `RecallProvider`, value types `RecalledItem` (using `SpanText` + `SpanId` from `rope.rs`), `RecallSource`, and `ProjectContext`.
- Add `project_context: Option<ProjectContext>` field to `AgentConfig` (default `None`).
- Add `assemble_rope_with_context(system_prompt, project_context, messages, tool_specs)` in `crates/mu-core/src/context/assembly.rs`. Have the existing `assemble_rope` call it with `None`.
- Wire the rope-build call site in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/mod.rs:770` to use the new function.

## Why

Wire surface before behavior (mu per-repo rule #4 / mu-036 pattern). v0's subprocess + project-file providers slot into this trait; v1's `EventLogRecallProvider` (full mu-phl spec) slots in here too without changing any other call site.

## Acceptance

- All existing tests pass — this commit is a no-op refactor on the runtime side; only the type surface grew.
- Span layout test verifies `[System] | [Memory] | [Files] | [ToolSchemas] | [Messages]` order when `project_context` is `Some(...)`, and `[System] | [ToolSchemas] | [Messages]` when `None` (existing test).
- New types use `SpanText` and `SpanId` aliases, not bare `Arc<str>` or `String`.
- Commit message has `## Files` block + `(closes <this-bead>)`.

## Blocked by

- mu-phl v0 Phase A (step 1) — uses the migrated `SpanText` `AgentConfig::system_prompt` field in test fixtures.

New module crates/mu-core/src/context/recall/ with RecallProvider trait + RecalledItem/RecallSource/ProjectContext value types (using SpanText/SpanId per mu-yqeq.2). New entry point assemble_rope_with_context in assembly.rs injecting MemoryInjection + FileLoad spans between System and ToolSchemas at RetentionClass::Startup (cacheable). Existing assemble_rope becomes a thin wrapper. 2 new tests + 2 tests.rs explicit-construction updates. cargo test --workspace: 757 pass (+2 from new tests). title: Future: $EDITOR prompt-edit audit events
description:
When mu supports $EDITOR for full prompt editing, prefer supervisor/UI-owned editor launch rather than launching arbitrary editors inside constrained workers. The worker asks the supervisor/broker for a prompt edit; supervisor creates a controlled temp file, launches $EDITOR, reads the result, then submits finalized text.

Consider durable events:
- PromptEditRequested
- PromptEditStarted { path/editor }
- PromptEditCompleted { sha256_before, sha256_after, bytes, duration_ms }
- PromptSubmitted

This preserves provenance while avoiding giving Capsicum-constrained workers broad editor/plugin/shell authority.

Related memory: c8137625.

title: goal-per-step-verification: /goal workers verify tool-call outcomes before continuing
description:
Standing discipline addition to the goal-protocol skill: autonomous /goal workers must verify every tool-call outcome before consuming the result downstream. The verification step is what prevents silent-success-of-actually-failed operations from compounding into wrong-path budget burns.

## Load-bearing argument (compounding-failure math)

Per harness-engineering.ai (and intuited from our own experiments): if each tool call succeeds 95% of the time and failures don't self-correct, a 20-step task succeeds 0.95^20 = 36% of the time. Verification at each step (e.g., schema check + precondition revalidation) restores end-to-end completion to ~96%, per their cited data.

Our own /goal experiments confirm the failure mode:

- **Experiment 4 (failed mu-ovl-A)** burned  cap on 11+ identical Edit retries because the Edit error 'File has not been read yet' wasn't verified before retry. Loop-guard halted the spin at the wrapper level, not the worker level.
- **Experiment 5 attempt 2 (permission-mode deadlock)** spun on Bash permission prompts. Same shape: tool failure not verified → retry with no change → loop-guard caught it.

Per-step verification at the WORKER level would catch these BEFORE they hit loop-guard, saving budget and producing more actionable failure signals.

## Discipline (skill-level rule, not a mu-code primitive)

Add to `~/.claude-personal/skills/goal-protocol/SKILL.md` standing rules:

> **Per-step verification.** After any tool call whose success-state is observable, verify the result before consuming it downstream:
> - For `Edit`/`Write` returning an error: do NOT retry the same call. Read the error text; if it names a precondition (e.g. 'File has not been read yet'), satisfy it first.
> - For `Bash` returning non-zero exit AND empty output: investigate before retrying.
> - For tool results that decide control flow (e.g. `cargo test` output determining whether to close a bead): verify the success signal explicitly (exit code AND test-result line), don't just check that the command produced output.
> - For `Edit` operations that should land specific text: optionally Read the file post-Edit to confirm the change took.

This sits in tool-conventions.md as a calling-convention discipline, not in mu-code.

## Out of scope for this bead (filed separately if/when surfaces)

- A mu-code primitive that auto-runs verification after each tool call (a Tool-trait extension or wrapper). The skill-level discipline above is the immediate fix; a mu-code primitive is a longer-term substrate that would let any agent (not just /goal workers) get verification automatically.
- Verification of OUTPUT SCHEMA (LLM-generated structured output validation). Different beast from tool-result verification.

## Acceptance

- SKILL.md gains a 'Per-step verification' standing rule with the patterns above
- Next /goal experiment (could be mu-2w7 design exploration or mu-fb0 live-loop adoption) demonstrates the discipline — worker either avoids the pattern OR halts cleanly on verification failure
- task_log entry from the next experiment confirms whether the discipline reduced wasted-turn cost vs experiments 4-5 baseline

## References

- harness-engineering.ai compounding-failure math: 95%^20 = 36% end-to-end completion without verification; 96% WITH verification
- Vercel finding (same article): 'Removing 80% of tools available to the model got measurably better results' — supporting evidence that less context + tighter verification beats more options + silent retries
- Anthropic effective-harnesses article: 'self-verify all features. Only mark features as passing after careful testing'
- Our /goal experiments 4-5 (memory anchors via task_log queries on those dates) — empirical confirmation

## Notes

Filed 2026-05-14 after harness-audit pass through OpenAI / Anthropic / LangChain / harness-engineering.ai articles. Operator antagonistic check: this is the ONE pattern out of 7 candidate gaps where the argument is directly backed by our own experiment data — the others were articulate-but-not-actionable-yet.
close_reason: Landed 2026-05-15: per-step verification discipline added to skills/goal-protocol/SKILL.md rule #10 and tool-conventions.md (new section with the four bullets + compounding-failure math + experiment 4/5 provenance). Worker jj rev vqzpqysk in dot-claude-personal repo (pushed to git@gitsah:dot-claude-personal). The rule was immediately applied by subsequent workers (mu-vha, mu-gih Stage 3) — both cited it in their envelopes.

title: mu-coding: session.delete_persistent — unlink session JSONL from disk on close (mu-u1ld follow-up)
description:
## Problem

`session.close` (or `close_session`) today evicts a session from the in-memory registry but does **not** delete its JSONL from `~/.local/share/mu/events/<daemon_id>/<session_id>.jsonl`. After mu-u1ld phase A landed, this means: a closed session reappears as a ghost on the next `mu serve` restart because rehydration finds its JSONL on disk.

For sessions the operator actually wants gone — short experiments, accidental sessions, sensitive content — there's no way to remove them short of `rm` against the live daemon directory.

## Design choice

Two shapes worth considering:

**Option A: extend close_session**

Add an optional `delete_persistent: bool` (or `delete_from_disk: bool`) parameter to the existing close RPC. Default false (preserve current behavior). When true: evict from registry AND unlink JSONL.

**Option B: new RPC**

`session.delete_persistent { session_id }` as a distinct RPC. Idempotent. Works on a closed session OR a still-active one (in which case it auto-closes first).

Option A is smaller; Option B is more orthogonal and composes with mu-770 (session.archive / session.restore / session.list_archived — already filed).

## Acceptance

- Closed sessions can be deleted from disk by the operator
- Idempotent (deleting an already-deleted session is OK)
- Capability-gated (likely `SessionAdmin` or extended close-session permission)
- Audit-log entry per deletion
- After deletion + daemon restart, the session no longer appears in `session.list`
- TUI affordance can wait (one operator surface at a time)

## Cross-refs

- Composes with mu-u1ld phase A rehydration scan (what this RPC is fighting against)
- Composes with mu-770 (session lifecycle epic — archive/restore/remove RPCs + TUI)

## Provenance

Filed 2026-05-18 from mu-u1ld close note (deferred follow-up; operator request).

title: provider-aware prompt budget: pre-validate prompt vs model context length (no silent backend truncation)
description:
Follow-up from mu-1mvq root cause (ollama num_ctx=32768 silently truncating ~50k-token review prompts to 32767, leaving 1 generation token, finish_reason=length, no error on any layer).

WORK: mu should know each model's context window and refuse/warn BEFORE dispatch when the prompt estimate exceeds it. The route catalog already probes ollama /api/tags at daemon start; /api/show exposes model context length (model_info context_length / num_ctx). Carry a window size per route-catalog entry; at ask/session dispatch, compare renderer.estimate_tokens against the window (with safety margin for chars/4 underestimate — see mu-wsgx ~15% low) and (a) warn loudly + (b) optionally refuse with a clear error instead of letting the backend truncate silently.

Scope: ollama first (the only backend that silently truncates); anthropic/openrouter return proper errors. ACCEPTANCE: a prompt known to exceed the model window produces a legible pre-dispatch warning/error naming prompt estimate vs window, never a silent 1-token reply.

title: specs/architecture/auditor-roles.md — narrow-checklist auditor dispatch for parallel code review
description:
Enable the operator's stated goal of "spread code review across multiple agents by feature/invariant/architecture element" by defining specialized auditor roles, each with a narrow checklist, so a PR or bead can be reviewed by 4-6 auditors in parallel instead of one agent doing full PR review.

Surfaced by gpt-5.5 during daemon ebccc9 session-2 (2026-05-20).

## Auditor roles to define

Each role gets a focused 10-15 item checklist, NOT a full style guide. Bead is to draft the spec + checklists.

1. **Rope/context auditor** — verifies provider-request context dataflow uses rope projections, not ad-hoc `Vec<AgentMessage>` or appended strings. Owns seam #1.
2. **Capability/security auditor** — checks `Capability`/Biscuit single-source, delegation attenuation, tool authority narrowing, fallback allow flags. Owns seam #4.
3. **Event-log/rehydration auditor** — verifies all state transitions are events, rehydration is correct, no missed events, archived/hidden sessions filter properly. Owns seams #2, #5.
4. **TUI state auditor** — checks TUI is projection of event log, not mutable buffer; session switches don't corrupt transcript. Owns seam #2 from the UI side.
5. **Immutability/Arc auditor** — validates Arc<str> usage, no unnecessary clones; all immutable-at-creation content uses `Arc<str>` per mu-yqeq.2. Owns seam #6.
6. **Shell/orchestration auditor** — reviews quoting, secrets handling, determinism in `~/.local/bin/agent-spawn*`, `agent-slot`, `claude-proxy`. Owns the wrappers/scripts surface (no specific seam in mu-core).

## Deliverables

- `specs/architecture/auditor-roles.md` (NEW): role definitions, scope per role, focused checklists, example findings, minimal patch templates.
- `templates/auditor-result.md` (NEW): standard output shape — role name, findings (file:line + invariant violated), pass/fail/iterate verdict.
- Dispatch mechanism: a workflow doc explaining how auditors are spawned (br bead per role per PR, or a single bead with role-tagged comments, or `/review-<role>` PR commands — designer's call during implementation).

## Why this isn't mu-7lwn

mu-7lwn is the Capability::auditor() runtime feature — the in-process Capability construction for read-only audit sessions. This bead is about the **code review process discipline** — how human/agent reviewers are organized around the seams. Different layers; complementary.

## Acceptance

- Spec exists with all 6 roles documented.
- Each role's checklist is ≤15 items, written in directly-testable language (cite file:line patterns to look for; cite mu-specific anti-patterns).
- Dispatch workflow is documented end-to-end: how a reviewer knows which role to play, where findings go, how iteration closes.

## References

- Blocks: requires seams.md (this bead's seam ownership refers to that spec)
- Related: mu-6ohp / mu-kz79 (current single-reviewer cycle — this generalizes that pattern)
- Memory: `delegation_protocol_6_iter_cycle_2026_05_19` (the worker → reviewer → postmortem cycle — auditor roles are how the reviewer step parallelizes)

title: daemon: watch tool — spawn a command, wake the session with its result on exit (kqueue via tokio)
description: Operator design question 2026-06-05 ('gpt needed a prompt to notice CI went green — popen + kqueue?'). A turn-based model is inert between turns; only the daemon can wake it. mu has all the pieces: (1) the agent loop already wakes on AgentInput while idle (input_rx.recv); (2) AgentInput::MailboxMessage is the existing external-attention injection path; (3) tokio::process::Child::wait() on FreeBSD is kqueue EVFILT_PROC NOTE_EXIT under the hood — no manual kevent needed. FEATURE: a 'watch' tool on the daemon tool surface: model calls watch(command, note) -> tool returns immediately ('watch registered'); daemon spawns the command (tokio::process), a task awaits exit and injects MailboxMessage{from: watcher, subject: note, body: exit status + output tail (tier-1 filtered per mu-2e0h)} into the session -> loop wakes -> model gets the result as a turn. Canonical use: watch('gh pr checks N --watch', 'CI for PR N') then end turn. LIFECYCLE: watches are session-scoped — killed on session end/cancel (mu-xac orphan-popen hang is the cautionary bead); cap concurrent watches per session; timeout with a killed-status wakeup so silence is impossible (a watch that dies must still wake the model — silence is indistinguishable from still-running). RELATED: mu-28u / spec mu-036 schedule_wakeup is the TIMER sibling (this is the event sibling — consider sharing the injection machinery); mu-solo-osc-notify-mbmn composes: woken turn completes while unfocused -> operator gets the desktop popup, closing the loop end-to-end (CI green -> model wakes -> does the merge -> popup tells the human).
close_reason: PR #211 merged: watch daemon tool — event sibling of schedule_wakeup on the same wakeup channel; kill_on_drop + Drop-abort registry (no orphans, mu-xac), cap 8, timeout kills-and-still-wakes. Follow-ups: mu-bjbl (tier-1 output filtering), mu-dvmu (durable watch lifecycle event). Clean worker run end-to-end.

title: TUI: arrow keys + Home/End/Backspace-word in prompt input mode
description:
## Why

In input mode (the prompt-typing affordance), only Backspace navigates — typed characters append, Backspace deletes the last char. There's no cursor movement: you can't arrow-left to fix a typo mid-prompt; the only path is to delete-back-to-the-typo. This is functional but feels broken because users have decades of muscle memory for readline-style editing.

Reported by user, 2026-05-11: "when trying to go back to change a typo, arrow keys aren't usable, so have to delete back to typo."

## What

Wire standard readline-ish keys into the input-mode handler in crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs:

| Key | Action |
|---|---|
| Left / Right | Move cursor one char |
| Ctrl-Left / Ctrl-Right (or Alt-B / Alt-F) | Move cursor one word |
| Home / Ctrl-A | Move to start |
| End / Ctrl-E | Move to end |
| Ctrl-W | Delete previous word |
| Ctrl-U | Delete to start of line |
| Ctrl-K | Delete to end of line |
| Backspace | (already works) Delete previous char |
| Delete | Delete next char |

State change: replace `prompt_buffer: String` with a (String, cursor_position: usize) pair — or use a small struct. Insert / delete operations now respect the cursor position.

Rendering: show a cursor caret at the current position in the input field (ratatui's `Span::styled(" ", inverted)` for a block cursor, or use the terminal's native cursor positioning).

## Scope

NOT in scope here (separate bead): full hx integration via Ctrl-X Ctrl-E. That's its own bead (TUI: $EDITOR integration). This bead is the basic-readline-in-place fix.

## Acceptance

- Typing 'helo' then Left, Left, Left, 'l' yields 'hello'.
- Ctrl-A jumps to start of buffer; End jumps to end.
- Ctrl-W deletes the last whitespace-delimited word.
- Cursor visibly tracks position (block cursor, blinking or static).

## Estimate

~50-80 LOC in the input-mode handler + a small struct for cursor state, plus rendering tweaks. Well-trodden territory; `tui-input` and `tui-prompts` crates exist if 'wire it ourselves' feels redundant.
close_reason: done

title: mu-companion: secondary in-session model for mid-turn user interaction (session-as-coroutine)
description:
Exploration bead. When the user types a follow-up while the primary (big) model is mid-turn, today the follow-up sits queued — the primary's response lands first, ignorant of the new input, and the user pays a round-trip to reconcile. This bead proposes a session-internal companion: a secondary (small/fast) model that engages the user in real time during the primary's processing, with both models sharing the same session memory.

## Framing

Not "two parallel models" — **one session with a coroutine pattern.** The session awaits the primary's response (slow); while waiting, the session stays engaged via the secondary (fast). When the primary's response lands, it integrates into the same event log. The session is the unifying frame; workers come and go.

```
session.handle(prompt):
  primary_future = async primary_model.respond(prompt)
  while not primary_future.done:
    if user_input arrives mid-turn:
      response = secondary_model.respond(latest_event_log)
      emit response  // actor: secondary
  emit primary_future.result  // actor: primary
```

This maps onto mu's existing agent-loop semantics: the primary's invocation is structurally identical to a long-running tool call. The secondary is what runs in the loop body during the await. No new conceptual primitive — different worker decomposition.

## Decisions (operator, 2026-05-16)

Open questions from earlier discussion are now decided:

1. **Scope**: input-only (initial). Secondary sees the event log including user follow-ups; does NOT see the primary's mid-stream output. Simpler. Solves "most all" of the failure modes worth solving today. Streaming-correlation version can come later when the rope/compaction substrate matures.

2. **UI affordance**: low-key. Subtle prompt-color change OR small-font model banner showing both with an arrow indicating which is active (e.g., "primary: opus > secondary: haiku"). Goal is to be **noticeable on inspection, invisible during flow**. The user shouldn't be surprised but shouldn't be interrupted.

3. **Event-log shape**: secondary's responses go inline into the same event log, with `actor: secondary_assistant` annotation. Same session memory, same conversation. No separate UI track. Distinguishing event variant means the primary model can correctly treat secondary responses as informational context, not as input requiring its own response.

4. **Opt-in**: per-session, off by default. Operator/agent opts in when starting a session, possibly via capability grant (composes with mu-fvy0 capability axis).

5. **Multiple queued follow-ups**: secondary handles all of them if it can within the primary's turn time. If secondary is itself slow on a particular follow-up, later messages may queue behind it — acceptable; this is a graceful-degradation property, not a correctness issue.

6. **Streaming intersection**: frame-per-response preserves ordering by START time. Late arrivals grow their frame in place; users see the conversation as chronologically consistent. If the visual feels wrong, users disable the feature.

7. **Self-loop prevention**: secondary's responses are events with `actor: secondary_assistant` (distinct from `actor: user` and `actor: primary_assistant`). The primary's prompt-construction logic treats `secondary_assistant` events as informational context, not as input. Without this distinction, the primary could mistakenly respond to its own session's companion, cascading. With it, the primary sees a richer conversation but acts only on user input.

## Composition with existing substrates

- **mu-fvy0 actor model**: secondary is a session-internal actor with restricted role. Falls out of the actor framing.
- **mu-romg judge plumbing**: secondary's "is this follow-up a refinement or contradiction?" call is structurally a judge call. Could share the JudgeAdapter trait. Optional Phase-2 hook for cancel-and-restart on contradiction.
- **mu-035 cancel_outstanding**: already exists. If secondary classifies a follow-up as contradiction (Phase 2), session can invoke cancel_outstanding to abort the primary, then restart with combined input.
- **mu-sfmd predictor**: closely related — both are secondary-actor patterns intervening in primary-model flow. Predictor acts on telemetry; companion acts on user input. They could share infrastructure (same secondary-actor session shape, different triggers and prompts).
- **Event log**: user input timestamped at type-time, not delivery-time. Mu already has this property. Turn N+1's primary prompt naturally includes turn N's input + any mid-turn user messages + secondary's responses, chronologically ordered.

## Phasing

**Phase 1 — input-only, ack-style**
- Secondary model invoked when a `Message::User` event arrives mid-primary-turn
- Secondary's prompt: full event log + system instruction "the primary model is currently processing — respond conversationally to the user's follow-up, acknowledge the in-flight work, do not duplicate effort"
- Secondary response emitted as `actor: secondary_assistant` event
- Primary's response, when it lands, is unaware of the secondary's reply (in this phase). Primary's next turn (N+1) will see both.

**Phase 2 — refinement/contradiction routing**
- Add a quick classifier (could be the secondary itself): is this follow-up a refinement (compatible with the primary's current work) or a contradiction (the primary should be cancelled)?
- On contradiction: secondary emits an explicit "this conflicts with the in-flight work; cancelling the primary and restarting with the combined input" message + invokes `session.cancel_outstanding` + restarts primary with the new combined prompt.

**Phase 3 — streaming intersection (deferred)**
- Secondary sees the primary's in-progress stream, can comment more substantively (e.g., "the primary is currently saying X about your original ask; your follow-up about Y will be handled after").
- Requires shared streaming substrate; defer until the rope/compaction work has fully landed.

## Acceptance (Phase 1 only)

- Per-session config: `companion: Option<{ provider, model }>` defaults to None
- New event variant `AgentEvent::SecondaryAssistantMessage { ... }` with serde-tagged actor
- Secondary worker invoked when user input arrives AND primary is in-flight AND companion is configured
- Primary's prompt construction filters `SecondaryAssistantMessage` events to be informational, not directive
- UI displays secondary responses inline with the agreed annotation pattern (model banner + arrow)
- Integration test: user sends prompt → primary starts → user sends follow-up → secondary responds → primary completes → both responses present in event log in chronological order
- Spec'd in `specs/mu-NNN-companion-session.md`

## Open questions remaining (smaller than the initial set)

- **Cost telemetry**: each companion turn adds tokens. Should the session's budget cap apply to the combined primary+companion spend, or have separate caps? mu-fvy0-telemetry should record provenance so post-hoc cost analysis is possible.
- **Default companion model**: Haiku 4.5 is the obvious starting choice (cheap, fast, capable enough for conversational acks). Should this be a config default with operator override, or always per-session-explicit?
- **What if the secondary's response is wrong?**: secondary acks "yes, I'll include that" but primary's response doesn't actually include the refinement (primary was already past that point in its reasoning). User sees an apparent promise the system didn't keep. Mitigation: secondary's prompt should be careful to NOT make commitments about the primary's output content; just acknowledge that the input was received and will be considered.

## Provenance

Operator observation 2026-05-16, in the postmortem-followup design thread. Specific framing of "the session sends off an async for information, and then hangs out with the operator while they both wait for the information to come back" is the unifying insight that maps this onto existing agent-loop coroutine semantics.

## Blocked by

None strictly. Composes well with mu-fvy0-telemetry (mu-5g7i) for cost accounting and mu-romg (judge plumbing) for the Phase-2 classifier, but neither blocks Phase 1.

## Companions to

- mu-romg (context ulimit + judge) — same JudgeAdapter substrate
- mu-sfmd (predictor) — both are secondary-actor patterns; share infrastructure

title: mu-detached-mode: mu serve as long-lived daemon on unix socket, lazy-start, idle-timeout shutdown
description: Run mu serve as a long-lived daemon listening on a unix socket. Lazy-started by the first client that needs it (mu mcp, mu-tui, etc.); idle-timeout shutdown (default 30 min, configurable). The substrate for pattern B' (Claude Code spawns mu, mu daemonizes). Default socket path `<state_dir>/mu.sock` (mu-l1z config). --daemonize flag performs standard Unix double-fork; pidfile + flock prevent dual-bind. Stale-socket/pidfile recovery at startup. Wire protocol unchanged — same JSON-RPC over a different transport. Co-required with mu-mh4 (without persistence, every restart loses workers; mu-mh4 effectively bumps to P2). Substrates mu-auth-handshake, mu-mcp-adapter.

title: mu-solo: turn reaching end_turn can emit no `done` terminus → live block never commits → whole turn lost from scrollback
description:
Observed in mu session 1a7812f064510d91:session-1 (verified 2026-05-30 by diffing the captured scrollback against the event log). ONE turn of 19 — the assistant response to user msg "ah. I did not even see the picker..." — is the only turn in the session with NO `done` event. Its final assistant_message_event reached stop=end_turn (loop finished) and the log has all 4 assistant_message_events + tool_call/result pairs, but no terminus/`done` was emitted.

mu-solo commits the live in-flight block to scrollback on finalize/session.done, so with no terminus the block never committed; the next turn redrew the live region and the ENTIRE turn text was lost from scrollback — both the pre-tool text ("Yeah — three real things..." 103c) and the final block ("Confirmed terrain. Three findings..." 3840c). Surrounding turns persisted fine (63/65 transcript segments present).

DISTINCT from the SU->CRLF scrollback-scroll fix (branch mu-solo-scrollback-fix @206f73c), which ensures *committed* content reaches native scrollback. THIS is a turn that never commits because its terminus is missing — commit-on-finalize path (app.rs handle_notification / the mu-d04a streaming state machine; mu-926g family).

Leading hypothesis (unconfirmed): a follow-up user message arriving as/just after the turn finalized raced the terminus/commit — fits this being the only such turn and occurring at ~180k/90% context pressure. Could be daemon-side (done not emitted for that turn) or mu-solo-side (pending_close not flushed before the new turn reset streaming state).

Next: trace the daemon turn-termination event-emission path (end_turn -> done); check mu-solo new-turn/cmd state reset vs pending_close flush ordering. Repro: submit a follow-up prompt immediately as a turn finalizes.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: EVIDENCE + ROOT CAUSE (2026-06-05). TWO lost-terminus species, sorted by event-log-decides: SPECIES A (this bead's original 2026-05-30 evidence, session 1a7812f064510d91): done absent from the DURABLE LOG -> daemon never emitted it. ROOT CAUSE FOUND: agent loop mod.rs — (1) plan_post_invoke_llm skipped MaybeFinish entirely when UserMessages were buffered during the final no-tool-call stream ('the loop continues naturally'); (2) MaybeFinish's early-continue on non-empty queue (racing UM drained from input_rx) skipped the Done emit. Regression test wf5w_buffered_um_does_not_suppress_done (gated MockProvider stream) — fails pre-fix with exactly the predicted signature: one Done, turn_count=2. SPECIES B (operator live repro 2026-06-05 ~14:04 EDT, daemon 4c2b267b33a613ad session-1, openai_codex/gpt-5.5, PRE-#186 client build): done id 334 IS in the log + task_telemetry id 335; client status line stayed frozen on 'thinking (0.7s)' ~6min until next prompt; later notifications processed fine. Daemon-side chain verified sound on current main (forward_events appends log + emits wire in same iteration; emit only fails on serde error; write_loop survived; client notif channel unbounded, select branch unguarded). Species B remains UNEXPLAINED on current code — needs the post-#186 renderer journal + a client-side notification journal to adjudicate wire-loss vs apply-failure on next occurrence. title: session.assistant_text_finalized notification — close TUI streaming-to-finalized swap gap
description:
There's a visible flicker between the yellow "streaming…" assistant block (built client-side from `session.text_delta` notifications) and the final white assistant block (rendered from the durable `AssistantMessageEvent` in the event log).

## Current behavior

On `session.done`, the TUI drops `streaming_text` and waits up to 500ms for `refresh_transcript_for_selection` to pick up the durable `AssistantMessageEvent`. During that gap, the transcript shows neither the streaming nor the finalized version. Visible flicker. May-14 Opus 4.7 review:

> The 500ms flicker between "yellow streaming…" and "white finalized" is the kind of visual jitter that makes a tool feel unfinished even when it's working.

## Why it's a protocol issue, not just a TUI bug

Every TUI consumer — and any future web dashboard, programmatic test harness, etc. — will reimplement the same client-side accumulator. The leaky abstraction is that `session.text_delta` isn't in the event log but `AssistantMessageEvent` is, and there's no atomic-swap signal between them.

## Proposed fix (per Opus review)

Add `session.assistant_text_finalized { session_id, model_call_id, text }` notification emitted IMMEDIATELY on the model's `Done` provider event, before `session.done`. The TUI swaps its accumulator atomically:

1. text_deltas accumulate as today
2. `assistant_text_finalized` lands with the same `text` that will appear in `AssistantMessageEvent` shortly
3. TUI replaces streaming_text with the finalized text in-place — no flicker
4. When the durable `AssistantMessageEvent` arrives later, the TUI confirms the text matches and clears its in-memory copy

## Scope

### In

- New notification `session.assistant_text_finalized` in `crates/mu-core/src/protocol.rs` and corresponding wire type.
- Emit it from the agent loop on the `ProviderEvent::Done` path, before `session.done`.
- Update mu-tui's `tick()` to consume it: replace `streaming_text[sid]` with the finalized text, drop on `AssistantMessageEvent` arrival as before.
- Test: drive an agent loop with a mock provider that streams text, finishes, confirm the new notification fires with the matching text.

### Out

- Alternative design (emit stream segments into the durable log post-hoc) — bigger spec change; defer until this is observed insufficient.

## Acceptance

- New notification spec'd + emitted + TUI consumes it.
- Visual test: long assistant response from a real provider shows smooth transition (no flicker).
- Existing tests pass + new wire-shape tests.
- cargo test --workspace green; clippy -D warnings; fmt --check.

## References

- Current accumulator code: `crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs` `tick()` — comment explicitly says "to render an in-flight assistant message in the transcript pane, we have to assemble it client-side from notifications"
- Provenance: Opus 4.7 review in mu daemon `fd76f5`, session-1, 2026-05-14.
close_reason: Merged in PR #51 (2537414) — session.assistant_text_finalized notification

title: glob tool schema foot-gun — pattern alone doesn't glob; requires glob:true
description:
The `glob` tool's schema treats `{pattern: \"*\"}` differently from `{glob: true, pattern: \"*\"}`. Models call it with just `pattern` (the obvious shape) and get unexpected behavior; they retry with `glob: true` and it works. Wasted turn(s) every time.

## Symptom

Opus 4.7 session 2026-05-14, event log `03b75bc4a9c48f82`, first two tool calls:

```text
1. glob {\"kind\":\"any\", \"pattern\":\"*\"}                    ← malformed
2. glob {\"glob\":true, \"kind\":\"any\", \"pattern\":\"*\"}     ← retry, works
```

Operator initially read this as \"tried grep first then switched to glob\" — the failure was visible enough in the TUI to look like a different tool. The model self-corrected, but the foot-gun was a wasted call.

This is a recurring failure mode — any model trained on \"glob = match files\" will assume `pattern: \"*\"` globs. The current schema breaks that assumption.

## Why this is a schema bug, not a model bug

A tool whose schema accepts `{kind, pattern}` and silently treats those as something-other-than-glob when `glob:true` is missing is a tool whose schema is misaligned with its name. The fix is to either:

### A. Invert the default (recommended)

`glob:true` becomes the default. Add a `literal: bool` (default false) for the rare case where the operator wants literal-string matching. Models calling `glob { pattern: \"*\" }` get the obvious behavior.

### B. Make `glob` a required field

Tool schema rejects the call without `glob` set. Model gets a clear error, retries once. Better than silent misbehavior but adds a forced-retry to every glob call until the model learns the new shape.

### C. Rename the tool

If the current behavior with `glob:false` is the more common case, rename the tool to `find` or `match` and let glob be a separate tool. Highest churn; probably not worth it.

## Recommended fix: A

Glob tools should glob. The `literal` mode is the niche case.

## Scope

IN:
- Update `glob` tool's schema in `crates/mu-coding/src/tools/`: default `glob: true`; add `literal: bool` (default false).
- Update tool documentation in the schema description to reflect new defaults.
- Tests: existing tests that explicitly passed `glob: true` continue to work; new test confirms `pattern: \"*\"` alone now globs.

OUT:
- Schema migration for cached model contexts mentioning the old shape (those are session-local).
- Renaming to `find` (option C).

## Acceptance

- `glob { pattern: \"*\" }` globs by default.
- `glob { pattern: \"foo*\", literal: true }` does literal matching.
- Existing tests pass with updated shape.
- New regression test for the default behavior.
- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings green
- One commit closes this bead

## References

- Symptom event log: `/home/tcovert/.local/share/mu/events/03b75bc4a9c48f82/session-1.jsonl`
- Tool location: `crates/mu-coding/src/tools/` (specific file TBD at impl time)
close_reason: Merged in PR #12 as e1ed424

title: Contract-test naming convention + tests/SEAM_COVERAGE.md mapping
description:
Formalize a test-naming convention so test names become living documentation of the architectural contracts. Audit existing tests to match the convention; surface missing seam tests in a `SEAM_COVERAGE.md` map.

Surfaced by gpt-5.5 during daemon ebccc9 session-2 (2026-05-20). The insight: test names like `b3_consecutive_tool_results_group_into_one_user_message()` already work as documentation — formalize the pattern across all crates so reviewers and search tools can locate the test for any seam by name.

## Naming convention

```rust
#[test]
fn {seam_name}_uses_{canonical_source}_not_{bypass_pattern}() {}

// Examples mapped to mu's seam inventory:
fn provider_request_uses_rope_projection_not_message_vec() {}
fn tui_transcript_is_event_projection_not_mutable_buffer() {}
fn memory_injection_uses_spans_with_provenance_not_opaque_strings() {}
fn delegation_cannot_widen_tools() {}
fn archived_session_hidden_after_restart_but_events_remain_queryable() {}
fn span_content_clones_share_underlying_bytes() {}
```

## Deliverables

1. **`tests/SEAM_COVERAGE.md`** (NEW) — table of (seam, contract test name, file:line, status: implemented | missing | partial). The map is the durable artifact; test names are searchable.

2. **Audit existing tests** in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/`, `crates/mu-core/src/context/`, `crates/mu-core/src/capability.rs`, `crates/mu-coding/src/session/`, `crates/mu-tui/src/`. For each test, decide: (a) keep as-is if name already documents an invariant; (b) rename to contract-style if it's testing a seam; (c) leave alone if it's a pure unit/regression test not about a seam.

3. **Add missing tests** for seams where no contract test exists today. Likely missing: TUI-event-projection invariant, memory-provenance invariant, the rope→provider seam (which mu-yqeq.D will land but needs a regression net before).

4. **Lint/CI check (optional)**: a script that scans `specs/architecture/seams.md` for declared seams and confirms each has a matching test name listed in `SEAM_COVERAGE.md`. Fail CI if a seam is added without a coverage entry.

## Acceptance

- `tests/SEAM_COVERAGE.md` exists, with all 6 initial seams from `specs/architecture/seams.md` mapped.
- ≥3 existing tests renamed to the contract convention (with bead-message rationale).
- ≥2 new contract tests added for previously-uncovered seams.

## References

- Blocks: requires seams.md
- Related: existing test files (`anthropic_tests.rs`, `openai_codex_tests.rs`, `openrouter_tests.rs`, `mu-tui` tests) — these become the regression net for the mu-yqeq cutover; their names should evolve toward this convention as they're touched
- Memory: `bead_pre_flight_scope_verification_2026_05_20` (test names as the documentation layer that survives bead-description rot)

title: Daemon-shutdown lifecycle regression test: mu serve must exit on stdin-EOF with a session holding spawn_worker
description: Regression guard for mu-qc08 (the SpawnWorkerTool strong-Sessions-clone shutdown deadlock, fixed in PR #150). Existing serve_smoke tests run with events_dir=None, so the per-session spawn_worker tool is never injected and the deadlock could not reproduce — the whole shutdown-lifecycle test class was absent (see notes/incident-2026-06-01-mu-qc08-worker-orchestration-deadlock.md, strategic action item). Add an integration test that runs serve_with_io_with_config with events_dir=Some(tempdir), creates a session (so its agent loop holds the injected SpawnWorkerTool), closes the client (stdin-EOF), and ASSERTS the server future returns within a timeout instead of hanging. Must fail on a strong clone and pass on the Weak fix.

title: Compaction trigger fires ~15% late on Anthropic: rope estimate excludes system prompt and chars/4 underestimates Anthropic tokenization (measured median 0.85, n=61)
description: Live session c76f6949f56efbe6 (2026-06-03, sonnet, first stamped+compacted session): per-call ContextAssembly.token_count_estimate / provider prompt-total ratio = 0.770-0.883, median 0.850 across 61 calls. Compaction fired at estimate 153,910 = ~176K actual provider tokens (88% of the 200K window) instead of the configured 150K. Two components: (1) STRUCTURAL — the rope never contains the system prompt (passed separately to handle_invoke_llm; call-1 breakdown has no 'system' key; gap ~4K on this session), so the estimate is blind to it; (2) TOKENIZER — renderer default chars/4 underestimates Anthropic BPE on code/markdown-heavy content, drifting worse as tool results accumulate (in-session agent measured the same: 0.845-0.883 clustering 0.857). REMEDIES (combinable): include system-prompt span in the estimate; per-renderer estimate_span_tokens calibration (Anthropic renderer override — could carry a measured ratio on ProviderCapabilities); or document that trigger_threshold_tokens is estimator-scale and default it lower (e.g. 130K). Note compaction_calibration.py (~/src/claude-personal/scripts) exists for measuring this.
close_reason: Merged PR #169 — predicted_prompt_total(): provider-actual anchor + estimate delta; fallback estimate+8K; storm guard on shrunk rope; anchor reset on provider switch

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: In-session agent's closing recommendation (c76f6949f56efbe6): the bias is consistent enough (0.845-0.883 across its 35-call measurement; our independent check: 0.770-0.883 median 0.850, n=61) to apply a calibration factor NOW — multiply estimate by ~1.167 (or divide threshold by 0.857) before the trigger compare. Its argument for firing earlier rather than just re-documenting: at 200K window, uncalibrated firing lands at ~88%% of window, and compaction has the cache-healing side effect, so early is cheap. Note if mu-tlri's fix lands (stop dropping the stable prefix) the cache-healing argument strengthens further. Where to put the factor: per-renderer estimate_span_tokens override (the mu-heqf hook exists for exactly this) keeps it per-provider rather than a global fudge.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Operator-directed design refinement (2026-06-03 evening): (1) FIX THE METRIC FIRST — split the gap with a linear fit over 67 calls: gap = 7,612 + 0.121×estimate. The constant ~7.6K is structurally uncounted content (system prompt passed outside the rope + request framing) — count it, don't calibrate it. The ~12.1%% multiplicative residual is true tokenizer bias. (2) THEN PREFER FEEDBACK OVER CALIBRATION: we receive exact usage after every call; predictor next_total = last_actual_prompt_total + (estimate_delta of new spans) measured on this session: median abs error 316 tokens, p90 1,957, worst 11,501 — vs raw estimate median error 20,982. 66x better, zero tokenizer dependency, self-calibrating across providers, and the UsageSemantics stamps (PR #164) give the exact last_actual per provider. (3) OPTIONAL BELT+SUSPENDERS: Anthropic exposes POST /v1/messages/count_tokens — exact, free, pre-send; call it only when the feedback predictor is within ~10%% of threshold. Renderer doc already anticipated this ('the Anthropic adapter can use the API's count_tokens endpoint'). Implementation seam: the trigger compare in loop_/mod.rs — replace raw estimate with predictor; first call of a session has no feedback yet → estimate + constant correction.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Tokenizer piece (operator direction, 2026-06-03 late): wire the EXISTING tiktoken-rs cl100k counter (compaction.rs::estimate_tokens, bench-only since mu-kgu.10) into the live path via the mu-heqf per-span hook — Anthropic renderer overrides estimate_span_tokens to BPE-count instead of chars/4. Key enabler: spans are immutable (Arc<str>), so memoize the count per span id — full-rope estimate becomes a sum of cached per-span counts, incremental cost only for spans new since last call (tiktoken on just the latest tool results, microseconds). Expected: ~12%% multiplicative bias → ~5%% (README's claimed cl100k-vs-Anthropic gap). Layering: memoized-BPE estimate (pre-call, call 1) → feedback predictor (calls 2+, median 316 tok error) → optional count_tokens confirm near threshold.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: OPERATOR DECISION (2026-06-03): skip the tokenizer wiring — 'no tokenizer dependency... better is the enemy of good.' The fix slice is: (1) include the system prompt in the estimate (kills the 7.6K constant); (2) feedback predictor as the trigger measure — next_total = last_actual_prompt_total (via stamped UsageSemantics) + chars/4 delta of new spans; call 1 falls back to raw estimate + constant. Measured median error 316 tokens. chars/4 stays for deltas (its 12%% bias on a 2-5K delta is ~300 tokens — noise). cl100k memoization idea stays on record above as considered-and-declined; count_tokens endpoint optional future belt-and-suspenders. This slice has no new dependencies and touches only the trigger compare in loop_/mod.rs + threading last_actual into the loop.

title: approval UI should show authority/provenance chain for privileged tool requests
description:
## Problem

Permission prompts that only say "allow sudo?" or show a raw tool name + args collapse very different risk profiles into the same UI.

Examples from 2026-06-03 discussion:

- "the model you have been working with all day to do systems-level work just asked for sudo again"
- "the Chinese/local/sandboxed model you spawned as a constrained worker wants sudo"
- "an MCP tool called by the main session delegated to another agent, and that nested actor now wants host authority"

Those should not render as equivalent prompts. The operator needs provenance and containment context to decide safely.

## Proposal

Add an approval-prompt model and TUI/mu-solo rendering that carries an authority/provenance chain, not just tool_name + args.

Prompt should surface at least:

- requesting session id / label
- model/provider identity
- parent/delegation chain, if any
- direct tool vs MCP server/tool vs nested delegated worker
- sandbox/containment scope: host, jail/pot, cwd/repo, network policy, readonly/readwrite
- requested capability/action: command/file/network target/sudo/etc.
- whether the request widens beyond the actor's declared sandbox/capability profile
- task/goal context if available

UI should visually distinguish common cases:

- main trusted session direct bash/sudo request
- attenuated worker request inside its sandbox
- worker attempting to escape/request host authority
- MCP/delegated nested request with opaque outer tool result

## Acceptance

- Approval request data structure includes provenance fields sufficient for the above.
- TUI/mu-solo approval dialog renders a concise human-readable authority chain, not raw JSON only.
- High-risk provenance changes (nested delegate, different provider/model, sandbox escape, host sudo) are visually emphasized.
- Event log records the same provenance chain for audit.
- Regression test constructs at least two prompts with the same requested command but different provenance and asserts they render differently.

## Related existing beads

- mu-bs8 per-session permissions UI / RAII attenuation
- mu-x4o security profiles with colored/labeled sessions
- mu-ikz authority-coloring
- mu-j7r approval lifecycle events
- mu-vkx surface per-tool approval policy at session start

## Provenance

Captured from 2026-06-03 mu-solo UX discussion after reviewing Claude Code hook/MCP permission shape: PreToolUse -> tool/MCP permission path -> PostToolUse. Key insight: authorization UI needs to answer "who is asking through what authority path?" before asking the operator to approve privileged work.

title: mu-coding: fix fmt_allowlist Box::leak — use HashSet<String> instead of leaked &str
description:
## Problem

`fmt_allowlist` (in mu-coding's bash tool helpers) constructs a
`HashSet<&str>` for deduplication and uses
`Box::leak(joined.clone().into_boxed_str())` to upcast each owned
String into the required `&'static str` lifetime. This intentionally
(or accidentally) leaks memory for the lifetime of the process.

Tiny — a few bytes per unique allowlist entry, used only at format
time — but it's an unnecessary leak that future readers will trip
over, and it's the kind of "why is this using Box::leak?" question
that distracts from real code review.

## Proposal

Replace `HashSet<&str>` + leaked-string upcast with `HashSet<String>`:

```rust
let mut seen: HashSet<String> = HashSet::new();
// ...
if seen.insert(joined.clone()) {
    entries.push(joined);
}
```

The leak's apparent "no clone" win wasn't actually a win since
`joined.clone()` was being called anyway to feed Box::leak.

## Acceptance

- `fmt_allowlist` uses owned String storage; no Box::leak in the path
- All existing tests pass
- `cargo clippy -D warnings` clean

## Cross-refs

- Provenance: gpt-5.5 code review of mu, daemon f3845422 session-4
  (2026-05-15), user-prompted "review the mu workspace, opine, give
  feedback on code quality"
- Bash tool policy companion work: mu-6cf, mu-6hl

title: soft/hard context window limits: compaction target vs provider maximum
description:
context_window_for() currently returns a single number (e.g. 200k for anthropic_api). This conflates two distinct concepts:

1. Soft limit (compaction target) — where we WANT to keep context. Triggers compaction when exceeded. Currently 200k for Anthropic.
2. Hard limit (provider maximum) — where calls fail. 1M for Opus 4.7, 200k for older Claude models, 1M for gpt-5.5, etc.

Should return a struct:
  ContextLimits { compaction_target: u64, provider_max: u64 }

Effects:
- Compaction fires at soft limit (unchanged behavior)
- Status line shows pressure against soft limit (tokens used / compaction target)
- Warning/error when approaching hard limit (provider will reject)
- Allows operator to configure compaction target independently of provider max (e.g. 'compact at 150k even though the model takes 1M' for cost control, or 'let it run to 500k' for deep sessions)

Current state: Opus 4.7 hardcoded to 200k when it actually supports 1M. Status line says 29% when true provider utilization is ~6%.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: 2026-05-27: RouteCatalog (mu-k56u, commit c840a0e8) now carries context_soft_limit and context_hard_limit per route entry. The data is there but not yet consumed by the compaction layer or the forwarder's context_window_for() — those still use the hardcoded function. Next step: replace context_window_for() with a catalog lookup, and wire compaction threshold from the soft limit.

title: Future: security profiles with colored/labeled sessions
description:
After Biscuit/capability integration, add configurable security profiles for spawning sessions/agents. Profiles are selected at spawn time from a popup/menu and define the default non-attenuated authority for the new session: readonly, read/write, limited commands, yolo, production-yolo, etc.

UI/UX:
- Each profile has a color/border/badge.
- High-risk profiles such as yolo-in-production render red/red-bordered.
- Readonly uses a calm color.
- Mixed/limited-write uses a medium-warning color.
- Sessions are labelable so operators can distinguish role/authority contexts, similar to tmux panes named machine/root/functional-account.

Security semantics:
- Profile selection mints/attaches the session Biscuit/capability set and tool-policy defaults.
- Child/delegate sessions may attenuate from parent but not widen beyond parent.
- Dangerous profiles should require stronger confirmation.

Surface profile+label in session list/tree, transcript header, approval prompts, and emergency controls.

Related memory: mu_security_profiles_colored_labeled_sessions.

title: mu ask --append-system-prompt CLI flag
description:
Wire `--append-system-prompt <FILE>` through `mu ask`. Read the file's content and populate `CreateSessionRequest.system_prompt`.

## Why

agent-spawn-v2 (the pot-based ephemeral agent spawner under ~/.local/bin) currently invokes `pi -p` and uses pi's `--append-system-prompt` to inject a context manifest from c137-memory-worker. Migrating from pi to mu (per 2026-05-16 operator directive: 'mu is our own construction; when it doesn't do something we need, we can change it') is blocked on this one flag.

## Why this is small

The protocol field already exists (`CreateSessionRequest.system_prompt: Option<String>` at protocol.rs:66, comment 'Optional system prompt override. None → daemon default'). The serve handler already honors it (session.rs:59, threaded through mu-n48). The TUI palette (mu-62s) already drives it. **The only missing piece is the CLI flag in `mu ask`** — pure additive plumbing.

## Acceptance

- `mu ask --append-system-prompt <FILE> --provider faux <prompt>` reads FILE, sends its content as `CreateSessionRequest.system_prompt`
- Unit test (faux provider) verifies that the system_prompt actually reaches the session config
- `--help` documents the flag
- Errors cleanly when FILE is missing/unreadable
- No behavior change when flag is absent (default = None)

## Out of scope

- TUI surface (already exists via mu-62s)
- Multiple --append flags / concatenation semantics — single-file v1 only
- agent-spawn changes (separate task: tier-4 migration)

## Related

- mu-6dyg (manifest-audit gap) — same domain
- mu-fvy0 cluster (telemetry, classification) — downstream beneficiary

title: Subagent context handoff via shared read-only events + initial pointer-set
description:
Define how a parent session shares context with a spawned subagent without giving the subagent a mutable view of parent state.

## Mechanism

ChildSession { parent_session_id, parent_events_view: ReadOnlyEventLogHandle, initial_pointer_set: Vec<PointerToParentSpan>, own_event_log, own_retained_set (points into BOTH parent and own) }

Subagent receives at spawn: read-only access to parent event log; initial pointer-set; optional synthetic spans summarizing parent context. Writes only to its own log.

## Eviction semantics

Parent compaction drops a pointer to span S: parent's retained set no longer points at S; parent's event log STILL HAS S (append-only); if subagent retained its own pointer to S, the subagent's rope is unaffected. 'Eviction from parent = forgetting that pointer.' Parent compaction never strands subagent.

## Scope

In: ChildSession shape; spawn API; span ID namespace ((SessionId, EventId, SpanWithinEvent) triples); capability attenuation at spawn (subset of parent's allowed predicates); tests; doc.

Out: subagent dispatch UI; cross-subagent communication (mu-008/d22f391a — separate concern); multi-level subagent trees (start depth-1).

## Why

Today's options: copy parent context (expensive, stale) or give nothing (rebuild from brief — wasteful). Shared-read-only-events + initial-pointers: subagent has cheap parent access (read-only handle, not copy); independent retained set; provenance through both event logs.

## Acceptance

Child spawned with initial pointer-set has those pointers in retained rope; child's rendered AgentView includes parent-span content; parent compaction dropping span S does NOT affect child's retained pointer to S; child's event log distinct; doc covers shape + eviction + capability attenuation; workspace tests green.

## References

Full draft: ~/.claude-personal/drafts/mu-bead-5-subagent-context-handoff.md
Blocked-by: mu-qk8, mu-nat
Memories: d22f391a, 5b69bcdf (cooperating-sessions mailbox — related, distinct)
Existing: event-sourced-context.md Session tree section

title: BUG: orphan mu serve / silent Popen hang under background-bash spawning
description:
Memory a1dd04fa has the full diagnostic pattern.

Symptom: Python orchestrator using MuClient hangs forever on fresh ask. No mu serve child appears in process tree; python sits in I (idle) syscall-state with ~0 CPU. No stderr. No exception.

Reproduces under Bash run_in_background=true especially with stale mu serve processes around. Foreground works.

Suspected mechanism: subprocess.Popen fork window stretching indefinitely under background-bash supervision. Possibly compounded by file-lock contention on ~/.config/mu/auth/openai-codex.json across daemons.

Workaround SHIPPED: mu_client.py defensive layer (jj qmlywmnm 186d5970, claude-personal scripts). read_timeout + on_progress + stderr drainer + orphan warning. Failures now surface as MuClientTimeout instead of silent stalls.

Real fix unclear without more diagnosis. Lower priority because workaround makes the failure visible rather than mysterious — but worth chasing when bandwidth allows.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: CORRECTION (2026-06-01): the suspected file-lock contention on ~/.config/mu/auth/openai-codex.json across daemons DOES NOT EXIST. Verified against our own code: FileSystemTokenStore (crates/mu-ai/src/auth/mod.rs:83-113) does plain fs::read on load and OpenOptions truncate+write_all on save — ZERO locking (no flock, no advisory locks). Lock contention cannot be a contributing mechanism here. The real latent hazard is the OPPOSITE — non-atomic save (truncate-in-place, no tmp+rename) can tear the file under concurrent refresh — filed separately (mu-6w96). Strike the file-lock line from this bead so it stops misdirecting. (Operator flagged it: we own the code, there is no reason for lock contention on reads — correct, there is none.)

title: TUI: implement F5 usage / cache dashboard
description:
Per mockup, F5 shows cumulative usage by provider/model, cost estimate, cache hit %, and a cache ledger panel.

Cumulative usage already exposed via daemon.stats (total_input_tokens, total_output_tokens). Per-provider breakdown needs a session.list grouped/aggregated client-side OR a new daemon.usage_by_provider RPC. Start with client-side aggregation; promote to RPC if needed.

Cost estimation needs a per-provider price table — track as a separate issue.
