title: README rewrite: 'Why this architecture pays off' lead with compaction benchmark (claim → mechanism → measurement)
description:
## Goal

Land a README section that converts the architectural thesis into a concrete cash-out a hostile reader can verify. Right now the README front-loads the substrate (most-demanding read for least-prepared reader); reorder so the proof shows up first.

## Proposed structure (per opus review)

1. One-paragraph what-and-why
2. **NEW: one concrete differentiator with a number** — the compaction story (124k tokens → ~6k in 17ms vs Anthropic's ~38s, ~2200x faster, calibrated against tiktoken cl100k_base; methodology in mu-kgu.9)
3. Quick start
4. What works today (rewrite — brag harder; TUI specifically — 'standalone mu-tui binary with F1-F9 dashboards, transcript, firehose, modal approvals, $EDITOR handoff, ~4500 LOC of polished Ratatui')
5. Architectural bet (current 'Why mu exists' + 'Design principles', moved down)
6. Deep-dive links

The compaction section format = claim → mechanism (where it lives in the code) → measurement (the numbers + how to reproduce).

## Why now

Per opus review event 194 and the followup 221: 'A reader who absorbs the compaction section has absorbed the *shape* of the architecture even though they only read about one feature.' First architectural payoff mature enough to measure — sets the template for auditor / capability / orchestration sections later.

## Acceptance

- README leads with compaction claim + measurement in the first 200 words after the what-and-why
- TUI status line accurately describes mu-tui (drop 'aspirational' framing)
- Architectural thesis moved below the cash-out
- Links out to specs/measurements/ (see follow-up bead)

## Source

opus-4-7 review session 3262c036eaca7daa events 194 + 221.
close_reason: Shipped this session: commit wwtymurs 267c4b33 — 'docs(readme): lead with compaction win; reframe TUI as working'. README now opens with compaction benchmark + claim/mechanism/measurement structure.

title: Prototype findings: claude-code wrap-and-compact mechanism
description:
Captures the prototype-run findings from 2026-05-23 discussion. Closed-on-create since the trivial prototypes were answered via docs.

## P1 — /clear context-reset behavior
**Status:** Confirmed via docs. SessionStart fires with `source="clear"`. Memory drops to zero, system + CLAUDE.md + skills + auto-memory re-load as on startup.
**Source:** code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide — SessionStart source values listed as {startup, resume, clear, compact}.

## P2 — Hooks behavior on /clear
**Status:** Confirmed by same source. SessionStart re-fires with `source="clear"` on every /clear.
**Implication:** mu's wrapper can rely on hook re-fire to know when claude-code has finished its post-clear ingestion and is ready for the next message.

## P3 — Paste size limit
**Status:** No documented limit. Engineering reasoning: stdin is normal text, model accepts up to 200K-token context window, a compacted context of 5-20K is trivial.
**Action:** Pressure-test at integration time. No design constraint emerges.

## Bonus finding — two compaction strategies available

SessionStart also fires with `source="compact"`. So mu's wrapper has TWO viable approaches:

- **Strategy A**: drive /compact (native claude-code summarization), inject mu-augmentations via SessionStart hook on source=compact. Cheaper, reuses claude-code's compaction infrastructure.
- **Strategy B**: drive /clear + paste mu's own compacted context. Full mu control over compaction policy and compactor model. Maps to user's original design.

Both viable. Bead foreign-runtime-wrapper (to be filed) should keep both strategies as options.

title: console cc scanner: filter isSidechain (subagent) turns from session rollups
description: Follow-up to mu-cc-sessions-console-lqqt.1 (PR #203). The cc scanner (console/cc_data.rs) counts every assistant/user envelope in a transcript, including subagent sidechain entries (cc marks these with isSidechain:true and a parentUuid/sidechain chain). These inflate assistant_count, tool_call_count, and summed usage for the parent session. Decide policy: exclude isSidechain turns from the parent rollup, or attribute them separately. Also consider: a session that switches models mid-run only surfaces the LAST assistant model in the index. Low priority — counts are approximate projections, not billing.
close_reason: PR #210 merged: sidechain turns excluded from rollups in both projections (defensive — current cc format keeps them in subagents/ subdirs), models_seen surfaces mid-run switches. Worker timed out in fg4j gate loop; director salvaged + adjudicated 4th fabricated compile-error REJECT.

title: mu-mcp: MCP (Model Context Protocol) plugin/tool-server support
description:
Filed 2026-05-13 after surveying Crush (Charmbracelet's coding agent), which lists MCP support as a first-class feature: 'Extensible: add capabilities via MCPs (http, stdio, and sse)'.

## What MCP is

Model Context Protocol (Anthropic-published spec). A wire protocol for agents to discover and call external tool servers. Three transport flavors: http (network), stdio (subprocess), sse (server-sent events). Decouples tool surface from agent binary.

## Why it'd matter for mu

Today mu's tool surface is built-in: bash, edit, grep, glob, read, write (in crates/mu-coding/src/tools/). Adding a new tool requires modifying the mu binary. MCP would let third-party tools register at session start (specified in config or session.delegate request).

## Relation to existing mu work

- **Capability-broker pattern** (memory 03b44633): MCP would be the WIRE surface; the capability broker is the SECURITY primitive. They compose — MCP tools get the same Capability-typed grants as built-in tools.
- **Skill activation**: mu's skill system already does dynamic capability registration via the rope (SkillActivation SpanKind, mu-ovl). MCP could be one shape of skill: 'this skill activates an MCP server and exposes its tools.'
- **AwsCapability** (mu-f5o): each MCP tool that touches AWS would need an attached AwsCapability for the broker to enforce. Same axis-based attenuation.

## Scope (when picked up)

IN (v1):
- MCP client implementation (probably stdio first — simplest transport, runs the tool server as a subprocess of mu serve)
- Tool registration during session setup
- Routing tool calls through the Tool trait surface mu already has
- Capability enforcement at the tool-dispatch layer (existing infrastructure)

OUT:
- HTTP/SSE transports (later, after stdio works)
- Built-in MCP server registry (operator manages config)
- Authentication / authorization beyond what Capability provides

## Trigger to actually pick this up

When a third-party tool (or a tool you want to write outside mu's tree) is needed. Today everything mu does is in-tree; until that pressure shows up, MCP is overkill.

## References

- MCP spec: https://modelcontextprotocol.io
- Crush's MCP integration: https://github.com/charmbracelet/crush (Go reference)
- Anthropic SDK MCP support: should already work in mu's anthropic provider — just need the client end to call/discover MCP tools
- Pattern shelf: mu-zh1 (Crush as the design reference)
- Security primitive: memory 03b44633 (capability_broker_pattern)

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: 2026-05-27: Verified from Opus mu-solo session that mu-solo sessions currently have ZERO MCP tools — only the 6 base tools (read/write/edit/glob/grep/bash). MCP servers exist and are healthy (code-index HTTP responds, orchestrator/warden/snake configured) but the daemon does not connect to them. Architecture decision: MCP client layer belongs in mu-serve (daemon), not mu-solo (TUI). The daemon discovers and registers MCP tools alongside built-ins. mu-solo renders what the daemon exposes. The rmcp client infrastructure landed today (status subscription uses rmcp ClientHandler). Same pattern extends to daemon connecting to external MCP servers. Two-tier model: per-session tools (stdio, each session spawns own) vs shared services (HTTP, one process many clients). See memory bb8f72af.

comments.text: P1 plan (validated 2026-06-03, terrain-checked end-to-end): mu-serve as outbound rmcp Streamable HTTP CLIENT to code-index-mcp (now permanent: rc.d service code_index_mcp at 10.1.1.172:7622/mcp, rmcp 1.7.0 both ends, tools code_recall+code_status; deploy artifacts in agent_tools code_index/deploy/). Conservative default policy on imported tools (discovery != trust). HTTP/shared-service tier only in P1; stdio per-session + auth layering + mu-ywr discovery filter = P2.

comments.text: P1 SHIPPED for review: PR https://github.com/sahuagin/mu/pull/158 — [mcp] config + McpClientRegistry-style importer (serve/mcp_client/) + RemoteMcpTool adapter + startup wiring w/ 5s per-server budget + collision skip. LSP path removed wholesale. Tests: stub rmcp Streamable HTTP server integration (full config->import->in-loop-call), degradation, unit translation/normalization, config parse. just ci green. P2 remains: auth layering, stdio transport, mu-ywr discovery filter.

title: queue-boundary timestamp pairs: generalize ProviderStatusUpdate's pattern to all internal queues
description:
Generalize the 'send/receive timestamp pair captures latency that hides in queues' pattern operator described 2026-05-16. mu already does this for the model-call path via ProviderStatusUpdate (AwaitingFirstToken→Streaming gives TTFT, Streaming→done gives streaming_ms). Extend to the other internal queue boundaries.

## Internal queue boundaries today

1. agent_loop → forwarder mpsc channel (events emitted, consumed asynchronously)
2. forwarder → SessionEventLog::append (synchronous but locks)
3. forwarder → NotificationWriter::emit (wire send to JSON-RPC peer)
4. session → mailbox post (mu-lho — local today, cross-daemon later)

None of these have endpoint timestamps recorded. Latency in any of them is invisible to current analytics.

## Pattern (cribbed from operator's production design)

For each queue boundary:
- Sender stamps a 'send_at_ns' (monotonic) at emit time
- Receiver stamps a 'receive_at_ns' at consume time
- Difference = queue-resident time
- A standalone timing event captures (boundary_id, send_at, receive_at) so analytics can aggregate per boundary

Operator's exact phrasing: 'the only place that latency can hide is in the buffers and network. They still are accounted for on round trips, but are only measurable by the send and recieve times on different services.'

## Implementation sketch

- Add a TimingEvent payload variant (or sidecar field) carrying boundary_id + send/receive monotonic timestamps
- Emit at every queue cross-point
- Analytics (mu-8ypx) aggregates: 'queue_latency_ms_p99 by boundary_id'

## Out of scope

- Cross-machine round-trip measurement (needs synchronized clocks — defer until mu has a real multi-daemon deployment story)
- Hardware timer (rdtscp) — std Instant is fine for v1; rdtscp is a follow-up if Instant overhead becomes measurable

## Cross-references

- mu-5g7i (TaskTelemetry — uses elapsed_ms today but no per-queue breakdown)
- mu-035 (ProviderStatusUpdate — already does this for one path; this bead generalizes)
- The 'event source identifier' bead (also from 2026-05-16 — same pattern conversation)

title: mu-tui: multi-line input expansion + buffer selection via xclip/editor escape
description:
## Problem

Two related input/output ergonomic gaps:

1. Input field is single-line; longer text scrolls sideways, making it
   hard to see/edit what's been typed. No surfaced way to use an external
   editor for longer messages.

2. No way to select parts of the transcript buffer for copy/paste into
   other programs. zellij solves this by dumping the whole buffer to the
   user's editor of choice via a screen+edit shortcut, plus xclip for
   clipboard integration.

## Proposal

### Input ergonomics

- Input field auto-expands from 1 line to up to 3 lines when text would
  otherwise scroll sideways
- Surface ctrl-x ctrl-e (or similar) for opening current input in $EDITOR
  for longer messages; tooltip/hint when input nears 3-line limit

### Buffer selection / clipboard

- Keyboard shortcut to dump current transcript buffer into $EDITOR
  (zellij-style) for selection/copy
- Integrate xclip (X11) / wl-copy (Wayland) / pbcopy (macOS) for direct
  buffer-to-clipboard flow when available
- Behavior respects runtime environment

## Acceptance

- Multi-line text input doesn't scroll sideways for typical message
  lengths (3 lines = ~240 chars at default width)
- ctrl-x ctrl-e (or chosen binding) opens current input in $EDITOR
- Buffer-to-editor shortcut works; can copy selected text into system
  clipboard via xclip on FreeBSD/Linux X11

## Cross-refs

- mu-to2 (mu-tui-input-history: arrow-up command history — different
  concern, same input field)
- Provenance: TUI feedback (mu daemon f3845422 session-2, 2026-05-15)

title: forensics: add CapabilityInflation outcome — tools_granted vs tools_actually_called
description:
Sub-bead 3 of mu-uvsq (overnight 2026-05-19).

## Scope

Add `Outcome::CapabilityInflation` — detect over-provisioned capability grants. When a task is granted N tools but actually calls <50% of them (with N ≥ 3 to avoid noise for narrow grants), classify as CapabilityInflation. Probable confidence.

## Why

Capability hygiene matters (mu-fvy0 north-star; capability invariants are mu's enforcement layer). A worker that's granted broad tools but uses few signals either:
- The operator over-provisioned the grant (capability hygiene issue, fixable upstream)
- The worker's plan didn't actually need that surface (suggests prompt + spec ambiguity)

Either way, surfacing it as a discrete outcome class lets the analytics layer aggregate the rate over time as a quality metric.

## Inputs

Already in TaskTelemetry envelope (event_log.rs:301-303): `tools_granted: Vec<String>` and `tools_actually_called: Vec<(String, u32)>`. The classifier needs these projected into TaskTelemetrySnapshot.

## Discriminator placement

After CI-based discriminators (CapabilityInflation is a HINT about quality, not a hard error — CleanSuccess still wins if CI passed cleanly). So in the discriminator order:

1-6 as today (terminal exits, narrative, lying, hollow, CI-based, unclassified-fallback)
**6.5** CapabilityInflation — only fires if no other class matched AND signal threshold is met (avoid double-classifying CleanSuccess as CapabilityInflation).

Or: CapabilityInflation can fire alongside other classes only in multi-classification mode (deferred — not in this scope).

For now: fires only as the FALLBACK before Unclassified. If the task is CleanSuccess by other criteria, no inflation note.

## Tests required

- Positive: granted=["read", "edit", "bash"], called=[("read", 5)] → CapabilityInflation (1/3 = 33% < 50%, granted ≥ 3)
- Negative: granted=["read", "edit"], called=[("read", 5)] → no inflation (granted < 3)
- Negative: granted=["read", "edit", "bash"], called=[("read", 1), ("edit", 2)] → no inflation (2/3 = 67% ≥ 50%)
- Edge: granted empty, called empty → no inflation (no grant means nothing to over-provision)
- Discriminator order respected: granted+called pair + CI all-pass → CleanSuccess wins (not inflation)

## Acceptance

- `Outcome::CapabilityInflation` variant
- TaskTelemetrySnapshot extended with `tools_granted` + `tools_actually_called` fields
- Discriminator placed correctly in the order
- Tests pass
- Documentation update

## Provenance

mu-uvsq scope; TaskTelemetry has these fields (event_log.rs:301-303) but the classifier currently ignores them. mu-fvy0 north-star wants capability hygiene as a first-class quality axis.

title: mu-7rk-b: Server-side BEARER auth handler + dispatcher (no enforcement)
description:
**mu-7rk decomposition phase B** — Server-side BEARER handler + dispatcher. **Records `AuthState` per connection but does NOT enforce it on protected RPCs (that's mu-7rk-c).**

## Why

With protocol types in mu-7rk-a, this bead lands the server-side handshake mechanics: a pluggable handler trait, BEARER implementation, and the dispatch wiring for `peer.auth_offer` / `peer.auth_initiate`. Enforcement and transport close are split into their own beads (mu-7rk-c, mu-7rk-d) so that this bead can land cleanly without the security/scope-expansion debate.

## Scope

### In

- `crates/mu-coding/src/serve/auth.rs` (NEW or update from v0 reference):
  - `AuthMechanismHandler` trait with `mechanism()`, `step_initial(initial_response: Option<&str>) -> AuthStepOutcome`, default `step_response()` rejecting multi-step
  - `AuthStepOutcome::{Done(Capability), Denied{code, reason}, Challenge{...}}`
  - `AuthRegistry` that **errors on duplicate mechanism registration** (per codex minor #1) — `AuthRegistry::new(handlers) -> Result<Self, DuplicateMechanismError>`
  - `BearerHandler`:
    - **Constant-time token comparison** via the `subtle` crate (per codex important #1) — store HMAC-SHA-256 digests of allowlisted tokens, compare candidate digests with `subtle::ConstantTimeEq`
    - **Token length cap** (e.g., 4096 bytes); reject with `MalformedExchange` (per codex important #2)
    - Default-empty allowlist fails closed
- `crates/mu-coding/src/serve/dispatch.rs`:
  - Handlers for `peer.auth_offer` and `peer.auth_initiate` RPC methods
  - Per-connection `AuthState` (records authentication; **NOT consumed by other handlers in this bead**)
- `mu-core::config::AuthConfig::Bearer { tokens: Vec<String> }`:
  - Custom `Debug` impl that **redacts tokens** (per codex important #5) — wrap in a `RedactedString` newtype or use `#[serde(skip)]` for Debug derivation
- Tests in `crates/mu-coding/tests/auth_smoke.rs`:
  - BEARER happy path (good token → `Done`)
  - BEARER bad-token rejection (bad token → `Denied{InvalidCredentials}`)
  - Unsupported-mechanism rejection (wire `Other("foo")` → `Denied{UnsupportedMechanism}`)
  - Malformed-exchange rejection (missing `initial_response` for BEARER)
  - Oversized-token rejection (per codex important #2)
  - Constant-time comparison (smoke test: timing variance bounded)
  - Duplicate-mechanism registration errors (per codex minor #1)

### Out

- **Enforcement of `AuthState` on session.*/mailbox.*/etc. RPCs** — that is mu-7rk-c (the load-bearing security bit)
- Transport close on Denied — mu-7rk-d
- Client-side flow — mu-7rk-e
- Rate limiting — mu-7rk-f
- Multi-step state registry (challenge/server_state_id machinery) — mu-7rk-g
- Any change to existing peer.hello / mailbox dispatch flow

## Acceptance

- All seven test cases above pass
- `cargo test --workspace` green; existing peer.hello + mailbox tests still green
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean
- `cargo fmt --check` clean
- New workspace dep: `subtle` (const-time comparison primitive); update root `Cargo.toml` `[workspace.dependencies]`. **One** new dep total. **No other** new workspace deps.
- Bearer token allowlist defaults to empty (fails closed for the BEARER handler itself)

## Universal don'ts

- Do NOT add a capability-enforcement check anywhere (that is mu-7rk-c)
- Do NOT add transport-close machinery (that is mu-7rk-d)
- Do NOT pin "unauthenticated session.* allowed" in tests — the v0 test that did this (per codex blocker #3) is the anti-pattern. Tests in this bead should be auth-mechanism-only and not assert anything about non-auth RPC behavior.

## References

- Codex review: `~/.claude-personal/experiments/codex-review-mu-7rk-output.txt`
  - Important #1 (constant-time HMAC), #2 (length cap), #5 (redact Debug), minor #1 (duplicate mechanism error)
- Reference diff (v0): jj rev `twztzykv 78978fdb` in `.delegations/mu-7rk-2026-05-15` — `BearerHandler` skeleton can be reused; replace `HashSet::contains` with HMAC + `subtle`
- Spec for SASL framing: RFC 4422 (conceptual model only — wire is JSON-RPC, not SASL)
- BEARER mechanism: RFC 7628
close_reason: Merged in PR #66 (real, this time) — mu-7rk-b BEARER handler + dispatcher. br dep graph still inverted (mu-5pn/mu-fnn/mu-oeo listed as 'blocking' when they're downstream consumers).

title: Wire ProviderMessages projection into provider.stream() — finish the rope→provider cutover
description:
## Problem

The rope/render/compaction/cache machinery in `mu-core` is built and wired into the agent loop **as a projection and event source**, but the actual provider call still goes through the un-projected `Vec<AgentMessage>` path. The computed `ProviderMessages` is annotated with cache boundaries and then literally discarded.

Verified against `mu` HEAD `c0eeaadf` on 2026-05-19.

## Specific evidence

In `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/mod.rs:770-848`, the live path is:

```rust
let rope = ... // assemble_rope()
let pre_compaction_tokens = renderer.estimate_tokens(&rope);
let rope = if pre_compaction_tokens > compaction_threshold {
    // ... compaction runs, returns either bg-started original rope OR sync-compacted result.rope
};

let mut projection: ProviderMessages =
    renderer.render(&rope, ProjectionTarget::AgentView);
let cache_boundaries = cache_strategy.boundaries(&rope);
cache_strategy.annotate(&mut projection, &cache_boundaries);
let _ = projection;                              // line 818: LITERAL DISCARD

// ... emit ContextAssembly event with span_count, cache_boundary_count, etc.

match handle_invoke_llm(
    provider.as_ref(),
    config.system_prompt.as_deref(),
    &messages,                                   // line 847: un-projected Vec<AgentMessage>
    &tool_specs,
    ...
)
```

And `handle_invoke_llm` at `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/invoke.rs:22-49` calls:

```rust
pub(crate) async fn handle_invoke_llm(
    provider: &dyn Provider,
    system_prompt: Option<&str>,
    messages: &[AgentMessage],                   // takes raw AgentMessage
    tool_specs: &[ToolSpec],
    ...
) -> Result<(AssistantMessage, Vec<AgentInput>), Outcome> {
    ...
    let mut stream = provider
        .stream(system_prompt, messages, tool_specs, cancel_rx)
        .await
        ...
```

## Consequences (today, in production)

1. **Compaction does not affect what the model sees.** `policy.compact(...)` runs on the rope, the rope variable is updated, but the provider call gets `&messages` (the un-compacted source). The model's actual prompt is the full `Vec<AgentMessage>`.
2. **Cache boundary annotations are not sent to the provider.** `cache_strategy.annotate(&mut projection, ...)` mutates the projection, then the projection is discarded. The provider request has no cache hints.
3. **`ContextAssembly` events are accurate about what was *rendered*, not what was *sent*.** The events describe a shadow projection; the actual wire payload diverges.
4. **Architectural claims about context-as-projection / structural compaction are not yet load-bearing on the inference path.** They're correct for the audit/event-log path only.

## What the cutover requires

Replace the `&messages, &tool_specs` arguments to `handle_invoke_llm` (and through to `provider.stream()`) with the projected `ProviderMessages` (which already carries messages + tool specs + cache annotations in one shape). Specifically:

1. Change `handle_invoke_llm` signature to take `&ProviderMessages` instead of `&[AgentMessage]` + `&[ToolSpec]`.
2. Update each `Provider::stream` impl (Anthropic, OpenRouter, OpenAI Codex, faux) to consume `ProviderMessages` directly, OR provide a default adapter on the trait that splits ProviderMessages back into the legacy args until each provider is migrated.
3. Verify byte-equivalence of the wire payload before/after when no compaction is active (the AI's "ProviderMessages cutover test" — pre-cutover and post-cutover requests should be byte-equal if no compaction/operator-only transforms ran).
4. Add a test asserting compaction actually shrinks the wire payload when active (today this isn't testable).
5. Remove the `let _ = projection;` discard.

## Verification of this bead (anti-hallucination)

The AI in session-1 (daemon 2fc69e9a, 2026-05-19) flagged this gap during a project review. I (orchestrator session, claude-personal) verified the claim by reading `mod.rs:770-848` and `invoke.rs:22-49` directly — the code matches the AI's description. No hallucination. The bead body cites the verified file:lines.

## Cross-refs

- Session 2fc69e session-1 (event log at `~/.local/share/mu/events/2fc69e9a05512041/session-1.jsonl`) — the original analysis
- AI's quoted invariant violated: "actual provider wire path still uses raw messages/tool specs" — confirmed via direct code reading
- Related but DIFFERENT issue: rehydration / continue-session is partially done; not filed as a separate bead (operator tracking)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `handle_invoke_llm` takes `&ProviderMessages` (or equivalent type that carries compacted + annotated state)
- [ ] All `Provider::stream` impls (Anthropic, OpenRouter, OpenAI Codex, faux) accept and use `ProviderMessages`
- [ ] `let _ = projection;` removed from `agent/loop_/mod.rs`
- [ ] Byte-equivalence test: pre/post cutover requests match when no compaction is active
- [ ] Shrink-test: compaction-active sessions have measurably smaller wire payloads than compaction-off
- [ ] `ContextAssembly` event content matches what was actually sent (no shadow divergence)

## Priority rationale (P1)

The project's headline architectural claim — context as projection / structural compaction / cache-strategy as a policy — currently does not affect inference. That's the load-bearing differentiation per the AI's review ("the most technically interesting part of the project"). Without this cutover, the differentiation is shadow-only: visible in event logs, invisible in actual model behavior. P1 reflects that this gates the project from being able to honestly claim its biggest architectural win.
close_reason: done

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
## Pre-flight scope-finding (2026-05-20) — decomposed into 8 sub-beads

A goal-protocol pre-flight survey on 2026-05-20 found that the cutover as originally scoped ("2-3 iter cycles, swap parameter type") is materially bigger. The architectural gap: `ProviderMessage.content` is `String`-typed and the rope's `flatten_assistant` (`crates/mu-core/src/context/assembly.rs:118-170`) discards tool-call structure (`ToolCall.id`, `ToolCall.arguments`, `ContentBlock::Thinking`) at ingestion. Today's three real providers (Anthropic, OpenAI Codex, OpenRouter) all need structured tool-call data to produce wire JSON. The "wire adapter" referenced in `crates/mu-ai/src/context/anthropic.rs:17-20` was never built.

mu-fb0 (closed 2026-05-14 PR #16) appeared to wire the rope into the live loop but only added the render+annotate machinery *alongside* the legacy `&[AgentMessage]` path — the projection has been discarded at `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/mod.rs:818` since. mu-yqeq finishes that work.

## Plan

Full plan at: `/home/tcovert/.claude-personal/plans/expressive-painting-puffin.md`

## Sub-beads (8 total)

```
mu-yqeq.1   Spec mu-044 (no code)                                [no deps]
   ↓
mu-yqeq.2   Arc<str> migration (Span/ProviderMsg/ContentBlock)   [blocks: .1]
   ↓
mu-yqeq.3   blocks field on Span + ProviderMessage               [blocks: .2]
   ↓
   ├─────────┬───────────┬──────────────┬──────────┐
   ↓         ↓           ↓              ↓          (C-beads, any order)
mu-yqeq.4  mu-yqeq.5  mu-yqeq.6     mu-yqeq.7
Anthropic  OpenAI-    OpenRouter    Faux
(.C2)      Codex      (.C4)         (.C1)
           (.C3)
   ↓         ↓           ↓              ↓
   └─────────┴───────────┴──────────────┘
                       ↓
mu-yqeq.8   Cutover + retire legacy cache_control. Closes mu-yqeq.  [blocks: all C]
```

## Decisions locked

- Structural design: parallel `blocks: Option<Vec<ContentBlock>>` field on `Span` + `ProviderMessage`. Content storage moves to `Arc<str>` for cheap immutable cloning. `ContentBlock::Text { text: Arc<str> }` shares bytes between flat content and structured blocks. (Operator confirmation 2026-05-20.)
- Spec mu-044 (mu-yqeq.1) lands as a standalone PR with no code, for operator review of the structural lock + Provider trait amendment proposal, BEFORE any implementation begins. Honors Stop Criterion #9.
- Phase C ordering: Anthropic (.4) first to surface design issues; then Codex (.5), OpenRouter (.6), Faux (.7).
- Cache annotation: rope's `AnthropicCacheStrategy` (`crates/mu-ai/src/context/anthropic.rs:111-130`) becomes the sole source in Phase D; legacy `build_request_body` annotation retires.
- All sub-beads tagged `goal:2026-05-20:mu-yqeq`. mu-yqeq itself is the tracking bead.

title: mu-yqeq.0 — Write spec mu-044-provider-messages-cutover (no code)
description:
First sub-bead under mu-yqeq tracking. Write `specs/mu-044-provider-messages-cutover.md`. No code change.

## Why

Per `specs/process/goal-protocol.md:32-38` Stop Criterion #9: any Provider trait change requires a matching spec amendment. mu-yqeq changes `Provider::stream`. Spec lands first as the gate.

## What to document

- **Structural decision**: parallel `blocks: Option<Vec<ContentBlock>>` field on `Span` and `ProviderMessage`; content storage moves from `String` to `Arc<str>` for cheap immutable cloning; `ContentBlock::Text { text: Arc<str> }` enables byte-sharing between flat content and structured blocks.
- **Provider trait amendment**: add `stream_with_messages(&self, system, messages: &ProviderMessages, tools, cancel_rx)` with default-impl delegating shim. Existing `stream(&[AgentMessage])` retained through Phase D as `#[deprecated]` shim; cleanup follow-on.
- **Wire-equivalence requirement**: Phase C beads must produce byte-identical wire JSON to today's `translate_messages` paths. Lists existing test files (`anthropic_tests.rs`, `openai_codex_tests.rs`, `openrouter_tests.rs`) as the pinned regression suite.
- **Cache-annotation consolidation**: once Phase D lands, `build_request_body`'s `cache_control` annotation retires. `AnthropicCacheStrategy` (`crates/mu-ai/src/context/anthropic.rs:111-130`) is the sole source. Phase 0 must verify `AnthropicCacheStrategy.boundaries` produces a cache prefix at least as inclusive as the current live-loop annotation at `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs:232-240, 259-261`. If not, Phase A or D must extend the strategy before Phase D's deletion is safe.
- **Thinking-block rule**: rope tracks `ContentBlock::Thinking` blocks via `blocks = Some([Thinking{...}])` (visible to OperatorView), but wire adapters MUST filter them out of provider input.
- **Migration plan**: the seven sub-beads with dependency edges (graph in plan doc).
- **Risk register**: abbreviated form of the plan's risk register, top three.
- **Acceptance per phase**: A.pre passes cargo test workspace-wide; A passes round-trip test; each C passes golden-JSON regression; D removes the discard and the duplicate annotation.

## Acceptance

Spec doc lands in `specs/mu-044-provider-messages-cutover.md`. Operator sign-off via PR review. No code changes. Closes mu-yqeq.0.

## References

- Plan: $PLAN
- Predecessor bead: mu-fb0 (closed 2026-05-14 PR #16) — left the cutover deferred
- Tracking bead: mu-yqeq
- Architecture: `specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md`
close_reason: Spec landed: specs/mu-044-provider-messages-cutover.md (4 revisions). Commit svuxqkqn fd499d72 on PR #114.

title: mu-yqeq.A.pre — Arc<str> migration for Span/ProviderMessage/ContentBlock content fields
description:
Mechanical String → Arc<str> migration. Zero behavioral change. Foundational for mu-yqeq.A (blocks field) and Phase C (wire adapters) — both depend on cheap immutable cloning + byte-sharing between flat content and structured blocks.

## Why

The rope's content is constructed once at ingestion and never mutated afterward (verified — no `&mut span.content` in codebase). The clone hot path (`compaction_baseline.rope.clone()` at `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/mod.rs:807-810`, `bg_compaction.start(... rope.clone() ...)` at `:785-790`, `assemble_rope`/`append_messages_to_baseline` at `crates/mu-core/src/context/assembly.rs:106-116`) currently deep-copies every span's heap-allocated `String`. With `Arc<str>`, clones become 8-byte refcount bumps. Matches the immutable-event-log semantic exactly.

Critically: `ContentBlock::Text { text: Arc<str> }` enables byte-sharing between flat `Span.content` and structured `Span.blocks` (added in mu-yqeq.A) — they point at the same underlying memory through Arc.

## Touches

- `Cargo.toml` (workspace) — add `serde = { features = ["rc"] }` for Arc<str>/Arc<Value> serde compatibility.
- `crates/mu-core/src/context/rope.rs:162-206` — `Span.content: SpanText`, `Span.id: SpanId` (type aliases for `Arc<str>`).
- `crates/mu-core/src/context/renderer.rs:124-145` — `ProviderMessage.content: MessageText`.
- `crates/mu-core/src/agent/types.rs:82-100` — `ContentBlock::Text { text: Arc<str> }`, `ContentBlock::Thinking { text: Arc<str> }`. Optionally `ToolCall.arguments: Arc<serde_json::Value>` (can defer to follow-on if bead size is a concern).
- All `Span::new(...)`, `ContentBlock::Text { text: ... }`, `ProviderMessage { content: ... }` callers across `crates/mu-core/src/context/`, `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/`, `crates/mu-ai/src/faux.rs`, and tests — wrap literals with `Arc::from(...)` or use the type alias.

## Optional sub-split

If the diff is uncomfortable to review as one commit, split into A.pre.1 (Span + ProviderMessage types) and A.pre.2 (ContentBlock + ToolCall.arguments). Both still mechanical, both still atomic.

## Acceptance

- `cargo test --workspace` green.
- Zero behavioral changes — diff is pure type-substitution; no logic touched.
- `git diff` shows no `.push_str` or `&mut content` patterns — all reads use `&*content` or `&content as &str` via `Arc<str>: Deref<Target=str>`.
- Provider wire-format regression tests (`anthropic_tests.rs`, `openai_codex_tests.rs`, `openrouter_tests.rs`) pass byte-identically.

## References

- Plan: $PLAN
- Architecture rationale: operator-confirmed laziness level — eager flat content + eager structured blocks with Arc byte-sharing (option A of the laziness ladder).
- Tracking bead: mu-yqeq
close_reason: Landed combined encap+Arc<str> on feat/mu-yqeq; commit osklkypk 84a4bb4d on PR #114.

title: mu-yqeq.A — Add blocks: Option<Vec<ContentBlock>> field to Span + ProviderMessage
description:
Add structured-content storage parallel to the existing flat `content: Arc<str>` field. Additive, non-breaking, dead-code-but-populated until Phase C wire adapters use it. Foundation for the rope→provider cutover.

## Why

`ProviderMessage.content: Arc<str>` is flat — providers can't reconstruct structured tool-call data (`id`, `name`, JSON `arguments`, `tool_use_id` binding) from flat strings. Today's `&[AgentMessage]` path passes `ContentBlock` variants directly to each provider's `translate_messages`. The `blocks` field preserves that structure through the rope so Phase C adapters can consume `ProviderMessages` natively.

Block text fields share bytes with `Span.content` via `Arc<str>` (from mu-yqeq.A.pre) — storing both views is byte-cheap.

## Touches

- `crates/mu-core/src/context/rope.rs:162-206` — `Span { ..., blocks: Option<Vec<ContentBlock>> }`. `None` for non-assistant kinds; `Some(...)` for spans built from `AssistantMessage` or `ToolResult` messages.
- `crates/mu-core/src/context/assembly.rs:118-170` — `message_to_span` populates `blocks` for `Assistant` and `ToolResult` variants alongside the existing flattening into `content`. `flatten_assistant` itself UNCHANGED.
- `crates/mu-core/src/context/renderer.rs:124-145` — `ProviderMessage { ..., blocks: Option<Vec<ContentBlock>> }`.
- `crates/mu-core/src/context/renderer.rs:330-344` — `FauxProviderRenderer::render` clones `blocks` from span to message.
- `crates/mu-ai/src/context/anthropic.rs:75-89` — `AnthropicProviderRenderer::render` clones `blocks` through.
- `crates/mu-core/src/agent/provider.rs:53-72` — add a default-implemented method `stream_with_messages(&self, system, messages: &ProviderMessages, tools, cancel_rx)` that delegates to the existing `stream()` initially (transitional shim). Subsequent C-beads override per-provider.

## Acceptance

- `cargo test --workspace` green.
- `let _ = projection;` at `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/mod.rs:818` UNCHANGED — Phase A is behaviorally inert.
- New round-trip test in `assembly.rs`: `assemble_rope → render → ProviderMessage.blocks` reconstructs `AssistantMessage.content` byte-for-byte for assistant turns with tool calls.
- Compaction round-trip test: post-compaction non-absorbed spans retain their `blocks` field.

## ToolResult call_id recovery

`ContentBlock` does not have a `ToolResult` variant — tool results travel as `AgentMessage::ToolResult { call_id, content, is_error }` at the message level. The `call_id` is embedded in span IDs as `msg-{idx}-tool-result:{call_id}` (`assembly.rs:139`). Phase A includes a helper `fn extract_call_id_from_span_id(&str) -> Option<&str>` with tests so Phase C wire adapters can recover the binding.

## References

- Plan: $PLAN
- Tracking bead: mu-yqeq
- Spec: mu-yqeq.1 (lands first as mu-044)
close_reason: Landed nsslzrtz 7ac09ebf on PR #114. Bead/spec divergence resolved spec-side per memory b49d681c.

title: mu-yqeq.C2 — AnthropicProvider stream_with_messages adapter (consume ProviderMessages, emit identical wire JSON)
description:
Implement `AnthropicProvider::stream_with_messages` to consume `&ProviderMessages` (with `blocks` populated) and produce byte-identical wire JSON to today's `translate_messages` path. Anthropic is the hardest C-bead — most wire-shape transformations — so it lands first to surface design issues with `blocks` before C3/C4.

## Why

The current path `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs:69-100` consumes `&[AgentMessage]` via `build_request_body` (`:211-265`), which calls `translate_messages` (`:129-169`). Tool calls become nested content blocks (`:185-195`); consecutive tool results group into one user message (`:131-161`); system blocks hoist into top-level `system` field; last tool spec gets `cache_control: ephemeral` (`:259-261`).

The new path consumes `&ProviderMessages` and walks `blocks` directly (no string parsing, no `[tool_call:...]` annotation reconstruction). `ToolCall.id`, `ToolCall.name`, `ToolCall.arguments` come from the structural blocks.

## Touches

- `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs` — add `stream_with_messages` method consuming `&ProviderMessages`. New private helper `translate_provider_messages` that mirrors `translate_messages` but reads `ContentBlock` variants from `ProviderMessage.blocks` instead of `AgentMessage` variants. Keep legacy `stream(&[AgentMessage])` and `translate_messages` LIVE (deprecation in Phase D).
- ToolResult `tool_use_id` recovery: parse from `ProviderMessage.source_span_ids` using the helper added in mu-yqeq.A.
- Consecutive tool-result grouping: detect by walking `ProviderMessages.messages` for consecutive `ProviderRole::ToolResult` entries; group into a single Anthropic user message with multiple `tool_result` content blocks (matching `:131-161` shape).

## Acceptance

- `cargo test --workspace` green.
- Existing tests at `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic_tests.rs:8, 18, 109, 160, 193, 218, 255` untouched and passing.
- New "structural input → wire JSON" parity test: same scenario through legacy `translate_messages` and new `translate_provider_messages`; assert byte-equal output. Cover at minimum: pure text turn, single tool call, consecutive tool results (`b3_consecutive_tool_results_group_into_one_user_message` shape), system prompt + tools (`mu_n48_system_prompt_set_emits_content_block_with_cache_control` shape).

## Open question for Phase D

System block hoisting + cache_control: the new path emits one message per span including `ProviderRole::System`. Phase D will need to either hoist System-role messages into Anthropic's top-level `system` field in the wire adapter, OR rely on the rope having a single System-kind span at index 0 that the adapter recognizes and hoists. The choice ripples through cache annotation positioning. Document the choice in mu-yqeq.C2's PR description.

## References

- Plan: $PLAN
- Spec: mu-yqeq.1
- Tracking bead: mu-yqeq
close_reason: Landed ykykyznz d93927ac on PR #114. 5 parity tests pin byte-equality with Legacy.

title: mu-yqeq.C3 — OpenaiCodexProvider stream_with_messages adapter (consume ProviderMessages, emit identical wire JSON)
description:
Extend `OpenaiCodexProvider::stream`'s `MessageInput::Projected` arm to consume `&ProviderMessages` (with `blocks` populated by mu-yqeq.3) and produce byte-identical wire JSON to the existing Legacy path. Follows the spec mu-044 §"Provider trait amendment" direction (sealed-enum match arm split) — NOT the bead text's original "add stream_with_messages adapter" shim shape. The bead description above predated spec mu-044's 4th revision; the spec wins per memory b49d681c. mu-yqeq.4 (Anthropic Projected arm) is the canonical pattern; replicate that structure here.

## Reference pattern from mu-yqeq.4

- New private helper `translate_provider_messages_<provider>(&ProviderMessages) -> (Vec<Value>, Option<String>)` mirroring the existing `translate_messages`/`translate_message` for this provider. Reads `ContentBlock`s from `ProviderMessage.blocks()` instead of `AgentMessage::Assistant.content`. Hoists session system prompt via `source_span_ids[0] == "system-prompt"` discrimination. Recovers tool-call binding from `extract_call_id_from_span_id`. Recovers `is_error` from `"error: "` content prefix.
- New private helper `build_request_body_from_projection(model, &ProviderMessages, &[ToolSpec]) -> Value` paralleling the existing wire-body builder, only differing in how `messages` and `system_text` are derived.
- `stream()` impl: split the `_` arm into explicit `MessageInput::Projected(pmsgs) => build_request_body_from_projection(...)` + tightened `_` fallback. Keep `Legacy(msgs)` arm using existing builder.

## Touches

- `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/openai_codex.rs` — new helpers + the `Projected` match arm + tightened `_` arm.
- `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/openai_codex_tests.rs` — 5 parity tests (pure text, single tool call, consecutive tool results if applicable to OpenAI shape, system+tools, Thinking-block skip).

## Wire-shape notes for OpenaiCodexProvider (Responses API)

The OpenAI Responses API has distinct items for text and function_calls (NOT nested content blocks like Anthropic). assemble_content() at `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/openai_codex.rs:537-552` is the current legacy path's content-assembly. The Projected path replicates the same Vec<Value> shape from blocks.

## Acceptance

- `cargo test --workspace` green.
- Existing tests at `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/openai_codex_tests.rs` unchanged and passing.
- 5 new parity tests assert `assert_eq!(legacy_body, projected_body)` for the canonical scenarios. Pattern: see `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic_tests.rs:yqeq4_parity_*` (mu-yqeq.4).
- mod.rs:818 `let _ = projection;` UNCHANGED (Phase A behaviorally inert until mu-yqeq.8).

## References

- Plan: `~/.claude-personal/plans/historical/expressive-painting-puffin.md`
- Spec: specs/mu-044-provider-messages-cutover.md §"Provider trait amendment", §"Per-provider wire adapters", §"Thinking-block skip"
- Canonical reference implementation: mu-yqeq.4 (commit ykykyznz d93927ac, file crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs §"mu-yqeq.4: Projected path")
- Tracking bead: mu-yqeq
close_reason: done

Extend `OpenRouterProvider::stream`'s `MessageInput::Projected` arm to consume `&ProviderMessages` and produce byte-identical wire JSON to the Legacy path. Follows spec mu-044 §"Provider trait amendment" (sealed-enum match arm split) — NOT the bead text's stream_with_messages shim shape. Memory b49d681c covers the divergence rule.

Replicates the mu-yqeq.4 pattern (canonical reference: commit ykykyznz d93927ac, crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs §"mu-yqeq.4: Projected path").

title: mu-yqeq.C1 — FauxProvider stream_with_messages adapter (consume ProviderMessages, echo last User message)
description:
Extend `FauxProvider::stream`'s `MessageInput::Projected` arm to consume `&ProviderMessages` and produce a behavior equivalent to the Legacy "echo last User message" path. Follows spec mu-044 §"Provider trait amendment" (sealed-enum match arm split) — NOT the bead text's stream_with_messages shim shape. Memory b49d681c covers the divergence rule.
