title: mu-yqeq.D — Cutover at mod.rs:818 + retire legacy build_request_body cache_control. Closes mu-yqeq.
description:
The behavioral commit. Replace the discarded projection with a real handoff into the provider, and retire the legacy live-loop cache annotation now that `AnthropicCacheStrategy` is the sole source. Closes mu-yqeq.

## Why

Today at `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/mod.rs:818` the rendered + cache-annotated `ProviderMessages` is computed and immediately discarded (`let _ = projection;`). The provider call at `:844-851` uses the un-projected `&[AgentMessage]`. Phases A.pre/A/C1-C4 build the new path; Phase D activates it.

Once active, the legacy `cache_control` annotation in `build_request_body` (`crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs:232-240, 259-261`) is duplicated work — both `AnthropicCacheStrategy` and `build_request_body` tag the same prefix. Phase D deletes the legacy.

## Pre-condition (verify in mu-yqeq.0 spec, enforce in D)

`AnthropicCacheStrategy::boundaries` (`crates/mu-ai/src/context/anthropic.rs:111-130`) must produce a cache prefix at least as inclusive as the current live-loop annotation. The live-loop tags system block + last tool spec (TWO positions). The rope strategy tags ONE position (last span in stable+cacheable prefix). The doc at `crates/mu-ai/src/context/anthropic.rs:37-45` admits these strategies are not identical.

If the rope strategy is strictly less inclusive: extend `AnthropicCacheStrategy` (in this bead or as a Phase D.pre) to tag the equivalent positions before deletion. Otherwise integration test catches a regression in `cache_creation_input_tokens`.

## Touches

- `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/mod.rs:818` — remove `let _ = projection;`.
- `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/mod.rs:844-851` — replace `handle_invoke_llm(provider.as_ref(), system, &messages, &tool_specs, ...)` with the structured-input version that takes `&projection`. Either via direct `provider.stream_with_messages(...)` call or via an updated `handle_invoke_llm` signature.
- `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/invoke.rs:22-49` — update `handle_invoke_llm` signature to take `&ProviderMessages` instead of `&[AgentMessage]`.
- `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs:232-240, 259-261` — delete the legacy live-loop `cache_control: ephemeral` annotations on the system block and last tool spec.
- `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs` — mark legacy `stream(&[AgentMessage])` `#[deprecated]` (cleanup follow-on, not in this bead).
- `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic_tests.rs:193-298` — update tests that pin the system + last-tool annotation positions to reflect the new single-source annotation. Add integration test measuring `cache_creation_input_tokens` against a recorded fixture.

## Acceptance

- `cargo test --workspace` green.
- Integration test: multi-turn session through Anthropic provider. `cache_creation_input_tokens` from the response is identical or higher (never lower) than a pre-cutover baseline.
- Manual verification: long-context turn that crosses compaction threshold; confirm `CompactionAssembly` event fires AND post-compaction turn carries tool-call structure through to the provider (validates Phase A's compaction round-trip).
- `let _ = projection;` is gone.
- `cache_control` is sourced exclusively from `AnthropicCacheStrategy`.
- Closes mu-yqeq.

## References

- Plan: $PLAN
- Spec: mu-yqeq.1
- Tracking bead: mu-yqeq
close_reason: done

title: aws-catalog: Mu-side AWS capability catalog types and validation
description: Add the first narrow Mu-side bridge from AwsCapability names to an external capability catalog, without live AWS calls. Scope: typed serde structures for the JSON catalog shape, materialized-vs-planned validation, and unit tests against a small fixture. This enables future aws-recon skill/broker work to resolve `AwsCapability.name` before materializing credentials.
close_reason: Merged in PR #2 as fbaebd0; post-merge CI green.

title: mu CLI: 'mu tui' subcommand bails — every Opus reviewer concludes the TUI doesn't exist
description:
## Symptom (single most-impactful repo-perception issue per opus review)

In crates/mu-coding/src/bin/mu.rs:

  Command::Tui | Command::Orchestrate { .. } => {
      anyhow::bail!("this subcommand is not yet implemented; mu is pre-MVP. ...")
  }

But mu-tui exists as a fully-fleshed-out ~4500 LOC binary in crates/mu-tui/. Every Opus session that has read the repo (6+ so far per operator tally) concludes 'the TUI is aspirational, should be implemented or removed' — they don't know it's already the most-developed frontend.

## Why this matters

This is the primary cause of agent-perception drift: bail message + README 'TUI is intended surface' framing → reviewers reliably mis-describe the workspace shape. Every fresh agent burns turns re-discovering what exists.

## Three candidate fixes (pick one)

1. **Git-foo style exec**: 'mu tui' looks up 'mu-tui' on $PATH (or alongside its own binary) and execs it. Mirrors how git delegates to git-subcmd binaries. Lowest friction.
2. **Feature-gate into mu-coding**: pull mu-tui under mu-coding as a feature, expose Command::Tui as a real subcommand. Heavier; tightens the dep graph.
3. **Update the bail message** at minimum: 'Run mu-tui --mu-binary $(which mu) — the TUI is a separate binary in this workspace.' Buys 30 seconds; doesn't fix the underlying perception gap.

Author preference (per opus review): #1 — git-foo lookup.

## Acceptance

- 'mu tui' starts the TUI (or prints a one-liner pointing at mu-tui)
- Fresh-read agents stop concluding the TUI is unimplemented
- README's status section can drop the 'TUI is aspirational' framing

## Source

Surfaced 2026-05-17 by opus-4-7 review (session 3262c036eaca7daa, event 194). Quoted: 'The current state is the worst of all worlds: the code exists, but newcomers (human or agent) reliably mis-describe it.'
close_reason: Shipped this session: PR (commit svqxpzlw 0f3a91a1) — 'fix(cli): `mu tui` delegates to the mu-tui binary'. Verified via `just tui` smoke 2026-05-18: TUI launches, connects to mu serve, rehydrates 9 sessions in ~1s.

title: mu: discovery + permission model for memory/MCP/skills (information-leak-resistant)
description:
## Problem

mu has (or will have) several extensible surfaces: memory entries, MCP
tools (mu-yc6 / mu-71q), skills, and capability-gated resources. Agents
need a way to list/discover what's available to them, but the discovery
mechanism itself is a security boundary:

- Naively exposing the full list (with blocked items marked) leaks the
  existence of restricted capabilities, which is itself information
  disclosure
- A multi-tenant or supervisor-of-many-agents setup amplifies this: an
  agent learning "there's an MCP server called `aws-prod-admin` but you
  can't use it" gives them attack surface they wouldn't otherwise have

## Design

### Two policies, default is the safer one

(a) **Filtered discovery (default):** agents see only what they have
    access to. The existence of items outside their capability set is
    not disclosed.

(b) **Marked discovery (opt-in):** agents see the full list with blocked
    items marked as inaccessible. Useful in single-tenant trusted
    contexts where the operator wants visibility into the full surface.
    Not the default. Marked as a security trade-off in docs.

Configuration is per-capability-axis (memory, MCP, skills) and per-agent-
context, so an enterprise can choose (a) for production multi-agent
topologies and (b) for development.

### Surfaces this applies to

- **Memory entries**: `recall` / list operations filter by capability
- **MCP tools**: tool-list / introspection filters by capability (ties
  into mu-yc6's tool registration)
- **Skills**: skill discovery filters by capability
- **Generally, anything capability-gated**: discovery surface enforces
  the same gate as the use surface

### Information-leak resistance

- Don't return counts that disclose existence ("3 available, 7 filtered"
  leaks)
- Don't return placeholder slots ("[restricted]" entries leak)
- Don't return error messages that confirm existence ("permission denied
  for X" leaks; "no such X" is the response for both nonexistent and
  inaccessible)

### Discovery capability separable from use capability

Discovery is itself a capability ("can-list-memories", "can-list-mcp-
tools"), separable from the use capability. An agent might be able to
USE a specific item without being able to LIST the broader set, or vice
versa, depending on operator policy.

## Acceptance

- `recall list`, `tools list`, `skills list` (or equivalent surfaces)
  filter by capability under policy (a) by default
- Policy (b) opt-in is configurable per-axis and per-agent-context
- Filtered discovery does not disclose existence of inaccessible items
  via counts, placeholders, or error message differentiation
- Discovery capability and use capability are separable

## Cross-refs

- mu-hh1 (SupervisorCapability axis — substrate for the capability checks)
- mu-7rk-* (capability decomposition — provides gating primitives)
- mu-yc6 (MCP client — needs to enforce discovery filtering on tool list)
- mu-71q (MCP server adapter — needs to enforce on outbound tool exposure)
- memory 03b44633 (capability broker pattern)
- Companion to mu-memory bead (filed same session)
- Provenance: user feedback session 2026-05-15

title: BUG: cargo test --workspace fails intermittently — env-var mutation in tool tests races with parallel test execution
description:
## Symptom

`cargo test --workspace` fails non-deterministically; observed today (2026-05-11 ~19:00 local) with:

- tools::grep::tests::b4_count_mode
- tools::grep::tests::b6_case_insensitive

Same assertion: `assert!(!result.is_error)`. `cargo test -p mu-coding tools::grep` alone passes (11/11). Running b4 in isolation also passes. Task 19603420 reported all 278 workspace tests passing at 14:59 — the race window has just widened recently (more total tests = higher concurrency pressure).

## Root cause

`crates/mu-coding/src/tools/grep.rs::tests::rg_unavailable_returns_clean_error` (line 534) mutates the process-global `MU_RG_BINARY` env var:

```rust
std::env::set_var("MU_RG_BINARY", "/no/such/binary/rg");
// ... test runs ...
std::env::set_var("MU_RG_BINARY", original);  // or remove_var
```

Parallel `#[tokio::test]`s in the same test binary call `locate_rg()` which reads `MU_RG_BINARY`. Any test reading the var during the bogus-value window fails. b4 / b6 lose this race more often than b5; bash tests don't currently fail because their env keys (`MU_TEST_BASH_*`) don't interact with the rg path, but the same anti-pattern exists.

## Same anti-pattern elsewhere

`crates/mu-coding/src/tools/bash.rs` has two tests that mutate process env:

- tests::b5_strict_env_scrub (line 541): sets `MU_TEST_BASH_API_KEY`
- tests::b10_yolo_env_passes_through (line 598): sets `MU_TEST_BASH_YOLO_VAR`

These currently don't race against anything (no concurrent reader of those keys) but the pattern is fragile.

## Rust 1.74+ context

`std::env::set_var` is documented as not thread-safe and is `unsafe` from Rust 2024 edition onward. `cargo test` runs tests in threads by default. So any `set_var` in a test is a future-trap.

## Fix options

A. **Minimal (chosen for triage)**: `#[ignore]` the actual offender; document follow-up here. Loses one test path's coverage; that path ("rg not found") is structurally trivial. Done in commit landing alongside this bead.

B. **Durable**: refactor `locate_rg` so the rg binary path can be injected at `GrepTool` construction time. Tests pass a bogus path directly, no env mutation needed. Same shape applies to bash.rs (inject env scrub policy / passthrough policy as struct config rather than process env). Estimate: ~1 hour each.

C. **Standard fix**: add `serial_test` to workspace dev-deps; `#[serial]` the three offenders. Quick, adds one dep. Doesn't fix the root cause (env mutation in tests) — just serializes it.

## Suggested

Land A now (in this conversation's commit). Open follow-up beads referencing this one to do B for grep and B for bash separately.
close_reason: done

title: mu-ai (Anthropic): per-message output_tokens undercounted ~100x — final stream usage not captured
description:
## Symptom

Operator ran a 10-turn opus-4-7 'project review' in mu-tui (2026-05-17, daemon 3262c036eaca7daa). F5 metrics showed:

  out tok: 257
  in tok: 1,456,208

across 37 tool calls + 25 assistant messages. 257 output is ~100x too low: event 194 alone has 21,781 chars of assistant text (~5,500 tokens) but its message.usage.output_tokens reports 1.

## Per-message breakdown (proof)

From ~/.local/share/mu/events/3262c036eaca7daa/session-1.jsonl:

  event 194 text_len=21781  output_tokens=1
  event 228 text_len=8690   output_tokens=1
  event 221 text_len=6585   output_tokens=2
  event 214 text_len=4659   output_tokens=5
  event 235 text_len=4506   output_tokens=3
  event 249 text_len=3226   output_tokens=1
  event 242 text_len=2291   output_tokens=6
  event 158 text_len=820    output_tokens=1

text_len = chars. With ~4 chars/token, event 194 should be ~5,500 output_tokens, not 1. The ratio is consistent across all long messages: we're grabbing 1-6 tokens regardless of actual length.

## Hypothesis

Anthropic's streaming API emits multiple message_delta events with cumulative usage.output_tokens, terminated by a message_stop. We are likely:

(a) reading output_tokens from the FIRST message_delta (which contains only the initial 1-2 tokens), not the LAST, OR
(b) reading from a non-cumulative delta field, OR
(c) reading from message_start.usage (which only sets the input baseline + cache breakdown; output starts at ~0).

Code to inspect: crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs (or wherever the stream-delta loop builds the final Usage struct).

## Why now

This has been present since the static-demo era. F5 budget is also hardcoded (/usr/local/bin/zsh.00 / .00 per main.rs:376) — cost calculator not wired up. The output-token undercount has been masked by the absent cost calc. Operator noticed today during a deep-review session.

## Acceptance

- Final message.usage.output_tokens reflects the full output for streamed messages on Anthropic
- Same per-Done usage gets aggregated in event_log::cumulative_usage and surfaces in daemon.stats.total_output_tokens
- Test: a streamed Anthropic response of N tokens reports output_tokens >= N (within 5%)
- F5 metrics show reasonable output counts on real-world sessions

## Cross-refs

- main.rs:1448 — cost_usd: 0.0 hardcoded (related but separate; pricing-table wiring)
- event_log::cumulative_usage (correct logic, garbage in → garbage out)
- handlers/daemon.rs:67-68 — saturating_add aggregation (correct)
- Discovered in session-1.jsonl of daemon 3262c036eaca7daa (preserved for forensics)
close_reason: Merged in PR #75 (2650fd1d → main as commit per gh, auto-merge --rebase). All 4 CI checks green (fmt + clippy + test + verify-claims) at 2026-05-17T19:41:04Z. Regression test mu_yz48_message_delta_top_level_usage_is_captured passes.

title: agent-spawn: replace awk-based TOML parsing with tq (~/.cargo/bin/tq)
description:
## Observation

`~/.local/bin/agent-spawn:81-83` extracts API keys from `~/.config/agent/config.toml` using awk:

```sh
OR_KEY=$(awk '/^\[openrouter\]/{f=1} f && /^api_key/{gsub(/[" ]/,""); split($0, a, "="); print a[2]; exit}' ~/.config/agent/config.toml | sed 's/#.*//')
ANT_KEY=$(awk '/^\[anthropic\]/{f=1} f && /^api_key/{gsub(/[" ]/,""); split($0, a, "="); print a[2]; exit}' ~/.config/agent/config.toml)
```

This is awk doing the job of a TOML parser. The `gsub` + `split` + `sed` chain handles quoted values, spaces, and trailing comments by approximation — it's good enough today but it would break on any of: multi-line strings, inline tables, escaped quotes, `api_key` appearing as a sub-key of a nested table, etc.

`tq` (TOML query, like jq for TOML) is available at `~/.cargo/bin/tq` on the threadripper. The replacement is one line per key:

```sh
OR_KEY=$(tq -r '.openrouter.api_key' < ~/.config/agent/config.toml)
ANT_KEY=$(tq -r '.anthropic.api_key' < ~/.config/agent/config.toml)
```

(Exact syntax depends on which `tq` is installed — there are at least two with that name. Verify with `tq --help` before committing the exact incantation.)

## Tradeoff (operator note 2026-05-21)

awk is universally available; tq is not. agent-spawn currently runs on the host where tq is installed; it does NOT currently run inside pots that might lack tq. So the tradeoff is benign for the host-side script.

**If agent-spawn or a derivative ever runs inside a pot/jail/worker template** where tq is not installed, the replacement would need either:
- Add tq to the agent-runtime pot template
- Keep an awk fallback path: `tq ... 2>/dev/null || awk ...`

For now (host-side execution only), straight `tq` replacement is fine. Document the dependency in the script header comment.

## Scope

- Update `~/.local/bin/agent-spawn` lines 81-83 to use tq.
- Update the script's header comment to note the `tq` dependency.
- Spot-check that agent-spawn-v2 / agent-slot / agent-fanout don't have their own awk-on-toml; per current survey they don't, but a re-grep at fix time is cheap.
- If the same awk pattern is duplicated in `agent-spawn.pre-mu-2026-05-16` and other `.pre-*` backups, leave those alone (they're frozen backups).

## Acceptance

- agent-spawn extracts API keys via tq, not awk.
- Spawning a worker still succeeds (sequential single-task spawn end-to-end, per the validation criteria in memory `agent_spawn_v1_overnight_2026_04_30`).
- Header comment notes tq dependency.

## References

- Memory: `agent_spawn_v1_overnight_2026_04_30` (id `fab12f5a`) — the agent-spawn v1 wrapper background
- File: `~/.local/bin/agent-spawn:81-83`

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Update 2026-05-21: operator confirmed tq should also be installed in agent-runtime pot template. Filed as sibling bead **mu-9rx9** (blocked-by: mu-yzvi). The 'Tradeoff' section in this bead's description is now obsolete — tq will be uniformly available host-side and worker-side. Coordinate both fixes within the same template-refresh cycle so awk/tq don't end up split between host and workers.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Further update 2026-05-21: mu-9rx9 (worker-side tq) is now an audit-first bead — workers may not actually parse TOML, so installing tq there is contingent on the audit finding a need. mu-yzvi (this bead) is unaffected: host-side awk → tq is the standalone improvement.

title: mu-delegate-mailbox-back-channel: pre-issue mailbox.post handle from child→parent at session.delegate time
description:
Extend `session.delegate` (mu-031) so child sessions receive a pre-issued `mailbox.post` peer handle to their parent at creation time. Eliminates the awkward `peer.hello` round-trip a child has to do just to talk back to the parent that spawned it.

## Use case

An orchestrator O calls `session.delegate { parent_session_id: O, provider: ..., ... }` to spawn a child worker W. W is going to post results / status updates / questions back to O via `mailbox.post`. Today W has to:

1. Wait until it's running.
2. Discover O's session_id (it's its own `parent_session_id` from the delegate event).
3. Call `peer.hello { to_session_id: O, want: { method: "mailbox.post" } }` against O.
4. Receive a handle.
5. THEN it can post.

That's a 3-step bootstrap every child does, against the parent it was just born from. Parent-child relationships are already known at delegate time — the handle could be issued THEN.

## Scope

### In

- Extend `DelegateSessionRequest` with `grant_back_channel: bool` (default `true` for explicit-orchestrator workflows; `false` for the legacy "child doesn't talk to parent" case).
- When `grant_back_channel = true`:
  - At `session.delegate` dispatch time, the parent session's `MailboxState::issue_handle` is called with `peer_session_id = <child's session_id>` and `allowed_methods = {"mailbox.post"}` (and optionally a budget / expiry — see "Open" below).
  - The handle token is returned in a new `DelegateSessionResponse.back_channel_handle: Option<String>` field.
- The child session needs the token to be visible to it. Options (decide as part of the bead):
  - (a) Returned only in the dispatch response — the orchestrator forwards it to the child via the child's input channel as part of session setup.
  - (b) Recorded in the child's event log at session-creation time so the child can find it via `session.events`.
  - (c) Exposed via a new per-session RPC like `session.back_channel` returning the child's pre-issued handle.

  Lean: (b) — event-log-recorded means it survives restart (composes with mu-mh4) and is discoverable without an extra RPC.

### Out

- Multi-parent back-channels (a child has at most one delegating parent; that's the only back-channel granted automatically).
- Granting back-channels via `peer.hello` after the fact (the parent can already do this manually).
- Auto-renewal of expired back-channel handles.

## Open decisions (capture in PR)

- **Should the back-channel handle have an expiry by default?** Lean: no — match the child's session lifetime. Parent can revoke explicitly later (when revocation lands; out of scope here).
- **Should the back-channel handle have a call budget by default?** Lean: no — orchestrator workflows want unbounded child→parent posts (status updates, progress). Operators who want bounding can pass `back_channel_max_calls: Option<u32>` in the delegate request.
- **What if the child later calls `peer.hello` against the parent itself?** Should be allowed; multiple handles per (caller, target) is fine. No de-duplication needed.

## Acceptance

- `DelegateSessionResponse.back_channel_handle` is `Some(token)` when `grant_back_channel = true` (default).
- The child session can locate its back-channel handle via the chosen discovery mechanism (likely event log).
- The child can call `mailbox.post { to_session_id: parent, peer_handle: back_channel, ... }` immediately after spawn, with no intervening `peer.hello`.
- Test: delegate-then-immediate-post round-trip works without any `peer.hello` from the child.
- Existing mu-031 tests still pass; the new field is opt-in default-true but legacy callers can pass `false` for byte-exact backwards compatibility.

## Composition

- Builds on mu-031 (closed) and mu-lho (closed).
- Composes with "result-correlation fields" follow-up: the back-channel handle is what a child uses to post results; correlation fields document what shape those results take.
- Composes with the future "supervisor capability axis": grant_back_channel is one slice of the broader "supervised session relationship."

## Provenance

Filed 2026-05-14 / 2026-05-15. Surfaced during mu-lho orchestrator exploration as the second of three Tier-1 follow-ups.

title: conversation identity UX: model attribution, aliases, and interleaved multi-model view
description:
Front-end UX for tracking which model is responding in a conversation, especially across mid-session provider switches and multi-model workflows.

## Ideas (noodling stage, not committed)

### Model attribution in thread
- Prefix assistant messages with model identity: opus-4-7/daemon:session or similar
- Makes it obvious who said what on playback or when switching between models
- User messages could show you → model direction

### Named aliases / agent personas
- Claude Code has /agents directory with role-specific prompts
- mu equivalent: named agents (c137, claude-work, not-c137) that map to provider+model+system-prompt combos
- On playback, alias name shows instead of raw model ID
- Could compose with /btw sidecars — each sidecar gets its own alias

### Interleaved multi-model view
- Single pane showing responses from different models in the same conversation
- Color/prefix differentiation (magenta for /btw already exists)
- Unknown yet whether combined-in-one-pane or split is better UX — needs testing

### Structured logging for playback
- Sidechannel for ERROR/DEBUG/INFO alongside the conversation
- 'hey user, we don't have anthropic_api/haiku-4-6 but there is anthropic_api/claude-haiku-4-6' as a system note, not an error
- Event log already has the data; this is about how the TUI surfaces it

All front-end / mu-solo concerns. Backend already logs provider_kind+model per event and per ContextAssembly. No daemon changes needed.

title: CompactionAssembly not persisted; its claimed mitigation (RopeEvent log) also non-durable
description:
## Problem

`CompactionAssembly` events are explicitly excluded from the durable `SessionEventLog`. The comment defending this exclusion claims the `RopeEvent` log carries the per-span decision audit — but the `RopeEvent` log has no durable persistence either. The result: **compaction decisions for any given session vanish on process exit**.

Verified against `mu` HEAD `c0eeaadf` on 2026-05-19.

## Specific evidence

### Site 1: `crates/mu-coding/src/serve/forwarder.rs:582-587` (the `to_log_event()` function — durable-log payload mapping)

```rust
| AgentEvent::InputRequired { .. }
// mu-kgu.4: CompactionAssembly is an in-process operator
// event; the rope's own RopeEvent log already carries the
// per-span decision audit. No durable-log surface needed
// until a consumer (TUI / inspector) requires it.
| AgentEvent::CompactionAssembly { .. } => None,
```

`to_log_event()` mapping `CompactionAssembly → None` means it never enters the JSONL session event log.

### Site 2: `crates/mu-coding/src/serve/forwarder.rs:208-221` (the `translate_event()` function — wire-level JSON-RPC notification mapping)

```rust
// ContextAssembly lands only in the durable event log
// (mu-032 v1); wire-level exposure is a future TUI/web-ui
// feature when there's a consumer. mu-kgu.4's
// CompactionAssembly follows the same pattern — the rope's
// own decision-log carries the audit detail; the event
// surface is for in-process consumers (TUI / inspector).
AgentEvent::AgentStart
| AgentEvent::TurnStart
| AgentEvent::TurnEnd
| AgentEvent::MessageStart { .. }
| AgentEvent::MessageEnd { .. }
| AgentEvent::ContextAssembly { .. }
| AgentEvent::CompactionAssembly { .. } => None,
```

This is the wire-not-notified path — fine in isolation but the comment incorrectly bundles ContextAssembly (which IS durable per a separate code path) with CompactionAssembly (which is NOT durable anywhere on disk).

### Site 3: `crates/mu-core/src/context/event.rs:42-114` (RopeEvent definition)

`RopeEvent` is serializable (has `from_value` / `to_value`), but I cannot find any `attach_disk_writer`, `from_jsonl`, JSONL writer, or fs::write path anywhere in `mu-core` or `mu-coding` that persists `RopeEvent` to a file. Confirmed via:

```sh
grep -rn "RopeEvent" --include="*.rs" | grep -iE "persist|disk|jsonl|writer"
# returns empty
```

So the rope's "own decision-log" is in-memory only. When the daemon exits, the per-span compaction decisions are gone.

## Consequences

1. **Compaction decisions are not replayable.** Given a JSONL session event log on disk (the standard mu artifact at `~/.local/share/mu/events/<daemon-id>/session-N.jsonl`), there is no way to reconstruct which spans were dropped/summarized by compaction, what policy ran, or what tokens-before / tokens-after looked like.
2. **The forensics layer can't analyze compaction.** `mu analytics` projects the durable event log into SQLite. CompactionAssembly being excluded means analytics can't report on compaction-frequency, compaction-effectiveness, policy-outcomes, or tokens-saved.
3. **Post-mortem of a "why did the model see / not see this content" question is impossible** for sessions where compaction was active. The ContextAssembly event records span counts but not which specific spans were compacted out.
4. **Doc/comment drift.** The forwarder.rs comment claims a mitigation (RopeEvent log) that doesn't exist on disk. Future readers will believe the audit data is durable when it isn't.

## Urgency context — relationship to mu-yqeq

Today this is operationally minor because compaction doesn't affect the wire (per bead `mu-yqeq`). The model sees the un-compacted `Vec<AgentMessage>` regardless of what compaction decided. So compaction decisions are "policy-only," with no behavioral effect to audit.

**This gets more urgent immediately after `mu-yqeq` lands.** Post-cutover, compaction will shape what the model actually sees. At that point, "which spans got dropped from the wire payload in this specific session" becomes a load-bearing forensics question, and the audit data needs to be on disk.

## Fix sketch

Option A (minimal, recommended near-term):

Add `CompactionAssembly` to `to_log_event()` mapping in `forwarder.rs:399` so it lands in the durable JSONL session log alongside `ContextAssembly`. The event already carries `policy_id`, `tokens_before`, `tokens_after`, `decisions_count`, `wall_clock_us` — enough for analytics-level audit. Per-span detail can come later.

Option B (full audit, deferred):

Make `RopeEvent` durable too — either:
- Persist `RopeEvent`s alongside `SessionEventLog` (separate per-session JSONL or interleaved with `wall_clock_us` ordering)
- Or include the relevant `RopeEvent`s as a field inside the `CompactionAssembly` payload so they ride to disk together

Option A unblocks analytics/forensics for the common case; Option B is the architecturally complete fix. Recommend A first, B after `mu-yqeq` cutover when per-span replay becomes load-bearing.

## Comment fix (do as part of either option)

Update the forwarder.rs:582-585 comment to either:
- "...until a consumer requires it" → "...rope decision-log is currently in-memory only; persistence is deferred (see mu-XXXX)" — accurate
- Or remove the "rope log carries the audit" claim entirely if Option A is taken (the durable event log itself becomes the audit surface)

## Verification of this bead (anti-hallucination)

The AI in session-1 (daemon `2fc69e9a05512041`, session-1, 2026-05-19) flagged that `CompactionAssembly` was not durably persisted. I (orchestrator session) verified the claim by reading `forwarder.rs:399, 582-587` AND `context/event.rs:42-114` AND grepping for `RopeEvent` persistence paths. Code matches AI's claim and reveals the additional gap (RopeEvent log is also non-durable). No hallucination; the bead's two findings come from direct code reading.

## Cross-refs

- Bead `mu-yqeq` (P1) — provider-message cutover; this bead becomes more urgent once that lands
- Session 2fc69e session-1 — original analysis
- mu-032 v1 (per comment) — the work that made ContextAssembly durable; CompactionAssembly should follow that pattern

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `CompactionAssembly` events land in the durable `SessionEventLog` JSONL (`to_log_event` maps to `Some(...)` instead of `None`)
- [ ] Replay from JSONL: `policy_id`, `tokens_before`, `tokens_after`, `decisions_count`, `wall_clock_us` recoverable for every model call where compaction ran
- [ ] `mu analytics` (or equivalent SQLite projection) exposes a query for compaction-frequency / effectiveness over time
- [ ] `forwarder.rs` comment updated to reflect actual durability of the audit data (no stale RopeEvent claim)
- [ ] (Optional) Per-span decisions also durable, either via persisted RopeEvent or expanded CompactionAssembly payload
close_reason: Merged in PR #161: EventPayload::CompactionAssembly with full decision audit, to_log_event maps it durably

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Durability of all event kinds is a correctness requirement of mu-ki1f (event log as a first-class system), not a side bug — track under mu-ki1f. Keep P2: the proposed P1 was justified by the consolidator (mu-5xbp), which is deferred. Note: non-durable CompactionAssembly is also why no compaction trace appears in event logs even when the trigger runs. See Sequencing review in specs/architecture/agent-experience-frictions.md.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Fix shipped in PR #161 (2026-06-03): AgentEvent::CompactionAssembly now carries the full Vec<CompactionDecision>; new EventPayload::CompactionAssembly mapped by to_log_event (was => None). Tagged JSONL encoding pinned by regression test (kind=compaction_assembly, decisions[].action/reason greppable). Joined to post-compaction ContextAssembly by model_call_id. Wire notification still None (no consumer). Close on merge.

title: mu-tui-bubbletea-reference: evaluate borrowing MVU / command / widget patterns from bubbletea-rs ecosystem
description:
Pattern-reference bead: evaluate whether mu's TUI could benefit from MVU / command / widget patterns from the bubbletea-rs Rust ecosystem. Not an adoption bead — the framework itself is pre-1.0 (v0.0.9 as of 2026-05-13) and mu's TUI is already substantial (~2800 LOC in main.rs alone, multiple open TUI beads).

## Reference

Repo: https://github.com/whit3rabbit/bubbletea-rs

Ecosystem:
- bubbletea-rs (v0.0.9) — core MVU framework, async runtime
- bubbletea-widgets (v0.1.12) — 13+ pre-built widgets (spinners, inputs, tables, progress, etc.)
- lipgloss-extras (v0.1.1) — styling DSL (colors, gradients, layouts)

Inspired by Charm's Bubble Tea (Go), which itself draws on Elm Architecture.

## What might be worth borrowing (NOT the framework wholesale)

1. **MVU pattern.** Pure-functional state transitions + commands that return future messages. If mu's TUI does not already follow MVU naturally, this is a cleaner refactor target than ad-hoc event loops.
2. **Command abstraction.** Async operations returning future messages instead of spawning tasks and threading channels. Could simplify how mu handles long-running async work in the TUI.
3. **Widget composition idioms.** How bubbletea-widgets composes widgets into a view tree could inform panel composition for mu-pex / mu-ib5 / mu-cha / mu-jb3.

## What argues against adoption

- bubbletea-rs is v0.0.9 — pre-stable, APIs explicitly stated to be "still evolving"
- mu's TUI is established and being actively developed (multiple open TUI beads)
- ratatui is the de facto Rust TUI; switching to a wrapper invites maintenance churn
- lipgloss-extras's styling is duplicative with ratatui's built-in Style/Modifier/Color

## Trigger to actually pick this up

When mu's TUI hits friction that MVU would clearly solve, or when starting a new substantial TUI surface where greenfield architecture choice is open. Until then: reference only.

## Notes

Filed 2026-05-13 after Thaddeus shared the link as 'don't know if it'd be useful to use some of the constructs.' Not urgent; placeholder so the reference doesn't get lost.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Additional TUI pattern reference (filed 2026-05-13 alongside bubbletea-rs): **oatmeal** — https://github.com/dustinblackman/oatmeal. Rust + ratatui terminal UI chat app for LLMs.

Specifically worth mining when working on:
- mu-pex / mu-jb3 / mu-ib5: panel/render TUI work — oatmeal's chat-bubble rendering is concrete reference for conversation display.
- Multi-backend provider abstraction: oatmeal's backend layer (ChatGPT / Ollama) is a smaller-scope analog of mu's provider abstraction.
- Slash command UX: oatmeal has slash commands; mu has Ctrl-X Ctrl-P (mu-62s); compare ergonomics.
- Neovim integration: oatmeal has a real Neovim plugin path. mu-tui currently has $EDITOR-handoff (Ctrl-X Ctrl-P) but no deeper editor integration. Could be a future bead direction.
- Themes: oatmeal supports themes. mu has color polish in mu-u8a (TUI color polish — moonfly/github_dark family).

This is reference material, not adoption material — like bubbletea-rs, oatmeal is its own application with its own architectural choices. Worth pattern-mining for specific surfaces, not wholesale framework borrowing.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Concrete widget candidate (filed 2026-05-13): **throbber-widgets-tui** — https://github.com/arkbig/throbber-widgets-tui. Direct fit for rendering mu-035's ProviderStatusSnapshot states as animated spinner+label. Filed as its own implementable bead (separate from this reference shelf) because it's a small concrete adopt-the-crate task, not pattern-mining. See mu-tui-throbber bead.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Three more ratatui showcase widgets surveyed 2026-05-13 (rapid-fire by operator browsing https://ratatui.rs/showcase/third-party-widgets/):

**1. tui-scrollview** — https://github.com/joshka/tui-widgets/tree/main/tui-scrollview
Simple scrollable views. Target beads where this directly fits:
- mu-ib5 (F9 mailbox view) — long mailbox lists almost certainly need scrolling
- Conversation history rendering generally (whatever mu-tui does today for long histories)

**2. tui-term** — https://github.com/a-kenji/tui-term
Embedded terminal emulator as a ratatui widget. Bigger architectural fit:
- Render mu's bash tool output as a LIVE terminal pane (vs. capturing stdout and rendering as text). Would let the user see commands execute in real-time, with proper terminal control sequence rendering (color, cursor, etc.).
- Could also replace the $EDITOR-handoff (Ctrl-X Ctrl-P, mu-62s) by embedding the editor in a pane instead of suspending the whole TUI.
- Bigger lift than a typical widget; worth its own design bead before commit.

**3. tui-tree-widget** — https://github.com/EdJoPaTo/tui-rs-tree-widget (or successor)
Tree view widget. **Direct fit for mu-cha (TUI: implement F2 session tree view)** — that bead is literally about rendering the session tree.

Pattern across all four widgets surfaced today (throbber + these three): ratatui's third-party widget ecosystem is rich enough that several open TUI beads have ready-made off-the-shelf primitives. Worth a sweep through the ratatui showcase before greenfield-building each bead.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
**Charmbracelet** (https://github.com/charmbracelet) — design-language reference (added 2026-05-13). All projects are Go; Rust ports exist but are third-party. Their projects ranked by mu-relevance:

- **Bubble Tea / Bubbles / Lip Gloss** (Go) — MVU framework / widgets / styling. Already covered: bubbletea-rs port noted earlier in this bead.
- **Glamour** (Go) — Markdown-in-terminal renderer. **Concrete fit for mu**: filed as separate bead (mu-tui-markdown) using termimad as the Rust analog.
- **VHS** (Go) — terminal recorder for making demo GIFs. Not on mu's critical path; relevant only if/when mu has a public README with demo content.
- **Mods** (Go) — AI on the command line. Adjacent to mu / claude-code / oatmeal but server-driven and Go-stack. Reference for product-shape ideas, not architecture.
- **Gum** (Go) — shell-friendly TUI commands. Not relevant to mu (mu is its own TUI, not a shell-script helper).

The brand pattern: Charmbracelet cultivates terminal aesthetic as a design language (consistent typography in banners, VHS-recorded GIFs, careful color palettes). Useful as design-philosophy reference even when not adopting specific libs.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
**Crush** (https://github.com/charmbracelet/crush) — most directly mu-shaped product Charmbracelet ships. Multi-model TUI coding agent (Go, built on their Bubble Tea / Lip Gloss / Glamour stack).

## Feature mapping to mu (current state + open beads)

| Crush | mu state |
|---|---|
| Multi-model (OpenAI/Anthropic-compatible + custom) | ✓ mu has anthropic / openai-codex / openrouter providers |
| Switch LLMs mid-session preserving context | mu-4ge (open, P3) — session.set_provider RPC. **Direct match.** |
| Session-based, multiple per project | ✓ mu has (mu-935 FileBackend, mu-cha session tree view) |
| LSP-enhanced context | mu-f6z **needs re-scoping** — crush uses LSP for *automatic context enrichment*, not just agent-queryable lookup. Bigger primitive than I originally captured. |
| MCP-extensible (http/stdio/sse) | **No mu equivalent.** Filed as new bead. |
| Cross-platform incl. BSDs | mu works on FreeBSD; BSD coverage otherwise untested |

## UX patterns worth studying (when looking at the demo)

- How mid-session model switch is surfaced in the UI (mu-4ge will need this decision)
- How LSP enhancement is exposed — silently inject context, or surface a 'context: file.rs:42 included' callout?
- How MCP tool registrations are listed / managed in-session
- Session-tree presentation (compare with mu-cha's planned F2 view)

## Worth noting

- Crush is Go; mu is Rust. Direct adoption isn't possible. Reference value is UX/architecture.
- They market 'Industrial Grade' / '25k+ applications' — claims to validate. Crush itself is newer.
- Their LSP-context-enrichment approach is the strongest take I've seen on 'agents should see semantic code'. Mu-f6z benefits from this framing.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
**Harness-audit catalog 2026-05-14** (field-awareness, NOT roadmap items):

Per OpenAI / Anthropic / LangChain / harness-engineering.ai articles, these are widely-cited harness primitives mu doesn't have. Filed here as awareness only — if symptoms surface later, the prior art is named:

- **Dynamic tool scoping per phase / skill** (Vercel: removing 80% of tools measurably improved performance). mu exposes all tools to every session; skills could gate. SkillActivation SpanKind exists; runtime loader doesn't.
- **Compaction runtime** for the rope. Compaction SpanKind landed in mu-ovl; auto-emission when context fills + demoting old spans isn't wired.
- **Tool output offloading**: head/tail kept in context, full output to filesystem. LangChain pattern. Useful for long-bash-output scenarios.

What we explicitly DIDN'T file as feature beads (after antagonistic-check pass):

- Checkpoint-resume with precondition validation — too abstract for now; events are the substrate; precondition revalidation is implementation-detail-ish
- Background gardening (recurring /goal cleanup runs) — useful but not yet needed at our scale
- AGENTS.md auto-injection — already covered in spirit by per-repo goal-protocol.md

The ONE thing we DID file as actionable: per-step verification discipline (P2) — see mu-(whatever the new ID is) — because the compounding-failure math (95%^20 = 36%) is concretely backed by our /goal experiment failures, not just article claims.

title: goal-protocol pre-flight: permission + reachability smoketest
description:
Add a permission-and-reachability smoketest step to the goal-protocol skill's required pre-flight checklist. Diagnosed 2026-05-23 after mu-nbi0 first launch burned $7.13 making zero progress because the autonomous `claude -p` session couldn't reach declared data sources or invoke declared sibling CLIs.

## Gap

Current pre-flight (SKILL.md "Required pre-flight") covers:
1. Goal-tracking bead in br
2. Sub-beads tagged
3. blockedBy edges between sub-beads
4. Experiment doc atomic-created
5. Auth + budget + max-turns recorded
6. Exemplar selection

What it DOES NOT cover: verify the session's permission profile can actually do the work the experiment doc says it will do.

## Symptom that prompted this

mu-nbi0 launch on 2026-05-23: 15 permission denials before the agent gave up. Cross-tree Read denied (skill references, experiment doc, corpus). Sibling CLI Bash denied (br, cargo, task_log, agent). Even Write into cwd queued repeatedly. Cost: $7.13 / $10 cap for zero output.

## Proposed pre-flight step (new item 7)

Before `/goal` invocation, run a smoketest that:

1. **Reads the experiment doc + extracts declared data sources** (paths the experiment says the session will read)
2. **Tests reachability** of each path via a non-interactive probe (ls, stat, head, or a small `claude -p < /dev/null --tools Read --print 'list these paths'` shape) — using the SAME launch flags the goal session will use
3. **Identifies external CLIs** the prompt mentions (regex for known names: br, cargo, task_log, agent, in-spline, etc.) + verifies each is reachable AND in the permission profile's allow list
4. **Halts and warns** if any check fails: operator either adjusts launch flags (add --add-dir, add --allowed-tools, switch --permission-mode), pre-stages files, or accepts the limitation

## Companion update (in SKILL.md OAuth-verification section)

Add an invocation pattern that includes:
- `--permission-mode auto` (autonomous = no operator to approve)
- `--add-dir <each declared data source path>`
- `--output-format=stream-json --verbose` (for log visibility — see memory 5a4e0ebc)
- `--include-hook-events` (for richer observability)

## Why this is worth a separate bead and not just a comment

This is a load-bearing skill change with operational consequences. Every future goal session benefits. Single comment risks getting buried.

## Related

- Memory 6fa52297 (goal_protocol_permission_preflight_gap) — full diagnosis
- Memory 5a4e0ebc (claude_code_print_mode_observability) — companion gotcha about text-mode buffering

title: mu-phl v0 Phase C.2 — ProjectFileRecallProvider (reads CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md hierarchy)
description:
Step 4 of mu-phl v0. Implements the project-file recall provider.

## What

`crates/mu-core/src/context/recall/project_files.rs` — `ProjectFileRecallProvider`. Walks `FILES_IN_ORDER` (project-relative + global paths), expands `~/` and `./` against cwd, canonicalizes + dedupes, returns one `RecalledItem` per present file with `source: ProjectFile { path }`, content as `SpanText::from(file_content.as_str())`.

File hierarchy (read in order, all included if present):

```
./CLAUDE.md                    -- project root
./AGENTS.md                    -- project root
~/.claude-personal/CLAUDE.md   -- operator's personal global
~/CLAUDE.md                    -- shared global
~/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md          -- pi-rust agent rules
```

## Why

mu sessions get the same documented context that claude-code sessions get via SessionStart hook + claude-md hierarchy. v1's mu-phl ingest pipeline (idempotent on content hash, emits MemoryIngest events) replaces this with event-sourced equivalents.

## Acceptance

- Unit tests: fixture tempdir; 0 files / 1 file / multiple files; symlink dedup; missing-dir tolerance.
- Files read in declared order; canonicalization stable.
- One commit with `## Files` block and `(closes <this-bead>)`.

## Blocked by

- mu-phl v0 Phase B (step 2) — uses the `RecallProvider` trait and `RecalledItem` / `RecallSource::ProjectFile` from the recall module.

## Composes with

- mu-phl v0 Phase C.1 (step 3) — both providers will be instantiated together at the daemon. Independent implementations; can be parallel after step 2.
close_reason: Landed (Phase C.2) on feat/mu-phl-v0. ProjectFileRecallProvider reads CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md hierarchy. Default hierarchy = 5 templates (./CLAUDE.md, ./AGENTS.md, ~/.claude-personal/CLAUDE.md, ~/CLAUDE.md, ~/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md). Symlinks dedupe via canonicalize. Empty files skipped. Stable_id = blake3 12-hex of canonical path. 7 unit tests. cargo clippy -D warnings clean; 769 tests pass.

title: Recall provider: pass --tier identity + user-first ordering (mu-42x8 lever a) — small-kernel injection
description:
mu-side half of the identity-tier injection. The recall provider (crates/mu-core/src/context/recall/subprocess.rs) shells out to 'agent memory context --cwd ...' and injects the output wholesale (15.9K tok measured). Change: (1) pass --tier identity so only the kernel comes back (~600-800 tok); (2) user-first ordering of the injected slice (mu-42x8 lever a — identity content leads). Spans keep RetentionClass::Startup (standing prefix, protected by mu-tlri).

CROSS-REPO DEP (br can't edge across DBs): blocked by the agent_tools bead adding --tier to 'agent memory context' (tagged goal:2026-06-04:mu-ac5k in the at DB). Do the at-side first.

Acceptance: live mu session's ContextAssembly token_breakdown.memory_injection drops from ~15.9K to <=1.5K; injected content is the labeled identity kernel; full context still reachable via recall (sibling bead).
close_reason: Merged PR #172 — [recall].tier default identity; small kernel live; full restorable via TOML

title: file_load injection is now the dominant startup cost (~5,997 tokens)
description: From the mu-vfan measurement readout (2026-06-04): after the mu-ac5k injection rework dropped memory_injection 15,890→1,279 tokens, file_load at ~5,997 tokens is now the largest startup injection. Investigate whether the project-file hierarchy load can get the same treatment (tiering, on-demand recall, or trimming). Evidence: live faux session breakdown in the durable JSONL, task_log 68596153.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
WAVE-2 MEASUREMENT (2026-06-05, orchestrator solo half; design decision pending operator):

Loader: crates/mu-core/src/context/recall/project_files.rs (mu-zj4e), DEFAULT_FILES_IN_ORDER, whole-file v0, no parsing. Measured composition for a mu-repo session (estimates ~4 chars/tok; sum 6,030 vs the 5,997 observed in the durable breakdown — match):

  ./CLAUDE.md (mu)                 ~394 tok   project-specific, high value
  ./AGENTS.md (mu)               ~1,001 tok   project-specific
  ~/.claude-personal/CLAUDE.md   ~1,800 tok   LARGELY CLAUDE-CODE-SPECIFIC (3-account setup, t4c, bot-jj, scripts) — low relevance to mu sessions
  ~/CLAUDE.md                    ~1,308 tok   shared agent context (map-vs-terrain, task_log, memory CLI, claiming) — relevant
  ~/.pi/agent/AGENTS.md          ~1,527 tok   pi-rust rules loaded as operating-rules proxy

DESIGN OPTIONS (not mutually exclusive):
A. CONTENT CURATION (zero code, biggest single win): a mu-specific context file replacing ~/.claude-personal/CLAUDE.md in the hierarchy (the ~/.config/mu/ migration the loader docs already anticipate) — saves up to ~1,800 tok of off-target content.
B. TIERING like mu-ac5k: load path + heading-level summary + span-hash handle per file; rehydrate on demand. Pairs naturally with mu-or85's context_recall tool (same rehydration mechanism, would share implementation).
C. CROSS-FILE DEDUP: shared/global files overlap thematically; content work, no code.

PRIORITY CHECK (changes the calculus): post-#177/#178, file_load spans sit in the cached prefix — recurring dollar cost is ~0.1x reads, near-zero. The 5,997 tok cost is ATTENTION + context budget + first-write, not per-call dollars. zr9k is a context-quality bead more than an economics bead now.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: ESCALATION of this bead's stakes (operator + 2026-06-05 gate incidents): the ~7k startup injection (file_load 5,997 + memory ~1k + tool_schema 935) rides into EVERY mu ask — including ai-review gate calls to local models whose effective context may be 8k/32k. Evidence: the 8hva REJECT review consumed 17,392 input tokens for a ~5k-token diff; if ollama num_ctx truncates silently, gpt-oss:20b reviewed a PARTIAL diff and confabulated the remainder — which matches the measured FP signature (nonexistent paths, contradictory Cargo claims, multi-file diffs worst) better than random model dips. Parallel session is verifying the actual context maxes. DESIGN IMPLICATION for zr9k: beyond tiering/curation, ask-mode needs a per-session-kind injection policy — a code reviewer needs ZERO project-file hierarchy (the diff IS its context); small-ctx providers may warrant automatic minimal-injection. The review-gate quality issue (mu-ai-review-argv-e2big-bv1u) and this bead are now coupled.

title: aws-recon-skill: spec smallest end-to-end AWS capability demo
description: Specify the smallest end-to-end AWS capability demonstration: an aws-recon skill that activates a concrete AwsCapability, exposes narrow recon/planner/audit tool surfaces through the existing AWS sandbox runner/catalog, records capability/catalog/session metadata for audit, and deliberately avoids live AWS credential materialization inside mu-core. Doc/spec only unless a tiny bead reference is needed.
close_reason: Merged in PR #6 as 786307e; post-merge CI green.
